PREFACE TO SIXTH EDITION .
Following thecustom of issuinga revisionof CARROLL'S OFFICIAL CIVIL
AND CRIMINAL CODES OF PRACTICE of Kentucky every sixyearsthepresent
editionispublishedwith a view of enablingthe bench and bar to procure
thelatesttextof thepracticelaws.
All amendments to the Civil and Criminal Codes enacted since the last
revisionhave been inserted,and a very greatnumber of new notesof de-
cisionsof the Court of Appeals construingor affectingthe textof the va-
rious sections have been added . The annotations have included cases from
theKentucky Opinions,and thedecisionscitedhave been broughtdown
to volume 182 of the Kentucky Reports (January,1919),the crossrefer-
encestotheSouthwesternReporterbeingalsogiven.
A new and ,itisbelieved,valuablefeatureof the presenteditionisto be
found in theinsertion,insmallertype,of varioussectionsof Carroll'sKen-
tuckyStatutes,which relateto procedure or which affect, in any way , sec-
tionsof the Codes . In thismanner vexatious and unnecessary delays,
which might otherwise occur through necessityof consultinganother
sourceforstatutesgoverningcivilor criminalprocedure,willbe avoided.
An endeavor has thus been made to render this revision of the Code a
completevolume of the practicelaw.
Another new featurewillbe found in the cross references, in black-
faced type, to sectionsof Newman's Pleading,Practiceand Forms (3rd
Edition) where theparticularsectionof theCode iscitedor referredto,
thus enablingthe practitionerto secure from thisreferenceand the sub-
joinednotes the fullesttreatment of the subject. In likemanner refer-
enceshave been made to Judge Grider's"JudicialSales of Real Estate of
PersonsUnder Disability," underSections489,etseq. Also,underthose
sectionsgoverning appeals to the Court of Appeals in civilcases cross
referenceshave beengiventoJudge Miller'streatiseon "AppellatePrac-
tice and Forms ." The several sections of the Criminal Code have been
treatedby Judge Gregory inhis"Kentucky CriminalLaw and Proced-
ure," and copiouscrossreferencesto thisKentucky textbook willaidin
administrationof thelaw. As eachof thesebooks (exceptthatof Judge
Grider) containsnumerous Forms theselatterwill,with theForms con-
tainedintheback partof thiseditionof theCodes,form a valuablesource
of aid .
3

PREFACE TO SIXTH EDITION .
A complete tableof amendatory actswillbe found in the index. It is
believedthata decidedimprovement over former editionswillbe found to
be the insertionof the referenceto the chapter and page of the Session
Acts where the amendatory act and itstitlemay be found. This has not
been given heretofore .
The practicelaws and laws governing the variouscourts,the laws gov-
erning removal of actionsto the Federal Court, etc., as containedin the
Appendix ,have been insertedaccordingto a revisedplan.
The Index has been revisedand enlarged,thereby increasingitsvalue.
In allways the endeavor has been to prepare for the courtsand attor-
ney,and forallotherpersonsdesiringtoconsultthepracticestatutes,an
accurate, completeand thoroughlyusefulcopy of the law in annotated
form .
THE BALDWIN LAW BOOK COMPANY .
LOUISVILLE, KY .,January, 1919.
4

PREFACE TO FIFTH EDITION .
The firsteditionof theseCodes was preparedand issuedby me in 1888,and since
thattime,at intervalsof about sixyears,new editionshave been issued,the present
being the fifthone .
This editionhas been entirelyprintedfrom new plates, and containsallamend-
ments to date,and the annotationsincludeallcasesreportedin 150 Kentucky, bring-
ing the notes of decisionsdown to December , 1912. There has alsobeen added to
the notes citationsof the South Western Reporter , so that the citationsinclude the
Kentucky Law Reporter,the South Western Reporterand the Kentucky Reports.
The Kentucky Law Reporter, in which allopinionsof the court were published
either in fullor in abstract, was firstissued in July,1880, and continued untilMarch ,
1908,when itspublicationwas suspended. During thistime therewere issuedthirty-
two volumes- volume thirty-threewas commenced and a few casespublishedin it,
but as no index has ever been preparedto thisvolume the publicationof the Re-
portermay be saidto have ended with volume thirty-two.
The South Western Reporter,in which allthe opinionsof the courtare published,
was firstissuedin August , 1880- beginningwith volume eightof the Law Reporter
and volume eighty-four of the Kentucky Reports and itspublicationhas continued
to the present time .
Prior to September, 1910- when the weekly publicationby the State of the Ad-
vance Sheets of the Kenturky Reports was begun- the Kentucky Reports do not
containallopinionsof the court, only such casesbeing publishedin the Kentucky
Reports as the court directed, but beginningwith 140 Kentucky Reports, in Sep-
tember, 1910,allopinionsof the courtare publishedin fullin the Kentucky Reports
and in theAdvance Sheetsof the Kentucky Reports.
In theAppendix willbe found sectionsof the Kentucky Statutesrelatingto sub-
jects that are often used in connection with the Code .
NEW CASTLE , KY .,January , 1913.
JOHN D. CARROLL .
PREFACE TO FOURTH EDITION .
The Codes of Practiceeditedby me have met with a generous receptionfrom
the professionand I have endeavoredto make some substantialimprovement in each
edition .
This editionhas been revisedand printed from new plates, and containsall
amendments and actsrelatingto the Code , includingthose passed by the General
Assembly of 1906.
The annotationsincludeallcasesinthefirsttwelvenumbersof 28 KentuckyLaw
Reporter, citedas "R ,"- bringingnotesdown to March 15, 1906.
When a case ispublishedin the Ky . Reports and the Ky . Law Reporter, both
5

PREFACES TO THIRD AND SECOND EDITIONS .
publicationsare cited. The Law Reporterbeginswith78 Ky . and 116 Ky . isthe
lastReport issued, sinceitsissualthe Law Reporteralone iscited.
In the Appendix willbe found the rulesof the Court of Appeals and the sec-
tionsof the Kentucky Statutesrelating:
1.To the jurisdictionof the courtsof the State.
2. To courtshaving sixjudges.
3. To courts having continuous sessions.
4. To selectionof specialjudges.
5. To change of venue in civiland criminalcases.
NEW CASTLE , KY ., June , 1906.
JOHN D. CARROLL .
PREFACE TO THIRD EDITION .
This editioncontainsallamendments and notes of decisionsto July 1, 1900, in-
cludingcases reportedin 101 Ky . and 21 Ky . Law Reporter. The book has been
entirelyreprintedand revised, and allnotes willbe found in appropriateplaces.
The number of the sectionson each page are placedat top of page, and other im-
provements have been made in the arrangement.
In theAppendix willbe found the rulesof the Court of Appeals and the sections
of the Kentucky Statutesrelating:
1.To the jurisdictionof the courtsof the State.
2. To courts having four judges .
3. To courtshaving continuoussessions.
4. To selectionof specialjudges.
5. To change of venue in civiland criminal cases.
Also the laws of the United Statesconcerningremoval of actionsand prosecu-
tions to the Federal courts .
NEW CASTLE , KY ., Sept., 1900.
JOHN D. CARROLL .
PREFACE TO SECOND EDITION .
This edition of the Codes contains all amendments and notes of decisions to
January, 1895, includingcasesreportedin 95 Ky . and 16 Ky . Law Reporter. Un-
reporteddecisionsof the Court of Appeals found in the Ky . Law Reportersbearing
upon sectionsof theCodes have alsobeen inserted.
An index in the middle of a book seems out of place, and I have put indexes to
both Codes ,as well as forms ,at the end of the volume .
The catch words to sections, the indexes and the notes have been revised, re-
written and made more complete, and many forms not in former editionshave been
added .
In an appendix willbe found the sectionsof the Kentucky Statutesrelating:
1. To the jurisdictionof allcourts in the State.
2. To courtshaving fourjudges.
3. To courts having continuous sessions.
6

PREFACE TO FIRST EDITION .
4. To selectionof specialjudges.
5. To change of venue in civiland criminalcases.
Also the laws of the United Statesrelatingto theRemoval of Actions and Prose-
cutionsto the United StatesCourts,and the Rules adoptedby the Court of Appeals
of Kentucky .
In the citationof cases I have not assumed to criticizeor comment , nor under-
taken to say what the Court of Appeals will decide when it comes to construe sec-
tionsnot heretoforepassed upon, but have contentedmyself with statingthe law
as I have found it in the cases. My observation has been that the court does not
always adopt the views of the commentator, and that expressionsof opinionare
more misleadingthan useful.
The Kentucky Statutescontainsthe generallaws of the Statenot found in the
Codes,and the relationbetween the Statutesand Codes isso intimatethatI have in
every instance where either a section of the Codes or a note thereto contained any
referenceto the Statutes, or any sectionof the Statuteshad any bearing on the
Codes, citedand noted the sectionof the Statutesapplicable.
NEW CASTLE , KY .,March , 1895.
JOHN D. CARROLL .
PREFACE TO FIRST EDITION .
The original Code of Civil Practice was adopted by the Legislature in March ,
1851; thisCode was revisedand amended ,a Criminal Code prepared, and, in 1854,
the Codes of Practice in civiland criminal cases were adopted .
The presentCodes were prepared,under an actof the Legislature, by RichardA.
Buckner and Joshua F. Bullitt, Commissioners, and Alvin Duvall, umpire; were
adoptedby the Legislaturein 1876,and took effectJanuary 1,1877.
This editioncontainsallamendments to date, and a number of acts relatingdi-
rectlyto the subject-matterof the Codes. I have added at the end of the CivilCode
an appendix givingoriginaland appellatejurisdictionof courtsin civilcases; and
article2, chapter66, General Statutesrelatingto rent- mode and manner of re-
covery .
The notes of decisions of the Court of Appeals have , as a rule, been confined to
casesdecidedsince1851- but a number of notesof decisionspriorto thatdatehave
been inserted- and includecases reportedin 84 Ky ., bringingnotes down to Jan-
uary term,1887.
The sectionsin bracketsreferto correspondingsectionsin the Codes of 1854;
followingthe index of the CriminalCode isa tableshowing correspondingsections
of that Code and Code of 1854 .
I trustthatthe notes, referencesand forms willbe found complete, the arrange-
ment convenient, and that, as a whole, the book will meet with favor and prove
usefulto theprofession.
I desireto acknowledge my deep obligationsto Judge Joseph Barbour for val-
uablesuggestionsand advicefrequentlyaskedfor,and cheerfullygiven. To E. W.
Hines, Esq., I am indebtedfor hiscourtesyin furnishingme advancepages of
84 Ky .
JOHN D. CARROLL .
NEW CASTLE,KY ., September,1888.

THE
CIVIL CODE OF PRACTICE .
AN ACT RegulatingPracticein CivilActions.
Be itenactedby the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Ken-
tucky ,as follows :
TITLE I.
PRELIMINARY PROVISIONS .
§1 . Civil cases defined .
$ 2 . Civil actions defined .
§3 . Special proceedings.
§ 1. [Civil cases defined.) Civil cases are actions or specialpro-
ceedings .
Cross reference: See Newman's Pl.and Pr.(3d Ed .),sec.5.
§ 2. [Civilactionsdefined.] A civilactionisa demand,by pleadings,
ina courtof justice,fortheenforcementof an allegedrightof a plaintiff
againsta defendant. ("Action " furtherdefined,sec.732-34.)
Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr . (3d Ed .),sec. 5.
§ 3. [Specialproceedings.) Every othercivilcaseisa specialpro-
ceeding .
Cross reference: See Newman's Pl . and Pr . (3d Ed .), sec. 5 .
§ 2. (1) Civil actions. See note to
sec . 3 .
(2) Provisional remedy , such as an at-
tachment or injunction, is not a cause of
action, it is incidental to the action in
which it is sought , and can only be ob-
tained in the court in which the action
was brought or ispending at the time it
issought. Moore v.Sheppard, 1 Met .97.§ 3. (1) Special proceedings. The
word "action" embraces specialproceed-
ings. Secs. 732-34. In the absence of
any special authority for a summary
remedy the party must resort to a civil
action for the enforcement and protec-
tion of his private rights. Gay v . Mor-
gan , 4 Bush 606, and it is only in cases
where a specificremedy is provided by
statute that rightscan be enforced other-
wise than by civilaction. Stephens v .

§ 4. Forms , not substance, abolished
by Code ; and the pleader must state in
ordinaryand conciselanguage the facts
constitutingthe cause of action. Every
factnecessaryto enablethe plaintiffto
recover must be alleged,and every essen-
tialallegationrequired to make a decla-

be alleged so as to enable the opposite

proved , and also that an issue may be

§ 6. (1 ) Distinction between courts
of equityand courtsof law has not been
abolished by the Code . but if an action
be brought in equity when it should have
been in ordinary, or vice versa, it shall
not on that account be dismissed , but
transferred to the proper docket. Fraley
v. Peters , 12 Bush 469 ; and see sec. 8.

§ 7 [6] [Lost or mutilated writing- action on.] An ordinary or
equitableactionmay bebroughtupon a billof exchange,or a noteorother
obligation,or upon an indorsementor assignmentthereof,which,or upon
a judgmentthe recordof which,islost,destroyed,mutilated,or defaced,
without fraud on the part of the plaintiff, or of those under whom he
claims. But no actionshallbe brought upon an instrument transferable
by delivery, merely, which is allegedto be lost, destroyed, mutilated, or
defaced,without a previoustenderby theplaintiffto the defendant,ifhis
name and placeof residencebe known totheplaintiff,of an indemnifying
bond,withgood surety;norshalljudgmentbe givenagainstthedefendant,
insuchaction,untilsuchbond isgiven,withgood suretyapprovedby the
court. (Form of bond,Form 95.)

that equity has jurisdictionof actions

maintain a common law action for dam-
ages for an injuryto property, he may ,
in a proper state of case,resort to equity
to prevent the injury. Greenup Co. v .
M. & B. S. R. Co., 88 Ky . 659, 11 R. 169,
11 S. W. 774 .
(4) Appeal from county court- ap-

In an ordinaryactionproperlycommenced assuch-
1. [Transfer for trialof severalequitableissues.] If therebe sev-
eralissues,allof which were ,before the firstday of August , 1851, cog-
nizableinchancery,thoughnone was exclusivelyso,eitherpartymay ,

(6) Practice- Motion for new trial.
When an ordinary action is brought and
heard in equity the court will treat the
evidence copied and certifiedby clerk as
part of record without a bill of excep-
tions. Fraley v. Peters, 12 Bush 469,
and a motion for a new trial is not nee-
essary. City of Cov. v. Limerick, 19 R.

of chancery is submitted to a jury a

ster v. Fort Jeff. Improvement Co., 28 R.
504 , 89 S. W. 509 .
(7 ) Practice . See further notes to
secs.11,12.
(S) Waiver of objection to form of
action. If an action at law is brought
in equity, and neither party moves to
transfer,and it is not transferred by the
court,itis the duty of the court to render
judgment according to the rights of the
parties. Ky . Mut . Co. v. Turner , 89 Ky .
665 , 11 R. 793 , 13 S. W. 104 ; Kineon v .
Rich , 100 S. W. 249, 30 R. 1107. Turner
v . Newman , 19 R. 231 , 39 S. W. 504 , and
itsjudgment on the facts will be treated
as the verdict of a jury by the Court of
Appeals . Fraley v . Peters, 12 Bush 469 ;
Louisville,St. L. & T. Ry . Co. v . Taylor ,
96 Ky .241,16 R. 579,28 S. W. 666.
(9) Waiver of right to transfer- mo-
tion too late. Fritts v . Kirchdorfer, 136
Ky . 643 , 124 S. W. 882 ; Harmon v .
Thompson , 119 Ky . 528, 84 S. W. 569, 27

30 R. 1107; Lewis v .Helton , 144 Ky . 595,
139 S. W. 772 ; Gray Tie Co. v. Clark ,30
R. 409 , 98 S. W. 1000 .
(10 ) Harmless error . An error in over-
ruling a motion to transfer a cause to
the ordinary docket and a motion for an
issue out of chancery was held harmless ,
where the jury could not have come to
any other conclusion than that reached
by the chancellor. Riffe & Jones v . Mc-
Kinney Deposit Bank , 171 Ky . 757, 188
S. W. 775 .
(11) Quiet title- actions to. A peti-
tion in equity seekingto quiettitleal-
leged that defendants were in possession.
The action must be treated as a com-
mon law ejectment , and the decision of
the chancellor as the verdict of a prop-
erly instructed jury, although no trans-
fer was made to the ordinary docket.
Reynolds v. Binion, 177 Ky . 189, 197 S.
W. 641. If a suit to quiet titleiştriedas
an equity suit, and no objection or mo-
tion is made to transfer it to the ordi-
nary docket, objection can not be made
after judgment . Perry v. Eagle Coal Co.,
170 Ky . 824, 186 S. W. 875.(12) Amendment of character of ac-
tion. A petition in equity sought an in-
junction. An amendment converted the
action into an ejectment . It was prop-
erly transferred to the ordinary docket .
Harrod v .Armstrong ,177 Ky . 317, 197 S.
W. 816 .
§ 11. (1) Defendant not entitled to
transfer of an ordinary action, properly
commenced as such , unless the answer
presents a valid equitabledefense. Jones
v . Letcher, 13 B. M. 363, but see sub-
section 4, sec, 10, and note 4 thereto. A
defendant in an action to enforce a me-
chanic's lien was not entitled to a trans-
fer of the case to the ordinary docket
for trial of issues of fact. Rieger v .
Schulte, 151 Ky . 129, 151 S. W. 395.
(2) Equitable defense. When an an-
swer filedin an ordinary action contains
a valid equitable defense, no judgment

made of the answer , and in such case
the action should be transferred to
equity. Bosleyv.Mattingly,14 B.M. 89;
Harreld v . Howard , 80 Ky . 51, 3 R. 554 .
(3) Equitable and legal defense. If
the answer to an ordinary action pre-
sents an equitableand a legaldefense,

§ 12. [Equitableaction- trialof issueby jury- practice.] In an equi-
tableaction, properlycommenced as such, eitherpartymay ,by motion,
the equitable issue should be tried by
the court and the legal issue referred to

issues should be disposed of before judg-

ington, 6 Bush 192.

are formed in an ordinary action, the

issueto equity, becauseifthe equitable
defense fail, the defendant is entitled to
a jury to try the legal issue; but if the
equitable issue is a complete defense to

Ficklin, 14 Bush 193.
(5) Error in transferring ordinary ac-
tion. If the plaintiffin an ordinary

error to transfer action to equity, and he
is not concluded by the judgment dis-
missinghispetition, althoughhe failed
to take proof to support his cause of
action. Creager v. Walker , 7 Bush 1;
nor does a party waive his right to a
trialby jury by consenting to a transfer
so that certain issues may be triedwith
a pending equitable action. Betz v .New-
port P. M. A. ,6 R. 222. Issue as to title
to land in action to enjoin destructive
trespasstriableby jury and not to be
transferred. Higdon v . Wayne Co. Se-
curity Co., 154 Ky .337, 157 S. W. 708.

jury trial, and a party can not be de-

table right that arisesout of them . Car-

Bridge Co.,95 Ky . 633, 16 R. 244,27 S.
a
W. 251, 78 Ky . 435. See further note 4,

erly brought at law to equity for the
purpose of having alleged mistakes cor-
rected is not a reversible error when the
case is disposed of by the judge in the
same manner as if it had been tried by
the chancellor. Mullins v. Pendleton Co.,
6 R. 598 .
(8) Issues triableby jury- Practice,
right of partiesto jury. Moraneck v.
Martineck , 107 S. W. 759, 32 R. 971, 128
Ky . 155.
(8) Judgment of chancellor in equita-
ble actionis not entitledto the weight
of the verdict of a properly instructed
jury. Farmer's Bank v . Stapp . 97 Ky .
432 , 17 R. 290, 30 S. W. 1000 ; Scott v .

Campbell v. McCampbell, 103 Kỵ . 745,

21 R. 366, 51 S. W. 444; Campbell v.
Trosper , 108 Ky . 602, 22 R. 277, 57 S. W.
245. See further cases cited in note 3 ,
sec . 10 .

actionin equity and if they do, it will
be so treated on appeal and disposed ofon its merits as an equity case. Cornet
v .Burchfield, 142 Ky . 357, 134 S. W. 466 ;
Northrup v . Summers , 132 Ky . 156, 116
S. W. 699 ; Campbell v . Trosper , 108 Ку .
602 , 57 S. W. 245 , 22 R. 277 .
(10 ) Fraud - issue of. An issue as to
whether or not fraud existed in a trans-
action was one of which courts of chan-
cery had jurisdictionbefore the firstday
of August , 1851 , and a defendant is not

right. Gray v.Grimm , 157 Ky . 603, 163

(2) Issue out of chancery . If the issue
involved in an equitable action is purely
a legal one , and the equitable right de-
pends upon the decisionof such issue,the
case, on motion , must be transferred for
a jury trial, and the court has no dis-
cretion in the matter ; but if the case is
purely equitable, the court may or may
not, in itsdiscretion, obtain the advisory
aid of a jury as to an issue of fact. Car-
der v. Weisenburgh , 95 Ky . 135, 15 R.

son Bridge Co.,95 Ky . 633, 16 R. 244, 27

255; Reese v. Youtsey, 113 Ky . 839, 24

R. 840,70 S. W. 45 ; Blakey v. Johnson ,
13 Bush 197; Kennedy v.Ten Broeck, 11
Bush 241 ; Landrum v . Wells , 122 S. W.
214 ; Wisdon v.Nichols-Shepherd Co., 139
Ky . 506,97 S. W. 18, 29 R. 1128 ; Barnes

ton, 144 Ky . 595, 139 S. W. 772 ; King

Barton v . Barton , 142 Ky . 487, 134 S. W.
902; Comingor v . Louisville Trust Co.,
128 Ky .697,108 S. W. 950, 111 S. W. 681.
(3) Jury trial- effectofverdict. When
a distinct legal issue is submitted to a
jury in an action begun in equity and
transferred for the settlement of the
legal issue, the verdict of the jury is to
be treated as in ordinary jury trials, and
will not be disturbed unless palpably
against the evidence. Hill v . Phillips, 87
Ky . 169, 10 R. 31, 7 S. W. 917; Mora-
neck v. Martineck , 128 Ky . 155, 107 S.
W. 759 ,32 R. 793 ,and cases cited in pre-
ceding note; but when an issue of fact in
a purely equitableaction is submitted
to a jury,theirverdictisnot necessarily
conclusive, and the chancellor may dis-
regard it. Hill v. Phillips, 87 Ky . 169,
10 R. 31, 7 S. W. 917 ; MeElwain v . Rus-
sell, 11 R. 649, 11 S. W. 1067 ; Ford v.
Ellis, 21 R. 1837, 56 S. W. 512 ; Mora-
neck v . Martineck , 128 Ky . 155, 107 S.
W. 759, 32 R. 793, and cases cited in pre-
ceding note .
(4 ) Motion for a new trialand bill of
exceptions is necessary when an issue
out of chanceryis submitted to a jury.
Sims v . Lanehart , 19 R. 1439 .
(5) Motion to transfer comes too late
after the court has rendered a judgment
determining all the issues involved.
Hartford Ins. Co. v . Haas , 87 Ky . 531,
10 R. 573 , 9 S. W. 720 ; the motion must
be made in a reasonable time or it will
be denied. Chenault v . Eastern Ky . Т.
& L. Co. , 26 R. 1078 , 83 S. W. 552 ; Lewis
v . Helton , 144 Ky . 595, 139 S. W. 772 ;
Blackburn v. Simpson, 144 Ky . 503, 139
S. W. 758, and see further notes to
sec . 10 .
(6) Reversible error to refuse trialof
issue, when party entitled to jury .
Tucker v . Russell, 26 R. 1086, 83 S. W.
555 .
(7) See further as to trialof issues-
Secs . 312 , 313 and notes thereto .
(8) Several issues- separate answers .
Where several issues are submitted they
should be answered separatelyby the
jury. Bannon v. Bannon Sewer Pipe Co.,
136 Ky . 556, 119 S. W. 1170.
(9 ) Certain issues - when transferable
and when transfer improper . Trespass ,
in action to enjoin threatened trespass.
Consolidation Coal Co. v . Vanover , 166
Ky . 172, 179 S. W. 43. Mechanic'slien
suit. Rieger v. Schulte, 151 Ky . 129, 151
S. W. 395. Division of land . Williams
v. Capital Mining , Lumber and Oil Co.,
153 Ky .772, 156 S. W. 409. Adverse pos-
session. Winchester v. Watson , 169 Ку .
213,183 S. W. 483.
(10) Amendment changing character of
action. Harrod v. Armstrong, 177 Ky .
317, 197 S. W. 816.
(11) Issue must exist. No right to
transfer under this section exists unless
there be an issue. Keystone Com . Co. v .
City of Maysville, 154 Ky . 239, 157 S.
W. 25 .

itors may sue where assignee refuses .

against the carrier. Smith v . Lewis , 3

recover stock subscribed to the corpora-
tion before its organization, and made

Lackey v. R. & L. T. P. Co., 17 B. M. 43.

roads county may maintain action. Law-
rence County v. Chattaroi R. R. Co., 81

Menifee Co., 93 Ky . 33, 13 R. 891, 18 S.
W. 1021 ; and Ky . Stat ..sec. 1840 .

and if broken the right of action is in

nally v . White ,3 Met . 584 .

an obligationexecuted to his debtor by a

plaintiffor defendant. Lytle v. Lytle,2

ning v.Monk ,4 Met . 103; but where the

to set aside fraudulent conveyance made
by a bankrupt shouldbe in the name of
assignee. Anderson v. Anderson,80 Ky .
638, 4 R. 579 ; and see Ky . Stat., sec. 84.
(20) Guaranty ,action on. In an action
by a depositorof a bank on a guaranty
to pay the depositors the amount of their
deposits, the other depositors are not
necessary parties. Stedman v . Guthrie ,
4 Met . 147 .
(21) Heirs at law. Allegation that
parties to action are heirs at law of the
deceased under whom they claim is not
sufficient to show them to be the real
parties in interest. Dailey v. O'Brien,
96 S. W. 521 , 29 R. 811 ,

positor, actionto recovermoney should

(25) Obligation to two persons. Neither
has a separate right of action unless
other has assigned to him . Quisenberry
v . Artis, 1 Duv . 30 ; but see Perry v .
Perry ,Exr .,98 Ky . 242, 17 R. 868, 32 S.
W. 755 .
(26) Obligor and payee same person .
When a note ismade payable to one of
the obligorsand a third person, the third
person can sue in his own name . Quisen-
berry v .Artis, 1 Duv . 30.
Hughes
(27) Officer'sbond. Action on may be
brought by a party in interest in his
own name , if there is any covenant in
the bond to the benefit of which he is
entitled. Hughes v .Cotton , 13 Bush 596 ;
Lane v. Kasey , 1 Met . 410; Bottom v.
Ayres , 110 S. W. 385; or actionmay be
brought in name of Commonwealth for

(28 ) Order unaccepted . The holder of
an unaccepted order drawn by the holder
of a note upon the payor for part of the
note can not maintain an action on the
order againstthe payor. Weinstock v.
Bellewood , 12 Bush 139.
(28a) Ordinance of a city. Persons
who are neitherresidentsnor taxpayers
of a city can not raise any question as
to the invalidityof an ordinance of that
city on the ground that it creates an in-
debtedness not authorized by law . Pat-
terson v . Davis , 152 Ky . 530, 153 S. W.
780 .
(29 ) Payor and creditor may unite .
One who is bound for a debt by parol
undertaking may unite with his creditor
to whom he has paid it in an action
against person who received benefit of
debt. Dean v.English,18 В. М. 132.
(30) See further, notes to sec. 21.
(31) Stakeholder . Action under stat-
ute to recover money from a stakeholder
must be brought in name of principal.
Donahue v . McDonald , 92 Ky . 123, 13 R.
413 , 17 S. W. 195 ; Ky . Stat., sec. 1959.
(32) Telegraph message . Party to
whom a telegraphicmessage is addressed,
as well as the sender, may maintain ac-
tion against company for damages for
failingto deliver it. Chapman v. W. U.
T. Co., 90 Ky . 265, 12 R. 265, 13 S. W.
880 .
(33 ) Trust property . Where a deed of
trust vests property in the cestui que
trust subject to his debts, any creditor
may sue for his debt without making
other creditorsparties, or allegingthat
he is only creditor. Goldburg v. Dra-
belle, 4 Bush 426.

(4a ) Citizen- against public utility. A
water company contracted with a city

furnish protection to the inhabitants and
the property of the city against fire. A
citizenwho sustaineda loss resulting

perform itscontract has a cause of action

on statutory bond . Heath v . Com ., 129

ecuted to county court by tax collector

intheenjoymententof thefranchise. City

(8) Guardian to sue in name of ward .
Guardian can not sue in his own name
for personal property of his ward un-
lawfully detained. The suit must be in
name of infant by the guardian . Ander-
son v. Watson , 3 Met . 510 ; nor can he
maintain in his own name action to eject

lease; suit must be in name of ward .
Powell v . Gossom , 18 В. М. 179 , 13 В. М.

215; but he may sue in his own name

his ward . McChord v . Fisher, 13 В. М.
194; and may sue a former guardian .
Mahan v. Steele, 109 Ky . 31. 22 R. 546,
58 S. W. 446 ; and may sue to have land
sold. Ellis v. Smith , 147 Ky . 99, 143 S.
W. 776 .
(8a) Landlord . One who rents land in
own name may prosecute a forciblede-
tainer. Hickman v . Fordyce , 179 Kу .
737 , 201 S. W. 307 .

name a contract is made for the benefit

106 Ky . 386, 20 R. 1944, 50 S. W. 545 ;
McClure v. Bigstaff, 18 R. 601, 37 S. W.
294; Harth v . Farmer , 21 R. 1217, 54 S.

erty to which he holds the legal title,

ceive and hold the property for the bene-

ing latter party . B. & L. R. R. Co. v .
Metcalfe , 4 Met . 199 ; City of Cov . v .

holders can maintain suit for sale of

beneficiaries, the court making a dis-
tinction between this case and the Met-
calfe case . To the same effect is Union
Trust Co. v. Brashears, 19 R. 37, 39 S.
W. 44 .

loe, 83 Ky . 493, 5 R. 936 ; but where a

(14) Trustee to whom property is con-

253 , 20 R. 1169 , 48 S. W. 1074 .
(16) Trustee in bankruptcy - may sue
in his own name to recover assets . Com-
ingor v . Louisville Trust Co., 128 Ky .
697 , 108 S. W. 950 .

§ 24. (1) Administrator of debtor. In

Quigley , 2 Duv . 399.

(2) Assignee . In an action by the

56 ; 18 В. М. 9.

§ 25. (1) Construction of section. It
isonly where a suitinvolvesa common
or general interestof many persons that
one or more may sue for all. It isnot
sufficientthat the matters presentedby
thepleadingsraisea questionof law of
common or generalinterest, nor is the
action of the lower court in permitting
one or more to sue for all conclusive .
Oswald v. Morris , 92 Ky . 48, 13 R. 355 ,
17 S. W. 167 ; and see Alexander v. Gish ,
88 Ky . 13, 10 R. 989, 9 S. W. 801 ; B. &
L. R. R. v.Metcalfe,4 Met .199; and the
persons who bring the action must have
an interest in common with those for
whom it isbrought . Overton v . Overton ,
123 Ky . 311, 96 S. W. 469, 29 R. 736.
Persons who sued a gas company to re-
coverdepositsseverallyrequiredof them
as a condition to furnishing servicedid
not have a common or general interest.
Union Light,Heat and Power Co. v.Mul-
ligan; Same v . Tracy , 177 Ky . 662, 197
S. W. 1081. A person who has paid usury
can not sue for the benefit of himself
and other personswho have paid usury
on loans to which he was not a party .
Thomas v. Ky . Trust & Security Co.,
156 Ky . 260 , 160 S. W. 1037.
(2 ) Where the parties interested are
so numerous as to vexatiously prolong
the suit and probably prevent a full
hearing , or it is impracticable to bring
all of them before the court in a reason-
able time , court may permit portion to
sue for all. Hendrick v . Money , 1 Bush
306; L. & O. T. P. Co. v . Ballard, 2 Met .
165; Robinson v. Robinson , 11 Bush 174 ;
Claflin v . Gibson , 21 R. 337, 51 S. W.
439; 4 Met . 199; and where partiesare
numerous and an action is brought by
some for benefit of all, unless others
object, it will be presumed they assent
to its prosecution. Flint v . Spurr , 17 В ..
M. 500. Trustee for bondholders , etc.
See note 12 to sec . 21 .

claims of the same character against a
city, one may sue for benefit of all. Gor-
ley v.City,23 R. 1782.

arate suits in an inferiorcourt against a

§ 26. (1) Construction of section .
Plaintiffholding two or more separate
obligationsagainst same parties may sue
upon all of them in one action, or if the
principal in two or more obligations is
same person and the suretiesin part the
same , he may in one action sue on both
obligations,omitting as defendants those
sureties not on each paper . Com . v .
Tate , 89 Ky . 608, 12 R. 9, 13 S. W. 117.
(2 ) Guarantors . Several signers of
contract of guaranty for the payment
each of $ 100 may be sued jointly. Wilde
v . Haycraft , 2 Duv . 309 ; Ky . Live Stock
Asso . v .Miller, 27 R. 39, 84 S. W. 301.
(3 ) Owners of property and city. The
city and owners of property fronting
streetsimprovedmay be sued in the
same action by the contractor. City of
Lou . v . Henderson , 5 Bush 515.
(4 ) Sub -lessee and assignee. A lessee
may sue jointlya sub-lesseeand his as-
signee upon a contract of the sub -lessee
to pay him rent. Trabue v. McAdams ,
S Bush 74 .
(4a ) Subscribers, several. In an action
to recover on subscriptionsfrom a num-
ber of subscribers, all may be joined as
defendants . Hastings Industrial Co. v .
Jones , 167 Ky . 714 , 181 S. W. 364.
(5) Warranty . Where severaljoinin
same deed, each warranting his undi-
vided interest, a suit against each for his
proportionof the amount due forbreach
of warranty is proper. Evans v. San-
ders, 10 B. М. 291 .
§ 27. (1) Joint obligors- separate ac-
tion against. The common law rule has
been changed by the Code, and persons
jointlyliablemay be sued separately.

Underwood v . Wood , 93 Ky . 177, 14 R.
129,19 S. W. 405.
(2 ) Bondholder - Mandamus - parties.
In proceedingby bondholder for man-
damus to compel city council to levy tax
topay intereston bonds,neitherthe tax-
payers , railroad company , that assigned
bonds , nor other bondholders are neces-
sary parties, Maddox v. Graham , 2
Met . 56 .
(3 ) Court may require new parties
made . When record shows that money
sought to be recoveredfrom a garnishee
belongsto one not a party,courtshould
requireowner brought before it. Fore-
paugh v.Appold,17 B. M. 626.
(4 ) In an action by a stockholder,
where the corporation was an indispen-

§ 29 ] [Title IIIParties to Actions Generally.
§ 29 [41, 257] [Claimantof propertymay be made party- practice.]
In an actionor proceedingfortherecoveryof realor personalproperty,
or for the subjectionthereof to a demand of the plaintiffunder an at-
tachment or otherlien, any person claiminga rightto, or interestin, the
propertyor itsproceeds,may ,beforepayment of theproceedstotheplain-
tiff, file, in the action,his verifiedpetition, statinghis claim and contro-
vertingthatof theplaintiff; whereupon thecourtmay orderhim to be
made a defendant; and upon thatbeing done,his petitionshallbe treated
sableparty,the courtsays: "It was not
proper for the court to require the plain-
tiffto bring the corporation before it.
This was plaintiff'sduty , as in the ab-
sence of the corporation no reliefcould
be granted." Shawhan v. Zinn, 79 Ky .
300, 3 R. 12.
(5 ) The court should order all persons
interested in the controversy to be
brought before it, although they have
agreed not to sue until the happening of
a certain event. Lou . v . Henderson , 5
Bush 515. See Com . v. Frankfort , 13
Bush 185. Gibson v. Higdon, 15 B. М.
209. And if, upon hearingthe evidence,
the court should believe that a party
mentioned in the pleadings has such an
interest as to render him a necessary
party , he should be brought in. Van-
buskirk v. Levy , 3 Met . 134 ; Board of
Education v. Allin, 134 Ky . 763, 121 S.
W. 676 .
(6) Court may determine controversy
without having allpersons interestedbe-
fore it when it can be done without
prejudiceto theirrights, or when their
rights can be saved . If this can not be
done ,such other persons must be brought
in. Johnson v .Chandler , 15 B. M. 584.
(7) When petition omits necessary
parties,the chancellorought , before final
hearing,notify the plaintiffand give him
reasonable time to supply defect. Ogle
v . Clough , 2 Duv . 145.
(8) Defect of parties- how objected to.
An objection to a pleading because of
defect of partiescan be made by de-
murrer ; secs. 92-4, or by pleading, sec.
118; and , if not made in one of these
ways , is waived , secs. 92, 118 ; Waits v .
McClure , 10 Bush 763, and can not be
made for firsttime in Court of Appeals .
Hardee v . Hall , 12 Bush 327 ; McAlister
v. Savings Bank , 80 Ky . 684, 4 R. 682 ;
10 Bush 23 ; 3 Bush 200.
(8a) Dismissalwithout prejudice. Ab-
solute dismissal for failure to bring in
necessary parties is erroneous. Puckett
v . Jameson , Sheriff, 157 Ky . 172, 162 S.
W. 801 .
(9) Misjoinderof partiescan only be
taken advantage of by motion to strike
out . Yeates v. Walker , 1 Duv . 84 ; Dean
v. English , 18 B. M. 132 ; 1 Met . 123.
(10) Lienholders to be made parties.
Vendor of land who has a lien on it
shouldbe brought beforethe court in a
suit in which the land is attached by
creditor of the vendee . Mills v. Brown ,
2 Met .404. And where mortgaged prop-
erty is attached, the mortgagee should
be made a party. Martin v.M. & O. R.
R. Co.,7 Bush 116. See further,sec.694
and notes thereto .
(11) Payee not necessary party to an
action by his assignee to enforce lien.
Leacock v . Hall, 13 B. M. 211.
§ 29. (1) Claimant who presentshis
petition to be made a party , and is con-
sidered by the court and parties as a
party , and the action proceeds as if he
was ,will be so considered, although actu-
ally never made a party. Schwein v.
Sims , 2 Met . 209. In Taylor v . Taylor ,
3 Bush 118, it is held that a claimant
need not be made a party to the original
suit; with its litigationhe has nothing
to do . The issue to be tried as to him
is the validityof the claim he presents.
See also Miller v. Desha , 3 Bush 212 ;
Bank of Col. v.Overstreet, 10 Bush 148;
Hewitt v . Craig, 86 Ky . 23 , 9 R. 232 , 5
S. W. 280 ; Board of Education v . Allin,
134 Ky .763, 121 S. W. 676.
(2) Claimant should be permitted to
filepetitionand assert claim at any time
before the dispositionof the property
againstwhich claim isasserted. Murphy
v .Cochran ,80 Ky .239,3 R. 727 ; Heaverin
v .Robinson , 15 R. 15,21 S. W. 876 ; but
the petition must be verified and set
forth the claimant's cause of action .
Lampton v. Lewis , 1 R. 66 ; and see
Ritchie v . C. , N. O. & T. R. R. , 14 R. 831 ,
21 S. W. 641, where the court holds that
unreasonable delay in asking to be made
party isa waiverof right.
(3) Creditors of a person who has as-
signed for benefit of creditors can con-
trovertattachmentsleviedon his prop-

[A married woman may sue, and be sued, as a single
Cross reference : See Newman's Form No. 148 .
§ 34. (1) Construction of section. The
common law rule is in force except in
the cases mentioned in this section , and
wife can not sue alone in any case not
embraced by it. A next friend can not
prosecute a suit in the name of the in-
fant wife against the wishes of her in-
fant husband . Anderson v .Anderson , 11
Bush 327 ,but see Ky . Stat., sec. 2128 .
(2) The only object of this section (49,
same as sec. 34-1 of this Code ) is to
dispensewith the necessityfor a next
friend where the action concerns the
separate property of the wife , or is be-
tween herself and husband . It confers
no new right. Matson v . Matson , 4 Met .
262, and see Ky . Stat ., sec. 2128 , confer-
ring on wife power to sue and be sued
alone in all cases .
(3) Judgment against husband for
debt or tort of wife made before mar-
riage,see Clark v.Miller,88 Ky .108, 10
S. W. 277, 10 R. 691 ; Medley v. Tandy ,
85 Ky . 566, 9 R. 168, 4 S. W. 308 ; Fultz
v. Fox ,9 B. M. 499 ; Beaumont v . Miller,
1 Met .68; Agnew v.Williams, 1 Bush 4;
6 Bush 34 ; 7 Bush 214 ; and see Ky . Stat.,
sec.2130,fixingliabilityof husband . For
tort of wife, see Curd v. Dodd , 6 Bush
681; and Lane v . Bryant , 100 Ky . 138,
18 R. 658,37 S. W. 584 ; holding that hus-
band is not now liable for torts of wife .
(4 ) Marriage - effect of. An infant
female having married an infant pend-
ing the action, the court may make him
a co-plaintiffand allow action to proceed
in name of her next friend as next friend
of both. Hopkins v. Virgin, 11 Bush
677. The mere suggestion of the mar-
riage of female parties is all that is
necessary to enable the court to make
the husbands parties. Dickson v . Trout ,
8 Bush 441 .
(5) Personal judgment against mar-
riedwoman . It was held in Sweeney v.
Smith , 15 B. M. 325 ; Agnew v. Williams ,
1 Bush 4 ; Adams v. Jett, 6 Bush 585 ;
6 Bush 659, and other cases, that a per-
sonal judgment could not be taken
against a married woman ; but see now
Ky . Stat., sec. 2128, permittingmarried
woman to be sued as a singlewoman .
(6 ) Right of wife to sue husband . It
was held in Kalfus v . Kalfus ,92 Ky .542,
13 R. 763, 18 S. W. 366; that a wife
could not maintain an action at law
against her husband; nor can she sue
himhimininequity; Leahyv.Leahy,97Ky.

(9) Infant married woman can prose-
cute in her own name an action against
her husband for alimony or divorce .
Snedager v. Kincaid, 22 R. 1347, 60 S.
W. 522 .
(10) Next friend can not commute
debts or judgments due infant. Forbes
v .Brents , 1 J. J. M. 441; and next friend
may be changed by leave of court. Burks
v. Shain ,2 Bibb 341.
(11) Non -resident guardian of non-
resident infants may bring suit for sale
of land for indivisibilityunder Code ,
sec.490, sub-sec. 2, if thereto authorized
by the county court of the county in
which the land lies. The failure to file
before judgment a copy of the order of
appointment or of the order authorizing
the foreign guardian to act may be cured
by filingsuch copies after a sale and ex-
ceptions thereto. Baker v . Weaks , 178
Ky . 515, 199 S. W. 53. A foreign guard-
ian may sue under our Code for his in-
fant. Miller v. Cabell, 81 Ky . 178, 4 R.
962. But a non -residentguardian has no
power to sue for the infantor otherwise
act as guardian in this State, unless
authorized by a county court of this
State,as provided by sec.2041,Ky .Stats.
Watts v. Wilson , 93 Ky . 495, 14 R. 463,
20 S. W. 505; and see Williams v. Dun-
can , 92 Ky . 125, 13 R. 389, 17 S. W. 330 ;
Shelby v. Harrison , 84 Ky . 144, 8 R. 83.
A dismissal as to the guardian is a dis-
missal of the proceeding. Miller v .
Cabell, 81 Ky .178,4 R. 962.
(12) "Person of unsound mind ," as
used in this section, embraces not only
lunatics but persons whose minds have
become so impaired by disease or other
cause as to be unable to take care of
their own interests. Howard v . Howard ,
87 Ky . 616, 10 R. 478 , 9 S. W. 411 ; and
see further as to persons of unsound
mind , Shaw v . Dixon , 6 Bush 644, Ky .
Stat., sec. 2149 , and Barron v . City of
Lex ., 105 S. W. 395, 32 R. 92.
(13) If a person alleged to be of un-
sound mind appears and protests that he
is not incapable of managing his estate
and that suit is prosecuted without his
authority, the chancellor should impanel
a jury to pass upon the question of his
capacity. Howard v . Howard , 87 Ky .
616 , 10 R. 478 , 9 S. W. 411 .
(14) The trustee of a lunatic having
sold his land and appropriated the pro-
ceeds , a creditor of the lunatic sued his
sureties to recover the money . A suc-
cessor of the trustee, having subse-
quently brought suit to have sale set
aside as fraudulent , it was held that he
and not creditorhad right to choose rem-
edy and maintain action. Trunk v.East-
ernAsylum ,6 R. 521.
(15) Where in an action in behalf of
an infant lunatic brought by his mother
as next friend, the defendant pleaded
that the lunatic had a committee resid-
ing in this State , the refusal of the trial
court to abate the action is not preju-
dicial error where during the pendency
of the action the lunatic dies and the
mother is thereafterpermitted to filean
amended petition and prosecute the ac-
tion in her name as heir of the infant
lunatic. Skidmore v . Harris, 157 Ky .
756, 164 S. W. 98.

§ 36. (1) Clerical misprision. Where
no defense is made for infants or report
of guardianad litemfiled, the judgment
againstthem, as providedin sec.517, is
a clericalmisprision, and the errorcan
not be considered on appeal until motion
is made in lower court to correct it, as
directed in sec. 516. Morrison v . Beck-
ham ,96 Ky .72,16 R. 294,27 S. W. 868;
Palmer v. Husbands , 119 S. W. 762, 134
Ky . 152; Reynolds v. Steel, 170 Ky . 153,
185 S. W. 820,and notes 7-9.
(2 ) Guardian ad litem- duty of court.
Action should not be dismissedbecause
plaintiffneglects to have guardian ad
litem appointed for the infant defend-
ants . It is the duty of the court to ap-
point one whether the plaintiffdoes or
does not apply, and where an action is
submitted before appointment of guard-
ian ad litem court should set aside sub-
missionand appoint. C. & L. R. R. Co.
v.Bowler ,9 Bush 468 .
(3) Guardian ad litem should be ap-
pointedinbastardyproceeding. Chandler
v . Com ., 4 Met . 66.
(4) Answer of guardian ad litem sufli-
ciently complies with Code , although
statement "that after careful examina-
tion of case he is unable to make de-
fense" is not found in the body of an-
swer , but in jurat thereto. Vissman v .
Bryant , 14 R. 874 ,21 S. W. 759 ; Gardner
v . Letcher , 16 R. 778, 29 S. W. 868 ; and
see Womble v. Trice, 112 Ky . 533, 23 R.
1939, 66 S. W. 370; as to duty and an-
swer of guardian ad litem.
(5) Guardian should conduct defense
for his wards, although a guardian ad
litem may have been appointed, unless
there is some validreason for denying
him the right. Walker v. Smyser , 80
Ky . 620, 4 R. 662 ; Miller v. Cabell, 81
Ky .178,4 R. 962; but the courtmay ap-
point a guardian ad litem to defend ,
although there be a guardian . McMakin
v . Stratton,82 Ky .226, 6 R. 154 ; Adams
v .De Dominques , 112 S. W. 663, 129 Ky .
599; and seeTabor v.Tabor,98 Ky .173,
17 R. 739, 32 S. W. 414; Gardner v .
Letcher, 16 R. 778, 29 S. W. 868.(6 ) Infant- appeal by - bar . Infant
after becoming twenty -one prosecuted an
appeal to reverse judgment rendered

although a guardian ad litem filedan-

with Code .
(9) It was held that where an infant
was before the court by actual process,
prior to amendment to sec. 52, a judg-
ment for the sale of his land without
appointment of guardian ad litem was
voidable, not void. Schuk v. Stoll, 6 R.
364 .
(10) Judgment againstinfantwithout
proof or reportof guardian ad litem is
erroneous but not void. Berryhillv . Hol-
land, 99 S. W. 902. 30 R. 832 ; Siler v .
Archer ,26 R. 557,82 S. W. 256 ; Reynolds
v. Steel, 170 Ky . 153, 185 S. W. 820.
Judgment may be objectedto in Circuit
Court . Reynolds v. Steel, supra .
(11) Infants owing distinctclaims can
not be sued jointly or be united as de-
fendants in an action against them and
a personwho isguardianforallof them.
The cause of action against each being
separateand distinct,they must be sued
separately. St.Joseph O. S. v.Wolpert,
80 Ky . 86,3 R. 573.
(12) Infant who has arrived at age
may answer for himself, although his
guardian ad litem had answered for him
while an infant. Mitchellv. Berry, 1
Met . 602 ; and an infant whose action is
prosecutedby a next friendmay , upon
arrivingat age,be substitutedasplain-
tiffby a suggestionon the record. Clem-
entsv.Ramsey ,9 R. 172.
(13) Non -residentinfant. Judgment
against infantdefendant constructively
summoned without appointment of
guardianad litem,and beforedefense, is
not void. Simmons v. McKay , 5 Bush25; Myers v.Pedigro,24 R. 1923,72 S.W.
734; Oliver v. Park, 101 Ky . 1, 19 R.
179, 39 S. W. 423; Ramsey v.Keith,25
R. 582,77 S. W. 693; and see important
case of Powell v.Baer, 143 Ky .282, 136
S. W. 629 .
(14) Persons of unsound mind - who
are, see Howard v. Howard , 87 Ky . 616,
10 R. 478, 9 S. W. 411; and Ky . Stat.,
secs. 2149-2156, and notes. In an action
to sellland of a decedent for payment
of his debts (Code, sec. 428), a lunatic
who had no committee was a defendant .
Processwas servedby deliveringa copy
of the summons to him and to the per-
son having chargeof him . Before judg-
ment a committee was appointed,but no
service was made on the committee , nor
did he appear. A judgment directingthe
sale of land was void as to the lunatic .
Barry v. Fains,Admr ., 172 Ky . 308,189
S. W. 220 .
(15) Practice. Consent order that in-
fantsbe made partiesdoes not dispense
with service of process upon them , or
authorizejudgment againstthem with-
out defense by a guardian. Pond v.
Doneghy , 18 В. М. 558.
(16) Presumption . Where the record
is lost, but it appeared that an answer
was filed for infants who were very
young , the presumption is that it was
filedby theirguardian. Johnson v.Mc-
Dyer , 11 R. 28, 9 S. W. 778 .
(17) Counter -claim against infant-
process. No process was necessary on a
counter-claim against infant plaintiffs;
but a defense or report should have been
made for them . Whalen v. Hopper's
Gdn .,152 Ky .727,154 S. W. 40.
(18 ) Infant becoming of age pendente
lite. When an infant defendant becomes
of age during the pendency of the suit
the factsshould be suggestedof record.
Gray v. Grimm , 157 Ky . 603, 163 S. W.
762 .

§ 37. (1) Affidavit- next friend. Per-

the person who brings the action for the

or respond to the warning order. New-

§ 62 [93] [Real property- concerning.] Actionsmust be broughtin
the countyin which thesubjectof theaction,or some part thereof, is
situated-
§ 62. (1) Attachment - Land may be
levied on and sold under an attachment
in othercounty than one in which action
is pending in which attachment is ob-
ained . Nixon v.Jack ,16 B. M. 174. See
alsoWebb v. Wright ,2 Bush 126; Daw-
kins v. Hough , 112 Ky . 855, 23 R. 1997,
66 S. W. 1047,and sec.201 .
(2a ) Decedent's estate. An action
brought by devisees of a decedent against
the other devisees for sale of land de-
vised under the will and division of the
proceedsmust be brought in the county
where the personalrepresentativequali-
fied. Boering v.Melcon,159 Ky . 14, 166
S. W. 612 .
(2 ) Enforcement of contract concern-
ing land where it does not involve a sale
of the land to satisfya lien istransitory.
Henderson v . Perkins , 94 Ky . 207, 14 R.
782, 21 S. W. 1035 ; and see Bullitt v .
Eastern Land Co.,99 Ky . 324, 18 R. 230,
36 S. W. 16 ; Todd v . Lancaster , 104 Ку .
427, 20 R. 623, 47 S. W. 336 ; Collins v .
Park , 93 Ky . 6, 13 R. 905, 18 S. W. 1013.
(3) Infant'sland- jurisdictionto sell.
Circuit court of one county has no juris-
diction to sell land of infant in another
county on petitionof guardianof infant,
although land descended to infant from
his father, who died intestatein county
where suit brought, and his adminis-
tratorqualifiedin that county. Mont-
gomery v.Montgomery ,2 Bush 49; Gold-
smith v .Hieatt ,28 R. 741, 90 S. W. 259 ;
unless the action comes under secs. 65 or
66. See those sections and notes thereto .

§ 62 ] [TitleCounty in Which An Action Must or May Be Brought .
1. [Recovery of realproperty.] For the recoveryof realproperty,
or of an estate or interest therein .
2. [Partitionof realproperty.] For thepartitionof realproperty
exceptas isprovided in section66 .
(4) Injuryto land. Action for injury
to land- as cutting trees- must be
brought in county in which land is lo-
cated. Meehan v. Edwards , 92 Ky . 574,
13 R. 803,18 S. W. 454.
(5) Injury to property in another
statecausedby actsof wrongdoer in this
state. Action may be brought in either
State. Smith v. Southern Ry . Co., 123
S. W. 678, 136 Ky .162.(6) Injunction bond - action on is
transitory. Smith v. Miler, 140 Ky .308,
131 S. W. 5 .
(7) Insolvent estate. In action to set-
tle,court may order sale of land situated
in another county . Fishback v. Green ,
87 Ky . 107, 9 R. 959, 7 S. W. 881; Me-
chanics' Trust Co. v.Cobb ,14 R. 444,20 S.
W. 391 ; but see now sec. 65 as amended
and Reddick v.U. S. B. & L., 106 Ky .94,
20 R. 1720 , 49 S. W. 1075 .
(8) Jurisdictionin two counties- ac-
tionmay be broughtin either. Galloway
v. Craig, 29 R. 1, 92 S. W. 320, 122 Ky .
447; and see Cent . Ky . Nat . Gas . Co. v .
Stevens , 134 Ky . 306, 120 S. W. 282.
(9) Land in two countiesadjoining.
Action to recover may be brought in
eithercounty . Harlan v . Howard ,79 Ky .
373, 2 R. 368 ; and so where a mortgage
lienis sought to be enforced. Hendrick
v.Nesbitt, 96 Ky . 652, 16 R. 746, 29 S.
W. 627 .
(10 ) Land - to enforce lien on . Circuit
court of one county has no jurisdiction
to enforce lienon land in another county .
Webb v. Wright, 1 Bush 107; Stoferv.
U. S. Building& Loan Ass'n'sAssignee,
106 Ky . 94 , 20 R. 1720, 49 S. W. 1075;
Shieldsv. Yellman, 100 Ky . 655, 18 R.
1092,39 S. W ..30; unlessthe proceeding
in rem attaches as an incidentalremedy ,
2 Bush 126. See Doty v . Deposit B. &
L., 103 Ky . 710, 20 R. 625, 46 S. W. 219 ;
Shields v. Yellman , 100 Ky . 655, 18 R.
1092, 39 S. W. 30.
(10a ) An action to enforce a lien on
minerals severed from the land is transi-
tory; an action to enforce a lien on min-
eralsfor purchase-money due and pay-
ablebeforeseverancemust be brought in
the county in which the land lies. Ken-
nedy v. Hicks, 180 Ky . 562, 203 S. W.
318 .
(11) Person or creditor having an in-
terestin thing sought to be subjected
may object to jurisdiction. Emmons v .
L. & C. Mining Co., 112 Ky . 91, 23 R.
1445 , 65 S. W. 593 .
(12) Land - injury to by act of com-
mon carriershould be brought in county
where land lies. Gillen v . IllinoisR. Co.,
125 S. W. 1047, 137 Ky . 375.
(13) Land - action to recover or sub-
ject must be brought in county where
land lies, although an attachment may
have been leviedupon it that issuedin
suitpending in another county. Bram-
lett v. Couch , 105 S. W. 460,32 R. 311.
(14 ) Land- jurisdiction- when court
has jurisdictionfor one purpose it may
take it for allincidentalpurposes . Mor-
ton Iron Works v . Moreland , 113 S. W.
481 .
(15) "Lien or other incumbrance "-
these words in sub -sec. 3 refer to liens
or incumbrances created by contract or
gment . Hatton v.Rogers, 121 S.W.
698,134 Ky .840.
(16) Mortgage - action to enforce
against heirs of mortgagor may be
brought in county where land is located.
Galloway v.Craig,29 R. 1,92 S.W. 320,
122 Ky .447 .
(17) Oil and gas- action to recover
royalty due for is not local to county
where land is situated. Central Ky . Nat .
Gas Co. v. Stevens , 120 S. W. 282, 134
Ky . 306.
(18) Rescission of contract concerning
land . Action for is transitory and may
be brought in county other than that in
which land is situated. Thompson v .
Elmore , 13 R. 692, 18 S. W. 235 ; but if
it is also sought to enforce a lien on the
land the action must be brought in
county where land is located; Bullitt v.
Eastern Ky . Land Co., 99 Ky . 324, 18 R.
230, 36 S. W. 16 ; and if defendant in
action for rescission is a non -resident
action,must be filedin county where land

enter his appearance if his objectionis
sustained. White v. Kirby,147 Ky .496,
144 S. W. 369 .
(6 ) See notes to sec . 78 .
(7 ) Fraudulent vendees in distinct
transfers can not be joined as defendants
inoneaction.Friedmanv.Sinnott'sEx.,150 Ky .430,150 S. W. 520.
(8) A defendant was properlyserved
in the county where the suit was brought .
The court obtainedjurisdictionagainst
other defendantsservedwith processin
another county . Austin v. First Nat .
Bk . of Scottsville, 150 Ky . 113, 150 S.
W. 8 .
(9 ) In an action against two persons
who were jointlybound on a debtprocess
was served on one defendant in the
county where the action was pending ; he
had been dischargedin bankruptcy, but
allowedjudgment to go againsthim by
default ; the other defendant who was
summoned in another county , could not
defeat the jurisdictionof the court by
pleadingthedischargeinbankruptcyofhis co-defendant . George Bohon & Co. v .
Moren & Sipple, 151 Ky . 811, 152 S. W.
944 .
(10 ) An indorser of a note went to
the county where a suit was pending
against himself and the maker and was
served there pursuant to an agreement
with the plaintiffthat he should do so;
the maker was served in another county .
The court had jurisdiction to render
judgment against both defendants.
Knoxville Banking & Trust Co. v. Mer-
shon , 152 Ky . 169, 153 S. W. 238.
(11) It was proper for the Circuit
Court to dismiss an action, transitory
in character , as to a defendant who
neither resided nor was found in the
county where the action was pending ,
after the same had been dismissed on a
demurrer against the party residing in
that county. Martin v. Franklin, 160
Ky .61,169S.W. 541.
(12) The makers and the indorsers of
a note were sued jointly; service upon
one indorser was made in the county in
which the actionwas brought,and upon
the other defendants in another county .
No defense was made . As to that in-
dorser the action was dismissed without
prejudice. A judgment againstthe other
defendants was void . Second Nat . Bank
v.Prichard, 172 Ky . 190, 189 S. W. 14.

§ 83 [111] [Causesof actionthatmay be joined.] Severalcausesof
action may be united, ifeach affectallthe partiesto the action,may be
brought inthe same county,and may be prosecutedby thesame kind of
action; and ifallof them be brought
1. [Upon contracts.] Upon contracts,expressor implied;or,
2. [Real property, concerning.] For therecoveryof realproperty
and the rents,profitsand damages for withholdingit; or,
3. [Personalproperty, concerning.] For the recoveryof specific
personalproperty,and damages for the takingor withholdingit;or,
§ 83. (1) Assignee- Action by to re-
cover possession of personal property
can not be united with an action by him
to settle and distribute the estate . Atch-
inson v . Jones ,8 R. 259 .
(2 ) Causes must exist at commence-
ment of action. The joinderofactionsre-
ferredto in thissectionappliesto causes
of action existingat time of suit and not
such as arisesubsequently; in actionfor
slander plaintiff can not recover for
slanderouswords spoken during the ac-
tion and set up in an amended petition.
Taylor v. Moran , 4 Met .127; see as to
liendebts ,secs.135,694 .
(3) Common interest. When it is of
common interestto severalparties to set
asidea judgment, which being done,all
questions will relate to the same parties
and subject-matter , there is no mis-
joinder. McCormick v. McCormick , 9
R. 519 ,5 S. W. 573 .
(4 ) Contract- fraud . Action upon con-
tractand for fraudcan not be joined.
Wilson v. Thompson , 1 Met . 123,unless
the cause of action for fraud or negli-
gence isdirectlyconnected with the cause
of action upon the contract. Jones v .
Johnson, 10 Bush 649; Gregg v. Woods ,
3 R. 526; Jones v. Johnson, 86 Ky . 530,
9 R. 789 , 6 S. W. 582 .
(5) Creditor against guardian and
wards . Guardian of several wards can
not be sued in same action for claims
due by each of his wards distinct from
the others. St. Joseph O. S. v . Wolpert ,
80 Ky . 86, 3 R. 573.
(6) Debtor and persons who won from
him gaming . See Caldwell v . Caldwell, 2
Bush 446 ; and Whemhoff v. Rutherford ,
98 Ky . 91, 17 R. 659, 32 S. W. 288.
(7) Distributeesagainst administrator.
Distributees can not sue jointly an ad-
ministrator for balance due by him on
settlement. Pelly v . Bowyer , 7 Bush
513; Jackson v. Robinett, 2 Bibb 292.
(8) Ejectment - Action for may be
brought against severaldefendants . Bry-
ant v . Stephens , 26 R. 718. 82 S. W. 423 .
(9) Improper joinder for the purpose
of giving a court jurisdictionnot al-
lowed . Johnson v . Bradford , 114 Ky .
96, 24 R. 864, 70 S. W. 193. A common
law cause of action against some of the
defendants can not be united in the same
petitionwithan equitablecauseofactionagainst all of them . Metcalfe v. John-
son, 151 Ky . 823, 152 S. W. 951 ; and
notes to sec . 78 .
(10) Joint owners against trespassers.
Joint owners of land may maintain joint
action for its recovery against several
persons claiming and holding separate
parcelsof it. Woolfolk v.Ashby ,2 Met .
288. See Sale v . Crutchfield, 8 Bush 636,

§ 83 ] [Title VIJoinder of Actions .
4. [Partitionof realor personalproperty.] For partitionof realor
personalproperty,or both ;or,
5. [Injuriestocharacter.] For injuriesto character;or,
6. [Injuriesto personand property.] For injuriesto personand
property. (And seeastoactionsfortrespass,Ky .Stat.,sec.12.)
Cross reference: See Newman's Pl.and Pr.(3d Ed .),secs.35,327b,328,330,360,367,
368, 370, 372, 374, 375, 376, 382, 580, 763, 767, 768 ; Forms Nos .
76 , 145 .
and Bryant v. Stephens, 26 R. 718, 82
S. W. 423 .
(11) Jurisdiction. Misjoindercan not
give as to defendant summoned out of
county . See note to sec. 86.
(lla) Demurrer to jurisdiction. Where
severalcauses of actionwere joinedof
one of which the courthad jurisdiction,
a demurer to the jurisdictionwas prop-
erly overruled. L. & N. R. R. Co. v .
Mitchell, 162 Ky . 253, 172 S. W. 527.
ac-(12) Land- damages - separate
tions . In action to recover land and
damages for its detention, the judgment
for the land and damages for its deten-
tion are as distinct as if separate judg-
ments were rendered in different suits,
and in such actionthe judgment for re-
covery of land may be affirmed and for
damages reversed. Shean v. Cunning-
ham , 6 Bush 123 .
(13) Land - rents- trespass. Plaintiff
may in one action sue for land and
rents, and in another to recover dam-
ages for trespasses and injuries com-
mitted by destructionof timber and
other property on the land; Burr v.
Woodrow , 1 Bush 602 ,18 B. M. 541. But
may , if he choose, sue for land in one
action and in another for rents; if he
unites both in same action he can not in
separate action sue for rents,but may in
separate action recover for extraordi-
nary expense such as was not and could
not have been set up in action for land
and rent. Walker v . Mitchel, 18 B. М.
541 ; and see 6 Bush 123 .
( 14) Libel and slander . Causes of ae-
tion for may be united. Hargan v .
Purdy , 93 Ky . 424 , 14 R. 383, 20 S. W.
432 ; Fred v. Traylor , 115 Ky . 94, 24 R.
1906 ,72 S. W. 768. An action for slander
and an action for alienation of affections
of a spouse can not be joined, but this
section was not violated by pleading in
an action for slander showing special
damages to plaintiffon the alienationof
her husband's affectionsby reason of the
slander. Feltyv.Felty,164 Ky .355,175
S. W. 643 ; and see note 22 .
(15) Lienholders. Two or more may
unite in same action for the purpose of
enforcingtheir respectiveliens. Boyd
v. Jones , 8 R. 602, 2 S. W. 552; Foster v .
Lyons , 19 R. 1906,44 S. W. 625 ; and see
secs. 692-4 and notes .
(16) Officials- in public and private
capacity. An action can not be main-
tained against officersof a municipal
corporationin both theirofficialand pri--
vate capacityforthe same injury. Han-
cock v. Johnson , 1 Met . 242 .
(17) Order of delivery- enforcement
of mortgage . Petition seking to recover
a debt , and to enforce mortgage lien to
secureitspayment ,and to obtainorder
of delivery for possession of mortgaged
property, which had been subsequently
mortgaged to another, is a misjoinder
of actions. McKee v. Pope , 18 B. М.
548 .
(18) Penalties. It was held in L. &
N. R. R. v. Com .,102 Ky .300,19 R. 1462,
43 S. W. 458 , that the Com . could not
unite in one action several offenses for
violationof sec.784,Ky .Stat. In Farm-
ers' Tobacco W. H. Co. v .Minor , 107 Ky .
287,21 R. 990,53 S. W. 641, the recovery
in one action of several penalties for
violationsof sec. 4803, Ky. Stat., was
upheld .
(19) Personalinjury. Action for may
be maintained against several who con-
tribute to it, whether by joint or sepa-
rate acts. Pugh v. C. & O. R. R., 101
Ky .77,19 R. 149,39 S. W. 695; L. & E.
Mail Co. v . Clara Barnes , 25 R. 2036 ;
Cumberland T. & T. Co. v .Ware , 115 Ку .
581, 24 R. 2519 , 74 S. W. 289. And two
separatedistinctacts of negligencemay
be sued for in same action . L. & N. R.
Co. v. Adams , 148 Ky . 513, 147 S. W ,
384 .
(20) Remote obligor in bond . The
owner of a slave hired her for a year to
A and B ,and they hired her to C , taking
from Ca covenant to A , with Das
surety, for the return of the slave. C
caused her death , and ran off. The owner
and A and B could maintain an action

1JoinderofActions. [§ 84
§ 84 [112] [Partymay strikefrom pleadingcauseofaction.] A party
may strikefrom hispetition,answer or replyany causeof action,before
thefinalsubmissionof thecasetothejury,or thecourt,ifthetrialbe by
thecourt. (May dismisswithoutprejudice,sec.371.)
on the covenant against D. Carney v.
Walden , 16 В. М. 388.
(21) Settlement of estate. In action
for, persons indebted to the estate can
not be joined as defendants . Citizens'
Bank v.Boswell,93 Ky . 92, 14 R. 17, 19
S. W. 174; Hilton v.Hilton , 110 Ky .522,
22 R. 1934,62 S. W. 6.
(22) Slander - false imprisonment -
maliciousprosecutiond. Action for false
imprisonment or malicious arrest and
slander can not be joined. Dragoo v .
Levi, 2 Duv . 520; Tandy v. Riley, 26
R. 98 , 80 S. W. 776 .
(23) Splitting causes of action, so as
to bring two suitsin place of one, will
not be allowed , and if it is done the
court should require the actions consoli-
dated . Powell v. Weiler , 11 B. M. 187 ;
and see Pilcher v . Gegan , 91 Ky . 228, 12
R. 860, 15 S. W. 513 ; Weinstock v . Bell-
wood , 12 Bush 139; Cole v. I. C. R. R.,
27 R. 1087, 87 S. W. 1082 ; Lou . Bridge
Co. v . L. & N. R. Co., 116 Ky . 258, 25
R. 405 , 75 S. W. 285 ; C. & C. B. Co. v .
Kliemier , 105 Ky . 609, 20 R. 1415, 49 S.
W. 484 ; Coles v. I. C. R. Co.,27 R. 1087,
120 Ky . 686, and 150 Ky . 564.
(24 ) Trespassers. Joint and several
judgments against; see Ky . Stat., sec.
12; Alexander v. Humber , 86 Ky . 565, 9
R. 734, 6 S. W. 453 ; Cent . Pass . Ry . Co.
v . Kuhn , 86 Ky . 578, 9 R. 725, 6 S. W.
441 ; and see cases cited in note 19 .
(25 ) Vendees - to set aside fraudulent
deed to. In an actionby an assigneein
bankruptcy to set aside as fraudulent
severalconveyancesmade by the bank-
rupt,the severalfraudulentvendeesmay
be joined as defendants . Anderson v .
Anderson ,80 Ky . 638, 4 R. 579.
(26 ) Warranty - fraudulent conceal-
ment . Causes of actionfor growing out
of same transaction may be united.
Gregg v.Woods ,3 R. 526.
(27 ) Contract- tort. Action to obtain
reliefon both grounds can not be joined.
C. & O. R. Co. v. Patton , 146 Ky . 656 ;
Wilson v.L. & N. R. Co., 112 S. W. 585;
Willis v.Tomes , 141 Ky .431, 132 S. W.
1043 ; Littlev.Consolidation Coal Co., 169
Ky . 514, 184 S. W. 873; Foy -ProctorCo.
v . Marshall & Thorn, 169 Ky . 377, 183
S. W. 940; but plaintiffmay waive tort
growing out of a tresspassand sue in
contract. Roberts v.Moss , 127 Ky . 657,
106 S. W. 297, 32 R. 525 .
(28) Settle estate and set aside deed
made by decedent - action can not be
joined. Dailey v . O'Brien, 96 S. W. 521,
29 R. 811 .
(29) Attachment . Action for malici-
ously suing out , can not be joined with
action on indemnifying bond . Bell v .
Thompson , 102 S. W. 830,31 R. 474.
(30) Joint tort feasors may be joined
as defendants . Pickerillv . City of Lou .,
125 Ky . 213 , 100 S. W. 873, 30 R. 1239 ;
and see cases cited in notes 19 and 24 .
(31) Personal injury - death. Action
for damages caused by death , and for
suffering can not be joined. Hendricks
v. American Express Co., 138 Ky . 704,
128 S. W. 1089; Lewis v . Taylor Coal
Co., 112 Ky . 845, 66 S. W. 1044, 23 R.
2218 .
(32) Alienation of affections. Actions
for alienation of affections and for crim-
inal conversationmay be joinedunder
this section. Merritt v .Cravens ,168 Ky .
155 , 181 S. W. 970. Alienation and libel
and slander. Felty v .Felty, 164 Ky .355,
175 S. W. 643 .
(33 ) Surety - bond of city comptroller.
A petition filedby a city stating a cause
of action and praying that certain spe-
cificsecuritiesbe returned to the city,
and in the alternative for their value if
not to be had , states but one cause of
action. Nat . Surety Co. v. City of Louis-
ville, 165 Ky . 38, 176 S. W. 364.
(34 ) An action, by a widow to cancel
an ante -nuptial contract and a subse-
quent settlement and deed in pursuance
thereof and also to recover dower in her
husband's land and half the personalty
was not a case of misjoinder within this
section. Robinson v. Robinson, 153 Ky .
828 , 156 S. W. 903 .
§ 84. Amended petition making mis-
joinder. In an action on contract plain-
tifffiledan amended petition setting up
and relying on fraud in obtaining con-
tract. Held, that plaintiffmight in an
amended petition set up action that
could not be joined with action in peti-
tion, and thus abandon his originalac-
tion and rely on that in amendment .
Hord v. Chandler , 13 B. M. 403 ; but see
secs.113,114 and note 1 to sec.85.

§ 85-86] [Title VIJoinder of Actions .
§ 85 [113] [Courtmay requirepartyto elect- may strikeout.] The
court,at any timebeforedefenseshall,on motionof a party,requirethe
adversepartyto electwhich of two or more causesof action,improperly
joined,he willprosecute;and,uponhisrefusalsotoelect,shallstrikeout
of the petition, answer or replyany causeof actionimproperlyjoined
withanother. (See further,sec.113.)
Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr . (3d Ed .), secs. 173d , 365, 370, 377, 396h ,
550 , 550f, 798; Form No. 172.
§ 86 [114] [Waiver of objectionto misjoinder.] Objectionsto the
misjoinderof causes of actionare waived,unlessthey are made pursuant
to section 85 .
Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr . (3d Ed .), secs. 365a , 377, 396h , 550e,
550f , 798 .
§ 85. (1) Amendment - objectionto
for misjoinder. If an amended petition
would make a misjoinderof actionsby
setting up a new cause, objection can be
made to its filing. Hancock v. Johnson ,
1 Met . 242 .
(2) Misjoinder- how objected to. Mo-
tion to dismisspetitionbecause thereis
misjoinderis not proper. Objectionto
misjoinder should be made by motion to
elect. Hunt v. Semonin, 79 Ky . 270, 2
R. 334 ; Humphrey v.Hughes ,79 Ky .487,
3 R. 273. An objectionto the misjoinder
of causes of action can not be raised by
demurrer to the jurisdiction. L. & N.
R. R. Co. v. Mitchell, 162 Ky . 253, 172
S. W. 527 .
(3) Motion to elect- duty of court. If
causes of actionbe misjoined, plaintiff
may on motion be required to elect, and ,
on failure, court should strike out one
of the causes misjoined; if plaintiffthen
refuse to proceed, the petition should be
dismissedwithout prejudice. Dragoo v.
Levi , 2 Duv . 520 ; Hilton v. Hilton, 110
Ky .522,22 R. 1934,62 S.W. 6 ; Jenkins
v . Taylor , 22 R. 1137, 59 S. W. 853 ;
Bannon v . Bannon , 119 S. W. 1170, 136
Ky . 556; Metcalfe v. Johnson, 151 Ky .
823 , 152 S. W. 951 .
(4) Where the plaintiffrefuses to
elect, the court should not dismiss the
action, but strike out the cause improp-
erly joined. Sheppard v. Stephens, 8 R.
603 .
(5) One cause but irrelevant matter .
No electioncan be requiredifpetition
state but one substantial cause of action ,
although it may containirrelevantand
redundant matter . Bonney v . Reardin ,
6 Bush 34 .
§ 86. (1) Misjoinder- waiver . Objec-
tion for misjoinder must be made in cir-
cuit court. McKee v. Pope , 18 B. М.
548; and failure to make motion at
proper time operates as a waiver of ob-
jection. Wilson v. Thompson , 1 Met .
123; Caldwell v. Caldwell, 2 Bush 446 ;
8 Bush 636 ; 79 Ky . 270, 2 R. 334 ; Mur-
ray v . Booker , 22 R. 781, 58 S. W. 788 ;
and waiver by filinganswer remains al-
though answer be withdrawn . Chiles v.
Drake , 2 Met . 146 ; Stovall v. Hibbs , 17
R. 906,32 S. W. 1087 ; Bryant v.Stephens ,
26 R. 718, 82 S. W. 423 ; Bannon v . Ban-
non , 119 S. W. 1170, 136 Ky .556.
(2) Misjoindercan not give jurisdic-
tion. Although misjoinder is waived by
not objectingin time, yet such mis-
joinder can not give court jurisdiction
over defendant summoned in another
county , when otherwise court would not
have jurisdiction. Ransdallv.Shropshire,
4 Met . 326 ; see Maddox v . Williams , 5
R. 695 ; Basye v . Brown , 78 Ky . 553 ; 1
R. 256 ; and see notes to sec. 78.
(3) Misjoinder shown by evidence. If
the pleadingsdo not show a misjoinder
of parties, and such misjoinder is dis-
closedby the evidence,a motion requir-
ingplaintiffto electagainstwhich party
he willproceed, may thereuponbe made .
LouisvilleGas and Electric Co. v . Wall ,
178 Ky . 33, 198 S. W. 745.

$ 87 . Pleadings defined.
CHAPTER I.
PLEADINGS IN GENERAL .
$ 88 . Form and sufficiencyof.
§89 . Pleadings allowed .
§ 87 [115] [Pleadings defined.) Pleadings are statementsby parties
to an actionof theircauses of controversy. (Caption of, sec. 110 ; what
may contain,sec. 113-2;verificationof,and by whom ,secs.116,117 ;issue
must be formed ,sec.114 ;when allegationsconfessed,sec.126 ; when must
be proved, sec.126 ;what writingsmust, or may , be filed,secs. 120, 128.)
Cross reference: See Newman's Pl.and Pr .(3d Ed .),secs.1,425 .
§ 87. (1) Allegationand proof. It is
as necessary to state a cause of action
as it is to sustain it by proof. The ob-sence of either prevents a recovery.
Murrell v. McAlister, 79 Ky . 311, 2 R.
323; and the plaintiffcan only recover
upon proof of the cause of action alleged
in his petition. Gossom v . Badgett , 6
Bush 97 ; 1 Met .339. As to variance be-
tween pleadingand proof, see secs. 129,
130, 131, and notes thereto.
(2) An allegation neither proved nor
admitted or a ground of defense not al-
legedin the pleadings,although proven,
is to be treated as if such allegation or
defense did not exist. Wilkins v .Barnes ,
79 Ky . 323. 2 R. 278 .
(3) Proof that a party introduces must
not contradict what he alleges in his
pleadings to be true; he will not be per-
mitted to recover on a state of facts he
says is not true; he must amend his
pleadings. Payne v.Hardesty,12 R. 336,
14 S. W. 348 .
(4 ) Construction of pleadings. Before
verdict, pleadings will be construed
against the pleader; after verdict, plead-
ingswillbe liberallyconstruedto uphold
the verdict, and a pleading insufficient
before verdictmay be good after verdict.
Worthly v . Hammond , 13 Bush 510. See
Skillman v. Muir , 4 Met . 282 ; Taylor v .
Cline, 18 R. 9, 35 S. W. 109 ; 16 B. М.
643 ; 10 Bush 185.
(5) Evidence- not to be stated. It is
not sufficient to state the evidence relied
93

§88-89 ] [Title VIIPleadings in General .
§ 88 [116] [Form and sufficiencyof.] The forms of pleadings,and
the rulesby which theirsufficiencyisto be determined,are thosepre-
scribedby thisCode. (See astorulesofpleading,sec.110.)
Crossreference: See Newman's Pl.and Pr.(3d Ed .),sec.126a.
§ 89 [117] [Pleadingsallowed.] The pleadingsallowedare-
1. [Petitions,answersand otherpleadings.] Petitions,answersand
replies,and suchadditionalpleadings,by way of rejoinderand rebutter,
as may be necessaryto form a materialissueof fact.
2. [Demurrers.] Demurrers.
Cross reference: See Newman's Pl.and Pr.(3d Ed .), secs.6, 509, 811, 861.
on , nor necessary to set forth evidence
of the truth of the facts relied on . L. &
P. Canal Co. v. Murphy , 9 Bush 522 ;
Bentley v.Bustard,16 B. M. 643; 2 Met .
490 .
(6) Evidence - pleadings. Pleadings
signedby the partiesmay be introduced
as evidence against them and then com-
mented on in argument . L. & N. R. R.
v. Hull , 113 Ky . 561, 24 R. 375, 68 S. W.
433 ; L. & N. R. R. v. Mulfinger,26 R. 3,
80 S. W. 499; Edwards v.Mattingly,107
Ky .332,21 R. 1045,53 S.W. 1032;Wyles
v.Berry,116 Ky .377,25 R. 606,76 S.W.
126 .
(7 ) Facts- how to be averred. The
facts reliedupon should be directly and
positivelyalleged,and not statedby way
of argument ,inferenceor belief. Patter-
son v. Caldwell, 1 Met , 489 ; Garnett v .
Finnell, 2 Duv . 166; Walters v. Chinn , 1
Met .499 ; 3 Met .61. The failureto state
a material fact can not be obviated by
the statement of another fact that raises
a prima faciepresumption of itsexist-
ence. Meguiar v.Henry ,84 Ky . 1,7 R.
695 ; 1 Duv . 59 ; 11 Bush 693.
(8 ) Verdict- answer . Defects in plead-
ings that are cured by. See note 12,
sec . 95 .
(9) Withdrawal of pleadings unless an
attestedcopy is leftnot proper. Wyles
v . Berry , 116 Ky . 377, 25 R. 606, 76 S.
W. 126 .
(10) Writings filed with pleadings.
See note 18, sec. 90.
§ 88. Forms , but not substance, abol-
ished by Code ; and every essentialaver-
ment required to make a declaration
good at common law must be made in
the petition. L. & P. Canal Co. v . Mur-
phy , 9 Bush 522 ; and see note to sec. 4.
§ 89. (1) Pleadingsallowed by Code
of 1854 were the petition, demurrer , an-
swer , reply, set-off and counter-claim ,
and by act of 1857, a cross-petition was
allowed. Myers ' Code, sec. 117.
(2) Pleas in abatement - have been
abolished by the Code . Scottish Ins. Co.

factswhich constitutea cause of actionin favor of the plaintiffagainst
the defendant,and must demand thespecificreliefto which theplaintiff
considershimselfentitled;and may containa generalprayerforany other
reliefto which theplaintiffmay appearto be entitled. If no defensebe
made , theplaintiffcan nothave judgment forany reliefnot specifically
demanded ; but, if defensebe made, he may have judgment for other
relief,under a prayertherefor. (Forms of petitionsin variousactions,
Forms 1-50,may be amended,secs.109,132,134;verificationof,secs.
116,137. See as to noticerequiredwhen lienon realpropertyisasserted,
Ky .Stat.,sec.2358а .)

§ 90. (1) Account . A promise is im-
plied to pay reasonable value from the
saleand deliveryof the goods at defend-
ant's request. Wood v . Wells , 2 Bush
197; Thruston v. Oldham , 6 Bush 16.
Averment of sale and delivery to defend-
ant at his request of goods worth a cer-
tain sum , or the rendition of servicesto
defendant at his request worth a certain
sum , is sufficientto authorize judgment
by default; a promise to pay is implied.
Skillman v . Muir , 4 Met . 282.
(2) A petition alleging in substance
that the defendant is indebted to plain-
tiffin the sum of $1,963for goods bought ,
the whole of which is due and unpaid , is
not good . There should be an averment
that the goods were sold and delivered to
defendant at his instance and request, or
that they were sold upon the promise of
defendant to pay . Drake v . Semonin , 82
Ky .291 , 6 R. 341.
(3) Administrator- against. It is not
necessary to allegethat the debt sued for
was demanded of administrator, accom-
panied by proper affidavit, before action
commenced . If demand has not been
made , it can be reached by rule. Rogers
v . Mitchell, 1 Met . 22 ; Thomas
Thomas , 15 B. M. 178 .
V.
(4 ) Assault and battery . How aver-
ments asking specialdamages should be
set out . Jesse v . Shuck , 11 R. 463, 12
S. W. 304. Petition for must set out
facts constitutingthe assault. Stivers v .
Baker , 87 Ky . 508,10 R. 523, 9 S. W. 491.
(5 ) Assignee against assignor. In ac-
tion by assigneeagainstassignorof a
note it is necessary to aver the consider-
ation paid for the assignment .Humphrey
v . Hughes , 79 Ky . 487, 3 R. 273 ; Ky .
Stat ., sec. 475. And before he can re-
cover he must institutehis action against
the payer at the firstterm of the court
and prosecute him to insolvency without
unreasonabledelay. Francisv. Gant, 80
Ky . 190 ,2 R. 285 .
(6 ) Attorney - against. In action
against attorney for money collected, de-
mand must be made before suit filed.
Roberts v .Armstrong ,1 Bush 263.
(7 ) Award - to set aside. To set aside
an award on the ground of fraud, the
specificacts of fraud relied on must be
averred. Phillipsv.Phillips,81 Ky .147,
4 R. 941 .
(8) Blanks in petition. Plaintiff, in
order to recover specifiedamounts ,should
state the amounts , and not leave blank
placesfor them in his pleading. Jesse
