§ 90 ] [Title VIIPetition .
v. Shuck , 11 R. 463, 12 S. W. 304 ; Lex .
R. Co. v.Britton, 130 Ky . 684, 114 S. W.
295 ; Lex .R. Co. v. Johnson , 139 Ky .328,
122 S. W. 830. A pleading having blanks
was construed most strongly against the
pleader. Holzbog v. Bakrow , 156 Ky .
161 , 160 S. W. 792 .
(9 ) Bond . In action on bond , the peti-
tion should set out the covenant of the
bond , and the breach of them relied on .
Mann v. Martin,82 Ky .242,6 R. 346.
(10) In action on bond , the terms and
stipulationsof the bond must be set out,
as well as the breach of it; filingbond
with petition, or referringto it, is not
sufficient. Riggs v. Maltby , 2 Met . 88 ;
Campbell v. Galbreath ,12 Bush 459 ; 14
Β . Μ . 252 .
(11) Petition on injunction bond con-
ditionedto pay judgment enjoined,ifin-.
junctiondissolved, is insufficientunless
it states that the judgment is unpaid .
Crawford v. Woodworth , 9 Bush 745 .
(12) Constable - against. In action
against constable for money collected, it
is necessary to allege a demand and re-
fusal to pay . Harris v . Perry , 2 Bush
101; Huston v. Hagar , 1 Duv . 24.
(13 ) Contract - for breach of. The
breach of a contractisan essentialpart
of the cause of action, and should be as-
signed in the words of the contract,or in
words which are co-extensive with the
import and effect of it. The rules of
pleading which require that the contract
shall be substantially set out, as well as
the breach of which plaintiffcomplains ,
are fundamental , and have not been
changed by the Code . Moxley v. Moxley ,
2 Met . 309 ; 8 B. M. 377. In an action for
damages for breach of contract, where
the agreements are mutual and depend-
ent, and each is to perform his part at
the same time , the petition must aver
that plaintiffwas ready to perform his
part of the agreement at the time and
place required. Sousley v. Burns, 10
Bush 87; 13 В. М. 464. But the aver-
ment of readinessto perform is only
necessary where the acts to be done are
concurrent, and is not required where
the defendant is to pay money or per-
form some act before plaintiffis bound
to do anything . H. & N. R. R. v. Leavell,
16 Β . Μ . 358. It is necessary to state
the contract, the breach and the facts
which show the loss or damage by rea-
son of the breach. Miles v. Miller, 12
Bush 134; and see Lewis v. Scott,95 Ky .
484 , 16 R. 49 , 26 S. W. 192 ; Meagher v .
Bridgeford & Co., 144 Ky . 479, 139 S. W.
750,for necessaryallegationsin petition
by servant against master to recover
damages forwrongfuldischarge.
(13a) Conversion. In an action for the
conversion of trees,the value of the trees
should be stated in the petition, but
where theirsizeand qualityare given it
willbe assumed,on demurrer and appeal
that they were of some value. Ford
Lumber & Mfg . Co. v. Burt & Brabb
Lumber Co.,157 Ky .706,163 S.W. 1105.
A(14) Creditor against assignee.
creditorcan not maintain an ordinary
actionagainstan assigneeto whom his
debtor has made an assignment , and re-
coverjudgment forhisdebt. Dobyns v.
Dobyns , 79 Ky . 95 ; 2 R. 274.
(15) Conclusionof law. An allegation
that certainpersons are the only heirs
at law of another is but a conclusion of
law. Daley v.O'Brien,96 S. W. 521, 29
R. 811 .
(16) Corporation- presumption. Itwill
be presumed that a corporationsuingas
such is a corporationuntilthe contrary
appears . Martin v . Ky . Lands Invest-
ment Co.,146 Ky .525, 142 S.W. 1038.
(17) Damages- specialdamages - lost
time . See full cases of Blue Grass Trac-
tionCo. v.Ingles,140 Ky .488,131 S. W.
278; L. & N. R. Co. v. Roney , 108 S. W.
343,32 R. 1326; L. & N. R. Co.v.Dickey,
104 S. W. 329, 31 R. 896.
(18) Departure in pleading. A new
causeof actionsetup in a replywillnot
support a judgment by default thereon.
Spiessv.Bartley,113 S.W. 127, 130 Ky .
277 .
(19) Defects cured by answer - verdict.
See note 12 to sec . 95 .
(20 ) Defense need not be anticipated.
It is not necessary to notice and remove
objections that may exist and can be
relied on for defense . L. & P. Canal Co.
v. Murphy ,9 Bush 522 ; ShelbyvilleTrus-
tees v . S. & E. T. P. Co., 1 Met . 54 ;
Bentley v .Bustard , 16 B. М. 643.
(21) Dower . In action by widow
against purchaser from her husband to
have dower allotted,the petitiondid not
aver that her husband was the owner of
the land during coverture, nor that she
was his wife when purchase was made by
defendant, and was not good. Yancy v.
Smith , 2 Met .408 ; see Fritz v . Tudor , 2
Duv . 173 .
(22 ) Exhibits filedwith or copied into
a pleading will not cure defects in it or
supply omitted averments ; the pleading
must be complete in itself. An exhibit
will not make a pleading good , but may
make itbad , as on demurrer the exhibit
will be considered for the purpose of
showing that petitionisnot good,and if

it contradictsthe pleading, the exhibit
will control. Altemus v . Asher , 24 R.
2416 ,74 S. W. 245 ; Newport Lumber Co.
v.Lichtenfeldt,24 R. 1969,72 S.W. 778;
Gardner v. Continental Ins. Co., 25 R.
426, 75 S. W. 283; Millerv. McConnell,
26 R. 181; Goffv.Janeway ,26 R. 525,80
S. W. 1103 ; B. & T. Co. v . Lou . R. W.
Co.,25 R. 122; and see sec. 128.
(23) Falseimprisonment. See Robin-
son v . Morgan , 100 Ky . 529 ; 18 R. 903,
38 S. W. 868; Reynolds v. Price, 22 R.
5, 56 S. W. 502 ; Southern R. Co. v. Shir-
ley,90 S. W. 597, 121 Ky . 863,28 R. 860 ;
Roberts v. Thomas , 121 S. W. 961, 135
Ky . 63. The allegationin a petitionthat
an arrestwas made by a deputy sheriff
in his officialcapacity, isnot the state-
ment of a fact, but a mere legal con-
clusion. Jones v. Van Bever, 164 Ky .
80, 174 S. W. 795.
(24 ) Fraud . If the petition sets out
the factsshowing the fraud they willbe
given theirlegaleffectalthoughthe word
" fraud" is not used . Ky . Electric Co. v .
Barrett , 116 S. W. 1186, 132 Ky . 717.
(25 ) Guaranty . When notice of the
acceptanceof an offerof guaranty is
requisitein action on guaranty , the alle-
gationof noticein the petitionshouldbe
special, to enable the court to determine
whether or not it is sufficient. General
averment "of all of which defendant had
notice " is not sufficient. Steadman v .
Guthrie , 4 Met . 147; Mast v . Lehman ,
100 Ky . 464, 18 R. 949, 38 S. W. 1056.
(26 ) Guardian's settlement - to sur-
charge . In action to surcharge settle-
ment of guardian, the petitionmust
specify the particular items objected to.
Tanner v . Skinner , 11 Bush 120 ; Fielder
v. Harbison, 93 Ky . 482, 14 R. 481, 20
S. W. 508 .
(27 ) Heir for debt of ancestor. Το
make the heir responsiblefor debt of an-
cestor it must be alleged that he re-
ceived assets from ancestor . Massie v .
Hiatt , 82 Ky . 314 ; 6 R. 176 ; 10 Bush
234; 80 Ky . 152, 3 R. 647; and see Ky .
Stat ., sec. 2084. As to proper averment
that one is heir; see Montgomery v .
White ,10 R. 905,11 S. W. 10 ; 7 Bush 51 ;
and 18 B. M. 72 ; Craig v . Welch Coal Co.,
24 R. 2225, 73 S. W. 1035.
(28 ) Husband - for debt of wife. In
actionby husband to recoverdebt made
by wife before marriage , it is not neces-
sary to allegethat the husband received
property by the wife. Beaumont v .Mil-
ler, 1 Met .68 ; Fultz v.Fox , 9 B. M. 499 ;
Medley v. Tandy , 85 Ky . 566, 9 R. 168,
4 S. W. 308; and seeKy .Stat.,sec,2130.
(29 ) Indorsers of note. In action upon
a note executedto plaintiffby D , pay-
able in bank and indorsed in blank by
F ,N ,and C beforeitsdelivery,whereby,
as alleged, they "intendedto be equally
bound as obligors," it was not alleged
that they indorsedthe note as accommo-
dation indorsers, or with the view of
having itdiscountedin bank , or for the
purposeof guaranteeingitspayment.Petitionheld not good againstindorsers.
Kellogg v.Dunn ,2 Met .215.
(30) The presumptionisthat a note in
the hands of the maker of it, indorsed
by the payee,has been paid, and one to
whom the maker has discounted it can
not recover against the indorser without
averring that itwas indorsed for maker's
accommodation . Callahan v . First Nat .
Bank ,78 Ky .604,1 R. 342; and seeGra-
ham v . Louisville City Bank , 103 Ky .
641, 20 R. 295, 45 S. W. 870.
(30a ) Insurance companies . Suits on
policies, allegationsin. Vicarsv. Aetna
Life Ins. Co., 158 Ky . 1, 164 S. W. 106 ;
Henry Fire Ins. Co. v. Barkley , 160 Ky .
153, 169 S. W. 747 ; Phil. Life Ins. Co. v .
Farnsley's Admr ., 162 Ky . 27, 171 S. W.
1004. Suit on contract made by agent ;
petitionnegativedagent'spowers. Cen-
tralLifeIns.Co. v.Vickers,164 Ky .848,
176 S. W. 375 .
(31) Interest. To recover interestac-
cruing anterior to the institutionof the
action on an unliquidated account , it is
necessary that plaintiffshould ask for
that character of relief. Adams Express
Co. v. Milton , 11 Bush 49 .
(32 ) Interest is allowable as a matter
of law upon an account from the time
when , by the contract, it is payable, or
from the time it becomes a liquidatedone . Henderson Mfg . Co. v . Lowell M.
S., 86 Ky . 668, 9 R. 831, 7 S. W. 142;
TravelersIns. Co. v. Henderson,120 Ky .
218 , 27 R. 653 , 85 S. W. 1090 .
(33) Judgments. In actionon foreign
judgment, itis necessaryto allegefacts
showing that the court rendering the
judgment had jurisdictionof the sub-
ject-matter and the person of the de-
fendant . Gebhard v . Garnier, 12 Bush
321; Wood v. Wood , 78 Ky . 624, 1 R.
358; 10 Bush 160; 12 Bush 274; 115 Ky .
157 , 24 R. 2004. As to pleading judg-
ment of courtof thisState, seesec. 122.
(34) Law of another State, when de-
pended upon, must be pleaded as any
other fact, so that the courtmay judge
of the effect of the law . Roots v . Mer-
riwether, 8 Bush 397 ; Bidwell v . Robin-
son,79 Ky .29, 1 R. 249 .

(35) Legal conclusion. Averment that
defendant extorted and obtained three
hundred dollars from plaintiffwithout
any consideration,and was thereforein-
debted to him in that amount , ismerely
a legalconclusion, and petitionis not
good. Murphy v.Estes,6 Bush 532. See
1 Duv . 30 ; 4 Met . 327; Stivers v. Baker ,
87 Ky .508, 10 R. 523, 9 S. W. 491. That
a certainjudgment and executionare
void. Angel v. Byars, 153 Ky . 208, 154
S. W. 1109. That a certain ordinance
was passed and that it required defend-equiredants to construct certain improvements .
City of Richmond v. Madison Female In-
stitute, 153 Ky .301, 155 S. W. 371. That
a deputy sheriffmade an arrest in his
officialcapacity. Jones v.Van Bever , 164
Ky .80,174 S. W. 795. An allegationthat
a judgment was recovered is not an alle-
gation of a fact of which the judgment
is prima facie evidence. Turner v. Ham-
lin, 152 Ky . 469, 153 S. W. 778.
(36) Legislatureno powerto prescribeform of pleadingthat dispenseswith al-
legations of essential facts, or cuts off
substantial defenses, City of Louisville
v.Cochran,82 Ky. 15,5 R. 833; Meguiar
v.Henry , 84 Ky . 1, 7 R. 695.
(37) Malicious prosecution. See Lan-
caster v . Langston , 18 R. 299, 36 S. W.
521; Lucas v. Hunt , 91 Ky . 279, 12 R.
871, 15 S. W. 781 ; Hegan Mantel Co. v.
Alford , 114 S. W. 290.
(38) Marriage contract. Where no
time and place are agreed upon for the
consumption of the marriage , no action
can be maintained by either party for
failureto perform, unlessplaintiffaver
an offerto perform and a failureof the
defendantto comply. Fiblev.Caplinger,
13 Β . Μ . 464 ; and see Shakleford v .
Hamilton ,93 Ky .80, 14 R. 11, 19 S. W. 5.
(38a) Mental injury- fright. There
can be no recovery for fright unless ac-
companied by physicalinjury. Southern
Ry . Co. in Ky .v.Owen ,156 Ky .827, 162
S. W. 110 .
(39) Misnomer of defendant . A party
who makes defense to an action can not
afterward set up that he was sued by
wrong name ,and thereby defeat recovery
by plea of limitation. Heckman v . L. &
N. R. R. Co., 85 Ky . 631, 9 R. 297, 4 S.
W. 342 ; and see L. & N. R. R. Co. v. Hall,
12 Bush 131 ; and see note 4 , sec.39.
(40 ) Money had and received. Aver-
ment that defendant received $400 for
the use of plaintiff, and thereby became
indebted to him , and had paid no part of
it, isinsufficient. The factsshowing how
it was received, such as created an in-
debtedness, must be alleged. Faught v .
Henry , 13 Bush 471.
(41 ) Negligence. It is sufficientin ac-
tionforpersonalinjuryto allegein gen-
eralterms that the injurycomplainedof
was causedby thecarelessnessand negli-
gence of the defendant. Chilesv.Drake,
2 Met . 146; L., C. & L. R. R. Co. v. Case ,
9 Bush 728. A generalcharge of negli-
gence is sufficientin the courts of this
State in personalinjuryactionswhether
arisingunder the employer's liabilityact
or not . L. & N. R. R. Co. v . Stewart's
Admrx ., 156 Ky . 550, 161 S. W. 557 ; but
if the petitionspecifiesthe negligence
complained of,a recovery can not be had
for any other negligence, and this rule
applieswhere the agent or officercharged
with the negligenceisspecified. Thomas
v . L. & N. R. R., 18 R. 164, 35 S. W.
910; Chun v. K. & I. Bridge Co., 23 R.
1092, 64 S. W. 649. It is not necessary
to allegethat therewas no contributory
negligenceon the partof plaintiff. L.
& P. Canal Co. v. Murphy , 9 Bush 522;
P. & M. R. R. Co. v . Hoehl , 12 Bush 41 ;
80 Ky . 82, 3 R. 576 ; 10 Bush 263 ; 84 Ky .
330,8 R. 376; andifpetitionchargessev-
eral acts of negligence and eitherof them
issustainedby the evidence, a recovery
may be had. L. & N. R. R. v.Shearer,22
R. 929, 59 S. W. 330 ; L. & N. R. R. v.
Gamble's Admrx .,156 Ky . 91, 160 S. W.
795 .
(42) Petition to recover for injury
arisingfrom negligenceor want of skill
should state such facts as would author-
ize an action in case at common law ;
and, if for an injury done with force,
such facts as would authorize action in
trespass. Kountz v. Brown , 16 B. M.577. An allegationof negligencein gen-
eral terms will not authorize a recovery
by a servant for failureof the master to
use care to furnish a safe place to work
or safe appliances. The failure to ob-
ject to evidence as to unsafe appliances
was not a waiver of thisobjection,where
the evidence so admitted was competent
on an issue in the case . Ohio Val . Coal
& Min .Co. v . Heine , 159 Ky . 586, 167 S.
W. 873. As to necessary allegations in
action by employe against employer,
where the injuryiscaused by defective
appliancesor machinery, see Mellottv.
L. & N. R. R., 101 Ky . 212, 19 R. 379,40
S. W. 696 ; Pfistererv . Peter & Co., 117
Ky . 501, 25 R. 1605, 78 S. W. 450.
(43 ) In an action under sec. 4, Ky .
Stat ., it is necessary to aver that killing
was not done in self-defense . Becker v.
Crow , 7 Bush 198; 13 Bush 636 ; Hol
98

lingsworthv.Warnock ,112 Ky .96,23 R.
1395 , 65 S. W. 163 .
(44) Negligenceresultingin personal
injury- how pleaded . Monroe v . Stand-
ard Mfg .Co.,141 Ky .549, 133 S. W. 214;
Gains v.Johnson ,105 S. W. 381, 28 R. 58,
133 Ky .507 .
(45 ) Negligence - averment of gross
includes lower degrees of. Fendley v .
IllinoisCent . R. Co., 92 S. W. 1, 28 R.
1325 .
(16 ) Negligence the petition may set
out as many separate acts of negligence
as the facts warrant . Ford v . Provi-
dence Coal Co., 30 R. 698, 99 S. W. 609,
124 Ку . 517. A petition to recover dam-
ages for injuriesinflictedby negligence
may set up separate and distinctacts of
negligence; and a recoverymay be had
upon any of the grounds , although the
others are not proved . L. & N. R. R. Co.
v . Gamble's Admrx ., 156 Ky . 91, 160 S.
W. 795 .
(47 ) New promise . Action on new
promise to pay an account is sufficient
when the petition sets out a good cause
of action on the account , and the prom-
ise to pay the amount due within two
years before suit. Adams v. O'Rear , 80
Ky . 129, 3 R. 605 ; Meyer v . Zotel,96 Ky .
362 , 16 R. 506 , 29 S. W. 28 .
(48 ) Note not assigned. Plaintiffcan
not maintain an action on a note aver-
ring that it was executed to himself
when the note was executed to another
person ,and not assigned to plaintiff, and
no allegation as to how be obtained it.
Dodd v.King ,1 Met .430 ; Haney v .Tem-
pest , 3 Met . 95 ; 1 Bush 70.
of all the members of the firm should be
stated. Newman's Pleadings and Prac-
tice,pages 218,287; Quinn v.N. N. & Μ .
V. Co., 15 R. 74, 22 S. W. 223.
(53 ) Partnership settlement - to cor-
rect. After the settlement of a partner-
ship,in an actionby one of the partners
attacking it for fraud or mistake , he
must allege the specificact of fraud or
the particularmistakesreliedon. A gen-
eral averment of fraud and mistake is
not sufficient. Loesser v . Loesser, 81 Ky .
139, 4 R. 942.
(54) Petition- provisions concerning.
A pleadingmay containas many causes
of action, legaland equitable, as there
may be grounds for on behalf of the
pleader (section113-2); but ,ifmore than
one, each one must be stated in a sepа-
rate,numbered paragraph(section113-3).
The statements of the pleading must be
consistent, and not inconsistent, with
those of any other pleadingpreviously
filedby the same party,unlesshe allege
in the alternative the existence of one or
another fact, and state that one , but
which one he does not know ,is true (sec-
tion 113-4). The pleading must be signed
by the party or attorney (section 115),
and need not state facts of which judicial
notice is taken , or the evidence (section
119-1). Private statute, how pleaded
(section 119-2), and judgment of court
(section 122 ).
(55 ) Petition must contain in its own
body and not merely by referenceto an-
other paper a statement of the facts con-
stituting the cause of action. Corbin v .
Oldham , 1 R. 327 ; Goff v. Janeway , 26
R. 525 , 82 S. W. 267 .(49 ) Note payable to two persons.
Neither has a separate right of action
upon itwithout an averment of assign- nary and conciselanguage the factscon-
ment from his co-payee . Quisenberry v .
Artis , 1 Duv .30 ; but see Perry v .Minor ,
98 Ky . 242 , 17 R. 868 , 32.S. W. 755 .
(50) Nuisance. Although nuisancebe
public in its nature , an individual may
recover damages caused by reason of it,
ifhe allegeand prove specialinjuryto
himself. Corley v . Lancaster , 81 Ky .
171 , 5 R. 39 .
(51 ) Obligor in name of obligee. One
of several joint and several obligorsmay
pay off the note,and ,by agreement with
the obligee, reserve the right to sue the
other obligorsin the name of the obligee.
Smith v . Latimer , 15 В. М. 75 .
(52 ) Parties - names of to be stated .
The full names of all the parties, plain-
tiff and defendant , and the fiducialor
other character in which they sue,or are
sued, should be stated in the body of the
petition; and ,ifpartners, the fullname
(56) The petition must state in ordi-
stituting the cause of action, and every
fact necessary to enable the plaintiffto
recover must be alleged,and every essen-
tial averment required to make a decla-
ration good at common law must be
stated. The facts must be so alleged as
to enable the opposite party to know
what is meant to be proved , and also
that an issue may be formed in regard
to the subject-matter in disputeand to
enable the court to pronounce the law
upon the facts as stated. L. & P. Canal
Co. v . Murphy , 9 Bush 522 ; Stivers v .
Baker , 87 Ky . 508 ; 10 R. 523, 9 S. W.
491 ; 14 B. M. 83 ; 7 Bush 532 ; 6 Bush
532 .
(57) Prayer of petition. The petition
stated the amount of claims sued on , and
prayer was for judgment for the amount
of said claims,with interestdue on each.
Held sufficientto entitle plaintiff to

v.Perry , 2 Bush 101; and see further as
to prayer forrelief,80 Ky .529,4 R. 470;
80 Ky . 371, 4 R. 255; Cum , T. & T. Co.
v.City of Hickman , 118 S. W. 311.
(58) The body of petitionand prayer
must be consistent, and the prayer for
general reliefmust be consistentwith
that specificallyprayed for. Crow v. 0 .
& N. R. R.,82 Ky .134,6 R. 6; Allsmiller
v. Freutchnect, 86 Ky . 198, 9 R. 509, 5
S. W. 746; Rochesterv. Anderson, Lit-
tell, S. C. 144.
(59) A party is not entitledto relief
that he does not ask for in his pleading.
Radford v . Southern Mutual Insurance
Co., 12 Bush 434 ; Howland v . Brown , 13
Bush 681; Duff v.Combs , 117 S. W. 259,
132 Ky .710; 11 Bush 49; and ifpetition
contains no prayer for reliefthe court
can not grant any . Bowman v.Ray ,25

ismade and thereisa generalprayer for

evidenceshows he is entitledto. Bank

elers Ins. Co. v. Henderson Mills, 27 R.
653, 85 S. W. 1090, 120 Ky . 218; Harbi-
son-Walker Ref . Co. v . McFarland's
Admr .,156 Ky .44,160 S. W. 798.
(60 ) In an action to enforce a mort-
gage lien the prayer was , "Wherefore
plaintiffprays that the mortgage, be
foreclosed, and for all other proper re-
lief," no defensebeingmade itwas error
torendera personaljudgment. Hansford
v.Holdam ,14 Bush 210.
(61) Prayer of petitionin action to
enjoin collectionof judgment "for all
properrelief" did not authorizejudgment
against defendant for amounts plaintiff
claimed to be entitled to . Walker v .
Thomas ,88 Ky .486, 11 R. 20, 11 S. W.
434. If no defense is made plaintiffcan
not have judgment for any reliefnot
specificallydemanded . Miller v. Allen,
104 Ky . 114,20 R. 463, 46 S. W. 523 ; and
see further as to relief that may be
granted under prayer for "general re-
lief," Lillard v. Brannin , 91 Ky . 511, 13
R. 74,16 S. W. 349 ; Bank v .Coke ,20 R.
291 , 45 S. W. 867 ; 80 Ky . 529, 4 R. 470 ;
80Ky .371,4 R.255.Alternativerelief,
see 9 R .. 166 .
(62 ) When the defendant answers and
there is a trial on the merits a liberal
rule should be applied in determining
the sufficiencyof the prayer to authorize
the reliefgranted . Abernathy v . Myers ,
99 S. W. 942,30 R. 844 ; and where issue
is joined in a suit in equity the court
may under a prayer for general relief
grant any reliefto which the parties
show themselves entitled. Heckling v .
Gehring, 100 S. W. 824, 30 R. 1198; Illi-
nois Cent . R. Co. v . Davidson , 115 S. W.
770; Alexander v . Owen Co., 124 S. W.
386, 136 Ky . 420 ; Harbison -Walker Ref .
Co. v . McFarland's Admr ., 156 Ky . 44 ,
160 S. W. 798; Ky . River Con . Coal Co.
v.Frazier,161Ky .374,170 S.W. 986.
(63) The body of the petitionand the
prayer should both be looked to, and a
deficiencyin one may be suppliedby the
averments of the other . Brashears v .
Brashears , 110 S. W. 303 ; Bradley v .
Sears,138 Ky .230,127 S. W. 782. Where
there are two or more paragraphs and in
each the same character of relief is
sought a prayer at the end of the last
paragraph willanswer forallthe para-
graphs. L. & N. R. Co.v.Adams ,148 Ky .
513, 147 S. W. 384.
(64) Paragraphing. See section 113
and notes thereto. Dailey v.O'Brien,96
S. W. 521, 29 R. 811.
(65) Personalinjuries- descriptionof
in petition. LouisvilleR. Co. v. Gaugh ,
118 S. W. 276, 133 Ky . 467 ; C. N. O. &
T. P. R. Co. v . Bennettee , 119 S. W. 181 ,
134 Ky . 19; MaysvilleR. Co. v. Willis,
104 S. W. 1016, 31 R. 1251.
(66 ) Precedent condition. At common
law the pleader was required not only to
aver the performance of the condition
precedent, but to state the time and
manner of its performance . This rule
was changed by sec.149 of Code of 1854.
Sec. 149 is omitted from this Code , and
its omission restores the common law
rule . Averbeck v . Hall , 14 Bush 505 ;
Ormsby v . City of Lou .,79 Ky . 197, 2 R.
297. The petitionmust aver the per-
formance of all conditions precedent in
the contract when the liabilityof de-
fendant depends on theirperformance.
Without such averments , no cause of
action exists. Frye v . L. & B. S. R. R.
Co., 2 Met . 314; Escott v. White , 10
Bush 169 .
(66a ) Subsequent condition. In an ac-
tion on a contract where the things to
be done by the plaintiffare subsequent
in point of time to the things done by
the defendant,the plaintiffneed not aver
performance;non-performanceismatter
of defense . Hall v . Inter . Lib . Union of
the World , 161 Ky . 299, 170 S. W. 631.(67 ) Promissory note immaterial
omissions. A petition founded on a note
must so set out the promise , its terms
and its breach as to enable the court to
render a judgment by default without
being compelledto resortto the note on
file; a promise or agreement to pay must
be averredor petitionwillnot be good.

Title VII ] [§ 90Petition .
Huffaker v. Nat . Bank of Monticello, 12
Bush 287 ; Davis 'v.Moxey , 19 R. 178, 39
S. W. 1116 Moore v. Moxey , 19 R. 160,
39 S.W. 420; seeHiblerv. Shipp,78 Ky .
64; Bank ofAnderson v.Foster,146 Ky .
179, 142 S. W. 225. In Totten v . Cooke ,
2 Met . 275 , it is held that the failure to
state date of note sued on and filed with
petitiondoesnotmakepetitiondefective,ifit state amount of note,time itis pay-
able and aver that defendant is indebted
in amount of note, naming it, and see
Burton v . White , 1 Bush 9. The acci-
dental omission of the words " the de-
fendants," the subject of the verb
"agreed," is not a substantialdefect.
"The omissionof thosethingswhich are
silentlyexpressedisof no consequence."
Warfield v.Gardner ,79 Ky .583, 3 R. 423 ;
and see Hibler v. Shipp , 78 Ky .64 ; Guig-
ley Arteburn , 17 R. 565 , 32 S. W. 165;
Graham v.LouisvilleCity Bank ,103 Ky .
641, 20 R. 295, 45 S. W. 870.
(68 ) The petition must state when
note fallsdue, or so specifydates that
the fact may be ascertainedwithout
going outside the pleading. Corbin v.
Oldham . 1 R. 327 .
(69 ) In an action on a note payable at
a certain time and place it is not neces-
sary to aver presentation and demand at
such time and place this is matter of
defense . New South Co. v. Price, 21 R.
11, 50 S. W. 963.
(70 ) Slander . In action for slander, it
is necessary to set forth the specific
words complained of; itis not sufficient
to state the effectof the words . Taylor
v . Moran , 4 Met . 126. And it is neces-
sary to aver that the words were spoken
maliciously. Williams v. Gordon, 11
Bush 693. In an action for libel and
slander, alienation of the affections of
the spouse of plaintiff resulting from
libelor slander may be pleaded as special
damages , althoughthe two causescould
not be joined. A recoveryin the action
for libelor slanderwhere such special
damages as pleaded would be a bar to a
new suitfor alienation. Felty v. Felty,
164 Ky . 356,175 S. W. 643 ; and see secs.
123 , 124 .
(71) Splitting cause of action not al-
lowable ,and where one has sued for part
of an entire demand he will not be al-
lowed to sue for remainder in another
action. Pilcher v . Ligon , 91 Ky . 228, 12
R. 861, 15 S. W. 513 ; Weinstock v . Bell-
wood ,12 Bush 139; Powell v. Weiler, 11
B. M. 187; Cole v.I.C. R. R.,27 R. 1087,
87 S. W. 1082; and thisruleappliesto
actions to recover land. Dills v . Justice,
137 Ky .822,and see 150 Ky .564.
(72 ) State. Action against agencies of
the government when allowable. Herr v.
CentralAsylum , 97 Ky .458, 17 R. 320,
30 S. W. 971.
(73) Surety againstcosurety. Action
can not be maintained by one surety
againsta cosuretyunlessthe principalis
insolvent, and this fact must be averred.
Bollingv.Doneghy, 1 Duv.220; Leev.
Forman , 3 Met . 113.
(74 ) Surety against principal. In ac-
tion by surety against principalto re-
cover money paid for him the petition
alegedthat the note was paid in fullby
plaintiffas surety, and that no part of
it has been repaidby defendant. The
petitionsetout a good causeof action
on the impliedpromise. Bridgesv.Reed ,
9 Bush 329 .
(75 ) Special damages - necessary aver-
ments in action to recover . I. C. R. Co.
v . Beeler, 142 Ky . 777, 135 S. W. 305 ;
L. & N. R. Co. v. Dickey , 31 R. 895, 104
S. W. 329 ; Lex . Ry . Co. v . Johnson , 139
Ky . 328, 122 S. W. 830. Damages of an
unusual and extraordinarynature, not
the necessary result of the wrong com-
plained of, must be pleaded as special
damages in the petition; an omissionto
plead them can not be suppliedby a bill
of particulars. Town of Erlanger v.Cody ,
158Ky .625,166S.W. 245.
(76) Tax title. Sheriff'sdeed is prima
facieevidenceof regularityof sale. Hus-
bands v . Polivick, 29 R. 890, 96 S. W.
825 ; Alexander v . Aud , 121 Ky . 105, 28
R. 68,88 S. W. 1103 ; Hughes v .Owens , 29
R. 140,92 S. W. 595.
(77 ) Title- to quiet- See Campbell v .
Disney, 93 Ky . 41, 13 R. 919, 18 S. W.
1027; Smith v. White , 19 R. 802, 41 S.
W. 436 ; Packard v . Beaver Valley Co.,
96 Ky .249 , 16 R. 451,28 S. W. 779 ; Hen-
derson v. Clark , 163 Ky . 192, 173 S. W.
367.
(78 ) Trespass . No one except the per-
son in actual possession can maintain an
actionfor trespass. Walden v.Conn ,84
Ky . 312, 8 R. 281 ; J. M. & I. R. R. Co.
v . Esterle, 13 Bush 667. Actual posses-
sion not necessary now to maintain ac-
tion. See Ky . Stat ., sec. 2361. In an
action for trespass on land , the petition
need not state a legal titlein the plain-
tiff. Stephens v. Stephens , 165 Ky . 353,
176 S. W. 1137 .
(79 ) Vendor's lien - to enforce . The
petition must set forth the terms of the
contract, the character of title to be
made , and that the vendor is able and
willing to convey according to the con-
tract . Calvin v . Duncan , 12 Bush 101 ;
Moore v .Moxey , 19 R. 160, 39 S. W. 420;
101

§ 90 ] [Title VIIPetition .
Williams v . Abrahams , 3 Bush 186 ; 79
Ky .465,3 R. 271 ; 78 Ky .607, and should
describe land sought to be subjected.
Lawless v . Barger , 9 Bush 665 ; Runyon
v.Darnall, 10 Bush 67 ; and see secs. 125,
692,694, and notes.
(80) Warranty in sale of land . Action
for breach of, see Mercantile Trust Co.
v. South Park Co., 94 Ky . 271, 15 R. 70,
22 S. W. 314; Jones v . Jones , 87 Ky .
82 , 9 R. 942 , 7 S. W. 886 ; Elliott v .
Saufley, 89 Ky . 52, 10 R. 958, 11 S. W.
200; Graham v . Dyer , 16 R. 541, 29 S. W.
340 .
(81) Writing should be set out. If
action is founded on a writing it should
be filed, and so much of it set forth as
willshow that the plaintiffby reasonof
allegedacts or omissions on his part,and
on part of defendants , is entitled to re-
lief; the petitionmust contain in itsown
body and not merely by reference to
another paper a statement of facts con-
stituting cause of action. Hill v. Bar-
rett, 14 B. M. 83 ; Murphy v. Estes, 6
Bush 532; 14 B. M. 252; 4 Met . 97. The
plaintiffcan not rely on a written con-
tract to support his action unless he has
it set up in his pleading. Thompson v .
Eversole ,162 Ky .836, 173 S. W. 165 ; and
see note 18, and sec. 120.
(82) Writing filedwith petition will
not cure defects in petition. Huffaker
v.Nat .Bank of Monticello,12 Bush 287 ;
Gebhard v. Garnier, 12 Bush 321 ; 6 Bush
532 ; 4 Met . 97 ; 2 Met . 88 ; 80 Ky . 368,
4 R. 192,and see note 18.
(83) If petitiondoes not aver that the
contractis in writing, or referto any
writing, it will be presumed it is parol.
Byassee v. Reese, 4 Met . 371; Hocker v.
Gentry , 3 Met . 463 ; 15 B. M. 443 ; 14
Bush 776 .
(84) Upon undertaking to pay debt of
another, petitionmust show undertaking
was in writing. Smith v . Fah , 15 B. М.
443 ; Linn Boyd Co. v . Terrill, 13 Bush
463 ; Smith v. Theobald , 86 Ky . 141, 9
R. 449, 5 S. W. 394 ; and see Ky . Stat .,
sec. 470 .
(85 ) An exhibit filed as the foundation
of the action controls the averments of
the petition. Kalfus v. Davie'sEx., 164
Ky . 390, 175 S. W. 652.
(86) Failure to filea writing under
Civil Code 120 is no ground for demurrer .
Com . v. Hume , 155 Ky . 475, 159 S. W.
966 .
(87) Trespass . A petitionwhich failed
to describe the real estate on which a
trespass was charged to have been com-
mitted was fatally defective. Hackney
v . Dillan, 9 Ky . Opinions 38.
(88) Land , recovery of. In an action
for the recovery of land it is not neces-
sary for the petitionto show how the
title was derived . War Fork Land Co. v .
Spivey , 162 Ky . 600, 172 S. W. 1042.
(89) Will, construction of. A petition
seeking to obtain the proper construc-
tion of a will in a case in which there
was a real controversy between the par-
ties in interest stated a cause of action .
Hanna v . Prewitt , 153 Ky . 310, 155 S. W.
726 .
(90) Corporation, suit on note of. In
an action against a corporation on a
promissory note,executed in the name of
the corporation, no allegation was made
as to the officerwho had executed it,nor
as to his authority. The petition was
good on demurrer . Williamsburg Can-
ning Co. v. Delaney , 158 Ky . 649, 166 S.
W. 192 .
(91) Death from negligence. In an
action under Kentucky Statutes 6, it is
unnecessary to allege that the decedent
did not know of the danger or defects.
L. & N. R. R. Co. v. Greenwell's Admr .,
155 Ky .799 , 160 S. W. 479.
(92) Reformation of pleadings on ap-
peal. Where the record is so imperfect
that the court can not determine with
fairness to the litigantswhat judgment
should be entered, the case will be re-
manded to reform the pleadings and take
such evidence as may be necessary to
present clearlythe issuesbetween the
parties, so that justicemay be done.
Nickels v. Collins,158 Ky . 117, 164 S. W.
984 .

§ 92. (1) Error as to proceeding. That
action is brought in equity when it
should have been at law is not ground of
demurrer . Trustees of Leb . v . Forrest ,
15 В. М. 168 ; 14 B. M. 348 , it may be
transferred to proper docket , sec. 8.
(2) Jurisdiction. When courthas no
jurisdictionof subject-matter , the error
is not waived by failingto demur .
Hughey v. Sidwell, 18 B. M. 259 ; Fidler
v.Hall ,2 Met . 461 ; Barton v. Barton , 80
Ky . 212 , 3 R. 746. When the court has
jurisdiction of the subject-matter , ob-
jection to the want of jurisdictionover
the person may be waived. Hughes v.
Hardesty , 13 Bush 364; 4 Bush 619 ; 80
Ky . 212 , 3 R. 746.
(3) Parties- defect of. In action in
the name of A. J. Morrison & Co. on a
note executed to A. J. Morrison & Co. ,
it does not appear from the petition
whether A. J. M. & Co. is one person or
a firm , and demurrer will not lie. Mor-
rison v . Tate , 1 Met . 569. If defect does
not appear in petition, it must be taken
advantage of by answer , or it will be
waived . Sec. 118 ; 17 B. M. 598 ; 3 Met .
134; 3 Bush 200 ; 80 Ky . 684, 4 R. 682 ;
10 Bush 763 ; and see notes to sec. 118.
Defect of parties is waived by failureto
raise the question in the Circuit Court .
New Bell JellicoCo. v. Braznell'sAdmr .,
149 Ky . 418 , 149 S. W. 888 .
(4) Practice- when special demurrer
is sustained and error not cured by
amendment action should be dismissed
without prejudice. Walton v. Washburn ,
23 R. 1008 , 64 S. W. 634 ; sec. 371 .
(5) Special demurrer not necessary
when plaintiffhas no right at all to
maintain action; question can be raised
by general demurrer . L. & N. R. R. v .
Brantley , 96 Ky . 297, 16 R. 691, 28 S. W.
477. A special demurrer reaches only
defects apparent on the face of the peti-
tion. Louisville& N. R. Co. v. Stewart ,
163 Ky . 164, 173 S. W. 757.
(5a) Where the court has jurisdiction
both of the subject-matter and the par-
ties, and defendants have capacity to
sue, and there is no other action pend-
ing between the same parties for the
same cause , and there being no defect of
parties, defendants' right to a set-off or
counter-claim can not be tested by spe-
cialdemurrer . Payne v .Vowels , 171 Ky .
377, 188 S. W. 413.
(6) Special demurrer - waiver . If legal
incapacity appear on face of petition,and
defendant fails to demur , it is waiver of
error. Pritchard v . Peace , 98 Ky . 99 ; 17
R. 662, 32 S. W. 296 ; Lee v . Russell,
18 R. 951 , 38 S. W. 874 ; Petty v . Malier ,
14 В. М. 246 ; 15 B. M. 584 ; 12 Bush 327 ;
80 Ky . 684, 4 R. 682 ; 79 Ky . 583, 3 R.
423; and filinga general demurrer is a
waiver of objection for defect of parties.
Walton v . Washburn , 23 R. 1008, 64 S.
W. 634 .
(7 ) Two actions pending . A brought
suit against B in the Wayne Circuit
Court to recover property ,and the action

(8) An action will not be abated on a
plea that there is an action pending be-
tween the same parties on the same debt
in a court of another State . The Code
only appliesto actionspending in this
State . Davis v .Morton ,4 Bush 442. But
the pendency of an action in the federal
courts of this State will bar an action
for the same cause in State courts . Wil-
son v.Milliken, 103 Ky . 165, 19 R. 1843 ,

but they must both be prosecuted for the

v. Ohio Val. Tie Co., 161 Ky . 212, 170
S. W. 633 .
(9 ) Pendency of an action in a court
that has no jurisdictiondoes not prevent
institutionofactioninpropercourt. Lou.
Water Co. v. Clark,94 Ky .47,14 R. 886,
21 S. W. 246 .
(10) An objectionthat a former action
is pending is removed by itsdismissal or
discontinuance even after plea of abate-
ment in the second suit. Citizens Bank
v . Forman , 111 Ky . 206, 23 R. 613, 63 S.
W. 454; Wilson v.Milliken,103 Ky . 165,
19 R. 1843, 44 S. W. 660.
(11) Verification- misjoinder. Objec-
tion that petition not verifiedcan not
be made by demurrer . Harris v. Ray ,
15 В. М. 628 ; it should be by rule to
verify. Nor can misjoinder of parties be
reached by demurrer . Dean v . English ,
18 В. М. 132. An objection to the mis-
joinder of causes of action can not be
raisedby demurrer to the jurisdiction.
L. & N. R. R. Co. v . Mitchell, 162 Ky .
253 , 172 S. W. 527 ; see sec. 85.
(12) Waiver . See sub-sec. 4 of this
section , and sec . 118 and notes thereto .
(13) Waiver . Although action is
brought in a county other than the one
in which it should be under sec. 62 the
objection to the jurisdiction will be
waived if not made in proper manner
and time . Gillen v . Illinois Cent . R. R.
Co., 125 S. W. 1047, 137 Ky . 375.
(14) Objection to jurisdictionmay be
made in separate paragraph of answer
and will not enter appearance . Louis-
ville Home Tel . Co. v . Beeler , 101 S. W.
397, 31 R. 23, 125 Ky . 366. Under this
section and sec. 118 the general appear-
ance of the defendant in a bastardy pro-
ceedings,beforemaking objectionto the
jurisdictionof the court, does not waive
such objection. Com . v . Adkins , 169 Ky .
487, 184 S. W. 372. A petitiondid not
show whether the county in which the
actionwas brought was or was not the
proper county in which to bring it; the
question of jurisdictioncould not be

§ 93 [121] [General demurrer defined- costs.] 1. A generalde-

raisedby demurrer. L. & N. R. R. Co.

on appeal . Klasener v . Robinson , 100 S.
W. 255, 30 R. 1032 ; but objection will be

Where a petitionor other pleading, on

sociation can not be sued in the name

Mine Work .of Amer . v . Cromer .159 Ky .
605 , 167 S. W. 891. The fact that in an

ate the proceedings; advantage may be

have capacity to sue can not be made by
general demurrer . L. & N. R. Co. v .
Herndon , 104 S. W. 732, 31 R. 1059, 126
Ky . 589.
(17) Objection,how made - Ifthe peti-
tion does not disclosethe want of jur-
isdiction the question should be raised
by pleadingas providedin sec.118.Rich-
ardson v . L. & N. R. Co., 112 S. W. 582,
129 Ky .449. A specialdemurrer can not
raiseobjectionto the jurisdictionby re-
citingfactsnot shown by the petition.

253 ,172 S. W. 527,and see note 15,supra .
§ 93. (1) Admissions by . Demurrer
admits the truth of allfacts well pleaded,
and every allowableand proper deduc-
tion therefrom . Norman v .Ky .Board of
Managers ,93 Ky .537, 14 R. 529,20 S. W.
901 ; Morgan v. Ballard, 1 Mar. 558 ; Bank
v .Newport , 1 B. M. 13 ; but exhibits re-
ferred to must be taken into view as con-
trollinganystatementsinconsistentwith
them . Bush v. Maderia , 14 B. M. 212 ;
Collins v . Blackburn , 14 B. M. 252 ; and .
see note 18, sec. 90 .
(2) The admission of facts by a de-
murrer is equivalent to proof by a wit-
ness in open court. Francis v . Wood ,
81 Ky . 16, 4 R. 616 ; but only facts well
pleaded are admitted and not legal con-
clusions. Norman v . Ky . Board of Man-
agers,93 Ky .537,14 R. 529,20 S. W. 901.(3) Answer effect of. Defendant
waives all objectionto petitionby an-
swering except objection to jurisdiction,
and that petitiondoes not state cause of
action; and upon demurrer to answer , no
objection to petition except these can be
considered. Mitchell v .Mattingly , 1 Met .
237 and seeSchoolerv.Yancy ,118 S.W.
940 , 133 Ky . 695 .
(4) Afterdemurrertopetitionisover-
ruled and answer filed, no objection to
petition is available, unless it amounts
to cause for arrestof judginent. Dun-
can v .Brown ,15 B. M. 186.
(5 ) Where an answer is filed at same
time with demurrer ,the fact that parties
proceed to trialwithout noticingde-
murrer authorizes inference that de-
murrer was overruled or waived , D. L.
& N. Co. v . Stewart , 2 Met . 119.
(6 ) Filing answer and demurrer at
same time does not preclude defendant

from waiving rightto trialupon merits
and standingby hisdemurrer. Bridgesv.

ant by filinga generaldemurrer waives
his rightto thereafterobjectto the jur-
isdictionof the court over his person .
McDowell v. C., O. & S. W. R. R. Co.,90
Ky . 346, 12 R. 331, 14 S. W. 338 ; Chapin
v . Fulkerson , 95 Ky . 277, 15 R. 635, 24
S. W. 1066; but does not by making mo-
tion to quash returnon summons or by
filingan answer objectingto jurisdiction.
C.,O. & S. W. R. R. v. Heath, 87 Ky .
651, 10 R. 646, 9 S. W. 832 ; Barbour v .
Newkirk , 83 Ky . 529, 7 R. 555 ; and see
note 9 , sec. 39 .
(8) Contributory neglect is a defense
which confessesand avoidsthe plaintiff's
case,and as a rule must be affirmatively
pleaded, but where the petitionstates
factsshowing that injury sued for was
caused by neglect of plaintiff, it may be
reached by demurrer . Favre v . L. & Ν .
R. R.,91 Ky .541,13 R. 116, 16 S. W. 370.
(9) Costs on . The failure of a party
to demur at proper time does not render
him liable for costs unless he subse-
quently enters a demurrer . American
Wire Co. v. Bayless , 91 Ky . 94, 12 R.
694 , 15 S. W. 10. But if the defendant
failsto demur to a defectivepetitionand
⚫ appealsfrom a judgment by default, he
must pay the costs in the Court of Ap-
peals. Moore v. Moxey , 19 R. 160, 39 S.
W. 420; Bush v. Lou . Trust Co., 24 R.
2182 , 73 S. W. 775 ; and see Combs v .
Pridmore , 19 R. 1934, 43 S. W. 681 ; hold-
ing that a failure to demur renders de-
fendant liablefor all costs incurred by
the failure .
( 10 ) Defects in form . Defects in form
of pleading can only be reached by mo-
tion to make averments more specific.
Posey v.Green,78 Ky .162; Ib.210; and
see sec . 134 .
(11) Although petition fails to show
that party is entitled to the specificre-
lief asked , if it shows a right to any
relief a demurrer should not be sus-
tained . Foster v . Watson , 16 B. М. 377 .
(12) Setting out several defenses in
one paragraph is not cause of demurrer ;
the objectioncan be reached by motion
to paragraph . Williams v . Langford , 15
B. M. 566 ; see sec. 113-3 . And if one of
severalparagraphs in an answer pre-
sents a defense , a demurrer should not
be sustained to it as a whole . Archer v .
Nat . Ins . Co. , 2 Bush 226 .
(13) Demurrer brings all previous
pleadings before the court, and judgment
on the demurrer shouldbe againstparty
who committed first error . Wile v ..
Sweeney,2 Duv.161; Youngv.Duhme,4 Met . 239 ; Martin v . McDonald , 14 В.
M. 544 ; 1 Met . 237 ; 79 Ky . 487, 3 R.
273 .
(14) Judgment on demurrer- bar. An
order sustaining a demurrer can not be
appealed from unlessa judgment follow
it. Alexander v . de Kermel , 81 Ky . 345,
5 R. 382 ; Com . v . L. & N. R. R., 16 R.
484 , 29 S. W. 331; but see Zable v. Or-
phans ' Home , 92 Ky . 89, 13 R. 385, 17 S.
W. 212 .
(15) Judgment on demurrer ,unless pe-.
tition states cause of action, is no bar
to futureactionallegingfactsentitlingplaintiffto recover. Birch v. Funk , 2
Met .544; Pepper v.Donelly,87 Ky . 259,
10 R. 140, 8 S. W. 441 ; Potter v . Benge ,
24 R. 24 , 67 S. W. 1005. But he will not
be allowed, after setting forth a state of
facts in his petition, whether sufficient
or insufficient, to authorize a recovery
aftera generaldemurrer has been sus-
tained to his pleading and his action dis-
missed , to bring another action settingforth substantially the same facts.
Woolley v. Lou . Banking Co., 81 Ky .527 ,
5 R. 562; Thomas v. Bland, 91 Ky . 1,
12 R. 640, 14 S. W. 955.
(16) Limitation. Demurrer to petition
upon ground that action is barred by
limitation can not be sustained unless
petition shows not only a sufficientlapse
of time, but the non -existence of any
ground of avoidance . Rankin v. Turney ,
2 Bush 555 ; Board v . Jolly, 5 Bush 86 ;
Chiles v . Drake , 2 Met . 146 ; Com . v .
Gardner , 17 R. 75 , 30 S. W. 413 ; Schnei-
der v .Schlentker,23 R. 951, 64 S. W. 505.
But when the petition shows that the
action is barred and that plaintiff is not
within any of the exceptions contained
in the statute which save his right to
sue, a demurrer will be sustained. Still-
well v . Leavy , 84 Ky . 379, 8 R. 321, 1
S. W. 590 .
(17) Misjoinder. That amended peti-
tion departs from cause of action is not
ground for demurrer . Motion to strike
out is proper way to reach misjoinder.
Hord v . Chandler ,13 B. M. 402. See sec.
85 , and sec . 113-4 .
(18) Misnomer of writing. The mis-
nomer of writing sued on is not cause of
demurrer . Bradley v.Mason .6 Bush 602.
(19) Objection in Court of Appeals to
sufficiencyof petition is confined to the
point that it does not state facts suffi-
cient to constitute a cause of action , and
if the issue in lower court was made by
answer or submitted to and tried by a
jury, defectsin petitionmust be disre-
106

Title VII ] [§ 94Demurrer .
§ 94 [122] [Amendment afterdemurrer- costs. Ifthecourtsustain
thedemurrer,thepartymay amend thepleading,withleaveof court;but
inallcaseshe shallpay thecostsresultingfrom thedemurrer.
Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr . (3d Ed .), secs. 190, 560, 581, 601, 902 ;
Forms Nos .407 , 410 .
garded in Court of Appeals . Davis v.
Wiley , 3 R. 315.
(20 ) Petition, how tested. On general
demurrer to petition, its sufficiency
should not be tested by blending with
itsaverments any matter of defense de-
fendant may have . Yewell v . Bradshaw ,
2 Duv . 573; Macklin v. Trustees,88 Ky .
592 , 11 R. 75 , 11 S. W. 657 .
(21) Practice. A general demurrer to
an answer containing several paragraphs
is not good if either of the paragraphs
presents a defense,but upon a demurrer
to "each of the paragraphs" itisproper
to sustain it as to those that are de-
fective . Sanford v . Lowenthall , 5 R. 206 .
(22 ) Plaintifffiledtwo amended peti-
tions , and then defendant demurred to
"whole case" ; this, though inartificial,
put in issue sufficiencyof all facts set
out in petition and amendments . Miles
v.Collins,1 Met . 308 .
(23) Where the courterroneouslysus-
tains a general demurrer it is proper
afterward to disregard the ruling. Ste-
venson v .Flournoy ,89 Ky .561, 11 R. 745,
13 S. W. 210; and although a defendant
does not demur to the petition the court
has a right upon the final hearing to
decide that no cause of action is pre-
sented. American Wire Nail Co. v. Bay-
less, 91 Ky . 94, 12 R. 694, 15 S. W. 10.
(24 ) When demurrer to answer is car-
riedback to petition,the petitionis to be
considered without reference to answer
or exhibits filedwith it, although a de-
murrer to petition has been overruled
before filingof answer . Macklin v . Trus-
tees,88 Ky .592, 11 R. 75, 11 S. W. 657.
(24a ) If the uncertainty in a pleading
is such that it states no cause of action
or defense, a demurrer is proper. Reid
v. Lyttle, 150 Ky . 304, 150 S. W. 357.
(24b ) In an action on an accident in-
surance policy the petitionfailedto
state that the death was accidental .
Issues of fact were made and tried ; but
none was made upon thispoint. A judg-
ment notwithstanding the verdict was
proper; but the defendant was liablefor
costs accruing from the time its answer
was filed. Farnsley's Admr . v . Phil. Life
Ins. Co., 156 Ky . 699, 161 S. W. 1111.
(25 ) Special demurrer is not necessary
where plaintiffhas no right at all to
maintain action; objection can be made
by general demurrer . L. & N. R. R. v .
Brantley ,96 Ky . 297, 16 R. 691, 28 S. W.
477 .
(26 ) Statute of frauds. When petition
shows want of necessary writing, it is
not necessary to plead statute of fraud ;
question can be raised by demurrer . Linn
Boyd Co. v . Terrill, 13 Bush 463 ; Smith
v. Theobald, 86 Ky . 141, 9 R. 449, 5 S.
W. 394 .
(27) Practice - amended petition.
Where the whole cause of action is set
out in an amended petitionits sufficiency
is to be determined by its averments
alone. Robards v . Bannon Sewer Pipe
Co., 130 Ky . 380, 113 S. W. 429.
§ 94. (1) Practice- court not re-
quired to offerparty leave to amend ,but
party may ask for that privilege. Wil-
son v . Com .,99 Ky . 167,18 R. 51, 35 S. W.
274 ; and see as to amendments , sec. 134
and notes .
(2) Amended pleading- delay in ten-
deringwhen not ground fordismissalfor
want of prosecution. Quinn v .Cincinnati
R. Co., 97 S. W. 379, 30 R. 15.

§ 95] [TitleVIIAnswer , Counter -Claim , Set-off, Cross-Petition.
CHAPTER IV .
ANSWER , COUNTER -CLAIM , SET -OFF , CROSS -PETITION .
§ 95. Answer - what itmay contain.
§ 96. Counter-claim, set-off, cross-petition.
§ 97. Proceedingsupon counter-claim,set-off,cross-petition- caption.
§ 95 [125]
tain-
[Answer - what it may contain.) An answer may con-
1. [Traverse.] A traverse.
2. [Facts constitutingestoppelor avoidance.] A statementof facts
which constitutean estoppelagainst,or avoidance of, a cause of action
statedin thepetition.
ANSWER .
§ 95. (1) Accord and satisfaction. Το
make the plea good , it should be alleged
that the matter was accepted in satisfac-
tion. Averment thatthe party agreed
to accept it in satisfactionis bad plead-
ing. Johnson v. Hunt , 81 Ky . 321, 5 R.
194 Hale v . Grogan , 99 Ky . 170, 18 R.
46 ,35 S. W. 282. See Williams v . Lang-
ford , 15 B. M. 566 .
(2 ) Administrator against distributee.
To a suit brought on a note by an ad-
ministrator against husband of distribu-
tee , the answer stated that no distri-
bution had been made , and asked to be
allowed to retainamount as advance-
ment ; held not good because it did not
aver that administrator had in his hands
for distribution an amount sufficient to
entitle each distributee to a sum equal
to amount of note. Haggard v.Hay , 13
Β . Μ . 174 .
(3) Amended petition. No answer is
required to amended petition when the
denials of the answer to petitionare suf-
ficient,and apply as wellto the amended
as to the originalpetition. Robinson v.
Williamson ,7 Bush 604 .
(4 ) Appearance to action is entered by
filinganswer . Elliott Co. v . Kitchen , 14
Bush 289 ; and see further notes to sec.
39,as to what will enter appearance .
(5 ) Attachment without cause . In ac-
tion for suing out an attachment with-
out good cause the answer was "that the
grounds allegedby the defendantas con-
stituting the cause for its issual did in
fact,as he believes,exist and were true;"
held insufficient. Mitchell v . Mattingly ,
1 Met .237; seefurther,Ky . Stat.,sec.7.
(6 ) Conclusions of law . In action to
recover homestead , statement in answer
that defendant is an "innocent pur-
chaser" is merely a conclusion of law .
Wing v.Hayden,10 Bush 276.
(7 ) In action against carrier for loss
of goods , averment in answer that acci-
dent was unforeseen, inevitable or un-
avoidable, or that it was caused by dan-
gers of the river, are conclusions of law
and not good ; the facts should be stated.
Bentley v . Bustard , 16 B. М. 643.
(8 ) In action against sherifffor failing
to collectexecution, answer stating that
allthe propertyowned by executionde-
fendant was exempt from execution,
without averringthat he was a house-
keeper with a family , is bad . Com . v .
Lay , 12 Bush 283 .
(9 ) Conjunctive denial- grouping facts.
Denial in the conjunctiveis not good.
Taylor v.Farmer,81 Ky . 458,5 R. 487;
nor can two or more facts be grouped to-
gether and denied ; each material fact
must be separatelydenied. Preston v.
Roberts,12 Bush 570; Morgan v.Booth,
13 Bush 480; Stephens v. Wilson, 115
Ky .27,24 R. 1832,72 S. W. 336 ; Johnson
v .Asher , 105 S. W. 943 , 32 R. 31.
(10) Contributoryneglect- how itmay
be pleaded . C. & . O.O. R. R. v . Smith , 101
Ky . 104, 18 R. 1079, 39 S. W. 832 ; reply
is necessary to plea of, L. & N. R. R. v .
Mayfield , 18 R. 224, 35 S. W. 924 ; L. &
N. R. R. v. Copas, 95 Ky . 460, 16 R. 14,
26 S. W. 179 ; Gore v . Ill. Central R. R.,
17 R. 799, 32 S. W. 754.
(11) Decedent's estate. In suit for
settlement of an estate,creditormay file
petition settingup his claim,and ifclaim
108

Title VII ] [§ 95Answer , Counter-Claim, Set-off, Cross-Petition.
3. [Set-offor counter-claim.] A statementof factswhich consti-
tute a set-off or counter -claim.
4. [Cross-petition.] A cross-petition. (Forms ofanswers,Forms
55-79;timeof filing,secs.102,104; may be filedinvacation,secs.107,
108;traversedefined,sec.113;what allegationsmust be traversed,sec.
126. See further,PracticeAct,sec.367a.)
Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr . (3d Ed .), secs. 397, 402 , 413, 418, 425 ,
426f,682 ; Forms Nos ,182, 184-258, 259-268.
is controverted, has a right to demand
that it shall be done by answer , as in
other cases. Horner v. Harris, 10 Bush
357; Callenderv. Callender, 24 R. 1145,
70 S. W. 844 .
(12) Defectiveallegationsin petition
cured by answer or verdict. It is a set-
tled rule of practicethat defectiveallega-
tionsof the plaintiff'spleadingmay be
supplied and cured by the answer ; and
the doctrine of the Code is that ver-
dicts and judgments which have been
rendered upon substantial issues of fact
fairlypresentedby the pleadingsshall
not be disturbed on account of mere tech-
nical defects not affectingthe merits of
the controversy. Code , sec. 134. Riggs
v. Maltby , 2 Met . 88 C. & O. R. R. v .
Thieman , 96 Ky . 507, 16 R. 611, 29 S. W.
357; the failureto allegea fact that
would have rendered the petition bad on
demurrer will be cured by an answer
denying the existence of the fact. L. &
N. R. R. v . Lawson , 88 Ky . 496, 11 R.
38 , 11 S. W. 511 ; Pugh v . White ,78 Ky .
210 ; 78 Ky . 435 ; 78 Ky . 463 ; 13 Bush
601; 82 Ky .291,6 R. 341; but if the peti-
tion fail to allege matter which is essen-
tial to make out a cause of action, the
defect will not be cured by answer .
Bogenschutz v. Smith , 84 Ky . 330, 8 R.
376 ; Drake v. Semonin , 82 Ky . 291, 6 R.
341; C. & O. R. R. v. Thierman , 96 Ky .
507, 16 R. 611,29 S. W. 357.
As to defects cured by verdict-
"The rule is that when there is any de-
fect, imperfection or omission , even of
substance, in a pleading which would
have been fatal on demurrer , yet if the
issuejoinedbe such as necessarilyre-
quiredon the trialproof of the factsso
imperfectly stated or omitted , and with-
out which it is not to be presumed the
judge would directthe jury to give, or
that the jury would have given the ver-
dict, such defect or omission is cured by
the verdict." Drake v. Semonin , 82 Ky .
291, 6 R. 341 ; L. & P. Canal Co. v . Mur-
phy, 9 Bush 522; Rogers v. Felton, 98
Ky . 148,17 R. 724, 32 S. W. 405; C. &
O. R. Co. v. Thierman,96 Ky .507,16 R.
611, 29 S. W. 357; the rule is that all
ambiguities and uncertainties in plead-
ingswillbe construedagainstthepleader
before verdict, yet after verdict plead-
ingswillbe liberallyconstruedto uphold
it. Worthly v.Hammond ,13 Bush 510;and see E. & P. R. R. Co. v . Pottinger,
10 Bush 185 ; 10 Bush 169; but verdict
will not cure failureto allegematter
essential to make out a cause of action .
Bogenschutz v. Smith, 84 Ky . 330, 8 R.
376, 1 S. W. 578 ; Drake v . Semonin , 82
Ky . 291, 6 R. 341; City of Henderson v .
Sizemore , 104 S. W. 722 ,31 R. 1134.
(13) Defense by one that goes to
merits of action presents defense for all.
Rouse v . Howard , 1 Duv . 31 ; Wiliams v .
Rogers , 14 Bush 776 .
(14) Defenses to be pleaded. The Code
requires all defenses, equitable as well
as legal, to be pleaded to an action at
law; and an equitableright available
may be lost unless thus litigated.
Hackett v . Schad ,3 Bush 353 ; sec. 113-2.
(15) Denial of knowledge or informa-
tion. Want of knowledge or informa-
tion sufficient to form a belief is not
sufficientdenial of the alleged presenta-
tion, demand , refusal of payment and
protestof a bill, when the billshowing
these factsis filedwith petition. Grid-
ler v . F. and D. Bank , 12 Bush 333 ; Ib.
287. Nor is denial that note was duly
protested, or that party received legal
notice of protest, sufficient. 7 Bush 474 ;
12 Bush 287. Denial of knowledge or
information by a personal representative
is equivalent to a direct denial. Drake
v.Semonin,82 Ky .291,6 R. 341; and see
sec . 113-7 .
(16) When the record as made up
when plea isfiledfurnishes the necessary
information, or when the fact is neces
sarilywithinthe personalknowledge of
the party , the plea of want of knowledge
or information is not good . Barrett v .
Godshaw ,12 Bush 592 ; Ib. 333; Augustus
v .Holt , 13 R. 8, 15 S. W. 1064 ; McClure
v .Bigstaff, 18 R. 601,37 S. W. 294 ; Lucas
109

§ 95 ] [Title VIIAnswer , Counter -Claim , Set-off, Cross-Petition.
v. Lucas , 18 R. 661, 37 S. W. 588 ; Mt.
Sterlingv. First Natl. Bank , 147 Ky .
376 , 144 S. W. 370 .
(17) Denial of knowledge sufficientto
form a beliefis not good ; it should be a
denialofanyknowledgeorinformation.
Trustees v. Fleming , 10 Bush 234, and
see C. & S. R. R. Co. v . Gray , 10 R. 292,
8 S. W. 876 .
(18) That defendant "knows nothing
on the subject of his own knowledge " is
not sufficient. Terrillv.Jennings,1 Met .
450. That defendant has " no knowledge
of any of the facts set up in the petition,
and no means of forming a belief, and
thereforedenies them all and requires
proof," is not good denial. Corbin v.
Com ., 2 Met . 380. Statement that de-
fendant does not admit certain facts and
callsfor proof, or does not owe debt sued
for, is not sufficientdenial. Clark v .
Finnell, 16 B. M. 329. See Hutchins v .
Moore , 4 Met . 109; and answer which
merely denies "each averment of the
petition" is no answer at all. Evans v .
Evans , 93 Ky . 510, 14 R. 628, 20 S. W.
605 .
(19) An answer to suit for goods sold
and delivered, denying "that defendant
is, to the best of hisknowledge and be-
lief, indebted to the plaintiffas stated
in petition," is insufficient. Francis v.
Francis, 18 B. M. 57 ; 8 Bush 583. Nor
is a denial that defendant is indebted as
alleged,or thataccountisjust,sufficient.
Thruston v.Oldham ,6 Bush 16.
(20) Itisonlywheretheallegationsof the petitionare not necessarilywithin
the knowledge of the party charged ,that
thischaracter of denial is good ; as where
the party is sought to be made liablefor
the action or misconduct of others, or
where some fact is asserted that defend-
ant could not say whether it is true or
false. Wing v. Dugan , 8 Bush 583 ; 12
Bush 608. Questions of law can not be
denied by a statement of want of knowl-
edge or information sufficientto form a
belief. Ky . Nav . Co. v . Com ., 13 Bush
435 ; 83 Ky . 410, 7 R. 419.
(21 ) In action against carrierfor neg-
lect in carrying goods a specificdenial
of itsnegligenceis necessary. N., C. &
St.L. R. R. v. Carrico,95 Ky .489, 16 R.
66 , 26 S. W. 177 .
(22 ) Denial of title. When a vendor
sues to enforcean executory contract,
averring that he is able, ready and will-
ing to convey title,and tendersconvey-
ance, vendee must aver he has no title;
or, if it is defective, must point out de-
fects. Logan v.Bull,78 Ky .607.
(23 ) In an action on a note executed
for price of a slave, statement in answer
"that the vendor had no title and had
not and could not make any title" does
not present a defense, as it failsto aver
anywarrantyoftitle. Patrickv.Swin-ney , 5 Bush 421.
(24) Fraud. Answer averring that
writing sued on was obtained, or its ex-
ecution procured, by fraud, misrepre-
sentationand covin, without specifically
averring the facts constitutingthe fraud ,
is good. Evans v. Stone, 80 Ky . 78, 3
R. 751 ; 2 Met . 584 ; Craft v . Barron , 28
R. 98 , 88 S. W. 1099 .
(25 ) General denial. To a suit for $620,
balance due for merchandise ,without any
specificationof articles sold, defendant
answered , denying that she was indebted
in more than $500. Held good defense to
allover $500. If petition,or account filed
with it,specifieditems , answer would not
be good . Webb v. Jeffries, 2 Bush 221.
(26) Answer to a suit on a note deny-
ing that defendant owes the debt , or any
part of it, is not good . Haggard v . Hay ,
13 В. М. 174. Answer denying that de-
fendant is indebted as alleged, or that
the accountisjust,isnot good. Thrus-
ton v . Oldham , 6 Bush 16.
(27 ) Denial of the correctness of each
and every item of the account sued on is
insufficient. Whitaker v .Sandifer, 1 Duv .
261; and see Baum v . Winston , 3 Met .
127. To present good denial, answer
shouldcontrovertseparatelyand specifi-
cally each charge made in the petition.
Morganv.Booth,13 Bush480.
(28) Inconsistentdefenses. Party may
plead as many defenses as he has ; pro-
vided they are not inconsistent, sec.
113-4 . In an action for slander defendant
can not in one paragraph deny, and in
another one admit , the speaking , and
justify. Rooney v. Tierney, 82 Ky .253,
6 R. 231. It was otherwise under Code
of 1854; see 10 Bush 447.
(29 ) Infancy . Statement in answer
that " at the time of the execution of the
note sued on defendant was an infant
under the age of twenty -one years," is
a good plea of infancy. Stern v. Free-
man , 4 Met . 308 .
(30) Legislature no power to prescribe
form of answer that cuts off substantial
defenses by limiting defendant to certain
defenses. City of Lou .v .Cochran ,82 Kу .
15, 5 R. 833 ; Meguiar v . Henry , 84 Ky .
1 , 7 R. 695 .
(31) Limitation. Statute of limitation
must be pleaded,althoughactionbe ap-
parentlybarred,unlessthepetitionshows
the non -existenceof every ground of

Title VII ] [§ 96Answer , Counter -Claim , Set-off, Cross-Petition.
§ 96 [126,128] [Counter-claim, set-off, cross-petition.] 1. [Counter-
claim,defined.] A counter-claimisa causeof actioninfavorofa defend-
ant againsta plaintiff,or againsthim and another,which arisesoutof the
contract, or transactions, statedin the petitionas the foundation of the
plaintiff'sclaim,or which isconnectedwith thesubjectof the action. (See
further, secs. 99, 372. See as to noticewhen lienon realpropertyisas-
serted,Ky .Stat.,sec.2358a.)
avoidance . Chiles v. Drake , 2 Met . 146;
5 Bush 86 ; 2 Bush 555. But when peti-
tion shows that action is barred and that
plaintiffis not within any of the exceр-
tions contained in the statute, question
can be made by demurrer . Stillwellv .
Leavy , 84 Ky . 379, 8 R. 321, 1 S. W. 590.
(32) Misnomer of defendant . As to
effect of when defendant enters appear-
ance . See Heckman v . L. & N. R. R. Co. ,
85 Ky . 631, 9 R. 297, 4 S. W. 342; L. &
N. R. R. Co. v . Hall, 12 Bush 131 ; and
note 4 , sec . 39 .
(33 ) No consideration. Plea that a
note , bond or other like instrument was
given without any considerationisgood,
Evans v . Stone , 80 Ky .78, 3 R. 751 ; and
see Coyle v . Fowler , 3 J. J. M. 472 .
(34) Non est factum . Plea of non est
factum must be made in affirmative lan-
guage and sworn to. Trusteesv. Flem-
ing, 10 Bush 234 ; Dugan v . Harris, 6 R.
596. As to sufficiencyof plea when made
by a surety, see Garrett v . Ashcraft , 19
R. 38, 39 S. W. 51 ; and see Ky . Stat.,
sec . 473 .
(35) Under general plea of non est
factum , a question as to the conditional
signing and delivery of a writing can not
be considered; it must be specially
pleaded . Hall v. Smith , 14 Bush 604 .
(36) Nul tielrecord. Plea of nul tiel
record is not good in an action in this
State on the judgment of a justiceof
the peace of another State. McElfatrick
v . Taft ,10 Bush 160.
(37 ) Payment . Plea of does not re-
quire a reply. Logan County Bank v .
Barclay , 104 Ky . 97, 20 R. 773, 46 S. W.
675; Ermert v. Deitz, 19 R. 1639, 14 S.
W. 138 .
(38 ) Statute of frauds . It is not nec-
essary to answer and rely on the statute
of frauds when petition shows want of
necessary writing; question can be made
by demurrer , Linn Boyd Co. v . Terrill,
13 Bush 463 ; Smith v. Theobald , 86 Ky .
141 , 9 R. 449 , 5 S. W. 394 .
(39 ) Taxes . Set -off is not allowable
against demand for taxes unless ex-
pressly authorizedby statute. Ander-
son v .City of Mayfield,93 Ky .230 ,14 R.
370, 19 S. W. 598.
(40 ) Traverse . Every material allega-
tion of a pleading must ,for the purposes
of the action, be taken as true, unless
specificallytraversed (sec. 126). A ma-
terialallegationis one which is necessary
for the statement or support of a cause
of action or defense (sec. 127). A tra-
verse is a denial of facts alleged in an
adverse pleading. See sec, 113-7; and
each material fact must be denied sep-
arately; it will not do to group two or
more facts together and deny them .
Preston v . Roberts , 12 Bush 570 ; Mor-
gan v . Booth , 13 Bush 480 ; nor is a de-
nial in the conjunctive good . 81 Ky . 458,
5 R. 487 .
(41) An answer which merely denies
" each averment of the petition" is no
answer at all. Evans v. Evans , 93 Ky .
510 , 14 R. 628 , 20 S. W. 605 ; nor is de-
nial that defendant is indebted as al-
leged, or that account is just sufficient.
Thruston v . Oldham , 6 Bush 16.
(41a ) A plea of avoidance must im-
port a confession of the traversed alle-
gations. Shirley v. Renick , 151 Ky . 25,
151 S. W. 357 .
(41b ) A traverse of a negative is not
good pleading. The defendant should
plead the fact affirmativelyif he relies
upon it. Vicars v. Aetna Life Ins. Co.,
157 Ky . 1, 164 S. W. 106.
(41c ) In an action for malicious prose-
cution advice of counsel need not be spe-
cially pleaded ; it may be given in evi-
dence under a traverse. Emler v . Fox ,
172 Ky . 290, 189 S. W. 469.
(42 ) Traverse of record- puts in issue
affirmativematter in pleading the same
as written traverse . Ky . Union R. R.
v . Conner , 17 R. 426, 31 S. W. 467.
(43 ) Unlawful contract - when relied
on as defense must be specificallyset
out. Powell v . Flanary , 109 Ky . 342, 22
R. 908 , 59 S. W. 5 .

§ 96. (1) Counter -claim. "First,those
causes of action which arise out of the

upon a contract,judgmentor award. (See further,secs.97,372,377.

of the transaction set forth in the peti-

(sec.732-34) and may be presentedin a

quired that the counter-claim itselfshall

it is sufficient that it arises out of the

(2) Dismissalof actionwithout preju-
dice does not prevent prosecution of

SET -OFF .
(1) Administrator - distributee. Ad-
ministrator or heirs may plead as set-off
114

Title VII ] [§ 96Answer , Counter -Claim , Set -off, Cross-Petition.
against distributee'sinteresthis indebt-
edness to the estate . Brown v . Mat-
tingly, 91 Ky . 275, 12 R. 869, 15 S. W.
353 .
(2) Assignee against. A set-off to be
available against an assignee must have
existed before notice of the assignment .
A debt or demand can not be used as a
set-off before it becomes due , and unless
itisdue beforenoticeoftheassignment,it is not available as a set-off against
the assignee. A surety,until he pays the
money for his principal, has no demand
against him which can be pleaded as a
set-off, even if the principalis insolvent.
Walker v . McKay , 2 Met . 294 ; 1 Met .
112; and see Chenault v. Bush , 84 Ky .
528 , 8 R. 490, 2 S. W. 160; Henderson N.
Bk . v . Lagow ,3 R. 173 ; nor will a party
who has notice that his note has been
assigned , although he may not know to
whom , be allowed to buy up claims
against assignor and set them offagainst
note . Small v. Browder , 11 B. M. 213 ;
but the defendant may after suit is filed
buy valid claims against the plaintiffand
plead them as a set-off. Overby v. Wells ,
21 R. 1316, 54 S. W. 955.
(3) Assignment of note to be used as.
Defendant can plead as a set-off a note
of the plaintiffassigned to him under a
contract to use the note as a set -off, and
account to the assignor only for the
amount he can make in that way , and
reassignthe balanceremainingunpaid.
Graham v . Tilford, 1 Met . 112 Dorsey v .
Reese , 14 B. M. 157 ; McBrayer v . Dean ,
100 Ky . 398, 18 R. 847, 38 S. W. 508 ;
and see Overby v. Wells,21 R. 1316, 54
S. W. 955. But where a note is so as-
signed, to be used as a set-off,a court of
equity , where the rights of others than
thepartywho owes the noteintervene,would be reluctant to sanction such a
speculation by aiding the party making
the venture to defeat an acknowledged
equity. Such contracts are not to be
favored , but where the debtor alone is
affected, he will not be heard to com-
plain. Tinley v. Martin, 80 Ky . 463, 4
R. 327 .
(4) Commonwealth - actionby. In a
suit by the Commonwealth against a
citizen,the lattermay ,by way of set-off,
make such defense as will defeat recov-
ery,but can not get judgment over. Com .
v . Ο . & N. R. R. Co., 81 Ky . 572, 5 R.
650; 9 Bush 708 ; and see Sinking Fund
Comr . v . Northern Bank , 1 Met . 174 .
(5) The Commonwealth by its judg-
ment againstthe sheriffobtaineda lien
on the sheriff'sclaimagainsthisdeputy;
at if the deputy , as surety for the sher-
iff,has been compelledto pay money for
him before the lien of the Commonwealth
attached, the sum so paid is a validset-
off against the Commonwealth . Harlan
v . Lumsden , 1 Duv .86 .
(6) Co -surety who pays entire debt
may plead the amount he is entitled to
recover from his co-surety as a set-off
to an actionagainsthim by the general
assigneeof saidsuretyon a noteexe-
cuted by him to such surety, although
the debt did not mature nor was it paid
by him untilafterthe assignment. Che-
nault v.Bush ,84 Ky .528,8 R. 490,2 S.
W. 160 .
(7) Damages unliquidated. A demand
for unliquidated damages for breach of
warranty of the qualityof a commodity
for which the note sued on was executed
may be reliedon as a set-off against
the note when the vendor is insolvent or
a non-resident. Forbesv.Cooper,88 Ky .
285, 10 R. 865, 11 S. W. 24; Taylor v.
Stowell,4 Met .175; Abernathy v.Myer-
Bridges Co., 100 S. W. 862, 30 R. 1236;
and see Boyd v . Day , 3 Bush 617 ; Adams
v.Craycroft, 1 R. 330. But where there
is an adequate remedy at law , and no
facts,such as insolvencyor non-residence
are shown , unliquidateddamages , al-
though arisingupon a contract, can not
be pleaded as a set-off. Garner v. Jones ,
94 Ky . 135, 14 R. 792, 21 S. W. 647 ;
Shropshirev. Conrad, 2 Met . 143; Doy-
sherv.Adams , 16 R. 582,29 S.W. 348.
A cause of action in favor of a defend-
ant against plaintiff for unliquidated
damages can not be sued as a set-off in
the absence of non -residenceor insolvency
of the plaintiffor some other sufficient
equity which authorizes itsuse as a set-
off. Cross v. Snyder'sAdmx ., 164 Ky .
370,175 S. W. 641.
(7a ) Same - See equitable set-off,post.
(8) Decedent's estate- against. A claim
against a decedent pleaded as a set-off
must be verifiedand proved as claims are
requiredto be when sued on by original
action; affidavitin body of petitionis
not sufficient; no demand is necessary.
Warfield v. Gardner , 79 Ky . 583, 3 R.
423. Distributee can not plead amount
due him as a set-offin a suit by the ad-
ministrator, in an action pending in
county in which administrator did not
qualify. 3 Met . 322.
(9) In a suitby an administrator,the
defendant had a rightto plead,as a set-
off, a demand due him by a decedent
whose estateplaintiff'sintestatewrong-
fullyappropriated. McKenzie v. Pendle-
ton, 1 Bush 164.

digate, 11 B. M. 174, but in a suitby a

vent defendant from prosecutingset-off,

(17) Equitable set-off. A bank having
on depositmoney due a depositorwho
makes an assignment for the benefit of

ing that a surety on a note to a bank

posit in the bank against the note when
the principal on note is solvent. In an
actionby a foreigncorporationhaving
no property in this State , upon a con-
tract, a claim for unliquidated damages

999 .
(17a) Where in a suit by a non -resi-
dent plaintiffupon promissory notes de-
fendant pleaded a claim for unliquidated

solvent; owned ample real and personal

and that defendant could have had and

(18 ) Ex delicto . In an action on a
note , the defendant has a right to waive

off the value of property wrongfully
taken by the plaintiff, and recoverthe

implied promise to pay it. Eversole v .
Moore , 3 Bush 49 ; Haddix v. Wilson , 3
Bush 523; and see Boyd v . Day , 3 Bush
617; Adams v . Craycroft, 1 R. 330.
(19) Generally. Where a housekeeper

against the purchase -price a debt due
him by the seller. Mulliken v . Winter , 2
Duv . 256 .
(20 ) In an action against the debtor
of a bank brought by commissioners , in
whose hands the funds of the bank were
placed, the debtor can plead as a set-off

a claim due him from the bank at the

§ 98 [132,133] [Reply may contain- what.] A replymay contain-
1. [Traverse.] A traverse.
2. [Factsconstitutingestoppelor avoidance.] A statementof facts
which constitutean estoppelagainst,or avoidanceof,a set-off,counter-
claim or defense stated in the answer .
3. [Counter-claim.] A counter-claimagainsta set-off.
4 . [Cross-petition.] A cross-petition.

§ 98. (1) Answer - cured by . A de-
fect in the answer may be cured by an
averment in the reply. Mitchellv.Ash-
by , 78 Ky . 254 .
(2) Contributory neglect. Plea of is
admitted by failure to reply. White v .
L. & N. R. R. Co., 15 R. 49,22 S. W. 219 ;
L. & N. R. R. v. Mayfield, 18 R. 224, 35
S. W. 924; Gore v. Ill. CentralR. R., 17
R. 799 , 32 S. W. 754 ; Brooks v . L. & N.
R. R.,24 R. 1318, 71 S. W. 507. But de-
fendant may ,by failingto raisequestion
in proper time and manner , waive his
right to judgment because of failureto
filereply. L. & N. R. R. Co. v . Copas ,
95 Ky . 460 , 16 R. 14, 26 S. W. 179 ; L. &
N. R. Co. v . Paynter ,26 R. 761, 82 S. W.
412 ; Lou .Ry .Co. v. Hibbett , 139 Ky . 43,
129 S. W. 319 ; Shulte v. L. & N. R. Co.,
128 Ky . 633 ,108 S. W. 941.
(3) Denial in- sufficiencyof. Reply to
a set-off, denying generallythe correct-
ness of each and every item, is not a
sufficient traverse of the items in the set-
off. Whitaker v. Sandifer, 1 Duv . 261 ;
Thruston v . Oldham , 6 Bush 16; and see
note 13 .
(4) Denial of any knowledge or in-
formation as to whether or not the vari-
ous items mentioned in the account
pleaded as a set-off, and alleged to have
been sold and delivered to plaintiff, were
delivered, is not good ; there should be a
specificdenial. Wing v. Dugan , 8 Bush
583 .
(5 ) The petition alleged that the debt
was unpaid , with a statement as to how
it was created . The denial in the an-
swer that anythingwas due,and an alle-
gationof payment ,made an issue. Rob .
inson v .Williamson , 7 Bush 604 .
(6) In an actionagainsta vendor to
recover damages for failing to convey
land and deliver possession, the defend .
ant by counter-claim sought a specific
execution of the contract. The plaintiff
couldnot in his replydemand a rescis-
sion, the reply being restrictedto mat-
ters of defense. Spalding v . Alexander ,
6 Bush 160 .
(7 ) Infancy- ratification. The plain-
tiffin a reply may plead a ratificationof
the contract by the infant where infancy
is relied on as a defense , Stern v . Free-
man ,4 Met .309.
(8) Limitation.Iflimitationispleaded,
the plaintiffmay in a reply relyupon a
new promise made before the limitation
expired; but if he depends on a new
promise made afterlimitationhas run he
must set it up in an amended petition.
Gilmore v . Green , 14 Bush 772 .
(9 ) No consideration- reply is neces-
sary to plea of. Brown v. Ready , 14 R.
583, 20 S. W. 1036.

gence; an amendment set up additional

paragraph must be complete in itself;
the averments in one paragraph can not

ered. Cumberland Tel Co. v . Logsdon ,

before trial, form a materialissueconcerningeach cause of controversy:
and itisthe duty of the court,upon or without motion,to compel them to
do so; and,forthatpurpose,theymay be requiredtoreformtheirplead-
ingsor thepleadingof a partywho isin faultmay be strickenfrom the
case .

§ 115 [142] [Written in Englishlanguage and signed- exception.]
Pleadingsmust be in theEnglish language,and as conciseas possiblecon-
sistentlywith clearness; and, exceptingthosementionedin section705,
must be in writing,and be signedby thepartieswho filethem ,or by their

§ 114. Material issue. (1) The court
should compel the parties to form a ma-
terial issue and strike out all redundant
or irrelevant matter so that the real is-
sue willnot be confused. Hale v .Grogan ,
99 Ky . 170, 18 R. 46, 35 S. W. 282 ; Bra-
shear v.Letcher Co., 22 R. 1763,61 S. W.
285 .
(2) Burden of proof. In an action on a
note the defendant claimed that a cer-
tain sum of money had been paid upon
the note . The reply filedalleged that the
money was paid,but was paid on another
debt . The burden of proof was on the
defendant. Ditto v. Hopkins, 164 Ky .
412 , 175 S. W. 658 .
§ 115. (1) Signing pleadings. The an-
swer of the defendant was not signed at
the end of it,as it should have been ; the
signature of the attorney on the back
below the indorsementis not a signing
in the meaning of the Code ; but the court
having permitted it to be filed, over the
objection of plaintiff, should not have
disregarded it on the hearing because
not signed. Ashbrook v. Roberts ,82 Ky .
298 , 6 R. 317 ; Abernathy v. Meyer-
BridgesCo.,99 S.W. 942, 30 R. 844; see
sec . 732-7 .
(2 ) Failure to properly sign a pleading
must be taken advantage of by rule, or
the objection will be waived . Voorheis
v .Eiting, 15 R. 161, 22 S. W. 80.
(3 ) The fact that the petitionwas not
signed at the time the term began was
no ground for a continuance. It would
be grounds for reasonable opportunity to
be given the defendant to tender an an-
swer . Baird v. Prewitt , 158 Ky .793, 166
S. W. 771 .
(4) Presumption . A judgment sus-
taining the defendant's demurrer to the
plaintiff'spetition and dismissing it is
not void where the record shows that the
defendant had filed no demurrer to the
petition, as it must be assumed that the
defendant made the objection orally, or
that the court itselfraised the objection.
Ford Lumber & Mfg .Co. v.Burt & Brabb
Lumber Co., 157 Ky .706 , 163 S. W. 1105 .
§ 116. (1) Verification. Pleadingsin
actions for injuriesto person or character
-as for libel- must be verified. Berea
College v . Powell , 25 R. 1220, 77 S. W.
382; and see as to waiver of objection
