§ 117] [Title VIIGeneral Rules of Pleading .
§ 117 [142,143] [Verification- by whom tobe made .] Pleadings,the
verificationofwhich isrequiredby thisCode,must be verifiedas follows:
1. [Pleadingsfor Commonwealth.] Pleadingsof theCommonwealth
must be verifiedby theattorneywho bringstheaction,or by theofficeror
agent who isauthorizedby law to have itbrought.
2. [County or corporation.] That of a county, or of a municipalor
privatecorporation, must be verifiedby itschiefofficeror agentupon
whom a summons inthe actionislawfullyserved,or might be lawfully
servedifitwere a defendant; or ifithave no such officeror agent residing
inthecountyinwhichtheactionisbrought,or ispending,itmay be veri-
fiedby itsattorney.
3. [Party must verify- exceptions.] That of any otherpartymust
be verifiedby him ,with theseexceptions:
a. If he be absentfrom thecounty,or mentallyincapableof takingan
oath,or physicallyunabletoattendbeforean officer,itmay be verifiedby
his agent or attorney.
b. Ifitbe a jointpleadingof severalparties,who areunitedininterest,
itmay be verifiedby eitherof them .
c. If the party be under disability, exceptingcoverture,or infancy and
coverturecombined,itmay be verifiedby thepersonwho isauthorizedto
file it.
4. [Partymay be requiredtoverifyinperson.] But,on motionof a
partywho fileshisaffidavit,statinghisbeliefthatan adverseparty,whose
pleadinghas been verifiedby a person other than himself, knows that a
statementthereof,inthe affidavitmentioned,isuntrue,and thatthe motion
isnot made fordelay,the court,ifsuch statementbe material,shallrequire
such adversepartyto verifythe pleading; and ,ifhe failto do so,within a
reasonabletime, shalltreatit, with regard to him , as if ithad not been
filed .
5. [Effect of verification.] Verificationof a pleadingdoes not make
other or greaterproof necessaryon the side of the adverse party than
the evidence. Worley v. Tuggle , 4 Bush
168; Enders v. Williams , 1 Met . 346 ; 17
Β .Μ . 642 ; 16 В. М. 174.
(2) Suitby county. County judgeis
proper person to verifypetitiond,but if
he refuse, county attorney may verify,
and if he refuse, some person authorized
to order institutionof action may verify.
Estill Co. v. Richmond R. R. Co., 91 Ky .
349 , 12 R. 906 , 15 S. W. 862 ; Combs v .
Breathitt Co., 18 R. 809 , 38 S. W. 138.
(3 ) Several parties united in interestParties
must each verify pleading when affidavit
mentioned in subsec. 4 is filed, and the
pleading will be treated as a nullity as
to those refusingto verify; but failure
of one to verify does not authorize court
to treat pleading as a nullity as to those
who do . Harrison v . Lebanon Water
Works , 91 Ky . 255, 12 R. 822, 15 S. W.
522 .
(4 ) Corporation. When a pleading of
a corporation is verifiedby an agent or
attorney, the affidavitshould state that
the officerrequired to verify it by sub-
sec. 2 is absent from the county . North-
ern Ice Co. v . Orr . 102 Ky . 586 , 19 R.
163, 44 S. W. 216 ; Dengler v. Krell-
French Co., 119 S. W. 757 ; First Nat .

waiver.] A party may ,by an answer or otherproper pleading,make any

§ 118. (1) Defect of parties - how
reached. If defect of parties appear in
petition it should be taken advantage of
by specialdemurrer , as provided in sec.
92-4; if defect does not appear , it must
be taken advantage of (in the Circuit
Court , New Bell Jellico Co. v . Braznell's
Admr ., 149 Ky . 418, 149 S. W. 888) by
answer , or the error is waived . McAlis-
ter v. Saving Bank , 80 Ky . 684, 4 R.
682 ; Albro v. Lawson , 17 B. M. 642 ; 10
Bush 763 ; 3 Bush 200 ; 14 B. M. 246 ; 4
Met . 385 ; Rudd v.Deposit Bank , 105 Ку .
443 ; 20 R. 1497, 49 S. W. 207 ; Puckett
v. Jameson , Sheriff, 157 Ky . 172, 162 S.
W. 801. A voluntaryunincorporatedas-
sociation can not be sued in the name
of the corporation. The point is waived
unlessraisedby specialdemurreror by
answer where the facts do not appear
upon the face of the petition. United
M. Workers of A. v .Cromer ,159 Ky .605,
167 S. W. 891 .
(2) Garnisheemay show. A garnishee
may show by affidavitthat there is a de-
fect of parties. Forepaugh v . Appold ,
17 Β . Μ . 625 .
(3) Jurisdiction- objectionmade by
answer - that is also a plea to the merits
will not enter appearance . Louisville
Home Tel. Co. v . Beeler, 101 S. W. 397,
31 R. 19, 125 Ky . 366. The general ap-
pearance of the defendant in a bastardy
proceedings, before making objection to
the jurisdictionof the court, does not
waive such objection. Com . v . Adkins ,
169 Ky . 487, 184 S. W. 372.
(4) Jurisdiction- objection to must be
pointed out in pleading if it does not
appear on the face of the petition that
court has not jurisdiction. Richardson
v . L. & N. R. Co., 112 S. W. 582, 129 Ky .
449 ; and unless made in proper time and
manner will be waived . Gillen v . I. C.
R. Co., 125 S. W. 747, 137 Ky . 375 ; L. &
N. R. R. v. Mitchell, 162 Ky . 253, 172 S.
W. 527; Fentzka's Admr . v. Warwick
Const . Co., 162 Ky . 580, 172 S. W. 1060.
A petition did not show whether the
county in which the action was brought ,
was or was not the proper county in
which to bring it. The question of jur-
isdictioncould not be raised by special
demurrer . L. & N. R. R. Co. v . Stewart ,
163 Ky . 164, 173 S. W. 757.
(5 ) Jurisdiction- appearance . When
the court has no jurisdictionof the sub-
ject-matterconsentcannotgiveit. Fid-
ler v . Hall , 2 Met . 461 Barton v . Barton ,
80 Ky . 212, 3 R. 746 ; 18 В. М. 259.
(6 ) When the court has jurisdictionof
the subject-matter , the objection for
want ofjurisdictionover the personmay
be waived by consent. Hughes v . Har-
desty, 12 Bush 364 ; Barton v. Barton , 80
Ky . 212 , 3 R. 746. Defense to the merits
of an action is waiver of the right to ob-
ject for want of jurisdictionover the
person of defendant . Baker v . L. & Ν .
R. R. Co., 4 Bush 619 ; and so is filinga
general demurrer to the petition. Me-

§ 119] [Title VIIGeneral Rules of Pleading .
§ 119 [144] [Facts that need not be stated- private statute.]
1. Neither the evidencereliedon by a party,nor presumptions of law,nor
factsof which judicialnoticeistaken,exceptingprivatestatutes,shallbe
stated in a pleading .
2. [Private statute, how pleaded.] In pleading a privatestatute, it
shallbe sufficientto refertoitby statingitstitleand theday on which it
became a law .
Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr . (3d Ed .), secs. 205, 210 , 211, 427, 551d ,
694 ,719, 741, 878, 872, 875, 876 ; Forms Nos . 52, 60, 135.
Dowell v . C., O. & S. W. R. R., 90 Ky .
346 , 12 R. 331 , 14 S. W. 338 .
(7) See further as to what steps a de-
fendant may take without enteringhis
appearance , and when his appearance is
entered,note 9,sec.39 .
(8) Non -residents. The provisionthat
a defect of parties appearing in petition
is waived by failureto demur applies to
actions against persons constructively
summoned . Gill v. Johnson , 1 Met . 649.
(9) Practice. Where the fact that
plaintiffhas not legal capacity to sue is
reliedupon in the answer and proved on
the trial, the plaintiffcan not have
judgment even if defendant failto insist
upon the defect by motion . Petty v .
Malier , 14 B. М. 246.
(10) When the answer disclosesa de-
fect of parties, the action should not be
dismissed or abated , but an order entered
requiring the additional parties to be
made on pain of dismissal without preju-
dice, and the action postponed until nec-
essary parties are made . Carpenter v.
Miles , 17 B. M. 598 ; 3 Met . 133.
(11) Pendency of another action. An
action will be abated by reason of the
pendency of another ; but they must both
be prosecuted for the same cause of aс-
tion . L. & N. R. R. Co. v . Ohio Val . Tie
Co., 161 Ky . 212, 170 S. W. 633.
§ 119. (1) Judicialnotice. The courts
take judicialnotice of all acts and reso-
lutions of the General Assembly . Ken-
tucky Statutes, secs. 1624, 1625. As to
what facts judicial notice is taken of,
see Newman's Pleading and Practice;
and as topresumptionsoflaw,seeIbid.;
Gregory v. McFarland , 1 Duv . 50 ; Wil-
liams v.Gordon , 11 Bush 693.
(2) It is unnecessary to prove facts of
common knowledge or the meaning of
words in the vernacular language . Ped-
igov.Com .,24 R. 1029,70 S. W. 659.
(3) Private statutes. As to what are
private statutes in the meaning of this
section, see City of Cov . v. Voskotter , 80
Ky . 219 , 3 R. 749 ; City of Cov . v . Hoad-
ley, 83 Ky . 444, 7 R. 487 ; Board of Ed .
v. L., H. & St. L. R. R., 110 Ky . 932, 23
R. 376,62 S. W. 1125. The Court ofAp-
peals will take judicialnotice of private
acts when they have been relied on in
thelowercourt,althoughthatfactis
not formally stated in the billof exceр-
tions. Lackey v. R. & L. T. P. Co., 17
B. M. 43 ; Bixler v .Parker , 3 Bush 166.
(4 ) In pleading a private statute it is
necessary to state its titleand the day
on which it became a law . Zable v . Lou .
Baptist Home ,92 Ky .89 , 13 R. 385, 17 S.
W. 212; Nicholsv. Bardwell Lodge, 105
Ky . 168, 20 R. 1236, 48 S. W. 426 ; Rudd
v.Deposit Bank , 105 Ky . 443,20 R. 1497,
49 S. W. 207; Gaertner v . Lou . Stone Co.,
114 Ky .160,24 R. 940,70 S. W. 293.
(5 ) City ordinances that court is re-
quired by statute to take judicialnotice
of, may be pleaded the same as private
statutes . Gaertner v . Lou . Stone Co. , 114
Ky . 160, 24 R. 940, 70 S. W. 293 ; Tenn .
Brick Co. v . Barker , 22 R. 1069 , 60 S. W.
690 ; Cabell v . City of Henderson , 28 R.
89 , 88 S. W. 1095 .
(6) Charter of town is not a private
statute that need be pleaded as required
by this section- it is a public act. Town
v . Weighans , 19 R. 1979, 44 S. W. 985.
(7) A stipulationin a pleadingthat a
private statute has not been repealed
will be construed to mean that it has
not been repealed by a specialact. City
of Lou . v . Vreeland , 131 S. W. 195, 140
Ky . 400.
(8 ) A petition for the removal of an
action which did not state the title of a
private statute but only the pleader's
conclusion as to its contents was defec-
tive. IllinoisCent . R. Co. v . Shegog , 103
S. W. 323,31 R. 691, 126 Ky .252 .
(9 ) Objection that a private statute is
not well pleaded must be made by special
demurrer . L. & N. R. Co. v . Robbins , 111
S. W. 283; Rudd v. Deposit Bank , 105
Ky .443,49 S. W. 207,20 R. 1495.

§ 120. (1) Exhibit filedwhich contra-
diets averments of pleading must con-
trol. Ky .Mut .Co.v.Logan,90 Ky .364,
12 R. 327, 14 S. W. 337 ; Boyd v. Bethel,

R. 426 , 75 S. W. 283 ; Kalfus v . Davie's

cases cited in note 18 , sec . 90. An ex-
hibit which contradicted the pleading
with which it was filedmade that plead-
ing bad on demurrer . Durham v. Elliott,

filedunder thissectionwillnot supply
an omitted essentialaverment ,but itwill
cure and aid a defective allegation. A

be aidedby avermentsin theproofproo of
loss attached as an exhibit. Henry Clay
Fire Ins.Co. v. Barkley , 160 Ky . 153, 169
S. W. 747 ; and see Black v . O'Hara , 175
Ky .623, 194 S. W. 811. Order by exhibit,
Fiscal Court of Franklin Co. v . Ky . Pub-
licService,Co., 181 Ky .245 ,204 S. W. 77.
(2 ) Pleading . A petition founded on a
writing or bond ought to statethe sub-
stance of it or at least so much of it as
is material to the action, and also refer
to it as a part of the petition. or state
why it is not filed. Collinsv .Blackburn ,

out setting out its terms is bad on de-

Nat . Bank , 12 Bush 287 ; but this rule
will not be strictlyenforced by the Court
of Appeals after judgment and when
partyhas failedto raisequestionby de-murrer . Combs v . Breathitt Co., 18 R.
809 , 38 S. W. 138 .
(3) A writingupon which an actionis
founded must be filed with and referred
to in the petition, and so much of it set

§ 121 ] [Title VIIGeneral Rules of Pleading .
§ 121 [147] [Irrelevantor redundantmatter strickenout.] Irrele-
vantor redundantmatterina pleadingshallbe strickenout,upon or with-
outmotion,atthecostof thepartywhose pleadingcontainsit.
Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr . (3d Ed .), secs. 383a , 383b , 384, 550d ,
550g ,872, 880.
forthas willshow that the plaintiffby
reason of alleged acts or omissions on his
part,and on the part of the defendant , is
entitledto relief. Hill v. Barrett, 14 В.
M. 83 ; and see cases cited in note 18,
sec . 90 .
(4) Record - writing filed part of.
Notes sued on and filedwith the petition
constitute part of the record. Dodd v .
King ,1 Met .430;Totten v.Cooke,2 Met .
275; Harmon v.Wilson,1 Duv .322; Day
Lumber Co. v. Mack , 24 R. 640, 69 S. W.
712; and see Gilbertv. Bamberger, 19
R. 1833, 44 S. W. 421, pointing out dif-
ference between this section and sec . 128 .
(5 ) An account not filed with and as
part of thepetition,but simplylodgedin
the papers before appearance or answer ,
is not part of the record, and need not
be noticed in the answer . Webb v . Jeff-
ries, 2 Bush 221 ; and see Dodd v . King ,
1 Met . 430 .
(6 ) In an action against a common
carrierto recoverthe value of baggagelost, a schedulefiledwith the petition
and made part thereof is a part of the
case, and the jury had a right to regard
the statements of fact it contained as
admissions upon the part of the plaintiff.
L., C. & L. R. R. Co. v. Mahan , 8 Bush
184 .
(7) Rule to file- practice. If a writ-
ing which is the evidence of the indebt-
edness or liabilityconstitutingthe cause
of action be not filed, the adverse party
may ask a rule to have it filed,and upon
failure to fileit, or to offer a sufficient
reason for not so doing .may have action
dismissed ; but if he fail to take steps
requiringit to be filed,and judgment is
rendered on the pleading, it can not be
reversed because the writing was not
filed. Preston v . Roberts . 12 Bush 570 .
An objection that a paper required by
Code , sec. 120 , is not filed can not be
taken by pleading nor is it availing un-
less taken before judgment . A motion
for a rule to fileis the proper procedure.
Thompson v. Sunrise Coal Co.'sTrustee ,
181 Ky . 158 , 204 S. W. 89. Failure to file
a writing under this sectionis no ground
for demurrer . Com . v . Hume . 155 Ky .
475 , 159 S. W. 966. After judgment it
is too late to raise the question as to
whether a certain exhibit should have
been filed. Hughes v. Grogan , 159 Ky .
411, 167 S. W. 381.
(8 ) A rule to filetitlepapers,or papers
of any kind where they are not referred
to in the pleadings, isunauthorized, ex-
cept upon sufficientgrounds made out by
affidavit, showing that the filingof the
papers is necessary to the substantial
ends of justice. Ruggles v.Moore ,18 B.
M. 821; and see Marion Nat . Bank v.
Abell,88 Ky .428,10 R. 980,11 S.W. 300;
for a fullstatement of practice regarding
production of books and documents .
(9) Writing - to be filed- waiver . Al-
though a writing upon which action is
founded is not filed, the adverse party
can not complain of it after judgment .
McGhee v. Sutherland , 84 Ky . 198, 8 R.
87, 1 S. W. 5 ; 12 Bush 570 ; especiallyif

§ 125 [152] [Land - pleadingsin actionfor- burden of proof- costs.]
1. [Petitionto describe.] A petitionfor the recoveryof land,or for its
subjectiontoa demand of theplaintiff,must describeitso thatitmay be
identified.
2. [Answer- what itmay contain- burden of proof- costs.] In an
action for the recovery of land, the answer of the defendant must state

answermust sodescribesuchpartthatitmay be identified. The making
of such statement,or of such statementand description,shallnot,of itself,
throw on thedefendant theburthen of proving hisrightto thelandclaimed
by him ; but, ifthe plaintiffrecover any part thereof,the defendant shall
pay the costsof the action.

(3) Pleading - evidence. When the
only answer is a denial of the speaking
of the words , nothing is admissible in

fense on the ground that they were

versed by the answer , and a counter-

§ 129 [156] [Variancewhen material- amendment.] No variancebe-
tween pleadingsand proof ismaterial,which does not mislead a party, to
hisprejudice,in maintaininghisactionor defenseupon the merits. A
§ 129. (1) Material variance. Where
petitionallegedthat certainproperty was
purchased from defendant and proof
showed that it was a purchase of double
the amount from defendant and another
the variance is fatal. Gray v . Garrison ,
2 R. 218 ; and see further, Bannister v .
Weatherford , 7 B. M. 271 ; Howard v .
Chiles , 8 B. М. 377 .
(2 ) Judgment can not be rendered
against a defendant as an individual
when the petitionshows that he executed
the obligation sued on in his fiducialca-
pacity as administrator. Lusk v . Ander-
son , 1 Met . 426 .
(3) Plaintiffinstitutedsuit upon a
note executed by the defendant to an-
other person, and allegedthat the note
had been executed to himself ; it had not
been assignedto plaintiff. Held that the
variance was fatal, and plaintiffcould
not recover. Dodd v.King ,1 Met .430.
(4) In an action upon an express con-
tract no recovery can be had on proof of
an implied contract in the absence of an
amendment conformingthe pleadingsto
the proof . Newton v . Field, 98 Ky . 186,
17 R. 769 ,32 S. W. 623 ; and see further,
Bull v .McCrea , 8 B. M. 422 .
(5 ) In an action for injuriessustained
by being thrown against a mail crane,
the plaintiffallegedthat he was a work-
man on the train . In fact he was not a
workman on the train, but a section
hand . Upon the entire record, however ,
this error was not prejudicial nor was
there a fatal variance between the plead-
ing and the proof. C. & O. Ry . Co. v.
Jesse, 159 Ky . 450, 167 S. W. 407.
(6 ) In a certainaction for personal in-
juries there was no such variance be-
tween the pleading and proof as to ren-
der the variance fatal . L. & N. R. R. Co.
v . Spatig , 158 Ky .263, 164 S. W. 811.
(7) Negligence. The allegationofwill-allegatiful negligence necessarilyincludes all in-
feriorgrades , and , upon failingto estab-
lishhis right to punitive damages ,plain-
tiffmay recover compensatory damages ,
if the defendant is a railroad company
and the person injuredwas not at the
time an employe . L., C. & L. R. R. Co.
v . Case , 9 Bush 728 ; Claxton v . L. & B.
S. R. R. Co.,13 Bush 636. And a general
allegationthatinjurywas causedby neg-
ligence and carelessnessof defendant is
sufficient. Chiles v . Drake , 2 Met . 146 .
(8) Negligence- where plaintiffspeci-
fiesthe characterof negligencehe relies
on he can not recover by showing a dif-
ferent state of negligence. McCain v . L.
& N. R. R. Co., 13 R. 809, 18 S. W. 537 ;
Thomas v . L. & N. R. R., 18 R. 164, 35
S. W. 910 ; nor can he prove a different
stateof negligencefrom that setout in
petition. Greer v. L. & N. R. R. Co., 94
Ky . 169,14 R. 876,21 S. W. 649.

(9) Practice. The proper mode to take
advantage of a variancebetween allega-

(11) Variance - not material. If the

in maintaining his action, and does not

ance between the contract sued on and

ices alleged to have been performed in

(15) A bill of exchange payable in

200 .

179 S. W. 835 .

155 ,181 S. W. 970 .
(20) Where negligenceis alleged in

ing to board the car, was held immate-
rial. Ky . Trac. & Terminal Co.v.Waits ,
167 Ky .236, 180 S. W. 356.
(21 ) The rules as to variance laid down
by CivilCode, secs. 129 and 131,are ap-
plicable in a prosecution for false swear-

§ 132 [159] [Amendment beforeanswer- notice.] Theplaintiffmay ,

§ 1001. The plaintiffmay , without leave, amend his petitionat any time
before answer is filed. (Ky .Stat., sec.1001.)
Cross reference: See Newman's Pl .and Pr . (3d Ed .),sec.576.
§ 133 [160] [Demurrer overruled, party may plead.] Upon a de-
murrer beingoverruled,thepartydemurringmay filean answer,replyог

§ 132. (1) Amendment -notice of
filing. One day's notice is only required
where the plaintiffamends his petition
without leave, and less than five days
before term . When the amendment is
made by leave of court, and in presence
of defendant , no notice is necessary.
Hunt v. Semonin ,79 Ky . 270 , 2 R. 334 ;
Bryant v. Cooney , 19 R. 423, 40 S. W.
918 .
(2) New cause of action- summons
necessary. Summons is necessary on an
amended petition which states a new or
distinct cause of action . Cecil v . Sow-
ards, 10 Bush 96 ; Cope v. Slayden ,24 R.
1734, 72 S. W. 284 ; although it is filed
after summons has been executed on
petition,and before answer . Rutledge v.
Vanmeter ,8 Bush 354 ; and see note 1 to
sec . 40 .
(3) If amendment does not present a
new or distinctcause of action, summons
is not necessary. Joyes v. Hamilton , 10
Bush 544; L.,C. & L. R. R. Co. v. Case,
9 Bush 728 ; McKay v . Mayes , 16 R. 862,
29 S. W. 327 ; Adams Oil Co. v.Christmas ,
101 Ky . 564, 19 R. 760, 41 S. W. 545.
(4) Where the originalpetition sought
to make defendant liable as indorser of a
bill,and an amended petitionchargedhim
with the conversion of the bill, the
amendment set up a new cause of action.
So,ifA shouldinstitutean actionagainst
B upon a note alleged to have been exe-
cuted for goods sold and delivered, and
it appeared B was not liableon the note ,
ifA shouldfilean amended petitionupon
an account for the same goods , it would
present a new cause of action. Hyatt v .
Bank ofKy .,8 Bush 193.
(5) In an action to sellland owned by
infants summons on petition was prop-
erlyexecuted,afterwardan amended pe-
titionwas filedasking the sale of town
lotsowned by them also. Summons on
amendedpetitionwas necessary. How-
ard v. Singleton, 94 Ky . 336, 15 R. 309,
22 S. W. 337 .
(6) Practice. The plaintiffhas a right
to filean amended petition before an-
swer filed, and the court has no judicial
discretionto rejectit. Champion v .Rob-
ertson, 4 Bush 17 ; L. & N. R. R. v. Hall,
115 Ky . 567, 24 R. 2487 , 74 S. W. 280 ;
but after answer is filed leave of court
must be obtained to filean amended pe-
tition. Mount v . L. & N. R. R.,2 R. 221 ;
Petry v . Petry , 142 Ky . 564, 134 S. W.
922 ; and see further sec. 134 and notes
thereto .
(7 ) Construction of section- this sec-
tion applies when no answer has been
filedand no demurrer sustained, sec. 94
applieswhen a demurrer has been sus-
tained. Quinn v . Cincinnati R. Co., 97
S. W. 379 , 30 R. 15 .
§ 133. Answer after demurrer over-
ruled. A defendant was permitted to file
a demurrer after the time allowed by
law . It was overruled. He then tendered
an answer which set up a defense. It
was error to overrule the motion to file
the answer. Chicago Bonding & Surety
Co. v . Johnson , 180 Ky . 589, 203 S. W.
317 .
§ 134. (1) Amendments - departure.
In an action by a ward against her
guardian, to recoveron an assignment
of a note, an amended petitionalleging
that the guardian had failed to account

Title VII ] [§ 134Mistakes in Pleadings- Amendments .
§ 134 [161] [Amendments generally- courtmay require- trivialer-
ror.] The courtmay ,atany time,infurtheranceof justice,and on such
terms as may be proper,causeor permita pleadingor proceedingto be
amended,by addingor strikingoutthename of a party;or,by correcting
a mistakeinthe name of a party,or a mistakein any otherrespect;or
by insertingotherallegationsmaterialtothecase; or, iftheamendment
do not changesubstantiallytheclaimor defense,by conformingtheplead-
ingor proceedingto the factsproved. And ,ifa proceedingtakenby a
party failto conform inany respectto the provisionsof thisCode, the
courtmay permitan amendment of suchproceeding,so astomake itcon-
formoney in his hands was a departure,
and should not have been allowed ; but ,
if filed, plaintiffshould have been re-
quired to elect which action she would
prosecute. Humphrey v.Hughes ,79 Ky .
487 , 3 R. 273 .
(2 ) An amended petitionseeking to re-
cover on claim originallysued on, but
setting up a different evidence of the
claim , is not a departure . Adams Oil
Co. v. Christmas, 101 Ky .564, 19 R. 760,
41 S. W. 545 .
(2a ) In an action for recovery for
servicesalleged to have been rendered to
a decedent , an amended petition setting
up a promise by decdent to devise prop-
erty to plaintiffas compensation was
properly allowed to be filed. Benge's
Admr . v. Fouts ; Same v . Creech, 163
Ky .796 , 174 S. W. 510.
(2b ) A certainamended petition in an
action for damages which specifieditems
of damage was no departure , nor was it
ground for continuance in the absence of
an affirmativeshowing that by reason of
same the opposite party could not con-
tinue with the trial in consequence of
the amendment . Phoenix -Jellico Coal
Co. v.Grant , 159 Ky . 95, 166 S. W. 812.
(2c) In an action by the payee of a
note aftera plea of non est factum and
alteration,the plaintiffmay amend and
seek a recovery upon the indebtedness for
which the note was given. Ramsey v.
Utica DepositBank , 156 Ky . 263, 160 S.
W. 943 .
(3) Appeal from inferior to circuit
court . On an appeal to the circuitcourt
the defendant has a right to make all
the defenses he has , and the court may
allow the defense to be changed or new
defenses to be made , as if the case had
been originallybrought in that court.
Willis v.McNeal , 8 R. 411; and see fur-
ther , notes to 726 .
(4) The plaintiffin justice'scourtfiled
an affidavitshowing defendant's indebt-
ednessto him in the sum of $59,and ob-
tained an attachment ; no petition was
filed. On an appeal to the circuitcourt,
the plaintiff was allowed to file an
amended petitionsettingup hiscauseof
action. Puff v .Hutcher , 78 Ky . 146 ; and
see further, notes to 726, Sec. 705 re-
quires pleadings, when amount is over
$50, to be in writing.
(5 ) Attachments . As to amendment
of petition, or affidavitfor, see sec. 268
and notes thereto .
(6) Bastardy warrant may be amended .
Com . v . Cantrell,20 R. 24, 45 S. W. 72.
(7 ) Contested election- amendment of
notice. Wilson v. Hines , 99 Ky . 221, 18
R. 233, 35 S. W. 627.
(7a ) In the trial of a contested elec-
tion an amendment to the petition set-
ting out additionalnames of illegal
voterswas rightlyrejected. The power
to allow amendments applies to such
amendments as undertake to perfect and
make more definitethe grounds set out
in the original petition, and an amend-
ment should not be allowed to be filed
which brings in the names of other
voters or charges additional irregulari-
ties. Clark v . Robinson , 159 Ky . 25, 166
S. W. 801 .
(8) Definite and certain- court may
requirepleading to be made - but a party
will not be obliged to furnish in detail
facts already known to his adversary or
that are equally accessibleto both par-
ties as the number of a train or the
names of the train men . Bogard v. I. C.
R. R., 116 Ky . 429, 25 R. 624, 76 S. W.
170; Howard v. W. U. Tel.Co.,25 R. 828,
76 S. W. 387 .
(8a) The remedy for indefinitenessis
by motion to make more specific. Daniel
v .Daniel , 166 Ky . 182, 179 S. W. 5.

a corporation, the petitionmay be cor-
rected by amendment which will relate
back to and become a part of the original
petition, where servicewas had on the
corporation'sduly constituted agent.
ImperialJellicoCoal Co.v.Neff, 166 Ky .
722 , 179 S. W. 829 .
(62 ) In view of this section and secs.
338, 756, testimony by plaintiff, in an
actionfor personalinjuries,that he had
fallen off the car, was held not to affect
substantialrights, where the issuewas
whether he was caused to fallby a vio-
lentand unusual jerk of the car,due to
the negligence in its operation. Roberts
v. LouisvilleRy .Co.,168 Ky .230, 181 S.
W. 1131 .
(63 ) Under this section and sec. 756 ,
error in an instruction in an action for
damages for breach of contract in sale
of timber with privilegeof millsite, in
not limiting the findings to damages
shown by the evidence,was not reversibleerror, where the jury acted on that
theory and the verdict was sustained by
the evidence. Baker v. Morris, 168 Ky .
168. 181 S. W. 943 .
(64 ) In view of this section and sec.
756 ,a judgment for an employe in an ac-
tion under federal employers' liability
act will not be reversed because of im
proper instructionon contributory negli-
gence, in the absenceof any evidencere-
lating thereto. Chesapeake & O. Ry . Co.
v.Cooper,168 Ky .137,181 S. W. 933.
(65) Where the petition, in an action
for personalinjuries, allegesthat plain-
tiff suffered internal injuries, and de-
fendant did not move to make the peti-
tion more definite, the error was waived .
Cassidyv.Berkovitz, 169 Ky . 785,185 S.
W. 129 .
(66) Under this section and secs. 338,
756 ,error, in order to be reversible, must
affectthe substantialrightsof the ap-
pellant. Watson Contract Co. v . Trumbo ,
170 Ky . 95, 185 S. W. 518.
(67) A judgment will not be reversed
because of error in the form of one in-
struction if the instructions taken to-
getherare simpleand easilyunderstood.
C., N. O. & T. P. Ry . Co. v . Troxell, 150
Ky . 185, 150 S. W. 41.
(68) A judgment was renderedupon a
lost note ; the bond required by sec. 7 of
the Code was givenbeforejudgment. The
judgment was affirmedalthoughthebond
should have been given before the bring-
ing of the action. Kimbrough v. Lex.
City Nat .Bk .,150 Ky .336, 150 S. W. 325.
(69) A guardian ad litem for three in-
fants fileda report in which he omitted
their names , but by mistake inserted the
names of three other infant defendants ;
afterwards he was appointed for the last
named three and fileda report setting out
their names . The court declined to set
aside a sale by reason of this omission.
Thomson's Gdn .v. Thomson , 151 Ky .296,
151 S. W. 658 .
(70 ) Where negligence is alleged in
general terms ,by a petitioncharging that
plaintiffwas struck by defendant's car,
the variance caused by evidence that
plaintiffwas not struck by the car but
slippedandfellunderitwhileattemptingto board the car , was held immaterial .
Ky . Trac. & Terminal Co. v.Waits, 167
Ky . 236, 180 S. W. 356.
§ 135. (1) Supplemental pleadings.
Where a mortgagee institutessuit to re-
cover possession of mortgaged property
before forfeiture , and the forfeiture oc-
curs pending the action,he may by sup-
plementalpetitionsetup the forfeiture.
This section applies to ordinary as well
as equitableactions. Brookover v.Hurst ,
1 Met . 665 .

§ 136-137] [Title VIIMistakes in Pleadings- Amendments .
§ 136 [163] [Continuanceonamendment.] Ifa partyamend a plead-
ingor proceeding,and the courtshallbe satisfied,by affidavitor other-
wise, thatthe adversepartycannotbe ready for trialin consequence
thereof,a continuancemay be granted to some day in the same term or to
anothertermof thecourt. (Costsof continuancetobe paidby partyfiling
amendment ,Ky .Stat., sec.887.)
Cross reference: See Newman's Pl . and Pr . (3d Ed .),sec. 646 ; Forms . Nos .470, 471.
§ 137 [164] [Verificationof petitionbefore trialsufficient.] A peti-
tionfiledwithout verificationshallnot be dismissedfor thatreason,ifthe
verificationbe made on or before the callingof the actionfor trial. (See
furtheras toverification,secs.116,117.)
Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr . (3d Ed .), sec. 526j.
(2 ) The facts which this sectionallows
to be presentedin the supplementalpeti-
tion must be material to the case as
presentedin the originalpetition; they
must be such as serve to explain or per-
fectthe causeofactionoriginallystated.
Taylor v. Moran ,4 Met . 127.
(3) The plaintiffin an actionfor slan-
der can not, by an amended petition, set
up slanderouswords spoken pending the
actionas a distinctground of recovery
or to enhance the damages ; but may set
them up to show the intentwith which
thewords chargedin the originalpeti-tionwere spoken. 4 Met . 127.
(4) A plaintiffwho holds lien notes
due and not due may set up all of them
in his petition, and, by suggestionof
record, have judgment for notes not due
as they mature , but this rule only ap-
pliesto liennotes, and a supplemental
pleading is requiredas to other notes.
Dant v.Head , 90 Ky . 255, 12 R. 153, 13
S. W. 1073 ; and see Wheeler v .Bleakley ,
18R. 254,35 S.W. 1031; Moshellv.Reed,
97 S. W. 372, 30 R. 10; Rittenhouse v .
Swango , 128 S. W. 299; and secs. 692,
694 and notes thereto .
(5) In an actionupon threeliennotes
judgment was renderedorderingsaleof
land to satisfytwo of them that were
due; afterward the thirdnote felldue,
and the former judgment was set aside
and judgment rendereddirectingsaleto
pay all the notes. Held that the court
had power to set asidefirstjudgment
afterthe term,and itsactionwas proper.
Carr v. Watkins ,10 R. 342,9 S. W. 218.
See further as to liennotes secs.692,694
and notes thereto .
(6) Leave of court must be obtained
to filea supplementalpleading. Petry v.
Petry,142 Ky .564,134 S.W. 922.
(7 ) In a proceeding for the entry in
the county court of an order calling a
localoptionelectiona supplementalpeti-
tion adding new names may be filedbe-
fore the entry of such order. Horton v .
Botts , 158 Ky . 11, 164 S. W. 352.
(8 ) In view of this section and secs.
335,756 ,an instructionallowing recovery
for ordinary negligence is harmless , al-
though the petitionavers and the evi-
dence shows that the negligence was
gross. Chesapeake & O. Ry . Co. v . Sham-
blen, 166 Ky . 789, 179 S. W. 837.
(9) Under this section, plaintiffin an
action on a vendor's lien notes some of
which were not due when the action was
instituted,isentitledtojudgment on the
notes which mature after the petition
was filed,without further process. Stone
v .Daniels,166 Ky .719,179 S. W. 831.
(10) This sectiondoes not authorize a
sale to satisfya debt , a portion of which
is not due when judgment is rendered.
Durham v. Elliott, 180 Ky . 724 , 203 S.
W. 539.
§ 136. (1) Continuance on amendment
-is addressed to sound discretionof
court. City of Paducah v . Johnson , 93
S. W. 1035, 29 R. 518 ; and if adverse
party desires a continuance he should
fileaffidavitshowing why . Troendle v .
Morgan Coal Co.,114 S.W. 312.
(2 ) When several causes of action are
set out . or the same cause is set forth
in differentparagraphs , the striking out
of any of thecausesof actionor para-
graphs isnot such an amendment as will
authorize a continuance . Branshaw v .
Berry ,2 R. 58.
(2a ) An amended petition was filed
which did not materiallychange the
cause of action . Refusal of a continu-
ance because of the amendment was held

Title VII ] [§ 138-139Mistakes in Pleadings- Amendments .
§ 138 [165] [Verificationnotnecessaryaftertrialbegins.] No objec-
tionshallbe taken,afterthecommencement of thetrial,to any pleading
forthewantof,or a defectin,theverification. (See furtheras toverifi-
cation,secs.116,117 ; when trialbegins, sec.590.)
Cross reference: See Newman's Pl.and Pr. (3d Ed .), sec.526j.
§ 139 [166] [Verification- amendments allowed without.] Courts
may ,intheirdiscretion,permittheamendments authorizedby thischapter
to be made without beingverified,unlessa new and distinctcauseof action
or defensebe therebyintroduced.
Cross reference: See Newman's Pl.and Pr . (3d Ed .),secs.526h ,580, 596,597 .
not to be error. Moore's Adm . v . Pierce,
160 Ky . 107, 169 S. W. 620.
(2b ) The court is not required to grant
a continuance because of the amendment
of a petition, which makes no material
change in the cause of action. Moore's
Admr . v . Pierce, 160 Ky . 107, 169 S. W.
620 .
§ 138. (1) Objection- how taken. Ob-
jection to pleading for want of verifica-
tionshouldbe by ruleagainstthe party
failingto verifyit, and upon his failure
to have it stricken from the record .
Wheeler v . Wales , 3 Bush 225 ; Harris v .
Ray , 15 B. M. 628 ; and see notes to secs.
116 , 117 .
(2) To take advantage of error in over-
rulingmotion to requireparty to verify
his pleading, the decision must be ex-
cepted to at the time . Cobb v. Stewart ,
4 Met .225 ; Mason v .Mason , 5 Bush 187.
(3) Waiver . Right to require plaintiff
to verifypetitionis waived by answer,
and the right can not be regained by
withdrawing answer . Butler v. Church
of Conception , 14 Bush 540 ; Meador v .
Turpin , 4 Met .93 .
(4 ) Although an answer denying the
executionof a note must be verified,the
objection for want of verification is
waived unless made before commence-
ment of trial. Meyer v .Douglass ,99 Ky .
267 , 18 R. 169, 35 S. W. 917.
(5) If a pleading impeaching the con-
sideration of a deed is verified, no other
verification is needed . If the omission to
verify the pleading is not objected to it
is waived. Hite v. Reynolds, 163 Ky .
502, 173 S. W. 1108,
(6 ) The want of verificationof the
petitionalleginga mistake in a deed will
be treated as waived unles sobjected to.
Hite v.Reynolds,163 Ky .502,173 S. W.
1108 .
§ 139. (1) Verificationof amendment .
Although an amended petitionwhich the
lower court refused to permit filedwas
not verified,its rejectionwas held to be
error, as it appeared that it was not re-
jected for this cause. Lebus v . Poindex-
ter, 18 R. 740, 38 S. W. 9.
(2 ) An amended answer set up a new
and distinctdefense, such as under Code ,
sec. 116, requires a verification. The re-
fusal of the court to require verification
was not in this case prejudicial error,
Ball v . Sandlin, 176 Ky . 537, 195 S. W.
1089 .
(3) A judgment was reversed for a
refusal of the circuitcourt to permit thefiling of an amended pleading. The
amendment was not verified. It did not
appear from the record that lack of veri-
fication was the reason for the refusal .
Caldwell Co. v. Durret Construction Co.,
180 Ky . 594, 203 S. W. 291.

§ 171. (1) Bail- liabilityof,how fixed.
The law requiresthat the execution shall
be placed in the hands of the officerwith-
in twenty days after it might have is-
sued, but does not require that the exe-
cution shall be returned within twenty

(3) To fix the liabilityof the bail, it
is indispensable that there should be a
valid execution against the body of the
defendant; that it should be placed in

prescribed, and be returned not found .
An execution commanding the sheriffto

isfyand pay the Commonwealth of Ken-
tucky the sum of $125" is insufficientto
fixthe liabilityof the bail. It shouldbe
to satisfyand pay the plaintiffin the
judgment. Abbott v. Daniel,3 Met .340.
(4) That executionupon the judgment
againstthe principalwas returned"not
found " before the return day thereof
does not prejudicethe bail. Jones v.

§ 194. (1) Attachment can not issue
until action commenced . Attachment is-
sued before summons issued, or warn-
ing order made ,is void. Hall v. Grogan ,
78 Ky . 11 ; Kellar v. Stanley , 86 Ky .240,
9 R. 388,5 S. W. 477; Redwine v. Under-
wood , 101 Ky . 190, 19 R. 366, 40 S. W.
462. When action commenced ,
sec . 39 .
see
(la) A summons and an attachment
issued on a claim exceeding $50- before a
written petition was filed. The attach-
ment was void. Smith v. Dungey , 178
Ky . 702, 199 S. W. 777.
(2 ) Attachment upon return of "no
property " found , see sec. 439 and notes.
(3) Concealment . The concealment
which will authorize and sustain an at-
tachment under subsee , 5 involves the
intention of the debtor to delay his cred-
itors,and may be accomplished by secret-
ing himself in his house or upon his
premises, or by departingsecretlyfrom
his place of abode to a more secure spot
eitherin or out of the county of his resi-
dence . Dunn v .Salter, 1 Duv .342 ; Lewis
v . Wright , 3 Bush 311 ; and the fact of
concealment may be shown by evidence;
it is not necessary that an attempt be
made to execute a summons . Johnson
v . Kaufman , 104 Ky . 494, 20 R. 684, 47
S. W. 324 .
(4 ) Court in which to be obtained. At-
tachment is incidental to the action in

§ 194] [Title VIIIAttachment - Grounds Of .
I. [Action for money.] In an actionfor the recoveryof money
against-
1. [Foreigncorporationornon-resident.] A defendant,who isa for-
eigncorporationor non-residentof theState;or,
2. [Absence forfour months.] Who has been absentherefrom four
months ; or,
3. [Departurewithfraudulentintent.] Has departedherefromwith
intenttodefraud hiscreditors;or,
4. [Avoidingsummons .] Has leftthecountyof hisresidencetoavoid
the serviceof a summons ;or,
5. [Concealmenttoavoidsummons .] So concealshimselfthata sum-
mons cannot be servedupon him ;or,
6. [Removal of propertyfrom State.] Isabouttoremove,or has re-
moved ,hisproperty,or a materialpartthereof,outofthisState,notleav-
ingenough thereintosatisfytheplaintiff'sclaim,or theclaimsof saidde-
fendant's creditors; or,
which it issought,and can only be ob-
tained in the court in which the action is
pending. Moore v. Sheppard, 1 Met .97.
(5) Express and implied contracts.
Provisions of Code authorizing attach-
ments make no distinction between ex-
press and impliedcontracts, and an at-
tachment may be issuedand sustained
upon an impliedcontract. Garriottv.
Jaffray, 10 Bush 413 .
(5a) A creditorhaving grounds for
generalattachment may sue out a gen-
eralattachmentand cause itto be levied
on personalpropertyon which he already
has a lien to secure the debt . Kline v .
Cofield, 159 Ky . 744 , 169 S. W. 477.
(6) Foreign Corporation- non -resident
--an attachmentmay be obtainedagainst
for any of the grounds and upon any
claim that would authorize it against a
residentdefendant ,but ifthe attachment
is sought on the soleground that the
defendant isa foreigncorporation or non-
resident it can only be obtained on a
claim arising on a contract judgment or
award . Bates Machine Co. v . Norton ,
113 Ky . 372,25 R. 931, 68 S. W. 423 .
(6a ) An attachment under subsecs. 2 ,
3 , 4 and 5 of this section can not be ob-
tained against one who has never been a
residentof thisstate; nor can an attach-
ment be obtained under subsec . 1 in an
action of tort unconnected with contract .
Lagerwahl v. White , 154 Ky . 162, 156 S.
W. 1079 .
(6b) An attachment may be obtained
againstone who has never been a resi-
dent of Kentucky in an action for tort
unconnected with contract if grounds
exist under subsecs,6 ,7 or 8 of this sec-
tion. Lagerwahl v . White , 154 Ky . 162,
156 S. W. 1079 .
(7 ) Four months ' absence. Where the
debtor leaveshis home , intendingto go
out of the State, and consummates this
purpose, and is absent four months , his
absence from his residence four months
authorizesan attachment,thoughunex-pectedcasualtymay have delayedhim in
the State a few days after leaving his
residence. Spaldingv.Simms ,4 Met .285.
(7 ) In 1876 the absence of principals
from the State was ground for an at-
tachment againstthe propertyof an in-
dorser or surety not absent from the
State . (This rule is believed to have
been changed by the present Code .) Bank
of Louisvillev. Smothers, 9 Ky . Opin-
ions 4 .
(8) Fraudulent conveyance . It is nec-
essary to show a fraudulent intent before
the attachment is sued out ; fraud orig-
inating afterward is not sufficient. War-
ner v . Everett , 7 B. M. 262. The state-
ment in the affidavit that the defendant
" is about to waste or dispose of his
property, with the fraudulent intent to
cheat, hinder and delay his creditors in
the collectionof theirdebts ," is sufficient.
Lane v . Robinson , 18 B. M. 623 ; and see
Clark v . Smith , 7 B. M. 273. As to sub-
jectingproperty fraudulentlyconveyed,
seeMartz v.Pfeifer,80 Ky .600,4 R. 592;
and Ky . Stat ., sec. 1907a .
(9 ) Where ground for attachment is
that debtor has made or is about to make
180

Title VIII ] [§ 194Attachment - Grounds Of .
7. [Disposingof propertywith fraudulentintent.] Has sold, con-
veyed,or otherwisedisposedof,hisproperty,or sufferedor permittedit
tobe sold,with the fraudulentintentto cheat,hinderor delayhiscredit-
ors ; or ,
8. [Attempt to disposewith fraudulentintent- provisionas to non-
resident.] Is aboutto sell,convey,or otherwisedisposeof,hisproperty,
with such intent. But an attachment shallnot be granted on the ground
thatthedefendantisa foreigncorporation,or a non-residentof thisState,
forany claimotherthana debtor demand arisingupon a contract,express
or implied,or a judgmentor award.
a fraudulentconveyanceof his property,
thecreditorhas a rightto an attachment
although theremay be one or more sol-
vent sureties on note. Perkins v. Scott,
7 R. 589 ; Marks v. Gauze ,24 R. 1949,72
S. W. 732 .
(10) Insolvency. This subdivision
authorizes an attachment against all the
property of the debtor not exempt from
execution, whenever he is without a suf-
ficiencysubject to execution to satisfy
debt sued on, although theremay be no
attempt on his part to defeat the collec-
tion of the debt . The evidence to sus-
tain the attachment must clearlyshow
that the debtor did not have sufficient
property subjectto executionto satisfythe debt sued on. Burdett v. Phillips, 78
Ky . 246 .
(11) To sustain the attachment , it
must appear first,that the defendant has
no property in this State subject to ex-
ecution,or not enough thereofto satisfy
the debt sued on ; and second, that the
collection of the demand will be endan-
gered by delay in obtainingjudgment,
or a return of no property found. One
of these conditions is not necessarily the
concomitant of the other, although it
may be so. Francisv. Burnett, 84 Ky .
23,7 R. 715 .
(12) The questionto be consideredis-
what property has the debtor in this
State subject to execution at time at-
tachment is issued his solvency or in-
solvency is not material. Goepper v.
Phenix Brewing Co., 115 Ky . 708 , 25 R.
84 ,74 S. W. 726.
(13 ) It is not sufficientthat debtor has
not propertysufficienttosatisfydemand,it must alsobe allegedand proved that
the collection of the demand will be en-
dangered by delay,and while the former
fact ordinarilyraisesa presumption of
the existenceof the latterthatpresump-
tion may be overcome . Dunn v . McAl-
pine,90 Ky .78,11 R. 884, 13 S. W. 363;
First National Bank v .Kiefer Milling Co.,
95 Ky .97, 15 R. 457 ,23 S. W. 675 ; Downs
v.Ringgold,101 Ky .392,19 R. 639,41 S.
W. 317; Hoobler v . Howland , 19 R. 1473 ,
43 S. W. 486 ; Deposit Bank v . Smith ,
109 Ky . 310 ; 22 R. 808, 58 S. W. 792 ;
when the fact of insolvency is admitted
the attachment should be sustained un-
less the presumption arising therefrom
that collectionof debt will be endangered
isovercome by evidence. Johnson v.Lou.
City Nat .Bk .,22 R. 118,56 S. W. 710.
(14) Where there are two or more
obligorswithin jurisidictionof this State,
an attachment can not be obtained
againstany of them under thissubdivi-
sion without allegingas to allof them
that they have no property in this State
subject to execution and the collectionof
demand will be endangered . 90 Ky . 78,
11 R. 884, 13 S. W. 363 ; and in an action
againsta firmitisnot sufficientto state
that the firm has not sufficientproperty
to pay debt ; it should also be averred
that the members of the firmhave not.
O'Connor v . Shirley, 107 R. 70 ; 21 R.
735, 52 S. W. 1056.
(15) If there is much doubt as to the
value of defendant's property , the court
will be reluctant to sustain attachment ,
and its value is to be determined by the
opinions of witnesses. Haynes v . Wiley ,
12 R. 299,2 S. W. 681 ; but if the debtor
volunteers to go on the witness -stand he
should be able to show that he has suffi-
cient property to invalidate the attach-
ment . Draddy v.Heile,17 R. 1182,33 S.
W. 1107 .
(16) It isalsoquestionablewhether a
plaintiffhas a right to purchase debts
againsta defendant, not in good faith,
but for the sole purpose of obtaining an
attachment on the ground that he does
not own sufficientproperty to satisfy the
demand . 84 Ky . 24, 7 R. 715 .

§ 194] [Title VIIIAttachment - Grounds Of .
II. [In actionupon contract,judgment or award.] In an actionfor
therecoveryof money due upon a contract,judgmentor award,ifthede-
fendanthaveno propertyinthisStatesubjecttoexecution,or notenough
thereoftosatisfytheplaintiff'sdemand ,and thecollectionof thedemand
willbe endangeredby delayinobtainingjudgmentora returnof no prop-
erty found.
III. [In actiontorecoverpersonalproperty.] In an actionto recover
thepossessionof personalpropertywhichhasbeenorderedtobe delivered
totheplaintiff,and whichproperty,orpartwhereof,hasbeendisposedof,
concealed, or removed , so that the order for itsdeliverycannot be exe-
cutedby the sheriff.
Cross reference: See Newman's Forms Nos .124, 135,136,138,357-382.
(17) Junior attaching creditor who
wishes to attack prior attachment on
ground that itwas procuredby fraudu-
lentcollusionbetween creditorand debtor
should proceed under act of 1856. De-
positBank v. Smith,109 Ky . 310,22 R.
808 , 58 S. W. 792 .
(18) Law to be strictlycomplied with .
Proceedings by attachment are summary
and extraordinary,and,to be good,must
conform to allthe requirementsof the
law . Pool v. Webster , 3 Met . 278.
(19) Leaving county to avoid service.
Where a debtorleavesthe county of his
residence to avoid arrest in a criminal
proceeding, therebypreventingserviceof
summons upon him in a civilaction for
the same wrong , subesc. 4 of this section
applies. Bank of Commerce v. Payne ,
86 Ky .446 , 10 R. 43 , 8 S. W. 856 .
(20 ) Mortgaged personal property in
the possessionof the mortgagor by con-
sent of the mortgagee can not be taken
under a general order of attachment-
the mortgagor's remedy is to proceed un-
der sec. 249. Gaar v . Lyons , 99 Ky . 672,
18 R. 500, 37 S. W. 73, 148. But see
Browning v. Crawford , 145 Ky . 279, 140
S. W. 530 , overruling this case.
(21 ) Non -residency. Attachment may
be issuedon theground of non -residency,
though defendant be casually in the
State at the time . Jackson v . Perry , 13
B. M. 230 ; and see Dudley v. Porter, 1 В.
M. 404; Dudley v. Donaldson, 2 B. M.
151 .
(22) A non -resident in an action in
this State may sue out an attachment on
the ground that defendant is a non -resi-
dent . Gray v.Briscoe,6 Bush 687.
(23) The plaintiffmay have an attach-
ment against the property of the defend-
ant on the ground that he is a non -resi-
dent ; Brand v.Brand , 116 Ky . 785, 25 R.
987 , 76 S. W. 868 ; but this fact alone is
not sufficientto authorize a judgment
setting aside a conveyance of his prop-
erty because fraudulent in the meaning of
sec. 1907 Kentucky Statutes. Little v .
Ragan ,83 Ky .321,7 R. 391.
(24) Sec. 58, subsec. 6, providesthat
the affidavitmentioned in that section
shall be sufficient evidence of the facts
thereinstated to support the warning
order and action, unless controverted.
This provision was not in Code of 1854,
and it was held that non -residency must
be proved , unless the affidavitmentioned
in sec. 409 was made . Buckner v . Bush ,
1 Duv .394; Jackson v.McElroy ,2 Bush
132. See also sec. 126-3 .
(25) Pleading - proof. If sufficient
grounds are not alleged, proof upon the
matter will not beconsidered . Dunn v .
McAlpine , 90 Ky . 78, 11 R. 884, 13 S. W.
363 .
(26 ) Property exempt from attach-
ment ,see note 11, sec.212.
(27) Removal of property from State .
A charge that defendant is removing
from the State, unaccompanied with the
statement that he is about to remove his
property , or a material part thereof, out
of the State,not leaving enough to sat-
isfy the demand sued for, is defective.
Clarke v. Seaton ,18 B. M. 226. See Mont-
gomery v.Tilley, 1 B. M. 155; Nutter v.
Connet,3 B. M. 199; Jennings v. Shrop-
shire, 9 B. M. 431, construingacts of
1828, 1838, similar to this subsection.
(28 ) Ordinary sales to customers out
of the State is not enough to justifyan
attachment on ground that debtor isre-
moving property from State. Goepper
v. Phoenix Brewing Co., 115 Ку .708, 25
R. 84 , 74 S. W. 726 .
(29) Tort. In Blewittv. Sprague, 24
R. 1860, an action for damages caused

(15) Jurisdiction- defectiveaffidavit.

§ 198. (1) Bond to be executed before
issual of . Bond must be executed before
the clerk before he issues the attach-
ment ; attachment issuingbefore bond
executed will be discharged. Anderson
v . Sutton , 2 Duv . 480 ; Horne v . Mitchell,
7 Bush 131. Bond not executed before
the clerknor in manner prescribedisnot
a defective bond that can be remedied as
provided in sec.682 ; 7 Bush 131.
(2) Bond executed to all the defend
ants jointly, or to defendants as a firm
is good , as any of the defendants dam-
aged may maintain an action on it.
Voorheis v.Eiting,15 R. 161,22 S.W. 80.
(3) Damages recoverable on bond . In
action on bond plaintiffcan only recover
damages for the injury sustained by
being deprivedof the use ofhisproperty,
or its loss or deterioration , and the costs
and expense incurred in defense of at-
tachment. Pettitv.Mercer,8 B. M. 51;
Kaye v . Kean , 18 B. M. 839 ; Mitchell v .
Mattingly, 1 Met . 237; and see Adam
Roth Co. v.Hopkins, 16 R. 678,29 S. W.
293 .
(4) The only damages that can be re-
covered on the bond are such as result
from the order of attachment , and not
the damages incurred by reason of the
action independent of the attachment .
Trapnall v . McAfee , 3 Met . 34 ; Shultz
v .Morrison ,3 Met .99 ; Burgen v . Sharer ,
14 Β . Μ . 497. The fact that an attach-
ment has been levied on land does not
deprive the owner of the use or posses-
sion of it, and hence the levy does not
entitlehim to recoverdamages for the
use of the land. Heathman v. Million,
17 R. 421, 31 S. W. 473 ; Duncan v. Citi-
zens Bank , 20 R. 237, 45 S. W. 774 ; or
expenses or attorney fees ifa lispendens
has been createdby filingpetition. Cald-
well v.DepositBank , 109 Ky . 197, 22 R.
684 , 58 S. W. 589 .
(5 ) If the whole costs and expenses are
incurredin defendingthe action,nothing
can be recovered on bond , but if incurred
indefendingattachmentalone,itcan be;
or if incurredpartly in defending at-
tachment and partly in defense of the
action, itis recoverableso far as appli-
cable to the attachment . Johnson v.
Farmers ' Bank , 4 Bush 283 ; McClure v .
Renaker , 21 R. 360, 51 S. W. 317.
(6 ) Attorney's fees can be recovered
only where plaintiffhas paid or con-
tractedto pay such feesand upon proof
that they are reasonable, and the re-
covery is limited to the amount paid or
agreed to be paid. Shultz v . Morrison ,
3 Met .99 ; 3 Met . 34 ; 8 B. M. 198. A de-
defendant who is successfulin defeating
an attachment levied upon his property
for the debt of another on the ground
that it was conveyed to him to defraud
creditors can not recover attorney fees
or expenses. Worthington v.Morris, 98
Ky . 54 , 17 R. 624, 32 S. W. 269 ; Martin
v. Turpin, 108 Ky .709, 22 R. 424, 57 S.

(7 ) When by an attachment a party is
prevented from performing a contract,
and materialpreparedto enablehim to
do so isthus depreciatedin valueto him ,
he can recover on the bond the damages
caused by the depreciation. Carpenter v .
Stevenson ,6 Bush 259 .
(8) To maintainan actionon the bond,
it is necessary that the attachment
should have been discharged; no action
will lie until attachment is discharged.
It is not necessary to allege either
malice or want of probable cause, but it
should be alleged that the attachment
was wrongfully obtained. Kaye v .Kean ,
18 Β .Μ . 839 ; Nolle v . Thompson , 3 Met .
121; see Cooper v.Hill, 3 Bush 219 ; Har-
bour-Pitt Shoe Co. v. Dixon, 22 R. 1169,

(9 ) Right to sue on an attachment
bond is transferred to assignee by gen-
eral assignment . Francis v. Burnett , 84
Ky . 23,7 R. 715 ; and see Cleveland Coal
Co. v. Sloan , 90 Ky . 308 , 12 R. 223 , 14
S. W. 279 .

(10) Maliciouslysuingout attachment.
Recovery of damages for maliciously
suing out an attachment is a bar to an
action for damages on the bond . In an
actionfor maliciouslysuing out an at-
tachment the plaintiffcan recover dam-
ages for allhis injuriesof every charac-
tergrowing out of the wrongful issuing
of the attachment . Hall v . Foreman , 82
Ky . 505, 6 R. 473. But the action will
not lie until the attachment has been dis-
charged . 3 Met .121 ; 3 Met .193. As to
malice and probable cause, see further,
Anderson v. Columbia Trust Co., 20 R.
1790, 50 S. W. 40 ; Abohosh v. Buck , 19
R. 1267,43 S. W. 425 ; Mark v . Christian,
22 R. 1102, 59 S. W. 1092.
(11) To maintain action for mali-
ciouslysuingout an attachment,plaintiff
must allegeand prove want of probable
cause and malice, and may recover for
loss of credit, injury to business, im-
pairedreputation,and costsand expense.
Wood v. Weir , 5 B. M. 544 ; Mitchell v .
Mattingly, 1 Met .237; Pettitv. Mercer,
8 Β . Μ . 51. See Fullenwider v . MeWil-
liams, 7 Bush 389 ; and Chelf v . Penn , 2
Met . 463 ; and action can only be main-
tained by the defendant against whom
the attachment was actually sued out .
Farmers'Tobacco Co.v.Gibbons,107Ky .
611, 21 R. 1349, 55 S. W. 2 ; Martin v .
Turpin , 108 Ky . 709, 22 R. 424, 57 S. W.
459 .
(12) Where an assignment for the
benefit of creditors is made after an at-
tachment has issuedagainsttheproperty
of the assignor, the right to maintain an
actionfor maliciouslysuing out the at-
tachment is in the assignor. Francis v .
Burnett , 84 Ky .23 ,7 R. 715.
(13) Where a firm make an assign-
ment after the issual of an attachment
they can not maintain an action against
creditorfor injuryto firmcreditor busi-
ness, it passed to assignee. Cleveland
Coal Co. v . Sloan , 90 Ky . 308, 12 R. 223,
14 S. W. 279 ; and a trustee may be sued
and is individuallyliablefor maliciously
suing out an attachment . Anderson v .
Columbia Trust Co., 20 R. 1267, 50 S.
W. 40.
(14) Wrongful seizure- action for. An
action may be maintained as provided in
sec. 7, Ky . Stat ., for suing out an at-tachment without good cause. In this
action plaintiffcan only recover damages
for the losssustainedby being deprived
of the use of the property , or for injury
resulting to it from the wrongful seizure
and damages for the sale, if properly
sold, and his costs in attachment suit.
The dischargeof the attachment iscon-
clusivethat it issued without good cause .
Mitchell v. Mattingly , 1 Met . 237 ; and
see notes to sec. 7 ,Ky . Stat .

§ 201 [226] [Attachmentmay issuetoany county--costs.] Ordersof
attachmentmay be issuedto thesheriffof any county; and severalof them
may ,attheoptionof theplaintiff,be issuedatthesame timeor insucces-
not,like a summons ,be executed by any

rected. Menderson v.Specker ,79 Ky .509 ,
3 R. 421. Sheriff can execute attach-

Turners v .Howard ,2 Duv .112.
(2) Reissualof order. The plaintiff
withdrew from the sheriff an order of at-
tachment on which he had indorsed time
of reception. A supplemental affidavit
was filed and the clerk reissued the same
order,erasingtherefromthe indorsement.
The order and levy made under itwere
held valid. Dean v.Garnett, 1 Duv .408.
(3) Requisitesof. Attachment which
does not run in the name of the " Com-
monwealth ofKentucky " isvoid. Words
and figuresin the writ should be written
in full. Yeaver v. Groves, 78 Ky . 278;

back of a summons which runs in the
name of the Commonwealth is valid,al-
though the words "Commonwealth of
Kentucky " do not appearon back of sum-mons . Northern Bank v. Hunt , 93 Ky .
67 , 14 R. 1 , 19 S. W. 3 .
(4 ) It is the attestation of the officer
who issuesthat gives validityto the
process, and in a conflictbetween words
in the body of the writ over officialsig-
nature , and unattested indorsement on
its back , the words in body must pre-
vail. Peters v . Conway , 4 Bush 565 .
(5 ) As to stating amount of plaintiff's
claim in the order of attachment, see
Burnam v . Romans ,2 Bush 191; but the
failure to state amount in order does not
affectitsvalidity,and may be corrected
by insertionof proper amount . Louis-
villeBanking Co. v. Etheridge, 19 R.
908, 43 S. W. 169 ; and if clerk issues
order for more than the amount claimed
in petitionthe errormay be corrected.
Lee v . Smyser , 96 Ky . 369, 16 R. 497, 29
S. W. 27.

§ 202. (1) Precedence of attachments
-duty of officer- presumption . The pre-
sumption will be indulged in that the
sheriffdischargedhisduty by levingfirst
the attachment that first came to his
hands,nothing being shown to the con-
trary. Buckner v. Bush , 1 Duv . 394 ;
Phelps v. Ratcliffe, 3 Bush 334.
(2 ) The provisions of the Code in re-
spect to the order in which attachments
shall be levied are directory, and the
failure of the sheriffto comply literally
with them will not vitiate the lien re-
sultingto the attaching creditorin whose
behalf the levy has been made . The
sheriffmay amend his return. Lane v .
Robinson ,18 В. М. 623 ; 3 Bush 334.
(3) If severalattachments be delivered
to a sheriff at different hours on same
day and all are levied at same time they
should be satisfiedaccording to order of
delivery. Sewell v. Savage , 1 B. M. 260.
(4 ) First levy gives prior lien as be-
tween several attaching creditors where
the writs are in the hands of different
officers. Kennon v . Ficklin, 6 B. M. 414 ,
but where attachments are delivered to
sheriffor his deputies it is his duty to
levy them in order received, and although
he may not observe this order, the court
in distributingfund will do so. Kennon
v. Ficklin, 6 B. M. 414; Clay v. Scott, 7
Β . Μ . 554 .
(5 ) Where an attachment is in the
hands of one officer and an execution in
the hands of another officer, if the at-
tachment be firstlevied it will have pri-
ority. Bourne v. Hocker , 11 B. M. 23.
(6) Attachment leviedon property in
this State has precedence over claim of
receiverfor benefitof creditorsappointed
in another State . Zacher v . F. T. & S.
Co.,109 Ky .441, 22 R. 987, 59 S. W. 493 ;
and see notes to sec . 212 .
(7) See further,notes to sec. 212.

§ 203 [228] [Manner ofexecuting.) The orderofattachmentshallbe
executedby thesheriffwithoutdelayinthe followingmanner-
1. [Upon realproperty.] Upon realproperty, by leavingwith the
occupantthereof, or, iftherebe no occupant, in a conspicuousplace
thereon,a copy of theorder. (SeeKy .Stats.,sec.2358a,astonotice;what
officer'sreturnmust show , sec.217.)
2. [Upon personalproperty.] Upon personalpropertycapableof
manual delivery,by takingitintohiscustodyand holdingitsubjecttothe
orderof thecourt; or, ifitbe heldby anotherofficerunder processor
undera distressfortaxes,by deliveringtohim a copy of theorder,witha
noticespecifyingthepropertyattached. (What officer'sreturnmust show,
sec.217.)
§ 203. (1) Acknowledgment of at-
tachment only creates lien from the time
of the acknowledgment of serviceby de-
fendant. Phelps v.Ratcliffe,.3 Bush 334.
(2) Amendment of return. Officermay
amend his return to conform to the
facts. Lane v. Robinson , 18 B. M. 623 ;
Newton v . Prather, 1 Duv . 100; and if
ambiguous itmay be explainedby other
évidence. Chamberlain v. Brewer , 3
Bush 561 .
(3 ) Attachment of real estate . The
same particularityisnot requiredin de-
scribingland levied on under attachment
as under an execution; it is sufficientif
the land can be identified. White v .
O'Bannon , 86 Ky . 93, 9 R. 334, 5 S. W.
346; Price v . Taylor , 110 Ку . 589, 22 к .
1945, 62 S. W. 270 ; Norfleet v . Logan ,21
R. 1200, 54 S. W. 713; Hatcher v. Wag-
ner, 27 R. 1016, 87 S. W. 778 ; Wren v .
Cooksey , 147 Ky . 825 , 145 S. W. 1116 ;
Ky . Stat.,sec.2358a .
(4) Garnishees. Lien is created by an-
swer of garnishee, although service upon
him is not sufficientto create lien. City
Nat .Bank v .Gardner ,5 R. 689 ; Paducah
Lumber Co. v. Langstaff, 6 R. 445.
(5) Leaving property in charge of a
third person . The sheriff levied on a
stock of goods , and left them in posses-
sion of the person in charge of the store,
without closingit. The levy was valid,
and the party held the goods as bailee
of the sheriff. Howell v . Com . Bank , 5
Bush 93 .
(6 ) Levy after return day void. The
levy of an attachment after the return
day isvoid. Petersv. Conway , 4 Bush
565 .
(7) Manner of executing on garnishee
-no notice describing the debt or de-
mand attached is necessary- the gar-
nishee should be summoned to answer
and an endorsement made on the order
and signed by the clerk stating that the
object of the action is to attach in the
hands of garnisheeany money ,property,
or other evidence of debt that he owes
to or holds for the defendant . Bell v .
Wood , 87 Ky . 56, 8 R. 917, 7 S. W. 550.
(8 ) Non -resident. In an action against
a non -resident, a non-residentcorpora-
tion may be summoned as a garnishee.
P.,C. C. & St. Louis R. R. v . Bartels, 108
Ky .216,21 R. 1670,56 S. W. 152 ; but see
sec. 1701a of Ky . Stat .
(9) Personal property must be first
taken , sec. 206, but if an officerlevy on
land, although there may be personal
estate, it will not render the levy in-
valid . 12 В. М. 112 .
(9a) A creditorhaving grounds for
general attachment may sue out a gen-
eral attachment and cause it to be levied
on personal property on which he already
has a lien to secure the debt . Kline v .
Cofield,159 Ky .744,169 S. W. 477 .
(10) Personal representative. Service
on to attach interest of heir in estate .
Wilkersonv.Phillips,26 R. 440,81 S.W.
691 .
(11) Powers of officerin levying at-
tachment , see Ky . Stat ., 4582..
(12) Presumption that officerdid his
duty . Where the return of the officer
showed that he "posted a copy of the
order on the premises , there being no
tenant," it will be presumed that he
posted it in a conspicuous place. Lewis
v . Quinker , 2 Met . 284. And if return
describes the property and states that it
was leviedon, without statinghow , it
will be presumed that he did his duty
and complied with the law . Anderson v.
Sutton ,2 Duv .480 ; Scott v .Scott,85 Ky .

§ 214. (1) Claimant must present his
claim. A claimantof attachedpropertywho executes bond and retains the pos-
sessionmust presenthis claim, as pro-
vided in sec. 29, or he willbe estopped
from assertingit afterward. Millerv.
Desha , 3 Bush 212 ; Deposit Bank v .
Thomason ,23 R. 1957, 66 S. W. 604.
(2) Common law bond . A bond exe-
cutedbeforethelevyof theattachment,
but after it came into the officer'shands ,
is good as a common law bond . Cook v .
Boyd , 16 B. М. 556.
(3) Defendant may execute bond for

tion. Hobson v.Hall, 13 R. 109, 14 S. W.

v .Reed ,97 S. W. 372,30 R. 12.
(4 ) Discharge of attachment . If the
defendant desiresto obtain a discharge
of the attachment and restitution of the
attached property,he may do so by giv-
ing the bond provided for in sec. 221.
When that bond is executed, all power
of the court or its officers over the at-
tachedpropertyceases,and the plaintiff
can look only to the bond . Bell v. West-
ern River Co., 3 Met . 557 ; the bond pro-
vided for under this section does not dis-
charge the attachment , it is only an obli-
gationfor the forthcomingof the prop-
erty. 3 Met .557.
(5 ) Enforcement of bond for the forth-
coming ofpropertymay be by ruleor by
action. Oppenheimer v. Riley, 6 Bush
118; and see sec.232 ; 6 В. М. 595.
(6 ) Liability of obligors if liens on
property . Where a forthcoming bond
has been executed, and the property is
not more than sufficient in value to sat-
isfypriorliensupon it,subjecttowhichthe attachment was levied, the obligors
in the bond are liableonly for nominal
damages for their failureto produce the
property. Hayman v. Hallam, 79 Ky .
389, 2 R. 419; Edwards BarnardCo. v.
Pflanz,115 Ky .393,24 R. 2296,73 S. W.
1018 .
(7 ) Loss of property . The claimant of
attachedproperty,having executedbond
and taken possession of the attached
property , failed to sustain his claim on
the trial of the action, and was held
liableforthe valueof the property(a
horse), it having died without his fault
during the pendency of the action. Dear
v . Brannon , 4 Bush 471 ; see Bush v .
Groom , 9 Bush 675.
(8) Nature of the bond . The bond is
to be regardedas a mere obligationfor
the forthcoming of the property; the
liencreatedby the attachment, and the
power of the court over the attached
property , subsist and continue as effec-
tuallyas if no bond had been given, or
the possession never taken out of the
hands of the officer. Bell v . Western
River Co., 3 Met . 557 ; Edwards Barnard

§ 221. (1) Bond to discharge attach-
ment - effect of . When this bond is ex-
ecuted, all power of the court and its of-
ficersover the attached property ceases;
and the plaintiff. can look only to the
bond . Bell v. Western River Co.,3 Met .
557. The attachment is dischargedby
operationof law, and the obligorsin the
bond are bound unconditionally to per-
form the judgment of the court in the
action. In an actionto enforcethebond ,
neither the sufficiencyof the grounds of
the attachment nor the liabilityof the
property leviedon can be inquiredinto,
nor can the claims which any person may
have to the attachedpropertybe investi-
gated. Hazelrigg v. Donaldson, 2 Met .
445; Taylor v. Taylor, 3 Bush 118;In-

ern River Co ..3 Met . 557 ; Thompson v.
Arnett ,23 R. 1082,64 S. W. 735 ; and see
sec. 214 and notes thereto as to execution
of forthcoming bond .

§ 271. (1) Effect of injunction. The
CivilCode has not diminishedor changed
the force or effectof an injunction.When
issuedby proper authority, it must be
obeyed by all persons to whom it is di-
rected. Keith v. Wilson , 3 Met . 202, or
who have noticethatithas been granted.
Bartram v. Ohio R. Co., 141 Ky .100,132
S. W. 188 .
(2) Grounds for- mandatory injunc-
tion. The only grounds upon which
courts of equity may grant injunctions
are not specifiedin the Code . The pro-
visionsof the Code upon this subject
were not intended to take away other
well-recognizedgrounds of equityjuris-
diction which may be found necessary
for the fulland proper administration of
justice, 79 Ky . 295, 2 R. 318. Manda-
tory injunction, Hager v. New South
Brewing Co.,28 R. 895,90 S. W. 608.
(3 ) Injunction- in what court to be
obtained. An injunction is merely inci-
dental to the action in which it is sought ,
and can only be obtainedin the court in
which the actionis pending. Moore v.
Sheppard ,1 Met .97.
(3a) An injunction was improperly
granted by a circuitcourt to restrainthe
enforcement of a judgment of a police
court in a city of the third class as the
circuitcourt had jurisdictionof an ap-
peal under Kỳ . Stat., sec. 3367. Pols-
grove v. Moss , 154 Ky . 408, 157 S. W.
1133 .

(5) Mandamus not stayed . A railroad
company obtained a writ of mandamus
to compel the county judge to issue cer-
tain bonds. The taxpayers could not
enjoin the judge from complying with
the writ . C. & O. R. R. Co. v . Washing-
ton Co. , 10 Bush 564 .
(6) Verdict- effect of, may be pre-
servedby injunction; as where a party
sued to recoverdamages for obstructing
passway and to enjoin future obstruc-
tions,and aftera jury returneda verdict
determininghisrightto passway he filed
amended petitionsettingup verdict,and
case being transferredto equity, injunc-
tion was granted . Hunt v. Kemper , 10
R. 593 ,9 S. W. 803 .
(7 ) Court of Appeals has no original
jurisdictionto grant an injunction. City
of Newport v. Veith, 144 Ky . 501, 139
S. W. 774 .
(8) Classesofcasesinwhich injunction
will issue. An injunction will issue in
favor of a justiceof the peace to restrain
another justiceof the peace from holding
court in the district of the first named
justice of the peace. Wheeler v. Schul-
man , 165 Ky .185, 176 S. W. 1017 .
(9 ) A lifetenant may be enjoined at
the suit of a remainderman from using
minerals and timber on the land for com-
mercial purposes. McCoy v. Ferguson,
Jr.,164 Ky . 136,175 S. W. 23 .
(10) An injunctionlies against the
commissioner of insurance on behalf of
a life insurance company to enjoin him
from requiringit to compile a written
list of names and addresses of former
policyholderswith informationrespect-
ing the policies. MetropolitanLife Ins.
Co. v . Clay , 158 Ky . 192, 164 S. W. 968.
(11) An injunctionmay be grantedto
a city againstthe owner of propertyto
restrainhim from erectinga building
made of inflammable material within a
designated firedistrictwhere the inflic-
tion of the penalty prescribedby ordi-
nance does not furnisha full, complete
and adequate remedy. City of Monti-
cellov.Bates,163 Ky .38,173 S. W. 159.
(12) Ordinarilyan injunctionwillnot
lieto restraincriminalproceedings. In
this case proceedingsby way of prose-
cution for obstructing a passway were
enjoined, as a property right was in-
volvedand irreparableinjurywould fol-
low unless the prosecution should be en-
joined. Sweigart v.C. & O. Ry .Co., 161
Ky . 463, 170 S. W. 1194.
(13) Reliefmay be had by injunction
againstan additionalassessmentwhich
is void for want of notice. Boske v.
Louis Marks & Bros., 161 Ky . 460, 170
S. W. 1175 .
(14) A number of persons brought
separate suitsin an inferiorcourt against
a defendant on causes of action growing
out of the same transaction . The de-
fendant was allowed to maintain an
equitable action in the circuitcourt to
enjointhe prosecutionof thesesuitsand
compeltheplaintiffsto assertwhat claims
they had in the injunctionsuit. Illinois
Cent .Ry . Co. v . Baker , 155 Ky . 512, 159
S. W. 1169 .
(15) An injunctionenjoininga party
from interferingwith the possession of
an infant until there could be in the cir-
cuitcourt a determinationof the rights
of the parties was sustained. An at-
tempt had been made to change the cus-
tody of the childby actionin a county
court without jurisdiction. Cullins v.
Williams, 156 Ky .57, 160 S. W. 733.
(16) An injunction to prevent the col-
lectionof an illegaltax upon personalty
was sustained. City of Lancaster v.Pope ,
156 Ky . 1, 160 S. W. 509.
(17) An injunction lies on behalf of
justices of the peace to restrain persons
claimingwrongfully to exercisepowers
of which the justicesof the peace are in
rightfulpossession. Hutchinson v. Mil-
ler,158 Ky .363,164 S. W. 961.

§ 298. (1) Action against receivercan
not be brought without leave of court
by which he was appointed. Spalding
v . Com ., 88 Ky . 135, 10 R. 714, 10 S. W.
420; May v. Ball, 24 R. 875, 70 S. W.
196 .
(2) Appointment of - powers . In an
action under the act of 1856, the statute
does not authorize the appointment of a
receiver except to take charge of prop-
erty in the possession, or under the con-
trol, of the transferee, or the person to
whom the conveyance is made ; and there
isno law authorizinga receiverto take
charge of property in the possessionof
the transferrer, until it has been first
adjudged that the conveyance operated
as an assignment. Griffithv.Cox ,79 Ky .
562 .
(3) Appointment of receiverislargely
in the discretion of the court . L. & N.
R. R. v. Eakin , 100 Ky . 745,19 R. 54, 39
S. W. 416 ; 12 Bush 608.
(3a) As the Banking Act of 1912,chap-
ter 4 ,page 8, provided a method for the
liquidationof insolvent banks a court of
equity can not appoint a receiver there-
for unless the commission fails to per-
form the duties required by the act.
Cartmellv. Com .Bk . & Tr. Co., 153 Ky .
798 , 156 S. W. 1048 .
(3b) A certain order removing a trus-
tee and directingthe funds to be turned
over to the receiver of the court was
not erroneous. Holbrook v. Fyffe, 164
Ky . 435 , 175 S. W. 977.
(4 ) Court has authority to appoint a
receiverat any state of the proceedings,
whenever the facts justify it, and an
applicationdeniedmay be renewed upon
a new or different state of facts . Co-
lumbia F. & T. Co. v. Morgan , 19 R.
1761 , 44 S. W. 389 , 628 , 45 S. W. 65 .
(5 ) When a receiver has been ap-
pointedand is in possession, hisposses-
sion is the possession of the court, and
may not be disturbed without leave of
the court; nor will the court, without its
sanctionbe firstobtained, permit any
one to intercept or prevent payment to
its receiver of anything which he has
been appointed to receive, although it
may not be actuallyin hishands. Hazel-
rigg v.Bronaugh, 78 Ky . 62.
(6 ) Under sec. 299 a receiver may be
appointedto preservethe property, but
not to sellit. Wilson v . A. & T. Co.,
91 Ky . 299, 12 R. 881, 15 S. W. 783.
(7) See further, Kentucky Statutes,
sec.392,as to receivers.
(8) Where a person has wrongfully in
his possession money belonging to an-
other, the court may require him to pay
money to a receiver. Rebhan v . Fuhr-
man , 21 R. 17, 50 S. W. 976.
(9 ) Receiver of insolvent corporation
may repudiateillegaltransfermade by
corporation and sue to recover assets
wrongfully diverted. Industrial Deposit
Co. v . Taylor , 26 R. 802, 82 S. W. 574.
(10) A joint owner of land may have
it under certain circumstances placed in
hands of a receiver to prevent the other
joint owner from cutting and removing

assignment . The mortgage did not ap-
propriate rents to the mortgage debt . A

(13) Non -residentreceivermay main-
tain action in courts of this State ,
Johnson v. Rogers, 19 R. 1272, 43 S. W.
234; Hallam v .Ashford , 24 R. 870, 70 S.
W. 197 .

funds. A receiverwillnotbepermitted
to speculatein, or deriveany profitor
benefit from, the purchase of property
under his controlor in his hands; and
any purchase or profitso made will inure
to the benefitof the partiesin interest.
Titheringtonv. Hodge , 81 Ky . 286, 5 R.

(15) Order refusing to appoint receiver
isfinaland may be appealedDealedfrom. Lea-
vellv.Poore,91 Ky .321, 13 R. 51, 15 S.
W. 858 .
(15a) A circuitcourt dismissed on the
face of the papers a motion for a re-
ceiver; this was an appealable order
being in effecta judgment refusingto
appoint a receiver. Metcalfe v . John-
son, 151 Ky . 823, 152 S. W. 951.
(16) Practice on appeal. On appeal
from an ordergrantinga motion to ap-
point a receiver, the evidenceheard on
such motion , whether oral or by deposi-
tion, may be embodied in a billof ex-
ceptions. Goldsmith v. Fechheimer, 16
R. 432,28 S.W. 21.
(17) Sheriff'ssureties are not liable
formoney collectedby him as a receiver
appointedby thecourt. Heeter v.Jewell,
6 Bush 510 .
(18) Taxation of funds in hands of-
circuit court should direct receiver to
listpropertyand pay taxes. Spaldingv.
Com ., 88 Ky . 135, 10 R. 714, 10 S. W.
125 .
(19) Vendor - Mortgagee - distinction
between them in reference to the ap-
pointment of a receiver. Columbia Trust
Co. v . Morgan , 19 R. 1761, 44 S. W. 389,
628; 45 S. W. 65 ; 14 Bush 788 ; Murray
v . Murray , 99 S. W. 301, 30 R. 587, 124
Ky . 426 .
(20 ) Illustrativecases. In the follow-
ing cases the court states facts that
illustrate when it will be and when it
willnot be properto appointa receiver.
Campbell v . Rich Oil Co., 96 S. W. 442,
29 R. 716 ; McClure v . MeGee , 108 S. W.
341, 32 R. 1319, 128 Ky . 464 ; Ingram v .
CincinnatiR. Co.,107S.W. 239,32R.
849; Summers Fiber Co. v . Walker , 109
S. W. 883 ; Murray v. Murray , 99 S. W.
301, 30 R. 587, 124 Ky . 426.
(21) Appeals in receiver cases occupy
the same positionas appeals in other
cases. Ingram v. Cincinnati R. Co., 105
S. W. 878, 32 R. 530, 127 Ky . 638.

§ 299 [329] [Mortgaged propertytotakechargeof.] In an actionby
a mortgageeforthesaleof themortgagedproperty,a receivermay be ap-

or materiallyinjuredor,thattheconditionof themortgagehas notbeen

resentatives,guardians,curatorsand committeesof personsof unsound
mind, neithera partyto an action,nor hisattorney,nor any personin-
terestedtherein,shallbe appointedreceiver.
§ 301 [331] [Oath and bond of receiver.) Beforeenteringupon his
duties,thereceivermust be sworn toperform them faithfully; and,with
one ormore suretiesapprovedby thecourtorjudge,must executea bond
tosuchpersonand insuchsum asthecourtshalldirect,to theeffectthat
he willfaithfullydischargethedutiesof receiverintheaction,and obey
