(22) Appeal from quarterlyor other
inferior court can not be taken direct to
the Court of Appeals. Brown v. Crump ,
96 S. W. 1112,29 R. 1227, 123 Ky .685.
§ 299. (1) Appointment in discre-
tion of court. The appointment of a re-
ceiver is a matter of sound legal discre-tion, to be made or refused on the
circumstances of each particular case.
Douglass v. Cline, 12 Bush 608; and see
Goldsmith v. Fletcheimer, 16 R. 432, 28
S. W. 21 .
(2) A receivermay be appointedwhen
the titleto land is involved, or there is
a proceedingto enforcea lien, and the
party in possessionclaimingtitleis in-
solvent and is committing waste , or
when the plaintiffis entitledto the rents
under his contract and the party in pos-
session is insolvent and the property in-
sufficientto pay the claim. Collins v.
Richart, 14 Bush 621 ; Hounshell v . In-
surance Co., 81 Ky . 304, 5 R. 267 ; and
see Columbia Trust Co. v .Morgan , 19 R.
1761, 44 S. W. 389, 628 ; 45 S. W. 65 ;
Ky . Stat ., sec.2336 .
(3 ) Construction of section. In a suit
to enforce a mortgage lien the court or
judge in vacation has no power to order
a sale of the mortgaged property in ad-
vance of a decisionupon the merits,and
can only appoint receiver to preserve,
not to sellthe property. Wilson v. A. &
T. Co., 91 Ky . 299. 12 R. 881, 15 S. W.
783; Hurst v. Nicola Bros., 23 R. 1406,
65 S. W. 364 .
(4 ) Mortgaged property- rents. In an
actionto enforcea mortgage lien, when
itappears that the condition of the mort-
gage has been broken , and that the prop-
erty isprobablyinsufficientto discharge
the mortgage debt, a receivermay be
appointed to take charge of the property ,
and in this way the rents and profits
arising from it may be secured for the
benefit of the mortgagee . Douglass v .
Cline, 12 Bush 608 ; N. & C. Bridge Co. v .
Douglass, 12 Bush 673; Woolley v.Holt,
14 Bush 788 ; Taliaferro v . Gay , 78 Ky .
496 ; L. & N. R. R. v . Eakin , 100 Ку .
745, 19 R. 54. 39 S. W. 416 ; Nesbitt v .
Wood ,22 R. 127, 56 S. W. 714 ; Mayfield
v . Wright , 107 Ky . 530, 21 R. 1255, 54
S. W. 864 ; Hindman v . Volk , 99 S. W.
660 , 30 R. 818 .
--§ 300. Interested party when a
litigant consents to the appointment of
an interestedparty he will not be heard
to object to his continuance in office.
East Tenn . Tel. Co. v. Watson , 147 Ку .
462, 144 S. W. 375.
§ 301. (1) Sureties of receiver who
is actingunder an agreed order of the
parties are bound for his acts. Claflinv .

(2 ) A receiver appointed to collect

port, in order to get time , executed a

tained to have been in his hands when

§ 302. (1) Powers of receiver. A re-
ceivercan not bring an actioninvolving
the titleto real estate, or submit a con-
troversy concerning real estate to arbi-
tration and bind without their consent
the parties in interest by the judgment

(2) A receiverhas no rightto retain

to whom he is directed to pay money ,
the amount due to him . The possession
of funds collectedby the receiveris the

(3) Power of court to punish one for
contempt for failingto deliverproperty

(4 ) An action can not be maintained

erty under his control. Titheringtonv.

ities of sureties . Farmers ' Bank v .

301 .
(7) Receiver appointed under Code,
or by virtue of sec. 409, Ky . Stat ., may
when so directed by the court maintain
an action. Mitchellv.Chenault,112 Ky .
267 , 23 R. 1544, 65 S. W. 447 .
(8) A receiverappointed under Ky .
Stat., sec. 616, may , without first ob-
taining the consent of the court, pur-
chaseproperty as receiverat a saleun-
der a judgment in his favor when the
purchase -price does not exceed the
amount of the indebtedness . The court
has power to withhold its approval of
what its receiverhas done, but this
power will not be exercised in an arbi-
trary manner or without grounds . Wil-
liams v. Owensboro Sav . Bk . & Tr . Co.,
Receiver, 153 Ky .789, 156 S. W. 899.
(9) Negligence and misconduct . If a
receiveris negligentor guilty of mis-
conduct , the court may refuse him com-pensationpensation. He isliablefornegligentfail-
ure to collectrents; if he mingles the
trust fund with his own he may be
charged interest. Higgins v. Shields, 151
Ky .227, 151 S. W. 391.

§ 311. (1) Trial of two cases to- .
gether , when prejudicial. It was error
to hear together by a jury trial two
casesin each of which the plaintiffwas
a competent witness for the plaintiffin
the other, but not for herself. Benge's
Admr . v . Fouts : Same v. Creech , 163
Ky . 796 , 174 S. W. 510.
§ 312. (1) Judgment of chancellor.
As to the weight and effectof the judg-
ment of the chancellor in a common law
action , see note 1 to sec . 10 .
(2 ) Laws and facts submitted to court,
admission of incompetent evidence not
reversibleerror. Andrews v. Hayden , 88
Ky . 455, 10 R. 1049, 11 S. W. 428, and
see notes to sec . 331 .
(3 ) Motion and grounds for new trial.
In a common law action when the law
and facts are submitted to the court, a
motion and grounds for a new trialare
necessary in order to a review by the
Court of Appeals of any alleged error
committed during the trial; in the ab-
sence of a motion and grounds for a new
trialnothing is before the court except
the inquiryas to whether the pleadings
state any cause of action or defense, and
whether the evidence authorizes the
judgment. Helm v. Coffey, 80 Ky . 176;

§ 314. (1) Continuance on ground
other than absence of evidence . An ac-
tion was continued because the defend-
ant was unable by reason of disease to
attend the trial or to give his deposi-
tion . It was not error to refuse a con-
tinuance asked for the same reason at
the next term . Rose v. Monarch , 150 Ky .
129 , 150 S. W. 56 .
(2) Defendant asked a continuance
because of the absence of an attorney
who was not an attorney of record in
the action ; defendant was himself an
attorney and was represented by compe-
tent attorneys. The continuance was
properly refused. Chapeze v .Hathaway ,
153 Ky . 519, 155 S. W. 1155.
( 3 ) The refusal of a continuance in a
certain case was not an abuse of discre-
tion. Eich v. Mader , 164 Ky . 522, 175
S. W. 997 .
(4 ) A circuit court may render final
judgmentin an action, althoughithas
previouslyenteredan order continuing
the action to another term . Staton v .
Staton , 164 Ky . 688, 176 S. W. 21.

tion did not use diligenceto procure the
taking of the testimony of his
defendant. The absenceof the codefend-
ant at the trial was not ground for a
continuance . Paducah Whar . Co. v.Mer ..
Tr . & Sav . Bk ., 164 Ky . 729, 176 S. W.
190 .
(6 ) A continuance was asked because
of lack of time to prepare the case for
trial; the motion was properly over-
ruled, upon the facts. Preston v . Town .
of Paintsville, 158 Ky . 700, 166 S. W.
188 .
(7 ) When a party goes to trialwith--
out objectionand does not seek a con-
tinuance, objection that the trial was .
premature can not be made . Murphy v ..
Hagan , 163 Ky . 407, 173 S. W. 1146.
(8) When this case was calledfor trial
the lawyer who had prepared the answer
withdrew from the case without any
previousnoticeto his clientof his in-
tended withdrawal . It was error to re-
fuse the client a continuance . Griffin v .
Russell, 161 Ky . 471, 170 S. W. 1192.
(9) The fact that the petitionwas not
signed at the time the term began was
no ground for a continuance ; it would
be ground for reasonable opportunity to
be given the defendant to tender an an-
swer . Baird v . Prewitt , 158 Ky . 793,
166 S. W. 771 .

term. "The case having been regularly

accommodation of parties, witnesses and .

§ 315 [345] [Postponementof trial- affidavitfor and effectof.] A
motion topostponea trialon accountof theabsenceof evidencecan be
made onlyupon affidavitshowingthematerialityof theevidenceexpected
tobe obtained,and thatdue diligencehasbeenusedtoobtainit; and,ifit
be for an absent witness, the affidavitmust show what factsthe affiant
believesthewitnesswillprove,and notmerelytheeffectof such factsin
evidence; and thatthe affiantbelievesthem to be true. If,thereupon,the
adversepartywillconsentthat,on thetrial,theaffidavitshallbe readas
thedepositionof the absentwitness,thetrialshallnot be postponedon
accountof hisabsence. (When witnessliablefor costof continuance,sec.
536. How personalattendanceof witnesssecured,sec.556. See Ky .Stat.,
secs.887 ,889,1734.)

attorneys, defendantshad no right to
assume , merely because the trialcourt
dischargedthe common law jury for the
term , that there would be no further
trialsduring that term. As therewere
to be no further trialsuntil the follow-
ing Thursday, itwas not only the right
but the duty of the court to discharge

wealth the unnecessary expense of itsattendance ." Gahren v. Parkersburg

§ 315. (1) Affidavitfor continuance.
The refusal to grant a continuance isnot
an abuse of discretion when the state-
ment of what the absent witness will
prove is admitted as evidence. Hutton
v . First Nat . Bank , 20 R. 225, 45 S. W.
668 ; M. & L. R. R. Co. v . Herrick, 13
Bush 122 .
(2) Affidavit for continuance may be
made by a person not a party to action
if he is informed concerning and can
state necessary grounds for continuance.
Hardesty v. Com ., 88 Ky . 537, 11 R. 43,
11 S. W. 589 .
(3) A party is not entitled to a con-
tinuance on account of the absence of a
witness who can not be required to at-
tend unless he shows that he has paid
or tendered the witness fees, or that the
witness has waived the payment . Thur-
man v . Virgin, 18 B. M. 785. See secs.
534 , 536 of this Code as to attendance
of witnesses; and Ky . Stat., sec. 1734,
as to fees .
(4 ) The defendant was not entitled to
a continuance on account of an absent
witness by whom he could prove that a
witnesswho would testifyforplaintiff
was not present when the assault com-
plained of in the petitionoccurred, be-
cause he did not set out in his affidavit
what the plaintiffexpectedto prove by
his witness . Slater v . Sherman , 5 Bush
206 .
(5 ) The application for a continuance
is addressed to the sound discretion of
the court , and unless this discretion has
been abused the action of the court will
not be disturbed, nor is the court bound
to accept the statements in the affidavit
as true, but may hear other affidavits
contradicting it when the judge con-
sidersthisproper under allthe circum-
stances of the case; but when the sole
ground for a continuance is the absence
of material witnesses it seems that coun-
ter affidavitsare not admissible, and
that the continuance must be granted
unless adverse party consents that affi-
davit may be read as a deposition. Mc-
Clung v.Igleheart,17 R. 913,33 S.W. 80.
(6 ) A party to a suit who is prevented
from attendingcourtby the seriousill-
ness of a near relative is entitled to a
continuance. Langdon Creasy Co. v .
Rouse , 24 R. 2095, 72 S. W. 1113.
(6a ) Certain grounds for a continuance
were insufficient. May v.May , 150 Ky .
522 , 150 S. W. 685 .
(6b ) An application for a continuance
was accompanied by the affidavit of
counsel that the just and proper effectof
the evidence of the absent witnesses
could not in a reasonable degree be had
without a personalexaminationincourt.
The circuit court refused a continuance .
Upon the whole of the record itwas held
that the substantialrightsof the party
asking the continuancewere prejudiced.
Mad .,H. & E. R. Co. v.Allen, 152Ky .
706 , 154 S. W. 5 .
(6c) A continuance was asked on aс-
241

§ 315 ] [Title 1XTrial in General .
count of the absence of a witness who
was out of the jurisdictionof the court
and who was an employe of defendant
and who was preventedby illnessfrom
attending; itwas errornot to permit an
affidavit of what he would state to be
read as hisdeposition. Ind.LifeIns.Co.
of Am . v. Williamson, 152 Ky . 818, 154
S. W. 409 .
(6d ) An affidavitfor a continuance was
admitted to be read as a deposition for
defendant; plaintiff'scounselreferredto
it in the argument as the affidavitof op-
posing counsel. This was improper and
the court should have instructed the
jury verballythat the affidavitshould
be treatedas the depositionof the ab-
sent witnesses and would have the same
force and effect as if they personally
made the statements contained in it.
Mad ., H. & E. R. Co. v.Allen, 152 Ky .
706 , 154 S. W. 5 .
(6e) A circuitcourt's refusal to grant
a continuance on account of the absence
of witnesseswas not error. C. & O. Ry ,
Co. v.Stapleton, 154 Ky . 351, 157 S. W.
702 .
(6f) Due diligence was shown in this
case; itwas not necessarythat the ad-
dress of the witness be stated in the
subpoena. LouisvilleRy . Co. v. Vessels'
Admx ., 159 Ky . 664, 167 S. W. 924.
(6g ) It is not error to refuse a con-
tinuance on affidavitfiled if the judge
givesthe party applyingforthe continu-
ance an opportunity to procure the at-
tendance of the absent witness and the
party declinesto take advantage of the
offer. Whitaker v. First Nat . Bank , 163
Ky .623, 174 S. W. 47 .
(6h ) An affidavitwhich failedto show
that a subpoena was issued for the ab-
sent witness and placed in the hands of
the sheriff was insufficient to sustain a
motion for a continuance . North River
Ins. Co. v. Dyche , 163 Ky . 271, 173 S.
W. 784 .
(6i) An affidavit as to what an absent
witness would prove was admitted as
evidence . It was not error to refuse a
continuance . Bon Jellico Coal Co. v .
Murphy , 161 Ky . 450, 171 S. W. 160.
(6j) It was not error to refuse to per-
mit a corporation to read as a deposition
an affidavitas to what itsgeneral agent
would testifyin the absenceof diligence
on the part of the company to procure
the presence of such witness . Masonic
Life Assn .v .Robinson , 156 Ky . 371, 160
S. W. 1078 .
(6k ) It is not an abuse of discre-
tion to deny a continuance because of
the absence of a witness,where the party
seeking to produce him makes affidavitas
to the facts he will testify to, which
affidavitisread to the jury as the wit-
ness's testimony . Bon Jellico Coal Co.
v .Murphy , 161 Ky .450 , 171 S. W. 160.
(61) Under this section and sec. 556 ,
the postponement of a trialbecause of
the absence of a witness is within the
sound discretion of the trial court . Crane
v . Hall, 165 Ky . 827, 178 S. W. 1096.
(6m ) A request for postponement of
the trial in order to procure the per-
sonal attendance of a witness was prop-
erly denied,where an affidavitas to what
the witness would testify to was read
as thewitness'deposition. Crane v.Hall,
165 Ky . 827, 178 S. W. 1096.
(6n ) Refusal to grant a continuance
because of the absence of witnesses was
held not error in the absence of the re-
quired showing by affidavit. Bolar v.
Browning , 168 Ky . 273, 181 S. W. 1109.
(7 ) Affidavit- amendment of, is not
permitted aftercourt has expressedan
opinion, so the court held in Singleton v .
Carr , 1 Bibb 554 ; Smalley v . Anderson ,
4 Mon. 367 ; but see Com . v . Hourigan ,
89 Ky . 305, 11 R. 509 , 12 S. W. 550 ;
where it is held that in criminal cases,
after a motion for a continuance is over-
ruled, court may allow amended affidavit
to be filed and motion renewed .
(8 ) Amendment of pleading. Grounds
of continuance , see sec. 136 ; and see
Watts v. McKenney , 1 Mar. 561 ; Ewing
v . Beauchamp , 4 Bibb 496 ; Eldridge v .
Duncan , 1 B. M. 101, holding that an
amendment entitlingparty to continu-
ance must be material, and not a mere
verbal one .
(8a ) Counter affidavit. The court does
not decide whether a counter affidavit is
permissibleto be read in opposing an
application for a continuance. Ford v .
House -Hasson Hard .Co .; Same v . Jellico
Gro . Co., 159 Ky . 669, 169 S. W. 879.
(8b) Depositionof adverseparty,fail-
ure to get. The fact that the defendant
did not succeed in getting the deposi-
tion of the plaintiffas if under cross-
examination prior to the trial, is not of
itselfgrounds for a continuance. Cumb .
Tele . & Tele . Co. v . Laird, 161 Ky . 800,
171 S. W. 386 .
(8c) Inability of defendant to obtain
the testimony of plaintiffby deposition
as if under cross -examination before the
trial was not ground for continuance,
where he was not taken by surprise, and
there was nothing in the testimony which
he was not in every way ready to meet .
Cumb .Tel.& Tel. Co. v. Laird, 161 Ky .
800,171 S. W. 386.

to take a depositionof the adverseparty,
as if under cross-examination is held to
the same amount of diligencethat would

witnessnota partyto theaction, (Sub-
sec. 8.) Home Ins.Co. of N. Y. v . Crow-
der, 164 Ky . 792, 176 S. W. 344.

of witnesses,and permittingdefendant's

sitions . Connecticut Fire Ins . Co. v .Har-
din , 168 Ky . 377, 182 S. W. 204.

continuance is reversible. McCarty v .

(10) See notes to sec. 189, Criminal

(11) Continuance . In the following
cases will be found statements of facts
illustratingwhen the affidavitfor a con-
tinuance showed diligence and when it

1047, 28 R. 20 ; Troendale Coal Co. v.

(12) Physician - continuance will not
be granted on account of absence of, as
his deposition should be taken- unless
he is absent when an order for his at-
tendance was made . Holzhauer v.Sheeny ,
104 S. W. 1034, 31 R. 1238, 127 Ky . 28 .
(12a) Where a physicianorderedto ap-
pear and testifyin a case did not do so
because of the illnessof a patient, the

plication. Bolar v. Browning, 168 Ky .

(13) Examination of witness after
readingaffidavit- when a witnesscomes
into court after his affidavit has been
read he may be cross-examined . Elkins
v. New LivingstonCo., 115 S. W. 203.

JURIES .

Selectionof Jury.
§ 2252. Twelve persons constitutein circuitcourt- six in inferiorcourts.
A petitjury inthecircuitcourtshallconsistof twelvepersons,and in alltrials
held in courtsinferiorto the circuitcourt, or by any county,police, or city
judge, or justiceof the peace, a jury shallconsistof six persons; but the
partiesto any actionor prosecution, exceptfor felony,may agreeto a trialby
a lessnumber of persons than is provided for in this section. (Ky . Stat.,
sec . 2252.)
§ 2264. Panel to be called- how deficiencysupplied. On the day the jury
shallbe summoned to attendcourt,thepanelshallbe called,and the names of
244

Title IX ] [§ 316Trial by Jury .
such as attend, and are not excused by the court, shallbe enteredof record;
and any deficiencyinthepanelshallbe suppliedas directedin thischapter.
(Id.,sec.2264.)
§ 2265. Names to be placedin box and jury drawn from . The clerkshall
writethe names of thejurorsenteredof recordon separateslipsof paper,as
near the same sizeand appearanceas may be,and when a jury iswanted for
the trialof a case the same shallbe drawn from a box afterthe papers shall
have been depositedthereinand wellmixed. The clerkshallprovideand keep
forthatpurposea suitablebox with a slidinglid. (Id.,sec.2265.)
§ 2267. Civilcases- how jury selected- deficiencysupplied. In allcivil
casesof jury trialthe clerkshalldraw from the box the names of eighteen
of the jury,and writethem , as drawn ,on two slipsof paper,and deliverone
toeachparty, from whichplaintiffand defendantmay eachstrikethreeand
return the listto the clerk, who shallcallthe firsttwelve names not erased
and swear them as a jury to try the case. But before eitherparty shallbe
requiredto strike, those on the listmay be challengedfor cause, and others
drawn and placedon the listin the placeof as many as may be setasidefor
cause . In allcaseswhere the jury shallbe completed,the names of persons
not on thejury shallbe returnedto the box , from which anotherjury may be
drawn ,and whenever,from any cause,itmay be necessarytomake up a jury,if
therebe not enough of thestandingjury remainingor in attendance, the court
shall order for the occasion a sufficientnumber to be summoned , as directed
in thischapter, to make up a panelas aforesaid. If a standingjuror, forany
cause, shallbe dischargedor excused from the jury, his placeshallbe sup-
pliedas directedin thischapter. (Id.,sec.2267.)
§ 2258. Challenges- number of peremptory- for cause. Each partylitigant
in civilactionsshallhave the rightof peremptory challengeto threejurors,
and the rightto challengeas now allowedby law. (See CriminalCode, secs.
197-216,for challengesin criminalcases.)
§2259 . Oath tojury. The following,in substance,shallbe the oath tobe
administeredto jurorsin a civilsuit: "You do swear thatyou willwell and
trulytry the issuejoined between A B , plaintiff, and CD , defendant (or if
therebe no issue, 'try the case'), and a true verdictgive, accordingto evi-
dence,unlessdismissedby the courtor withdrawn by the parties."
$ 2254. Juriesde medietatelinguae. Juriesde medietatelinguaemay be
directed by the court.
§ 2255. Juror may testify. Jurors knowing any fact materialto the issue
shalldisclosethe same in open court,upon oath, as evidence.
§ 2268. Three -fourths of jury may make verdict. That in alltrialsof civil
actionsin the circuitcourts,three-fourthsor more of the jurors concurring
may returna verdict,which shallhave the same forceand effectas ifrendered
by the entirepanel. But where a verdictisrendered by a lessnumber than
thewhole jury, itshallbe signedby allthe jurorswho agree to it. (Id., sec.
2268. )

larityor misconduct of jury.

tion a juror for cause . Shellman v .

INSTRUCTIONS .
(7) Abstract principles of law . It is
error to give instructions which contain
mere abstract principles of law ; as to
say to the jury "that where there is a
conflict in the testimony of witnesses , the
one side being of an affirmativeand the
other of a negative character , the affirm-
ative character of testimony is preferred
and is entitledto the greater weight by
the jury in making up their verdict."
Lou . Chem . Works v. Com ., 8 Bush 179.
(7a ) An instruction should not be
247

§ 317] [Title IXTrialby Jury- Conduct Of.
given unless there is evidence on which
to base it. McCoy v. Com ., 149 Ky . 447,
149 S. W. 903 ; Worley v . McCurd , 153
Ky . 104,154 S. W. 909.
(7b ) An instruction should not be
given on an issuenot presentedby the
pleadings. Forrest v. Winter, 149 Ky .
759, 149 S. W. 981.
(7c) Neither party isentitledto have
the jury instructedon a subjectnot put
in issue by the pleadings nor upon an
issueunsupportedby evidence. Edge v.
Ott , 151 Ky . 672, 152 S. W. 764.
(7d ) It was not error to refuse an in-
struction as to plaintiff'saggravation of
damages by her own negligencein the
absence of a pleading presenting that
issue. Cumberland R. R. Co. v. Baird ,
156 Ky . 255, 160 S. W. 919.
(7e ) Instructions should be predicated
upon the issues made by the pleadings
and the facts appertaining to such is-
sues, furnished by competent evidence
introducedon the trial; and in writing
them brevity and clearnessof expression
should be used by the court, in order
that theirmeaning may be readilyun-
derstood by the jury. The refusalby
the trialcourt of an instructionpredi-
cated upon an issue not made by thepleadingsis not error. Moore v. Dam-
ron, 157 Ky . 799, 164 S. W. 103.
(7f) The trial court should not in-
struct upon an issue not made by the
pleadings. Turner & Frazer v . Frazier,
157 Ky . 388 , 163 S. W. 245 .
(7g) It isan elementaryruleof prac-
ticethat instructionsto the jury should
be confinedto the issuespresentedby
the pleadings and the evidence. Bowlin
v .Archer , 157 Ky . 540, 163 S. W. 477.
(7h ) It is improper to give an instruc-
tion announcing a naked legalproposi-
tion, however correctit may be,unless
it bears upon and is connected with the
issues involved; and , unless, further,
there has been received some competent
evidenceto which the jury may apply it.
Bowlin v. Archer, 157 Ky . 540, 163 S.
W. 477 .
(7i) Where an arresting officerwas
sued for conspiringto bring about the
killingof hisprisoner, the petitionrely-
ing solelyupon the allegedconspiracy,it
was error for the trial court to instruct
the jury to findfor the plaintiff, ifthey
believed from the evidence that the offi-
cerhad failedto protecthisprisonerfrom
assault. Bowlin v.Archer, 157 Ky . 540,
163 S. W. 477 .
(7j) Adverse possession. For a pos-
sessionto ripeninto a titleby time it
must be actualopen,notorious, continu-
ous and peaceable. LeMoyne v.Neal,158
Ky . 316, 164 S. W. 964.(7k) In an instruction the circuit
court used the words " actual and adverse
possession." No request was made for
an instructiondefining those words . The
failure to define them was not error .
LeMoyne v . Meadors , 156 Ky . 832, 162
S. W. 526.
(71) A certain instruction as to the
kind of adverse possession which will
give titleto land was improper. Terry
v. Loudermilk, 158 Ky . 353, 164 S. W.
959 .
(8 ) After argument begun or after
case has been submitted to jury addi-
tionalinstructionsmay be given or an
erroneous instruction corrected . Sears
v . L. & N. R. R., 22 R. 152, 56 S. W.
725 .
(Sa) It is the proper practiceto in-
structthe jury before the argument is
made , but it is not necessarilypreju-
dicialerrorto instructthe jury during
the argument or even after the argu-
ment is closed. When , however, an in-
structionisgiven afterthe argument is
closed, the court should always permit
the attorneys,ifthey desireso to do,to
re-argue the case, at least to the extent
that the issuesare affectedby the be-
lated instruction. First Nat . Bank v .
Stephens & Steely, 157 Ky . 663, 163 S.
W. 1097 .
(8b ) A jury after a case had been sub-
mitted came into court and asked fur-
ther instructions. The court gave an
instructionon the point inquired of,
which was correct. This was not ground
for reversal. L. & E. Ry . Co. v. Boat-
right,164 Ky .374, 175 S. W. 648.
(8c) Assault . In an action for as-
sault and battery among other things
provocation was pleaded. The Court of
Appeal directed certain instructions to
be given upon the whole case, including
the effectof provocationas mitigation.
LouisvilleRy . Co. v. Frick, 158 Ky . 450,
165 S. W. 649 .
(8d) The giving of an erroneous in-
structionas to punitivedamages in an
actionfor assaultand battery willnot
be ground for reversalif only compen-
satory damages were given . Hunt v .
Burlington, 165 Ky . 384, 176 S. W. 1156.
(9 ) Assuming certain facts. Instruc-
tions which assume the existence of cer-
tain facts that the jury have a right to
determine , should not be given. Murphy
v . May , 9 Bush 33 ; but an uncontra-
dicted fact may be assumed in an in-
struction. L. & N. R. R. v. Earl,94 Ky .
368, 15 R. 184, 22 S. W. 607; and the

every inference that is fairly deducible

ant,in moving fora peremptory instruc-

the evidence has been heard upon both

nist, assuming that evidenceand every

ings." United Shakers v. Underwood , 11

therewas some evidenceauthorizingthe

§ 318. (1) View of premises. Court
may permit jury to view placewhere an
accident occurred, even after they have
retired to consider their verdict, when
the jury so request. L.,N. A. & C. R. R.

93 Ky . 449 , 14 R. 455 , 20 S. W. 392 ;

versiblethat jury viewed premisesafter
dark , it appearing that they could see

eral Electric Co., 107 Ky . 485, 21 R.

§ 326 [336] [Verdictsdescribedand defined.] The verdictof a jury
isgeneralor separate-general[or special.*]
1. [Generalverdict.] A generalverdictisthatby which thejurypro-
nouncesgenerally,upon alltheissues,fortheplaintiffor forthedefendant.
2. [Separate-generalverdict.] A separate-generalverdictisthe find-
ing,upon any of theissues,infavorof theplaintiffor of thedefendant.
3. [Specialverdict.] A specialverdictisthefindingof factsby a jury,
as shown intheiranswers to questionssubmittedto them in writing.*
*Words in brackets repealed. See editorialnote to section 317.
Cross reference: See Newman's Pl.and Pr .(3d Ed .), sec.396d ; Forms Nos .397,399,
374-406 , 406a .
GENERAL VERDICT .
§ 326. (1) Verdict- defects in plead-
ing cured by . Where there is any defect,
imperfectionoromissioninanypleading,
whether in substance or form, which
would have been fatalon demurrer,yet
ifthe issuejoinedbe such as necessarily
requiredon thetrialproofofthefacts,
so defectivelystated or omitted, and
without which it is not presumed that
eitherthe judge would directthe jury
to give, or the jury would have given,
the verdict, such defect, imperfectionor
omissioniscuredby the verdict. Drake
v. Semonin, 82 Ky . 291, 6 R. 341; L. &
P. Canal Co.v.Murphy ,9 Bush 522; and
note 12 , sec. 95 .
(2) Where there is a total omission to
state a cause of action, or some fact es-
sentialto the cause of actionhas been
wholly omitted , the verdict will not cure
the defect. Bogenschutz v . Smith , 84
Ky . 330, 8 R. 376, 1 S. W. 578 , 82 Ky .
291, 6 R. 341. See further, note 12 to
sec . 95 .
(3) Verdict is not a judgment - the
court must render judgment upon it;
and the court can not refuse to render
judgment upon the verdictupon proper
notice and motion made after the expira-
tion of the time to move for a new trial.
Fuqua v . Mullen , 13 Bush 467.
(4) Verdict - how construed. When a
questionbetweenA and B as to theOwnershipownershipofa horseissubmittedtoa
jury,and theverdictisthatthepropertywas owned by B , it willbe regardedas
meaningthatB was theownerat thetime of the levy. Schweim v . Sims , 2
Met . 209; see Noel v . Hudson , 13 B. М.
205 , and notes to sec. 325 .
(4a ) A verdict for plaintiffdid not
state against which of two defendants it
was rendered . It was held to be a ver-
dict against both . Atkinson v .Lawrence ,
161 Ky . 166, 170 S. W. 528.
(4b) A findingby a jury in these
words: "We ,nine of the jury,agree for
each party to pay theircost," was void
and no verdict. The failureof a litigant
to objectto such a verdictat the timeof its rendition waives nothing . The
objection is sufficientif made in the
grounds for new trial. Davis v. Stone ,
172 Ky . 696, 189 S. W. 937.
(5 ) Verdict in absence of proof. In an
action for the value of work and labor,
the jury have a right, in the absence of
all proof of the value of such labor, to
find a verdict for the price of the work

SEPARATE -GENERAL VERDICT
DEFINED .
"The meaning of the expression'sep-
arate -general verdict' is that the verdict
is separate as to the particular issue as
distinguishedfrom any otherissuein the
case and generalas to the particular
issue. That isitwas intendedto apply
in cases where there is more than one
issue. For instance, an action upon an
alleged contract when the issues pre-
sented are- first, was the contractpro-
cured by fraud or duress ? And , second,
ifitwas so procured,and thereforevoid-
able, was it subsequently ratifiedby
the defandant after a fullknowledge of
the fraud and the removal of the duress ?
If, in such case, the jury should finda
general verdictfor the defendant, the
court could not determine whether it
was based upon the ground that the evi-
dence authorized the conclusion that
there was fraud or duress sufficient to
invalidate the contract, or upon the
ground that there was not evidence
requisiteto establisha ratification. The
evidence might preponderate in favor of
the conclusion that there was fraud or
duress sufficient to invalidate the con-
tract , but at the same time the evidence
upon the issue of ratificationmight be
suchthatthepreponderancein favorof
a ratificationwould be so overwhelming
that the court should grant a new trial.
If a separate-general verdict is asked for,
the court shall grant it, and with it
shall require the jury to return a general
verdict. In such cases it is not only
proper, but necessary, that the jury
should be instructedby the court as to
the law applicable to the particular is-
sues to be found by the jury as fully
as if there was only one issue in the
case and a jury were requiredto finda
generalverdictthereon." Witty v.C. & .
O. R. R. Co.,83 Ky .21, 6 R. 321.
SPECIAL VERDICT .
(1) Special findings permissible. In
Tinsleyv. Ross, 15 R. 44, 22 S. W. 313,
specialfindingswere submitted to the
jury,and on theirfindingsthe courtren-
dered judgment. In answer to the ob-
jectionthatthe law permittingspecial
findingshad been repealed, and it was
thereforeerror to submit them ,the court
held the objectionnot available, as the
judgment was correct. The provisionsof
the Code permittingspecialverdictshave
been repealed. See amendment to sec.
327; but as occasionally,as in thiscase
or by agreement,specialfindingsmay be
submitted , I have inserted cases follow-
ing.-Ed.

§ 333 [363] [Exception- how taken- objectionwhen necessary.]
1. [Exceptiondefined.] An exceptionisan objectiontakentoa decision
of the courtupon a matter of law.
§ 333. (1) Amendment . An exception
taken to the filingof an amendment that
is allowed to be filedwithout objection
is not available; objectionshould have
been made to the filing. Helburn v.
Mofford ,7 Bush 169.
(2) Demurrer. An errorin overruling
a demurrer must be exceptedto at the
time. Beaven v. Phillips, 83 Ky . 88, 7
R. 9 .
(3) Depositions. When an exception
goes to the whole of a deposition. and
some of it is competent, it should be
overruled. L. & N. R. R. Co. v . Graves ,
78 Ky . 74; see further,sec. 586.
(4 ) When depositions are excepted to,
it must appear that the exceptionwas
acted on, or it will not avail. Corn v .
Sims , 3 Met . 391 ; Lewis v . Wright , 3
Bush 311; Walrath v.Viley,1 Bush 266.
(5) Evidence. A party must objectto
the introduction of incompetent evi-
dence and , if it is allowed over his ob-
jection,must except to it at the time , or
the error is waived . Crabb v . Larkin , 9
Bush 154; Helburn v . Mofford , 7 Bush
169; Russell v. Marks , 3 Met . 37 ; L. &
N. R. R. Co. v. Graves, 78 Ky . 74, 1
Met . 450 ; where an objection was en-
tered to the admission of evidence, and
at the close of the evidence the bill of
exceptions stated "to all of which the
defendant excepted," held sufficientex-
ception. L. & N. R. R. v . Ritter, 85 Ky .
368,9 R. 22, 3 S. W. 591.
(5a ) An error in excluding testimony
offeredon the jury trialcan not be con-
sideredon appeal unless the rulingof
the court was excepted to ; an avowal
as to what the witness would state is
not sufficientto bring up the question.
Posey v. Cont.Ins.Co.,149 Ky .631,149
S. W. 984 .
(6) Final orders. Final judgments,
whether upon issuesof law or fact,need
not be objectedor exceptedto. Where
a demurrer to an answer is sustained
and judgment renderedagainst the de-
fendant,or where a peremptory instruc-
tion isgiven, no exceptionisnecessary.
Coffman v . Wilson , 2 Met . 542 ; Cray-
craft v. Duncan , 6 R. 651; Loving v.
Warren Co., 14 Bush 316; Meacham v.
L. N. R., 20 R. 112, 45 S. W. 363. Nor
is it necessary to except to a void order.
Smith v. Blakeman , 8 Bush 476 .
(7 ) Instructions offeredby adverse
party must be objectedto at time and
if objection is overruled and instruction
given an exception must be taken at
time; if this is not done any error in
giving instruction will be waived ; there
must be both an objection and an ex-
ception; neither of itselfis sufficient. L.
& N. R. R. Co. v. Graves , 78 Ky . 74 ; Cox
v. Winston, 3 Met.577; Reed v. Com .,7 Bush 641 ; Garrot v . Ratliff, 83 Ky .
384 , 7 Ky . 463 ; L. & N. R. Co. v . Bo-
cock, 107 Ky . 223, 21 R. 896, 51 S. W.
580; 53 S. W. 262 ; 14 Bush 316 ; 1 Duv .
387; 81 Ky . 51, 4 R. 596 ; but if the in-
struction is given by the court on its
own motion, no objectionis necessary;
an exception is sufficient; and so if the
court instructsthe jury by direction of
both parties. L. S. R. R. v. Hooe , 18 R.
521, 35 S. W. 266 ; 38 S. W. 131.
(8 ) The circuit court can not adopt
any rule of practice in contravention of
the provisionsof the Code requiringex-

of the adverseparty.

ceptions and objections to be taken and
made as therein directed. Kennedy v .
Cunningham , 2 Met . 538; Letton v.
Young , 2 Met . 558 ; I. C. R. R. v. Glass-
cock,24 R. 1937,72 S. W. 769.
(9 ) Neither an objection nor excep-
tion is necessary to enable a party to
complain of a peremptory instruction.
Loving v. Warren Co., 14 Bush 316 ;
Meacham v . L. & N. R. R., 20 R. 112, 45
S. W. 363 ; 2 Met . 542 .
(9a) Where no objection was offered
to an instruction at the time it was
given, complaint of it upon appeal can
not be considered. Chesapeake & Ohio
Ry .Co. v. Kelley,157 Ky . 724, 163 S. W.
1088 .
(10) Waiver of error. Neither party
can rely for reversal upon an erroneous
decision made at the instance of the ad-
verse party unless he objected to the
motion or offer of the adverse party at
the time itwas made ,and then excepted
to the decision; but where the court acts
upon its own motion an exception alone
is sufficient. Branson v. Com ., 92 Ky .
330, 13 R. 614, 17 S. W. 1019.
(11) Objection and exception both nec-
essary when decisionis made at instance
of adverseparty. Moss Tie Co. v.Myers ,
116 S. W. 255; Smalling v. Shaw , 144
Ky . 458 ,139 S. W. 779.
(lla) The provision that objections
and exceptions must be taken by a com-
plainingparty does not apply so as to
bind the court in which the order is
made ; a party can not avail himself of
erroneous rulingsin such cases in the
Court of Appeals without complying
with those provisions in the circuit
court . First Nat . Bk . of Louisville v .
Bickel, 154 Ky . 11, 156 S. W. 856.
(11b) The factthat appellee'scounsel
used certainlanguage will not be con-
sideredon appealunlessitbe authenti-
cated by bill of exceptions; an affidavit
as to the language used is insufficient.
So. Ry . Co. of Ky . v . Thacker's Admr .,
156 Ky . 483 , 161 S. W. 236.
(1lc) In the absence of a bill of evi-
dence the court was unable to say
whether a certainargument by the at-
torney of the successful party was im-
proper . Fish v . Welch's Admr ., 156 Ky .
541, 161 S. W. 512.
(11d) The overrulingof a motion to
dismiss and remand a case to the county
court can not be assigned as error, where
the defendant did not except to the ruling
at the time it was made . Delk v . Com .
166 Ky . 39, 178 S. W. 1129.
(12) See further notes to secs. 737-
742.
§ 334. (1) Bill can not be filedin va-
cation. A bill of exceptions can not be
filedin vacation in the clerk'soffice, nor
265

§ 334 ] [Title IXTrial- Exceptions .
at any time except during term time.
Nor can thisrulebe changed by an order
of the court; and it is questionableif
by agreement of parties,enteredof rec-
ord, a bill filedin vacation can be con-
sidered. Allard v . Smith , 2 Met . 297 ;
Freeman v.Brenham , 17 B. M. 603; and
see Vandever v.Griffith,2 Met .425 ; Cor-
leyv.Evans ,4 Bush 409; Com .v.Lewis,
19 R. 170, 39 S. W. 438; Craftv. Allen,
21 R. 243 ,51 S. W. 169; Adkins v . Com .,
102 Ky . 94, 19 R. 1300,42 S. W. 834; 44
S. W. 132 ; Knecht v . Louisville Home
Tel.Co., 121 Ky .492,28 R. 456, 89 S. W.
508; Dupoyster v. Fort Jeff. Imp . Co.,
28 R. 504, 89 S. W. 509.
(la) A billof exceptionscan not be
filedin vacation. Beckett v . Com ., 154
Ky .209 , 157 S. W. 12.
(2) Bill filedby agreed order at the
term at which judgment was rendered
an order was made statingthat billof
exceptions and evidence was filed, ap-
proved and made part of record, but as
a matter of fact the bill was not filed
or approved untilnext term, the court
held that the bill could not be consid-
ered. I.C. R. R. v.Glasscock,24 R. 1937,
72 S. W. 769 ; N. N. & Μ . V. R. R. Co. v .
Stavig, 98 Ky . 533,17 R. 1113, 33 S. W.
620 .
(3) Death of party before bill filed.
The death of the appelleebefore the time
allowed for filingthe bill of exceptions
will not prejudicethe appellantif his
billbe tendered within the time allowed ;
althoughtheremay have been no revivor.
Hayden v. Ortkeiss, 83 Ky . 396,7 R. 399.
(4 ) Jefferson Circuit Court . The Jef-
ferson Circuit Court has the same power
over itsjudgments for sixtydays that
the circuitscourts have during the term .
If under any circumstances the court is
authorized to extend the time to file a
bill to a day beyond a period of sixty
days from the judgment, it can not be
filedafter the day so fixed if no notice is
taken of the matter on that day . Bailey
v.Villier,6 Bush 27. If time be extended
by that court beyond sixty days from
date of judgment, and the billis filed
within the time allowed without objec-
tion, the error, if any, willbe regarded
as waived . Downing v . Bacon , 7 Bush
680; see further, Johnson v . Stivers, 95
Ky .128,15 R. 477,23 S. W. 957; holding
that not more than one hundred and
twenty days can be allowed afterjudg-
ment to filebill. The courtcan not by
an orderfixa lesstime than sixtydays
from judgment in which to filebill, and
a bill will be considered if it is filed
within the sixty days. Connelly v.
Adams , 19 R. 1084, 42 S. W. 1133 ; Metz
v . L. & N. R. R., 102 Ky .225, 19 R. 1200,
43 S. W. 215 ; but if time is not extended
withinthe sixtydays the billfiledafter
that time will not be considered. City
v.Wilson ,23 R. 1722,66 S. W. 8 ; Knecht
v . L. Home Tel. Co., 28 R. 456, 121 Ky .
492 , 89 S. W. 508 ; Zehe v .City of Louis-
ville, 123 Ky . 621, 96 S. W. 918, 29 R.
1106; LouisvilleRy . Co. v. Wellington,
137 Ky .719,128 S. W. 1077.
(5 ) Nunc pro tunc order that "the
partiescame by theirattorneys, and it
appearing at the trial of this cause at
the last term of this court the court
allowed defendants time to prepare a bill
of exceptions," and thereupon "the bill
was filed as part of the record," was
invalid, as no time had been given at a
former term to file the bill. Vandever v .
Griffith.2 Met .425 ; Blue Grass Traction
Co. v.Crossdale, 143 Ky . 196, 136 S. W.
204 ; but where an indorsement on bill
showed that it had been filed on a cer-
tainday ,althoughno orderfilingitwas
made , it was proper afterward to enter
a nunc pro tune order filingbill as of
day endorsement was made . Washing-
ton Life Ins.Co. v. Menefee , 107 Ky . 244,
21 R. 916, 53 S. W. 260; smalling v.
Shaw , 144 Ky . 458, 139 S. W. 779.
(6 ) Pendency of motion for new trial.
The time for filinga billof exceptions
is reckoned from the overrulingof a
motion for a new trial, and not from
the renditionof the judgment on the
verdict, and the court has no power
pending the motion for a new trial to
prescribe the time within which the bill
of exceptions shallbe filed. 10 Bush 447.
(6a ) A motion for a new trialin a civil
case suspends the judgment, and the
cause may be continued and passed upon
a subsequent term . Stearn's Coal &
Lumber Co. v.Com., 163 Ky .837, 174 S.
W. 771 .
(7) Special judge . When a special
judge,who has trieda case,extendstime
forfilingbillto a day in next term,and
is not present on that day , the party
who then tenders billin open court is en-
titled to have offer to file continued to
succeeding term, and then have bill
signedby specialjudge. McFarland v.
Burton, 89 Ky.Ky .294,11 R. 499,12 S.W.
336; Phillipsv.BeattyvilleM. & T. Co.,
100 S. W. 244, 30 R. 1103 ; the special
judge who triescase can sign billat a
subsequentterm. Murray v. East End
Imp . Co.,22 R. 1477,60 S. W. 648; and
the billmust be signedby special, not
regularjudge,L. S. R. Co. v.Lewis, 101
Ky . 296 , 19 R. 570, 41 S. W. 3 .
266

Title IX ] [§ 334Trial- Exceptions .
(8 ) Time to filebill- When time is
given it must be to a certainday . 8
Bush 476 ; Johnson v.Stivers,95 Ky . 128,
15 R. 477,23 S. W. 957; and when time
isgiven untila day in the next term to
filea billan order may be made on the
day specifiedextending the time to an-
other day certain in the term ; and for
sufficientreason,furtherextensionsmaybe given from one day certainto an-
other day certainduring the term . Smith
v.Blakeman ,8 Bush 476.
(9) When time is given to filebillof
exceptions, it may be filedduring that
term and before the day specified. New-
port News R. R. v. Thomas ,96 Ky . 613,
16 R. 706 ,29 S. W. 437 ; L. & N. R. R. v .
Turner , 81 Ky . 489, 5 R. 647 ; Metz v. L.
& N. R. R.,102 Ky .225,19 R. 1200,43 S.W. 215. When a billis tendered in court
and taken forconsiderationby the judge,
it is to be regarded as filedon the day
it is tendered,and may be filedby him
on a subsequentday , but billmust be
tendered on or before day specified.
Toner v . South Cov . Ry . Co., 109 Ky .
41 , 22 R. 564, 58 S. W. 439 ; 81 Ky . 489,
5 R. 647; Meaux v. Meaux ; White v.
Allen , 10 R. 1025, 11 S. W. 364 ; and the
orders should state the facts . Nance v .
Newport News R. R.,13 R. 554,17 S. W.
570 .
(10) A bill of exceptions filed at a
term later than the next succeeding term
after judgment is rendered will not be
considered by the Court of Appeals , al-
though it is filedpursuant to an order of
court. Combs v. Combs , 19 R. 439 ,41 S.
W. 7 ; Johnson v . Stivers, 95 Ky . 128, 15
R. 477, 23 S. W. 957 ; Knecht v . Lou .
Home Tel . Co., 28 R. 456, 121 Ky . 492 ,
89 S. W. 508 ; Dupoyster v . Fort Jeff.
Imp .Co.,28 R. 504,89 S. W. 509; Wool-
sey v .Kennon , 117 S. W. 943.
(11) A billof exceptionsshowing by a
memorandum of the clerk, but not an
order of court, that it was filedin time ,
will not be considered. Padget v. Mays ,
2 R. 213 .
Bush 547. Code provision that exception
must be saved and time allowed to pre-
pare billismandatory . Dalton v.Dal-
ton,146 Ky . 18,141 S. W. 371; Smalling
v. Shaw , 139 S. W. 779, 144 Ky . 458 .
(14) Bill can not be filed in clerk's
officeor signed or approved in vacation,
nor can time in which to filebill be ex-
tendedin vacation. Forsythev. Huey,
25 R. 147,74 S. W. 1088 ; Adkins v.Com .,
102 Ky . 94, 19 R. 1300,42 S. W. 834 ; and
see Proctor Coal Co. v . Strunk ,28 R. 241,
89 S. W. 145 .
(15) Where time isgiven to a day in
next term to file a bill it means in ab-
sence of any thing to contrary next reg-
ular term, and the holding of a special
term in the interval does not affect the
order. Mayes v. Lane, 116 Ky . 566, 25
R. 824, 76 S. W. 399.
(16) Where no objection is made in
lower court to the filingof billan error
in respect to the time in which it is filed
will be consideredwaived. Walling v.
Eggers , 25 R. 1563, 78 S. W. 428 ; L. &
N. R. Co. v. Setser, 138 Ky . 476, 128 S.
W. 341, 7 Bush 680.
(17) If parties consent that bill may
be filed at second succeeding term or
failto objectto the order giving this
time they willbe estopped from raising
any objection on appeal . Hill v. Penn .
Life Ins. Co., 27 R. 567, 85 S. W. 759;
120 Ky . 190 ; Vertrees v . Head , 138 Ky .
83, 127 S. W. 523.
(18) Delay without excuse from week
to week , for six months in preparing
billof exceptions, is sufficientreason for
refusingto considerit over objectionof
adverse party . Schneider v. Hesse , 11 R.
433, 12 S. W. 271.
(18a) Time was given on the thirteenth
day of a certain term of court to filea
bill of exceptions. Court was not in
sessionon that day . The filingin the
officeof the clerk was sufficient. City
of Henderson v . Ky . Peer . Dist. Co., 161
Ky .1, 170 S. W. 210.
(12) Giving time to filebill of excep-
tionsdoes not operateto stay proceed- ceptions,furtherextensionmay be given
ings on the judgment. Wright v.Wool-
folk,14 Bush 308.
(13) If time isgiven in which to pre-
pare and filea billof exceptions, the
record must show that the time was
given, or it can not be filed, although
by neglector oversightthe ordergiving
time was not made . Blue Grass Trac-
tion Co. v. Crossdale, 143 Ky . 196, 136
S. W. 204 ; L. & A. Coal Co. v. Morris ,
116 S. W. 330,132 Ky . 223 ; Vandever v .
Griffith,2 Met .425; Linch v.Reynolds,6
(18b) Where time is given until a day
in a succeedingterm to filea billof ex-
from a day certain in the term to an-
other day certain within the term for
presentation of the bill. U. S. Fidelity
& Guaranty Co. v. Cole'sAdmr .,165 Ky .
823 , 178 S. W. 1057 .
(19) Retention of billby judge- effect
of. Where time is given to succeeding
term to filebill and within the time the
billis given to the judge who holds it
until the next term it may be then filed.
Smalling v. Shaw , 144 Ky . 458, 139 S. W.
779 .

§ 335] [Title IXTrial- Exceptions .
§ 335 [365] [Billof exceptions- how prepared.] 1. [How billpre-
pared.] No particularform of exception, or billof exceptions, isre-
quired. A partymust not,exceptas hereinafterprovided,statetheevi-
dence, but must statethe materialfactswhich the evidence conduced to
prove; and ,ifthe exceptionbe to a decisionadmittingor excludingtesti-
mony ,or concerningitsmeaning or effect,so much of theevidenceas is
necessaryto explainthe exception,and no more ,shallbe stated.
(20 ) Transcript of evidence must be
filedin time . If a bill of exceptions is
filedin time but the transcript of the
evidenceisnot,the evidencewillnot be
considered . Knecht v . Louisville Home
Tel .Co.,89 S. W. 508,28 R. 456, 121 Ky .
492 ; Zehe v .City of Louisville, 96 S. W.
918, 29 R. 1107, 123 Ky . 621 .
(21) Equity cases. When oral evi-
dence isheard in an equitycase the evi-
dence must be made a part of the record
by a billof exceptionspreparedin the
same time and manner as in ordinary
cases. Dupoyster v. Fort JeffersonImp .
Co.,89 S. W. 509 ,28 R. 504,121 Ky . 518 ;
Knecht v. Home Tel. Co., 89 S. W. 508 ,
28 R. 456,121 Ky .492 ; Shannon v .Strat-
ton,144 Ky . 26 , 137 S. W. 850.
(22) Time how counted. The day on
which the judgment was renderedmust
be counted in computing the time within
which a billof exceptions must be filed.
LouisvilleRy .Co. v.Wellington, 137Ky .
719, 128 S. W. 1077.
(23) Extensionof time to filebillmay
be granted before the time allowed to
fileit has expired. L. & N. R. Co. v .
Setser, 138 Ky . 476, 128 S. W. 341.
(24 ) Must be filedwithin time allowed
or bill will not be considered. City of
Catlettsburgv.May , 140 Ky .367, 131 S.
W. 15; L. & N. R. Co. v.Turner,81 Ky .
489 , 5 R. 647; L. & A. Coal Co. v . Morris ,
116 S. W. 330, 132 Ky . 223 ; Nave v .
Riley, 146 Ky . 276, 142 S. W. 388.
(24a ) A certain order extending the
time to filea billof exceptions was aft-
erward set aside by the court. The
courthad power during the term to set
aside the second order and leave the first
order in force. City of Henderson v.
Ky . Peer.Dist.Co.,161 Ky .1,170 S. W.
210 .
(24b ) Upon the overruling of a motion
for a new trial, time may be granted
until the next term to file a bill of ex-
ceptions,althoughthe motion for a new
trial was not acted upon at the term
at which the judgment was rendered.
Stearns Coal & L. Co. v . Com ., 163 Kу .
837 , 174 S. W. 771 .
(24c) The extension of time to filea
billof exceptionsto a day in the next
term may be made at any time during
the term at which the judgment become
final. Carter Coal Co. v .Clouse, 163 Ky .
337, 173 S. W. 794.
(24d) A billof exceptionsnot filedin
time will be stricken from the record on
motion. Bauer Cooperage Co. v. Ewell,
149 Ky . 838, 149 S. W. 1137.
(24e ) A bill of exceptions containing
the evidence,which isnot tenderedwithin
a definite time fixed by the court or
within a definiteextension thereof, can
not be considered, and will be stricken
from the record. U. S. Fidelity & Guar-
anty Co. v . Cole's Admr ., 165 Ky . 823,
178 S. W. 1057 .
§ 335. (1) Absence of bill of excep-
tions- only sufficiencyof pleadingsto
support the verdict can be considered
and every presumption willbe indulged
that the pleadingsauthorize. Martin v.
Richardson , 94 Ky . 183, 14 R. 847, 21 S.
W. 1039. A motion for new trial or bill
of exceptions is not necessary to take an
appeal, but in their absence only the
sufficiencyof the pleadings to support
the verdict will be considered. See note
37, sec.340 .
(la) In the absence of a bill of ex-
ceptionsthe Court of Appeals willcon-
sideronlythe sufficiencyof thepleadings.
Tyler v.Woerner ,158 Ky .710,166 S. W.
178 .
(lb) In the absenceof a billof excep-
tions the only question considered was
whether the pleading supported the ver-
dict. Clark v. Wallace Oil Co., 155 Ky .
836 , 160 S. W. 506 .
(le) A judgment in ejectmentwas af-
firmedas the billof exceptionshad been
struck from the record and the pleadings
were sufficientto sustainthe judgment.
Bobbitt v. Blakemore, 153 Ky . 170, 154
S. W. 941 .
(2) Affidavits- in Warren v.Nash , 24
R. 479 , 68 S. W. 658 , it is held that the
court will consider affidavits filed with
motion for new trial and made part of
recordalthoughthe errorpointedout in

Title IX ] [§ 335Trial- Exceptions .
2. [Refusalofjudgetosign- verdictagainstevidence.] Ifthejudge
refusetosignthebillof exceptions,as presented; or ifthepartywish to
appealupon theground thattheverdictisnotsustainedby theevidence,
itshallbe statedinfull,and thebillof exceptionsbe presentedtothejudge.
(See sec.339.)
3. [Copy of one of severalsimilarwritings.) But ifa number of in-
strumentsof substantiallythesame characterbe usedupon a trialitshall
be sufficientto furnisha copyof one of them inthebillof exceptions,ac-
companiedby a statementshowingitsresemblancetotheothers,and how
many such otherswere used on the trial; and such copy and statement
shallhave the same effectas ifallof said instrumentshad been copied in
thebillofexceptions. (When writingsfiledasexhibitsarepartofrecord,
sec.128 ; form of bill,Forms 182-186.)
Cross reference: See Newman's Forms Nos .466,467,468 .
See Miller's Ky . Appellate Practice and Forms , secs. 70, 71, 75.
affidavitsdoes not appear in billof ex-
ceptions.
(2a) An affidavitwhich was offered to
be filedwas not made a part of the rec-
ord by order of the court, or by a bill
of exceptions. The Court of Appeals
struckit from the transcript. Crews v.
Com ., 155 Ky .122, 159 S. W. 638.
(2b ) Amendment . The Court of Ap-
peals will not consider an amendment
tenderedand not made part of the rec-
ordby orderor billof exceptions. Koke's
Admr . v.Andrew SteelCo.,149 Ky . 627,
149 S. W. 368 .
(3) Bill of exceptions- when not nec-
essary. Where a verdictfor the plaintiff
is set asideand a judgment enteredfor
defendant on the ground that the peti-
tion does not state a cause of action, a
bill of evidence is not necessary to en-
able the Court of Appeals to consider the
sufficiencyof the petition, and reverse
the case if a reversalis proper. Johnson
v. LouisvilleR. W. Co., 10 Bush 231 .
(4 ) Bill of exceptions - requisites of.
A positive or direct statement in the
bill that it contains all the evidence, or
all the instructionsgiven or refused, is
not essentialto make the billcomplete.
When an appeal is prosecuted upon an
issue of fact and the law applicable
thereto, and reversal is asked because
the verdict is not sustained by the evi-
dence, the bill should contain all the
evidence in detail, and each and every
instructiongiven or refused to which an
exceptionwas taken. If the objectionto
the judgment is not want of evidence to
support the verdict, but because of the
admissionor rejectionof testimony, the
material facts must be stated that the
evidence conduced to establish. The pre-
sumption will be in favor of the truth of
the bill, and the affidavitsof counsel
will not be consideredto destroy the
verity of the record as certifiedto by the
judge. Garrot v. Ratliff, 83 Ky . 384, 7
R. 463; Bracken Co. v. Robertson Co.,
6 Bush 69 ; Wing v . Dugan , 8 Bush 583 ;
Murphy v. Roney , 26 R. 634, 82 S. W.
396. Form of BillofExceptionson pages
718-22 .
(5 ) The bill of exceptions does not
state the facts which the testimony con-
duced to prove , when it only states that
"the evidencein the case was conflict-
ing and conduced to show the facts re-
spectivelyclaimedby the partiesin the
pleadings." The bill should state the
material facts which the evidence con-
duced to prove . Beavens v. Phillips, 83
Ky . 88, 7 R. 9 .
(5a) Circuit court conclusions of fact.
In the absence of a bill of exceptions,
the Court of Appeals was unable to say
that any of the conclusions of fact found
by the circuitcourt were not supported
by the evidence. Daniels,Exr.,v.Comp-
ton, 151 Ky . 714, 152 S. W. 753.
(5b ) Counsel , remarks of. This court
will not consider an objectionto improper
argument of counsel where the same is
not certifiedin the billof exceptions.
City of Louisvillev.Hehemann , 161 Ky .
523, 171 S. W. 165.
(5c) Improper and prejudicialremarks
of counsel in argument willnot be heeded
by the Court of Appeals unless the same
269

§ 335 ] [Title IXTrial- Exceptions.
be incorporated in the billof exceptions.
St. P. Fire & Mar. Ins.Co. v .Kendle , 163
Ky . 146,173 S. W. 373.
(5d) A judgment willnot be reversed
on account of improper argument of
counsel unless the objectionableargu-
ment is set out in a bill of exceptions
showing that objectionwas taken. C. &
O. Ry . Co. v. Stapleton, 154 Ky . 351, 157
S. W. 702 .
(6 ) Depositions used as evidence upon
trialcan not be consideredupon appeal
unless embraced in bill, or identifiedby
order of court. L. & N. R. R. v. Finley,
86 Ky . 294 , 9 R. 660, 5 S. W. 753 ; nor
willdepositionscopiedintotherecord,but not embodied inbillof exceptionsor
read on trial, be considered. Harlan v .
Howard , 79 Ky . 373, 2 R. 368 .
(7 ) When depositions offered as evi-
dence are rejected,the record should show
that an exception was taken to the rul-
ing rejectingthem, and the grounds of
objection, as well as the rejecteddepo-
sitions, should be incorporatedinto the
bill of exceptions. Walrath v . Viley, 1
Bush 266 ; sec . 589 .
(8 ) When two depositions are given
by a witness and one only isread,if both
are copied in the record, it should show
which one was read. Crabb v. Larkin , 9
Bush 154 .
(9 ) When depositionshave been regu-
larly taken and are lodged with record
and read on trial, they shouldbe copied
as partof record,althoughneverindorsed
as filed. Long v.Kerrigan, 15 R. 65,21
S. W. 99 .
(9a ) A billof exceptions showed that
a certaindeposition was read to the jury
upon the trial,and showed the rulingof
the courtupon whatever exceptionswere
interposed. It was not necessary that
the depositionbe copiedinto the billof
exceptions. Harrison v. Ford, 158 Ky .
467 , 165 S. W. 663 .
(10) Entries on order book- when a
motion ,order,objection, or exception ap-
pears on the order book - as where there
isobjectionto the filingof an ainendment
and exception taken to order allowing it
to be filed- it is not necessary that it
should appear in billof exceptions- the
orders copied into record will show it.
McAlister v. Ins.Co., 78 Ky . 531.
(11) Evidence rejected- how made
part of record. When objection is made
and sustained to a witness answering a
question, the party offeringthe witness
should make an avowal of what he ex-
pected the witness would state if per-
mitted to answer the question, and the
recordshouldshow it,or elsethe alleged
error in refusingthe evidencecan not
be considered. Manion v . Lambert , 10
Bush 295 ; Bowler v . Lane , 3 Met . 312 ;
Dickerson v . Talbot , 14 B. M. 60 ; L C.
& L. R. R. Co. v. Sullivan, 81 Ky . 624,
5 R. 722 .
(12) When a party objectsto the in-
troduction of evidence the record should
show his objectionsand exception, and
so much of the evidence as is necessary
to explain the grounds of it. Easley v .
Easley.18 B. M. 86; and seeas to state-
ments in billas to evidence, note 4.
(13) A billof exceptionswhich shows
that it contains all the evidence is suffi-
cient,althoughitdoesnotstateinsomany words that it contains all the evi-
dence . Bracken Co. v . Robertson Co .. 6
Bush 69 ; and see 83 Ky . 384, 7 P..463 ;
83 Ky .88 , 7 R. 9 .
(14) In absence of any part of evi-
dence court will not consider any ques-
tion relating to the testimony offered or
introduced on trial. L. & N. R. R. v .
Finley,86 Ky .294,9 R. 660,5 S. W. 753.
(15) Facts appearing upon face of pa-
pers, no bill of exceptions is necessary.
Collinsv. Richart, 14 Bush 621; 78 Ky .
531 .
(16 ) Form of bill of exceptions- see
Forms 182-186 .
(17) Instructions. Express statement
in bill that it contains all the instruc-
tions is not required. If it shows that
certain instructionswere aked by plain-
tiffand defendant , and either given or
refused, in the absence of anything show-
ing that others were given or refused,
billwill be sufficientin this respect. L.
& . N. R. R. v . Finley, 86 Ky . 294, 9 R.
660, 5 S. W. 753 ; Warren v. Nash ,24 R.
479 , 68 S. W. 658 .
(18) An alleged error in giving or re-
fusing instructionscan not be considered
in the absence of the testimony . Beaven
v. Phillips, 83 Ky . 88, 7 R. 9 ; and where
the bill does not contain all the instruc-
tions given the court can not reverse for
an error in instructingthe jury. Hig-
gins v. L. & N. R. R., 18 R. 899, 38 S.
W. 876 .
(19) Instructions should be made a
part of recordeitherby an orderof court
or by embodying them in bill of exceр-
tions,the propermode isto includethem
in billof exceptions, if not made part of
record in one of these ways they will
not be consideredby the court,although
the clerk may copy them in the record.
Huffman v. Charles, 97 S. W. 775, 30 R.
197; Rogers v. Zumbiel, 114 S. W. 323;
Nave v. Riley , 146 Ky . 276, 142 S. W.
388; 81 Ky . 475,5 R. 548; 81 Ky . 51, 4

(21) Pleadingsnot filedby an order
of court but lodged in clerk'sofficeand

(22) Pleadings rejected- how made
part of record. When the record shows
that an amended pleading was not al-
lowed to be filed, unless it is made a
part of the record by an order of court,
or by the bill of exceptions, it can not
be considered, although copied into the
record. Horstman v . C. & L. R. R. Co.,

there must be an indorsement thereon

(23 ) Presumption . The Court of Ap-

sence of a material part of the record

part sustainedthe judgment; and if a

tecum that the originalbillmay be in-

the instructions embodied in the bill

ern R. W. v . Thurman , 25 R. 804, 76 S.

Bridge Co. v. Neafus , 110 Ky . 571, 23 R.
183, 62 S. W. 2 ; Courier-Journal Co. v .

324; and see Ky . Stat ., sec. 4644, and
full case of Postal Tel . Co. v . Louisville
Cotton Oil Co., 136 Ky . 843, 122 S. W.
852. Courts in continuous session, page
641, and form of attestation and ap-
proval, page 719 of this Code .
(25a ) It is the better practice to in-
clude the stenographer's transcript in
the bill of exceptions,yet the billof ex-
ceptionsmay be prepared in the narrative
form insteadof a transcriptof the evi-

ARTICLE 5 .

§ 340. New trialdefined- causes for.

§ 340 [369] [New trialdefined- causesfor.] A new trialisa re-
examinationinthesame courtof an issueof factaftera verdictby a jury
or a decisionby thecourt. The formerverdictordecisionmay be vacated
and a new trialbe granted,on theapplicationof thepartyaggrieved, for
any of the followingcausesaffectingmateriallyhis substantialrights.
(See furtherprovisionsas tonew trial,sec.518.)
1. [Irregularitythatpreventsa fairtrial.) Irregularityin theproceed-
ings of the court,jury or prevailingparty, or any order of the court, or
abuseof discretion,by which thepartywas preventedfrom havinga fair
trial.
2. [Misconductofjuryorparty.] Misconductofjury,of theprevail-
ing party,or of hisattorney,
3. [Accidentor surprise.] Accidentor surprisewhich ordinarypru-
dence couldnot have guarded against.
§ 340. (1) Accident- surprise. Known
defensesmust be pleaded,and the court
will not grant a new trialto allow par-
ties to avail themselves of a defense
known to themwhen judginentwas ren-dered . Dickinson v . Trout , 8 Bush 441.
See further, sec. 518 and notes thereto.
(2) Although one of the appellant's
counsel was prevented by sickness from
attending the trial, if there was a lack
of diligenceon the part of appellantin
not attending the trial, or providing
other counsel, he will not be heard to
complain. Landrum v. Farmer , 7 Bush
46. As to unavoidable absence of at-
torney, see further, Triplettv. Scott, 5
Bush 81; of party,see Yowell v.Gaines,
2 Bush 211 .
(3) Mistake of counselin the prepa-
ration of a case,or as to its standing on
doeket resultingin a judgment against
a party is not such "casualty or mis-
fortune " as will authorize a new trial.
Prather v. Campbell, 110 Ky . 23, 22 R.
1510,60 S. W. 918 ; 6 Bush 662 ; and see
notes to sec . 518 .
(4 ) After judgment by default has
been rendered, if the defendant desires
a new trial on account of accident or
surprise, he should tender an answer
presentinga gooddefense. Hayman v.Hallam , 79 Ky . 389, 2 R. 419 ; Prather v .
Campbell , 110 Ky . 23, 22 R. 1510, 60 S.
W. 918 .
(5) When a party seeks to have a
judgment set aside he should show that
he has not been guiltyof any lack of
diligence. Alexander v. Lewis, 1 Met .
407. "When a party or his counsel are
taken by surprise, whether by accident
or fraud, on a material point or circum-
stance which could not reasonably have
been anticipated, and where want of
skill, careor attentioncan not be justly
imputed , and injustice has been done ,
a new trial will be granted ." McCall v .
Hitchcock, 9 Bush 66; L. & N. R. R. v.
Bickell, 97 Ky . 222, 17 R. 107, 30 S. W.
600 ; Kirk v. Grover , 96 S. W. 824, 29 R.
1047 .
(6 ) The fact that a witness testifies
contraryto what he didon a formertrial

Sutton ,24 R. 868, 70 S. W. 188; Lou . R.
Co. v. DeGore , 27 R. 54, 84 S. W. 326.
(8) When a party institutes an ordi-
nary action it is his duty to exerciseor-
dinary diligenceto inform himself when
it is set for trial and to be in attend-
ance, and neitheraccidentnor surprise
is cause for settingasidejudgment in
the absence of such diligence. Ross v.
L. & N. R. R., 92 Ky . 583, 13 R. 801, 18
S. W. 456 ; and see Chaffin v . Fulkerson ,
95 Ky .277 ,15 R. 635,24 S. W. 1066.
(9 ) A party who has prepared his
case for trial, but is prevented from at-
tendingby the sudden and seriousill-
ness of his child or himself, is entitled
to a new trial. Steel v. Seale, 4 R. 42 ;
Langdon Creasy Co. v .Rouse ,24 R. 2095 ,
72 S. W. 1113; Ray v .Arnett , 106 S. W.
826 , 32 R. 562 ; and so where the de-
fendant is absent because he has entered
into an agreement to arbitrate the mat-
tersin controversy. Perry v. Fisher,19
R. 1733,44 S. W. 378.
(10 ) An attorney prepared an answer
and , being compelled to leave the court,
left the answer with his associate coun-
sel to file, and he , being unwell , did not
attend court; judgment being rendered
by default, a new trialwas proper.
Reinicke v. Morse , 10 R. 767, 10 S. W.
468. But the fact that a defendant per-
mitted a judgment to go against him
under the erroneous advice of his coun-
sel that he had no defense does not en-
title him to a new trial. Mouser v . Har-
mon , 96 Ky . 591, 16 R. 651, 29 S. W. 448.
(11) Plaintiffand his attorney being
both absent from the court room when
judgment was rendered by default, it
was proper to grant a new trial, as it
appeared that the attorney was unex-
pectedlyabsent and the plaintiffa short
distance offwaiting to be notifiedby his
attorney when case was called. C. &
O. R. R. v.Hickey, 15 R. 112, 22 S. W.
441 .
(12) To entitleparty to new trialon
ground of accident or surprise, it should
appear that the party has been injured
in his rights,as well as that his failure
to appear was caused by accident or mis-
fortune. Robinson v . Amann , 1 R. 326.
(13) When a party is taken by sur-
priseduring the trialof a case,the cor-
rect practice is to move for a continuance
or postponement , and not to take the
chancesof a verdict, or failingin that,
to apply fora new trial. Shipp v. Sug-
gett, 9 B. M. 5; Ky . Dis. Wh . Co. v.
Wells ,149 Ky .275 .
(13a) A deed was notproperlyindexed.
Failureto discoverit and produce itat

Title IX ] [§ 340New Trials .
the trial was excusable . A new trial was
granted upon the discoveryof the deed.
Smith v.Chapman ,153 Ky .70,154 S. W.
915 .
(13b) Certainfacts (includinga mis-
take of a stenographerof defendant's
attorney ) constituted an accident which
ordinaryprudencecouldnot have guarded
against. Bentleyv.Ben WilliamsonHdw .
Co., 177 Ky . 799, 198 S. W. 232.
(13c) A new trialwill not be granted
on the ground of surprise, unless the
party surprisedask that the swearingof
the jury be set asideand the trialpost-
poned. The Liverpool& L. & Globe Ins.
Co. v.Wright & Allen; Old Colony Ins.
Co. v .Same ; CitizensF. Ins.Co. v .Same ;
People'sNat . F. Ins. Co. v. Same , 158
Ky . 290, 164 S. W. 952.
(13d ) A defendant failed to ask for a
continuancebecause of surpriseat the
statement of a witness ; a new trialwas
properlyrefused. Howard v.Strawbridge
& Clothier, 165 Ky .88, 176 S. W. 977.
(13e) The inabilityof the stenogra-
pher,who had taken down the evidence
at the trial, to transcribe his notes is
not good ground for the granting of a
new trial. Crouch v. O'Banion, 163 Ky .
581 ,174 S. W. 3.
(13f) Appellantmoved for a new trial
on the ground that itwas surprisedbe-
cause its witness failedto testifyas
she indicated she would before the trial
commenced . Held, "While there might
be cases in which this character of sur-
prise would be ground for a new trial,
this is not one of them ." City of Louis-
villev . Lee , 157 Ky . 285, 162 S. W. 1141,
(13g) Agreement of partieswill not
authorize new trial. A new trial will not
be granted simply because both parties
agree that a new trialshallbe granted.
John B. Carter Co. v. Cox, 159 Ky .710,
169 S. W. 472 .
(13h) The default of defendant's at-
torney to answer within the time re-
quired was held excusable neglect where
the attorneydid not know of a change
in the terms of court made by a Session
Act subsequentto the last compilation
of the statutes, which act was not in-
dexed . Callahan Const . Co. v . Thomas ,
160 Ky .496 , 169 S. W. 828.
(14) Cross appeal- will not be consid-
ered in absenceof a motion and grounds
for new trial. L. & N. R. R. v . White-
head . 24 R. 2315, 73 S. W. 1128.
(15) Discretion of court in granting a
new trialwillbe interferedwith only in
exceptional cases. C. & O. R. R. v .
Hickey , 15 R. 112,22 S. W. 441 ; Ivers v .
Avery ,6 R. 220; Hurt v.L. & N. R. Co.,
116 Ky . 545, 25 R. 755 , 76 S. W. 502 ;
Taylor & Sons v . Lou . Pub . W. H. Co.,
24 R. 1656, 72 S. W. 20 ; Brown v . L. &
N. R. Co., 144 Ky . 546 , 139 S. W. 782 ;
and only when there is a flagrant abuse
of discretionin granting a new trialwill
the court reversesecond judgment and
direct that firstjudgment be entered.
L. & N. R. R. v . Coniff, 16 R. 296, 27 S.
W. 865 ; Couadeau v . Am . Accident Co.,
95 Ky . 280, 15 R. 667, 25 S. W. 6 ; Cald-
well v. Bright , 8 B. M. 525 ; Miller v.
Ashcraft , 98 Ky . 314, 17 P. 894, 32 S. W.
1085. In the following cases the Court
of Appeals held that new trialshad been
erroneously granted and directed that
former judgment set asideby lower court
be entered. Rinchards v. L. & N. R. R.,
20 R. 1478 , 49 S. W. 419 ; L. & N. R. R.
v . Ricketts, 21 R. 662, 52 S. W. 939 ;
Crowley v. L. & N. R. R.,21 R. 1434,55
S. W. 434 .
(15a ) The Court of Appeals refused to
reversea judgment renderedon a ver-
dicton a new trial, granted because of
excessive damages . Snyder v. Louisville
Ry . Co., 150 Ky . 816, 150 S. W. 986.
(15b ) The Court of Appeals will not
reverse because the court below granted
a new trial unless there was an abuse of
discretion. Nantz v . Sizemore , 156 Ky .
539 , 161 S. W. 552 .
(15c) A judgment willnot be reversed
by the Court of Appeals because of the
granting of a new trialbelow unless
such granting was an abuse of discretion.
Chenoa-Hignite Coal Land Co. v. Phil-
pot's Admr ., 152 Ky . 385, 153 S. W. 457 ;
Conley v . Cent . Ky . Trac . Co., 152 Ky .
764 , 154 S. W. 41 ; Pack v . Camden In-
terstateRy .Co., 154 Ky . 535, 157 S. W.
906 .
(15d) The denial of permission to call
witnesses and examine them orally under
an application for a new trialis not an
abuse of discretion . Smith's Admx . v .
Middlesboro Elec. Co., 164 Ky . 46, 174 S.
W. 773 .
(15e) A judgment settingasidea ver-
dict and granting a new trialwillnot
be reversed unless it appears that there
was an abuse of discretion on the part
of the trial court. Thus , where there
are several grounds for a new trial, one
of which is insufficient, and it does not
appear from the record that the trial
court, in awarding the new trial, acted
upon the insufficientground , but may
have awarded the new trial upon other
grounds which were sufficient, the order
granting the new trial will not be set
aside . Norvell v . Paducah Box & Basket
Co., 157 Ky . 703, 163 S. W. 1106.

(17) Errors must be specified. The
grounds reliedon for a new trialmust
be specificallyset out. The general
ground, "because of errorsof law oc-
curringat the trialand exceptedto by

guage in the grounds fornew trialas in-

with reasonable and ordinary certainty,
the particular errors which are com-
plainedof,soastoenablethecourt,by

dence will not be considered on appeal,

pany . It was proper to refuse to admit

ary as a minister. There was other
evidence of his ability to earn money .

ages in certain condemnation proceed-
ings was excessive.. L. & N. R. R. Co.

ed. Sturgeon'sAdmr . v. McCorkle, 163

a(21) When verdict is flagrantly
against the evidence, the court can not
refusea new trialbecause party remits
part of judgment; but iferrorin amount
recovered is caused by an erroneous in-

was due to such instruction, if it can
be ascertained,may be remittedand re-

ner, 19 R. 302, 40 S. W. 779 ; and see

and moved for a new trial; the judge
announced that he would not grant a

cept$1,000; the plaintiffagreedto do
this, and the motion for a new trial was
overruled . The court says : In this in-
stance the court virtuallyassessedthe

(23) Grounds should be filedby order
of court. Motion and grounds for a new
trial should be filedby order of court,
entered on the order book and should
also be indorsed filed. When this is done
the motion and grounds become a part

§ 340 ] [Title IXNew Trials .
of the recordwhen copiedintothe tran-
script,and itisnot necessaryto include
them in the billof exceptions. McAllis-
terv.Ins.Co.,78 Ky .531; and seenotes
to sec . 342 .
(23a ) Harmless error. The admission
of the opinions of non -expert witnesses
as to the conditionof an injuredperson
will not be ground for a new trialwhere
on the whole caseitwas not prejudicial.
LouisvilleRy . Co. v. Sweeney , 157 Ky .
620 ,163 S. W. 739.
(24) Improper argument - is ground
for new trial and when it appears that
the jury was unduly influencedby the
improper argument it is cause for re-
versal. McHenry Coal Co. v. Suedden,
98 Ky . 684, 34 S. W. 228, 17 R. 1261 ;
L. & N. R. Co. v.Hull,113 Ky .561,24 R.
375, 68 S. W. 433 ; and notes to sec. 317.
(24a ) The fact that appellee'scounsel
used certainlanguage will not be con-
sideredon appealunlessit be authenti-
catedby a billof exceptions; an affidavit
as to the language used is insufficient.
So. Ry . Co. of Ky . v . Thacker's Admr .,
156 Ky . 483, 161 S. W. 236 ; Fish v .
Welch's Admr ., 156 Ky . 541, 161 S. W.
512 .
(24b ) Improper questions. The circuit
court should have awarded a new trial
because counsel for the successfulparty
during the trialpersistedin askingques-
tions which had been adjudged incom-
petent by the trialcourt and which might
influence the jury. Shield's Admr . v .
Rowland , 151 Ky . 136, 151 S. W. 408.
(24c) Improper statements . A state-
ment made by counselin his argument
may be of such a prejudicialnature that
the trialcourt should set aside any ver-
diet in favor of his client. The Liver-
pool & London & Globe Ins.Co. v .Wright
& Allen ; Old Colony Ins. Co. v . Same ;
Cit. F. Ins. Co. v. Same ; People'sNat .
F. Ins.Co. v. Same , 158 Ky . 290, 164 S.
W. 952 .
(24d) A judgment was reversedbecause
of improper statements made in argu-
ment by counsel for successfulparty,
which were not supported by the record.
Kt .of Maccabees of the World v . Shields,
162 Ky .392,172 S. W. 696.
(24e) The court having excluded a
statement made by the attorney, and
cautionedthe jury to disregardit,a new
trial will not be granted for this mis-
conduct. LouisvilleRy .Co. v. Sweeney ,
157 Ky . 620 , 163 S. W. 739.
(25) Irregularityof judge, jury or
party. After the jury had retiredfor
consultation, one of the witnesses, who
had been examined on the trial, was sent
for by them , admitted into the jury
room and re-examined without the knowl-
edge of the court or parties. Held that,
althoughhe was thewitnessof theparty
seeking the reversal, the misconduct of
the jury authorized a new trial. Lutrell
v. M. & L. R. R. Co., 18 B. M. 291; and
see 3 Mon. 411 .
(26) That juror was on jury that had
tried the case at a former term is not
cause for a new trial. The objection to
him should have been made before the
jury was sworn , or before the jury had
retired, if not discovered before that
time . Fitzpatrick v.Harris,16 B. M. 561 ;
Throckmorton v. Com ., 20 R. 1508, 49 S.
W. 474; Taylor v. Combs , 20 R. 1828,
50 S. W. 64; and although the jury, or
some of them , had been guilty of im-
proper conduct, the verdictwill not be
set asideunlessit appearsthat the suc-
cessfulparty,or some one in hisbehalf,
was a party to it. Drake v. Drake , 107
Ky . 32, 21 R. 636, 52 S. W. 846. If the
complaining party knows of the miscon-
duct of a juror, he must make the ob-
jectionat the time,or hisrightto do so
willbe waived. Drake v.Drake, 107Ky .
32, 21 R. 636, 52 S. W. 846.
(27) When a jurorduringthe trialex-
presses an opinion as to how case should
be decided it is ground for new trial,
for the party againstwhose interestthe
remark is made . Rice v. Wyatt , 25 R.
1060, 76 S. W. 1087.
(28 ) New trialwill not be granted be-
cause of relationshipof juror to party
when the relation is remote . Rice v .
Wyatt ,25 R. 1060,76 S.W. 1087; North-
cut v . Jouett , 18 R. 327, 36 S. W. 179 ;
or is not known . Miracle v. Com ., 148
Ky . 453, 146 S. W. 1136.
(28a) The mere factthat a jurorin a
civilcase had served as juror upon a
former trialof the case, does not of it-
self require the verdict to be set aside.
McKee v.C.,F. & S. R. R. Co., 161 Ky .
711 , 171 S. W. 425 .
(29) Affidavitsof jurors can not be
received to impeach their verdict for
misbehavior in themselves or their fel-
low jurors. Allard v . Smith ,2 Met .297 ;
as to when jurorswillbe allowedto ex-
plain their verdict or correct mistake in
it, see Alexander v . Humber ,86 Ky . 565 ,
9 R. 734, 6 S. W. 453 ; Johnson v .Daven-
port , 3 J. J. M. 390 ; Eversole v . White ,
112 Ky . 193, 23 R. 1435 , 65 S. W. 442 .
(30) An affidavitof a jurorisnot com-
petent evidence to show that the ver-
dict was the result of an agreement that
such a verdict should be rendered as was
favor by a majority of the jury . Lucas
