§ 439. (1) Action in circuitcourt on
judgment of inferiorcourt. The circuit
court has jurisdictionof an action to
subject an equitable interestin real
propertyto the satisfactionof a judg-ment rendered in the quarterly court,
upon which an execution had been re-
turned,no propertyfound,both from the
quarterlyand the circuitcourt. Jones v.
Jeffress, 11 Bush 636 ; but return from
both courts is necessary. Clements v .
Waters , 90 Ky . 96, 11 R. 880, 13 S. W.
431; Behan v. Warfield , 90 Ky . 151, 11
R 960 , 13 S. W. 439 ; and see Burns v .
Cade , 10 Bush 251 ; Counts v . Howes , 98
Ky . 397, 17 R. 1071, 33 S. W. 395.
(2) Action may be brought in circuit
court to subjectpersonalty, upon a re-
turn of no property in a justice'scourt.
Austin v. Payne , 7 Bush 480 ; but not
land that can be levied on under an exe-
cution. Behan v. Warfield, 90 Ky . 96,
11 R. 880, 13 S. W. 431 ; Mansfield v .
Wilkinson , 16 R. 276 ; Weatherford v .
Myers , 2 Duv . 91.
(3) Bastardy case- to enforcejudg-
ment in. In a bastardy proceeding, judg-
ment was rendered in the county court
against the father. On a return of no
property on this judgment, the circuit
court had jurisdiction to set aside a
fraudulent conveyance made by the
father,and subject the property. Bright-
well v . Com ., 79 Ky . 537, 3 R. 310.
(4 ) Conclusiveness of judgment . Until
reversed or vacated as provided by law ,
or attacked for fraud, judgment upon
which actionon returnof no propertyis
based is conclusive, and defendant can
not show he was not before court in con-
tradiction of the record . Stevenson v .
Flournoy , 89 Ky .561, 11 R. 745, 13 S. W.
210; but judgment obtainedby fraud
may be attacked in action to enforce
satisfactionof it. Fort Jeff. Imp . Co. v .
Greene , 112 Ky . 85, 23 R. 1342, 65 S. W.
161; Duerson v . Semonin , 17 R. 51 , 29
S. W. 635 ; and see as to rights of trus-
tee and heirs to contest judginent against
cestui que trust or personal represent-
ative. Roberts v. Yancey , 94 Ky . 243, 15
R. 101, 21 S. W. 1047 ; Willis v . Roberts ,
90 Ky . 122, 11 R. 929,13 S. W. 358.
(4a ) Corporation, judgment against
firm sued as. An action may be brought
on a judgment which purports to be ren-
dered against a corporation to impose
liabilityon the members of a firm which
was erroneouslysued as ifa corporation.
Heavrins v. Lack Malleable Iron Co., 153
Ky . 329, 155 S. W. 729.
(5 ) Creditor may maintain action upon
return of no property ,and in such action
acquire a lien upon and subject property
that was and is subject to execution. He
has a right to rely upon officer'sreturn,
although itbe false. Clements v.Waters ,
90 Ky . 96, 11 R. 880, 13 S. W. 431.

judgment defendant in may be attached
by service on administrator. Wilkerson
v . Phillips, 26 R. 440 , 81 S. W. 691.
(7 ) Execution must be directed to and
executed by an officerauthorized to exe-
cute it, or the return will not authorize
proceedings under this chapter. Johnson

RECORDS - HOW LOST SUPPLIED .
§ 3991. Judgment or order- lostor mutilatedsupplied evidence- costs.
When anyjudgmentor finalorderofanycourtofrecordof thisStateremains
unexecuted, and the recordthereofhas been lost, mutilated, defaced or de-
stroyed, itshallbe lawful for any person interestedtherein, upon ten days'
notice,inwriting,to the adverseparty, to move the courtin which suchjudg-
ment was rendered or finalorder was made to re-enter the same of record;
and ,upon satisfactoryproof thatsuch judgment or finalorderhad been there-
tofore entered of record, thatthe same had been mutilated, defaced or de-
stroyed, and the purportthereof, itshallbe the duty of the court to re-enter
the same of record; which judgment or finalorder so enteredshallhave the
same effectas the originalto allintentsand purposes, and officialcopies
thereofshallbe receivedas evidencefor allpurposesforwhich likecopiesof
the original,ifin existence,would be competent. No judgment forcostsshall
be renderedagainstthe defendant in such motion, unlesshe shallcontrovert
theplaintiff'sright,and failinhisdefense. (Ky.Stat.,sec.3991.)
§ 3992. Bond having force of judgment- lostor mutilatedsupplied- costs.
When any delivery,sale,forthcoming,replevinor otherbond,having the force
and effectof a judgment, has been lost, destroyed, mutilatedor defaced, it
shallbe lawfulfor any person interestedtherein,upon ten days'writtennotice
to the adverseparty,tomove thecourtinwhich saidbond was on fileto award
executionthereon; and upon satisfactoryproof that such bond had existed,
that the same had been lost, destroyed, mutilatedor defaced, the purport
thereof, and thatthe same remains undischarged, itshallbe the duty of the
courtto award executionthereon, endorsed as though executionwere issued
on such bond, if in existence; but no judgment for costsshallbe rendered
againstthe defendantin such motion,unlesshe shallcontrovertthe plaintiff's
rightto an executionand failinhis defense. (Id., sec.3992.)
§ 3993. County court- lostor mutilatedorder or bond in- supplied. If an
order of the county court, appointingan administratoror guardian, or ap-
pointingany person to any place, station,or trust;or,ifthe bond executedin
the countycourtby any such person,or by an officershallbe destroyed, oblit-
erated,or lost,thecourtshall,by the appropriateproceeding,due noticethereof
beinggiven, supplythe record,and requiresuch person to executea new bond,
with good and sufficientsureties;and on hisfailureto do so,shallenterup an
order vacatinghis officeor station. (Id., sec.3993.)
§ 3994. Records or papers of any court supplied- commissioner. If the
recordsor papers of any courtshallbe lost,destroyed,defaced,or obliterated,
such courtshallappointa commissioner,who shallhave power and authority
to fixon a convenientplaceto meet and sit,from time to time,givingreason-
ablepublicnoticethereof. (Id., sec.3994.)
(2) If parties see proper to proceed by
pleadings, the rulesapplicableto plead-
ings in action obtain, Sargent v . Farrar ,
2 R. 212 .
(3) Practice. A party can not be de-
prived of the right to a jury trial by
proceeding by motion if he is entitledto
have question tried by a jury. See Con .,
sec. 7, in Ky . Stat ., and notes thereto.
(4 ) Motion and grounds for a new
trialare necessary in order to a review
of the evidence. Carr v. Brownlee , 102
Ky . 160, 19 R. 1163, 42 S. W. 1099.
(5) Motion for possession of land un-
der sec. 1689, Ky . Stat ., is tried under
these provisions of the Code . Scott v .
Mitchell, 19 R. 218, 39 S. W. 507 ; and
defense need not be in writing.

§ 3995. Commissioner- powers and dutiesof. The commissioner may , at

or paper so destroyed, defaced,or obliterated; which depositionshallbe legal
evidence, and shallbe returnedto the clerkof the court and safelykept by
such clerk. Before any such proof istaken,the partyofferingitmust make
and filewith the commissioner an affidavitthat there is no attested copy of
such recordor papersinexistenceknown to him . If such affidavitisnot made ,

Lost Records - Justices' Courts - Restoration .
§ 1091. Destructionor loss of records- how supplied- appeals. If the
recordsof a justiceof the peacebe destroyedor lost,and a judgment so de-
stroyedor lostremain unsatisfied,the justicemay ,on reasonablenoticeto the
adverseparty, rendera new judgment for whatever isdue. The justiceshall
state in his record of such trialsthat the proceedings are founded on a judg-
ment which has been lostor destroyed. An appealliesfrom suchjudgment as
in other cases. (Ky .Stat., sec.1091.)
NAME - HOW CHANGED .
§ 3717. Persons whose name may be changed. Any person of the age of
twenty-one years,and not a married woman ,may have hisor her name changed
by the county courtin which he or she resides. (Ky .Stat., sec.3717.)
§ 3718. Infant- name of may be changed. The father,or ifhe be dead,or
shallhave abandoned his infantchildor children, the mother and guardian,
or, ifno mother is living, the guardian aloneof an infant,may ,in likeman-
ner, have itsname changed . (Id ., sec.3718.)
§ 3719. Facts to be enteredof record. The originalname , age and place
of birth, and the name to which the change is made , and the names of an in-
fant's father and mother , if known , and of the person on whose motion the
change is made , must be entered on the order-book of the court. (Id., sec.
3719. )
§ 3720. Index to be keptby clerk. The clerkof every court shallkeep an
alphabeticalindex for each order-book,referringto the proper page in which
each person's name is changed , giving the name from and to which it is
changed . (Id .,sec.3720.)

COLLECTED .

which the money may be collected, shall,afternotifyingthe attorneyto show
cause againstthe same, suspend him from practicein any court for twelve
months, and untilthe money shallbe paid. Before any such motion shallbe
entertained, a demand of the money shallbe made of suchattorneyin the

§ 451. (1) Amendment of award can
not be made after award is reduced to
writing as specifiedin submission, and
where such an attempt is made the sec-
ond award willbe disregardedand the
originalenforced. Martin v. White , 1
K. 347; Eddy v.Northup, 15 R. 434, 23
S. W. 353; 1 Met .434 .
(2) Appeal does not liefrom an award
under a statutory submission although
by entry of recordit is made the judg-
ment of the court, unless some excep-
tion is taken affectingthe validityof
theaward. Payne v.Payne,8 R. 591.
(3) Common law submissions- parties
may stipulateas they please as to notice
or other conditions. Martin v . White , 1
R. 347 .
(3a ) The common law respecting arbi-
trations and awards is still in force .
Woolfork v. Calloway, 9 Ky . Opinions
161; Jones v. Hatchett, 9 Ky . Opinions
164 .
(3b) Condemnation proceeding. Par-
tiesto a condemnationproceedingagreed
of record to refer the question of dam-
ages to arbitrators. The award was filed
and no exceptions to itwere filed; itwas
conclusiveastothequestionofdamages.Hensley v. The Wasiota & Black Mt. R.
Co.,153 Ky .17,154 S.W. 385.
(4) Duty of arbitratorsand parties.
The arbitratorsare to act upon the evi-
dence beforethem ,and it isthe duty of
each party to bring beforethem allof
his evidence. Adams v. Ringo , 79 Ky .
211 , 1 R. 251 .
(5 ) When referenceis made to several
they should hear togetherthe evidence,
and consultjointlyabout it and their
award , and all should sign the award in
the presence of each other. Henderson
v .Buckley , 14 В. М. 292 .
(6) Where an action for the settlement
of a partnershipis referredto arbitra-
tors, whatever claim either asserts
againstthe otheras growing out of the
partnership, although not mentioned in
the pleadings, is a matter in controversy
and embraced in the submission . Adams
v . Ringo , 79 Ky . 211, 1 R. 251 ; and see
Newton v. West , 3 Met .24.
(7) Exceeding authority. If the arbi-
tratorsexceed theirauthority and act
upon matters not submitted, that part
of their award will be stricken out and
the residueupheld. 79 Ky . 211, 1 R.
251 ; and see Carson v . Carson , 1 Met .
434 ; Eddy v. Northup , 15 R. 434, 23 S.
W. 353; Allen-Bradley Co. v. Anderson,
99 Ky . 311, 18 R. 216, 35 S. W. 1123.
(8) Form - mistakes . Mere mistake of
law made by arbitratorsis not sufficient
to set aside an award . This mode of
settlingcontroversiesistobeencouraged,
and mere formal objections will be dis-
regarded when they do not affect the
substantialrightsof the parties. Adams
v . Ringo , 79 Ky . 211, 1 R. 251 ; Snyder
v . Rouse , 1 Met . 625 ; Rudd v . Jones , 4
Dana 229; Overly v. Overly, 1 Met . 117;

made and returned,but the courtmay enlargethe time.
2. [Oath of arbitratorsand umpire.] Arbitratorsand theumpire,if
therebe one,before they proceed to act shalltake an oath to decide the
controversyto them submittedaccordingto law and evidenceand the
equityof thecase,to thebestof theirjudgment,withoutfavoror affec-
tion; and a certificateof such oath shallbe returned to the court with the
award .
3. [Witnesses- how attendanceof coerced.] Any one of the arbi-
tratorsshallhave power to issuesubpoenas for witnessesto attend their
sittingsand give evidencetouchingthe questionsreferredto them, to
which allsheriffsand other likeofficersshallgive obedience. Witnesses
failingto attendbeforearbitratorsat the time and placedesignated, or
who shallrefuse to give evidencewhen they do attend, shallbe reported
to the courtby the arbitrators,and be proceeded againstand punished as
ifthe casehad been pending in court.
4. [Fiduciariesmay submit controversy.] The personalrepresenta-
tiveof a decedent,the guardian,committee or curatorof a person under
disability,or any trustee,may agreeto such submission. Upon such a sub-
mission,made ingood faith,theaward shallbe binding,and be enteredas
thejudgmentof thecourt. No suchfiduciaryshallbe responsibleforany
lossresultingfrom an award adversetotheinteresthe represents,unless
caused by his faultor neglect.
Whittaker v. Wallace , 1 R. 271 ; Cook v .
McRoberts , 5 R. 764 .
(9 ) Fraud - to set aside award for . In
an action to set aside an award upon the
ground of fraud , there must be aver-
ments of the specificfraud relied on .
Phillipsv. Phillips,81 Ky . 147,4 R. 941.
to their aid of their country R. 123 R. E
(10) Judgment on award . It is error
to renderjudgment upon an award not
deliveredto the partiesten days before
the commencement of the term at which
judgment is rendered. But this is a
clericalmisprision, and motion must be
made to correct it in the circuit court .
Carson v. Carson , 1 Met . 434 .
(11) Majority of persons to whom a
questionis submittedmay act. Hewitt
v. Craig, 86 Ky . 23, 9 R. 232, 5 S. W.
280 .
(12) Notice- waiver of. Upon an ar-
bitration made under common law rules,
either party may waive notice of the
meeting of the arbitrators or decline to
be present. When the award is made
the arbitratorshave no power over it,
and a promise by them to reopen the
arbitration and hear additional testi-
mony will not affectthe award . Whit-
lock v . Ledford , 82 Ky . 390 , 6 R. 387.
(13) When the parties are present and
heard by the arbitratorsit is no objec-
tion to the award that it does not state
that the parties had notice of the time
and place the arbitratorsinet and acted.
Newton v . West , 3 Met . 24 .
(14 ) Oath of arbitrators . The officer's
certificate accompanying the award
stated that the arbitrators appeared
." and made oath according to law that
they would faithfullydischarge the
duties devolved upon them as arbitrators

§ 451 ] [Title XArbitrations and Awards .
5. [Arbitratorrefusingtoact- proceedings.] Ifany arbitratorfailor
refusetoact,thecourtmay appointanother,or setasidetheorderof ref-
erence .
6. [Meetings- powers- award.] The arbitratorsand umpire,ifthere
be one,shallmeet at such convenienttimesand placesas may be neces-
sary,of which thepartiesshallhave reasonablenotice,and hearsuchevi-
dence as eitherpartymay adduce. They shallhave power to administer
oaths. They shallmake theiraward inwriting,statingthereinwhen itis
made ,and signit;and shalldelivera copy of ittoeachof thecontending
parties,and returnthe originalto thecourtwith indorsementsshowing
the time of deliveringa copy to each party.
7. [Award enteredasjudgment--when .] The award shallbe entered
asthejudgmentofthecourtatthefirstterm,tendaysbeforewhichcopies
of the award are deliveredto the parties,unless,on exceptionsfiled, itbe
setasideby the court.
8. [Power of court over award .] No award shallbe setasidefor the
want of form. But courtsshallhave power over awards on equitable
principlesas heretofore.
9. [Arbitrationof littlecontroversies- practice- appeal.] Contro-
versieswithinthejurisdictionof a justiceof thepeacemay be submitted
forarbitrationin the same manner ,and the proceedingsshallbe the same
as are above authorizedand required. Eitherpartymay appealto the
circuitcourt from the judgment of the justiceon the award , as in other
cases. (Controversymay withoutsuitbe submittedtocourt,sec.637.)
and umpire ." Held sufficient. Snyder
v . Rouse , 1 Met . 625 ; and , if court is
satisfiedby parol evidence that oath was
taken , it will be sufficient. Rhodes v .
Ward , 17 R. 875. 32 S. W. 590.
(15) In the absence of proof to the
contrary it will be presumed that the
arbitrators were sworn , and that they
compliedwith allthe requirementsof the
law . Carson v . Carson , 1 Met .434 .
(16) Parol submission . The rule of
the common law that any matter in con-
troversy might be submitted by parol
agreement to arbitration unless it di-
vested the parties of some right that
could only pass by a written agreement ,
has not been changed by the Code or
Statutes. Thomasson v. Risk, 11 Bush
619; Royse v. McCall , 5 Bush 695.
(17) Statutory arbitrationand award .
See Ky . Stat., sec. 69 .
(18) Submission by order of court. To
authorize the reference of a controversy
to arbitration by rule of court, when
there is no action pending ,it is necessary
that there be an agreement in writing
filedand noted of record, or an entry on
the record stating the matter submitted .
A judgment upon an award without these
prerequisitesis unauthorized . Carson v .
Carson , 1 Met . 434.
(19) Time in which award to be made .
When a time is fixed within which the
award is to be returned,it must be made
within the time; and the arbitrators
should decide all questionssubmitted;
or a failureto comply with eitherof
these requirements,the award will be set
aside. Burnam v. Burnam , 6 Bush 389.
(20 ) Umpire - selection and duties.
Umpire may be selected at any time
after arbitratorsare chosen. Whittaker
v .Wallace ,1 R. 271 ; and see as to duties
of umpire and part he should take in
deliberations, Couchman v. Maupin , 78
Ky . 33 ; 1 R. 271 .
(21) The umpire shouldonly actwhen
the arbitrators differ, but the fact that
he occasionally takes part in the discus-
sion of questions will not invalidate the
award . 79 Ky . 211, 1 R. 251 ; Whittaker
v . Wallace , 1 R. 271 .

Title X ] [§ 451Arbitrations and Awards .
BY WRITTEN AGREEMENT WITHOUT COURT ORDER .
§ 69. Controversieswhich may be submitted- how submissionmade . All
controversieswhich might be the subjectof a suitor actionmay be submitted
to the decisionof one or more arbitrators, or two and theirumpire. Said
submissionmay be in the mode specifiedin the CivilCode, or itmay be in
writing, and the agreement of submission shallbe binding on the parties
thereto, if it states the matter submitted and who are to be the arbitrators.
(Ky .Stat., sec.69.)
§ 70. Covenant of parties. The partiesto the submissionmay enterinto
a mutual covenant, substantiallyas follows: "We , A B and C D , having a
controversyrespecting(here insertthe matter in controversy), for the pur-
pose of settlingthe same, have mutuallychosen and arbi-
trators,who shallchoosean umpire in case of disagreement,whose award we
do hereby bind ourselvesto perform,provided itis in writing. In witness
whereof, we have hereunto setour hands this day of . " (Id.,
sec.70.)
§ 71. Arbitrators- duty- noticeof time and place of meeting--selection
of umpire. The arbitratorsshallmeet ata time and placetobe fixedby them-
selves,of which reasonablenoticein writingshallbe given to each party,and
shalltake an oath fairlyand impartiallyto decide the controversyto them
submittedaccordingto law,justiceand the equityof the whole case. If they
selectan umpire,he shall,in likemanner ,be sworn beforethe hearingbegins.
They shallhear alltestimonyofferedby eitherparty relativeto the matters
in controversy,and shallhave power and authorityto summon witnesses,com-
pel theirattendance,and punish for contempt by fineor imprisonmentto the
extentjusticesof the peace may do ; shallhave authorityto administeroaths
to partiesand witnesses,and to preserveorder and decorum while engaged in
the determinationof the matter. (Id., sec.71.)
§ 72. Award tobe inwriting- copiesdeliveredto parties- actionon award .
The award of the arbitratorsmust be in writing, signedby them and the um-
pire,ifany,and shallbe a finalsettlementof the controversybetween thepar-
ties. Copiesof the award must be given withinreasonabletime to each party,
and the same shallbe bindingupon both. For a failuretoperform the award,
an actionby ordinaryproceedingmay be maintainedby eitherpartyagainst
the other. (Id ., sec.72.)
§ 73. Award not setasidefor want of form- equityjurisdiction. No
award shallbe set asidefor want of form ; but courtsof equityshallhave
power over awards on equitableprinciplesas heretofore. (Id.,sec.73.)
(22) On a submission to two arbitra-
tors,who in caseof disagreementare to
choose an umpire, the umpire is only
authorizedto act when they disagree;
and if they allact togetherthe award
will be set aside. Royse v. McCall, 5
Bush 695. Unless authorized so to do by
terms of submission , arbitratorshave no
power to selectan umpire and an award
made by an umpire so selected will be
void. Allen-Bradley Co. v. Anderson, 99
Ky . 311, 18 R. 216, 35 S. W. 1123.
(23) Who may submit to arbitration.
A county court may submit to arbitra-
tion matters in controversybetween it
and an individual. Remington v.Harri-
son County , 12 Bush 148.
(24) A personal representativemay
submit matters to arbitration with the
same effect and the same freedom from
liabilityout of court, as with the con-
sent of court. Overly v. Overly, 1 Met .
117 .

§ 452 [500] [Forcibleentryand detainerdefined.] I. The words
"possession," "entry," "detainer," in thischapter, referto lands and
tenants .
§ 452. (1) Acquittal no bar to an-
other writ. An acquittal on a charge of
forcible detainer will not bar a writ for
a forcible detainer committed at a sub-
sequenttime. Shepherd v. Thompson ,2
Bush 176; nor is writ dismissed because
defectivebar to another writ. Hamel
v . Lawrence , 1 Mar. 330. t
(2 ) Deeds . Deeds may be introduced
to show the extent of possession, but
not the right of possession. Beauchamp
v .Morris ,4 Bibb 312 ; Carpenter v. Shep-
herd , 4 Bibb 501 ; Taylor v. White , 1
Mon. 37 ; Richie v. Owsley , 121 S. W.
1015, 137 Ky .63.
(3) Detainer defined- who may prose-
cute . Before a writ of forcible detainer
can be maintained against a tenant , it is
necessary that he should have refused to
restorethe possessionafterthe expira-
tion of his term ; and a mere continuance
in possession, without such refusal, or
the commission of some act construc-
tivelyconstitutinga refusal, isnot suf-
ficientto convict a tenant of a forcible
detainer. Shepherdv.Thompson ,2 Bush
176 ; Thompson v .Marsh ,4 Bush 423 .
(4 ) A tenant's conduct may amount
to a refusal, although no formal demand
for possession has been made , and this
factshouldbe leftto the jury. Beyn-
roth v. Mandeville,5 Bush 584; and dis-
claimer of tenancy subjectstenant to
warrant for forcible detainer even before
expiration of tenancy .
(5 ) Parties in possession of land sold
under decreeleasedland from purchaser,
and failingto surrenderpossessionwhen
lease expired, warrant of detainer was
proper remedy. Walker v. Bush, 6 R.
514 .
(6 ) A party in possession, under an
agreement to occupy and take care of
landfora term ofyears,with the under-
standingthat he might securethe title
by the purchase of it when he should
become able, was not a tenant in the
sensethat he couldbe evictedby a writ
of forcible retainer. Reeder v . Bell, 7
Bush 255 ; and see Col. H. & B. Asso . v .
368

Title X ] [§ 452ForcibleEntry and Detainer .
II. A forcibleentry is-
1. An entrywithouttheconsentof thepersonhavingtheactualpos-
session .
2. As to landlord,an entryupon thepossessionof histenantat will
orby sufferance,whetherwithor withoutthetenant'sconsent.
III . A forcible detainer is-
1. The refusalof a tenanttogivepossessionto hislandlordafterthe
expirationofhisterm;or of a tenantatwillor by sufferancetogivepos-
session to the landlord after the determination of his will.
2. The refusalof a tenantof a personwho has made a forcibleentry
togivepossession, on demand ,to thepersonupon whose possessionthe
forcibleentrywas made .
3. The refusalof a personwho has made a forcibleentryupon the
possessionof one who acquireditby a forcibleentrytogivepossession,
on demand ,to him upon whose possessionthe firstforcibleentrywas
made .
Harvey , 23 R. 1009, 64 S. W. 676, in
which the court held that under the con-
ditionalcontract of purchase in that case
the tenant could be evicted .
(7) Under a contractby which the
tenantagreesto cultivatea cropand the
landlord is to receive one -half of pro-
ceeds; if tenant failsto perform his con-
tractthe landlordmay ejecthim by writ
of forcibledetainer. Wood v . Garrison ,
23 R. 295, 62 S. W. 728 ; Ky . Stat ., sec.
2327 ; and see as to tenancy from month
to month , J. W. Reccius v . C. F. & T.
Co., 27 R. 880, 86 S. W. 1113 ; tenant
holdingover,Asher v.Johnson,26 R. 586,
82 S. W. 300 .
(8) Where W sold land to G and exe-
cuted to him a titlebond , and G after-
ward sold the land to B and assigned to
him the bond , with the agreement that
G should retain possession of the land
untilthe March following,B could not
maintain a writ of forcible detainer
againstG. Goldsberryv.Bishop,2 Duv .
143 .
(9 ) To maintain the writ of forcible
detainer, the relationof landlordand
tenant must exist; and the reservation
of rent in some form , and allegiance to
the title, are the distinguishing charac-
teristicsof a contract by which the rela-
tion of landlard and tenant exists . 2
Duv . 143 ; see 1 Duv . 151; 8 Bush 238 ;
Cuyler v. Estis, 23 R. 1063, 64 S. W.
673; and see further, Ky . Stat ., secs.
2293, 2295, 2296, 2326, 2327 ; Lovely v .
Stacey , 171 Ky . 338, 188 S. W. 389.
(9a) One having a tenancy from year
to year is a tenant for a term within the
provisions of division 4 , subsection 3 , of
this section, and if one forciblyenters
upon possession of a tenant from year to
year the landlordmay by forciblede-
tainerproceedingsrecoverthe possession
from him , after demand and refusal. Am .
Coal Land Co., et al., v . Miller, et al.,
182 Ky . 51.
(9b) One joint owner can not bring
forcible detainer against his cotenant.
Derrington v . Childers, 156 Ky . 452, 161
S. W. 216.
(10 ) Tenant can not dispute title of
his landlordnor avail himself of title
derived from another . Clinton v . Clin-
ton,2 Bibb 433.
(11) While B was occupying land as
the tenant for years of A , C acquired
the legal titleto the land by the judg-ment of the circuit court, and was
awarded a writ of possession. Without
a formal eviction by the writ, B sur-
renderedpossessionto C and took a lease
from him ; and , having refused to sur-
render possession when his time expirea,
C could maintain a writ of forciblede-
taineragainsthim . McMurty v.Adams ,
3 Bush 70 .
(12) One or more of several joint own-
ers can sue out writ without joining
other joint owners , Lancaster v . Lan-
caster, 19 R. 577, 41 S. W. 344.
(13) Forcible entry . A sub -tenant de-
liveredthe key of the house he had been
occupying to the tenant ,who had a lease
of the property for fiveyears .and aban-
doned the premises, but immediately

§ 452 ] [Title XForcible Entry and Detainer .
4. The refusalof a personwho has made a forcibleentryupon the

thereafter he borrowed the key from the

who tookpossessionof the premises.The tenant could maintain a writ of

Haupt v.Pittaluga,6 Bush 493.
(14) The statuterelatingto forcible
entry and detainerdoes not affectthe
common law rightof entry, when exer-
cised in a peaceable manner , except so
far as the statute itself furnishes a rem-
edy to regainpossession. A person hav-
ing the right to enter, who makes an
entry in pursuance of that right, can
only be removed by a writ of forcible
entry. Tucker v.Phillips,2 Met .416.
(15) Residence upon part of a tract
claiming the whole , though part is not
inclosed,may give such possession of the
whole tract as will authorize the person
to maintain a writ of forcibleentry for
an intrusionupon any part of it. Henry
v .Clarke.4 Bibb 426; Smith v. Morrow ,
5 Litt.210 ; Richie v . Owsley , 121 S. W.
1015, 137 Ky . 63 .
(16) A personmay have possessionin
fact of land without residingupon it,
and though itbenot cultivated,improved
or inclosed. Brumfield v .Reynolds ,4 Bibb
388; Wall v. Nelson , 3 Litt. 395 ; Chiles
v .Stephens , 3 Mar. 344 ; Young v .Ringo ,
1 Litt. 226 ; Howard v . Whitaker , 22 R.
1775, 61 S. W. 355 ; Richie v . Owsley ,
121 S.W. 1015,137 Ky .63.
(17) An entry upon uninclosedland,
with the intent to clear and fitfor culti-
vation without actuallyinclosingit, is
a forcibleentry. Humphrey v. Jones, 3
Mon. 261. Entry within the lines and
fencing of another without his consent
constitutes a forcible entry. Henry v.
Clarke ,4 Bibb 426.
(18) Entry with intentto cut timber,
or occasionalchoppingor cuttingand re-
moving timber, is not such an entry as
will authorize a writ of forcibleentry.
It is only a trespass. Grughler v . Wheel-
er, 12 B. M. 183 ; Humphrey v . Jones , 3
Mon. 261 .
(19) Tenant in possessionis proper
personto maintain writ of forcibleentry
and not the landlord. Pogue v. McKee ,
3 Mar. 128 ; Steele v . Logan , 3 Mar. 394 .
(20 ) Issue involved is whether party
complaining was or was not in the actual
possessionof thepremisesforciblyen-tered, no question of titleor right of
possession can arise, nor does it make
any difference whether the defendant
had or had not the right to enter. Dils
v . Justice, 10 R. 547, 9 S. W. 290 ; Hunt
v .Wilson , 14 B. M. 44 ; Robinson v .Mar-
shall, 25 R. 1785,78 S. W. 904 ; Cuyler v .
Estes,23 R. 1063,64 S.W. 673; Richiev.
Owsley , 121 S. W. 1015, 137 Ky . 630.
(20a ) One can not maintain forcible
entry unlessat the time of the alleged
entry he isactually in possession. Lovely
v .Stacey , 171 Ky .338, 188 S. W. 389.
(21) Although a person may have the
right of entry and be entitled to the
property , he will be guilty of forcible
entry ifhe enters against the will of the
person in actual possession. Young v.
Young , 109 Ky . 123,22 R. 627, 58 S. W.
593; McCormick v. McDowell , 90 S. W.
541, 28 R. 854 , 121 Ky . 832.
(22) Title papers are not competent
exceptto show the extentof the posses-
sion or the boundary of the land in dis-
pute. Bush v. Coomer , 24 R. 702,69 S.
W. 793 ; and note 2 , sec. 452 .
(23) Where one trespasser succeeds
anotherinthepossessionof landtheper-
son in possessionwhen firsttrespasser
entered may maintain a writ of forcible
entry against each trespasser. Alexan-
der v .Fowler ,6 R. 444 ; Robinson v.Mar-
shall, 25 R. 1785, 78 S. W. 904.
(24 ) To maintain a writ of forcible
entry plaintiffmust have been at time
of entry in the actual possession of the
premises . Neither right of possession
nor constructive possession is sufficient.
Dils v. Justice, 10 R. 547 , 9 S. W. 290 ;
Childersv.Hieronymous, 105 S. W. 979,
32 R. 394 .
(25 ) Joint tenants should join in writ.
4 Bibb 334; but one or more of several
joint owners may sue out writ and re-
cover. Lancaster v . Lancaster, 19 R. 577,
41 S. W. 34 .
(26) Conflictingtitles. Where two per-
sons have deeds to a lot and one takes
possessionthe forcibleentry of the other
is a forcibleentry in meaning of Code .

ceeding of forcibledetainer the title is

der tenant who has right to sublet may

(28 ) Entry on possession of tenant is

obtain writ of forcibleentry against in-
truder. Willisv. Whayne , 142 Ky . 194,

ner,96 S. W. 818,29 R. 959 , 123 Ky . 552.

ure by tenantof rightto holdpremises

953 ,30 R. 925,124 Ky . 669.
(31) Breaking door and taking posses-
sion of house on land during absence of

Ky . 832 .
(32 ) Judgment for defendant in forci-
ble entry proceedingsis not a bar to a
forcibledetainer proceeding. Johnson v .
Gordon,118 S.W. 372.
§ 454. (1) Policejudge having con-
current jurisdictionwith justicesof the

(la) Police courts in towns of the
sixth class may issue and try warrants
of forcibleentry and detainer. Allen v .
Moore , 173 Ky . 394, 191 S. W. 93.
(2) Requisites of warrant - amend-
ment . It is necessary that the warrant
for forcibledetainer should charge that
the complainant was in the peaceable
possession of the premises , and the rela-
tion of landlord and tenant should be
alleged, or in some form appear on the
face of the warrant . Powers v . Suther-
land, 1 Duv. 151; Taylorv. Monohan, 8
Bush 238 ; 2 Duv . 143 .
(3) Warrant may charge a forcible
entry or detainer in the alternative. Car-
penter v. Shepherd , 4 Bibb 501 ; Mc-
Brayer v .Wash , 6 J. J. M. 465 ; they are

§ 4849. Jurisdictionof courtsto probate will. Wills shallbe proved be-
foreand admittedto recordby,thecountycourtof thecountyof thetestator's
residence; ifhe had no known placeof residencein thisCommonwealth , and
land isdevised,then in the county where the land,or partthereof, lies; ifno
land isdevised,then in the county where he died,or thatwherein hisestate,
or part thereof,shallbe,or where theremay be any debt or demand owing to
him. (Ky .Stat.,sec.4849.)
§ 4850. Appeals- trialof- time in which to be taken effectof verdict-
argument. An appealmay be taken from the county courtto the circuitcourt
of the same county,and thenceto the courtof appeals, from every judgment
admittinga willto recordor rejectingit. The circuitcourtshalltryboth law
and factunlessa jury be required. The court of appealsshallnot hear any
matter of factpertainingthereto,otherthan such as may be certifiedfrom the
circuitcourt; and the same effectshallbe given to the verdictof a jury in a
willcaseas isgiven to the verdictof a jury in other civilcases. The appeal
to the circuitcourt shallbe withinfiveyears afterrenderingthejudgment of
probateor rejectioninthe countycourt,and prosecutedtothecourtof appeals,
withinone year afterthe finaldecisioninthecircuitcourt. The propounder of
thewillshallhave the rightto concludethe argument in the circuitcourt, and
the courtof appealsshallprescribethecourseof argument inthatcourt. (Id.,
sec .4850. )
§ 4851. Appeal to circuitcourt- when tobe takentostayproceedingsunder
will. An appealto the circuitcourt from an order of a county court,admit-
tinga willto record,or the rejectionof it,shallnot preventthe appointment
of an administratoror executorby the propercourt,and the settlement, dis-
tribution, and divisionof the decedent'sestate, unlessproceedingsare com-
menced in the propercircuitcourtwithintwelvemonths from the enteringof
the order admittingthe willto record or rejectingitby the county court:
Provided, The circuitcourt in which proceedingsmay be thereafterpending
in appealmay make an order restrainingthe furtherdistributionand division
of the estate. (Id., sec.4851.)
§ 4852. Will not receivedas evidenceuntilprobated- effectof probate.
No will shall be received in evidence untilit has been allowed and admitted
to recordby a county court; and itsprobate before such court shallbe con-

§ 471. Kentucky Statutes- concerningadopted.
§ 472. Settlements of fiduciaries---recording of.
§ 471 [520] [Kentucky Statutes- concerningadopted.] The provi-
sionsof article14,chapter28,of theGeneralStatutes,regulatingtheset-
tlement of the accounts of personal representativesand guardians, are
adopted as part of thisCode .*
*This articleof theGeneral Statutesisnow supersededby Sections1062to
1067, both inclusive, of Carroll'sKentucky Statutes, as follows:
JURISDICTION OF THE COUNTY JUDGE .
§ 1062. Settlements with fiduciaries- specialcommissioner - evidence . The
county judge shallmake settlementswith personalrepresentatives, trustees,
assignees, committees, curatorsand guardians in his county; or may , if he
thinksproper, appointa specialcommissioner to make such settlements; evi-
dence may be heard for or againstany item, and alloral evidenceshallbe
reduced to writingby him, and no evidenceshallbe presumed to have been
heard exceptsuch as is reducedto writingand reported. (Ky . Stat..sec.
1062. )
§ 1063. Examination of fiduciaries. He may interrogatefiduciaries,under
oath,touchingany matter connectedwith the settlement, and theirstatements
shallbe reduced to writing; and witnessesmay be summoned at the instance
of eitherpartyto giveevidencewhen the settlementismade ,or upon the trial
of exceptionsthereto, and theirattendancemay be coercd as in other cases.

§ 473 [522] [Who may enter- statementand verificationof.] 1. If

not forthebenefitof theperson againstwhom itisentered.
2. [Copy tobe filedwithcircuitclerk.] A copy of thecaveat,certified
by the register, shall, within sixtydays from the time it is entered, be
lodged with the clerkof the circuitcourt of the county where the land or
thegreaterpartthereoflies. Failuresotolodgesuchcopy shallbe deemed

upon the clerk'scertificateof such failurebeing filedin his office.

lodgedwithinsaidperiod,itshallbe treatedas a petition,and theproceed-
ingsupon itshallbe the same ,includingan appeal to theCourt of Appeals,
as those in an ordinaryaction.
4. [Summons on practice.] If the summons be not returnedin due

shown thatthenon-executionor non-returnwas procuredby theplaintiff
or resultedfrom hisneglect.

of the defendant ,must be delivered into the land officewithin three months
from the time itisrendered; or a new caveat may ,for that cause,be en-
teredagainstthegrant. If thejudgmentbe fortheplaintiff,and a copy

CHAPTER XII .
WRITS OF MANDAMUS AND PROHIBITION .
§ 474. How obtainedand prosecuted.

§ 474 [523] [Writs of- how obtainedand prosecuted.] Writs of
mandamus ,exceptthoseused by a courtfor enforcingitsjudgmentsor
orders,and writsof prohibitionshallbe obtainedby motion,and prose-
cutedin themanner providedin TitleX.,chapter5,exceptthattheap-
plicantshallfilea petition,whereinhe shallstatethecauseand ground of
his applicationbeforegivingnoticeof his motion; to which the party
againstwhom themandamus or prohibitionissoughtshallfilea demurrer
or an answer ,at or beforethe time fixedfor making themotion .
Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr . (3d Ed .), sec. 156a ; Form No. 328.
§ 474. (1) Definition of mandamus .
The writ of mandamus is not now , as
formerly, a high prerogativewrit, ema-
nating from the grace or favor of any
one , but it is a statutory writ or order
of a courtof competent jurisdiction,and
in a proper case is to be granted on the
motion of any party aggrieved, Maddox
v .Graham , 2 Met . 56 .
(2 ) We have no other than peremptory
writs of mandamus ; it can not be in the
alternative . 10 Bush 564 .
(3) Defense against. In a proceeding
by mandamus ,a counter-claim to recover
back money paid on the contract is not
proper; but if the defendant state suffi-
cient reasons for not paying the claim ,
the writ will not be awarded . 18 В. М.
848 .
(4 ) Discretion not controlled. The per-
formance of a plain, positive duty may
be compelledby mandamus , but where
there is discretion as to the result that
may be arrived at, it can not be con-
trolled. If an inferior tribunal has a dis-
cretion, and proceeds to exercise it, its
discretioncan not be controlledby man-
damus , but if it refuses to entertain a
questionwhich the law has enjoinedupon
its consideration, mandamus will lie.
Com .v . Boone Co., 82 Ky . 632, 6 R. 755 ;
see Nelson Co. v . Washington Co., 14 В.
M. 92 ; Dickens v. Cave Hill, 93 Ky . 385,
14 R. 347 , 20 S. W. 282 .
(5) Mandamus will not lie to control
the exerciseof a judicialdiscretion. City
v .Kean , 18 B. M. 9 ; Goheen v.Myers , 18
B. M. 423; Gayle v.Owen Co.,83 Ky .61,
6 R. 789 ; City of Berry ,80 Ky . 354, 4 R.
185 ; Ohio Co. v.Newton ,79 Ky . 267, 2 R.
306 ; Wright v . Baker , 94 Ky . 343, 15 R.
109,22 S. W. 335; Highbaugh v.Hardin
Co.,99 Ky . 16,17 R. 1313,34 S. W. 706;
LeslieCo. v.Wooten , 115 Ky . 850,25 R.
217, 75 S. W. 208 ; Hardison v . Pace , 121
S. W. 671 .
(6) Mandamus liesto compel an in-
feriorjudicialtribunal to act,but not to
controlitsjudgment. Cassidyv.Young ,
92 Ky .227, 13 R. 512, 17 S. W. 481 ; Gail-
brath v.Williams, 106 Ky .431,21 R. 79,
50 S. W. 686 ; to set a court in motion
but not to controlitsdecision. Shine v.
Ky . CentralR. R., 85 Ky . 177,8 R. 748,
3 S. W. 18 .
(7) County courtwillnot be compelled
by mandamus to probate a will where
the court has heard evidence and deter-
mined that it had no jurisdiction, the
remedy is by appeal. Preston v. Fidel-
ity Trust Co., 94 Ky . 295, 15 R. 130, 22
S. W. 318.
385

§ 474 ] [Title XWrits of Mandamus and Prohibition .
(8) Justice of the peace will not be
compelled by mandamus to approve a
traverse bond which he regards as in-
sufficient. McDonald v.Jenkins, 93 Ky .
249 , 14 R. 157,19 S. W. 594.
(9) Election cases when mandamus
proper remedy to compel officerscon-
nectedwith electionsto do theirduty.
Bach v. Spencer ,24 R. 354, 68 S. W. 442 ;
Anderson v. Likens , 104 Ky . 699, 20 R.
1001, 47 S. W. 867 ; Booe v. Kenner , 105
Ky .517,20 R. 1343, 49 S. W. 330 ; Denny
v . Bosworth , 113 Ky . 785 , 24 R. 554, 68
S. W. 1078; Weaver v . Tony , 107 Ky .
419 , 21 R. 1157, 54 S. W. 732 .
(9a ) A voter may institutea proceed-
ing for a mandamus against the clerk to
have a candidate's name placed on the
ballot under the primary election act;
from thejudgment inthat proceedingan
appeal lies. Hager v. Robinson , 154 Ky .
489,157 S.W. 1138; Hager v.Turner,154
Ky . 489, 157 S. W. 1138.
(10) Injunction can not stay . A court
of equity can not enjoinan officerfrom
doing an act which a courtof law has by
writ of mandamus required him to do .
10 Bush 564 .
(11) Interpleader. When a partyhas
appliedfora mandamus againsta minis-
terialofficer(the auditor) to compel him
to issuehis warrant, a thirdparty will
not be allowed to come in and litigate
his claim with the plaintiffupon the
ground that he has a lienupon the debt
due him. Hewitt v. Craig, 86 Ky .23, 9
R. 232,5 S. W. 280 .
(12) Legal duty to perform act must
appear . It must appear upon every ap-
plication for a mandamus that it is the
legalduty of the respondentto do that
which it issought to compel him to do,
and that upon proper applicationhe re-
fused to perform that duty . Lowe v.
Phelps, 14 Bush 642; Norman v. Board
of Mgrs .,93 Ky .537,14 R. 529,20 S. W.
901 .
(13) Mandamus - when granted . Man-
damus is the proper remedy to prevent
the clerk and judge of the county court
from recording the vote under a "local
option" law , if the law is unconstitu-
tional. Gayle v. Owen Co., 83 Ky . 61,
6 R. 789 .
(14) And to compel the clerk of the
county court to record a deed or other
instrument which it is made his duty to
record. Wulftange v. McCollom , 83 Ky .
361 , 7 R. 334 .
(15 ) And to require the county court
to show cause why it did not permit a
deputy sheriffto qualify. Applegate v.
Applegate , 4 Met . 236 ; Prather V.
Strother, 11 R. 831, 13 S. W. 252.
(16) And to compel the county judge
to accept the bond of a sheriffand per-
mit him toqualify. Cate v.Ross,2 Duv .
243; and to compel the State Board of
Pharmacy to enter a " graduate in phar-
macy." StateBoard v. White, 84 Ky .626, 8 R. 678, 2 S. W. 225.
(17) And to compel county court to
levy a tax to pay a medical account for
servicesrenderedto a poor personby re-
quest of the county judge and justices.
Rodman v . Larue Co. , 3 Bush 144.
(18) And to compel county court to
levy a tax to pay for a bridge built,
under a contractwith thecounty.Ander-
son Co. v . Stone , 18 B. M. 848.
(19) And to requiretrusteesof a town
to repairthe streets. Hammar v. City,
3 Met .494 ; Trustees v. Kinner , 12 Bush
334; and pass ordinance striking off ter-
ritory. Steelev.Willis,23 R. 826,64 S.
W. 417 .
(20) And against auditor, to compel
him to pay claims. Auditor v. Adams ,
13 В. М. 151; Haly v. Auditor, 4 Bush
490; 14 Bush 284; 3 Bush 231; 80 Ky .
336; 4 R. 34; 78 Ky . 577, 1 R. 253; 8
Bush 98.
(21) Corporationmay be compelledby
mandamus to elect officers. Orr v .
Bracken Co.,81 Ky .593,5 R. 632.
(22) Where county is divided and a
new county established, the old county
may by mandamus compel the new to
levy a tax to pay indebtedness created
before division. Montgomery Co. v.
Menefee Co.,93 Ky . 33, 13 R. 891, 18 S.
W. 1021 .
(23 ) When a city council isauthorized
and requiredto levy and collecta tax to
pay the interest on bonds , a writ of
mandamus may be maintained by any
of the bondholdersto compel it to dis-
charge that duty. Maddox v. Graham ,
2 Met .56 ; and see, to same effect, Flem-
ing v .Dyer , 20 R. 689. 47 S. W. 444.
(24) A county bondholdermay compel
by mandamus the county court to levy
and collecta tax to pay bonds issued by
the county. ElliottCo. v. Kitchen, 14
Bush 289. And a corporationmay have
the writ to compel the county to issue
bonds in compliancewith a subscription.
C. & O. R. R. Co. v.Washington Co., 10
Bush 564 .
(25 ) Against board of examiners to
compel them to issuecertificateto school
teacher. Northington v. Sublette, 114
Ky .72 ,24 R. 835 ,69 S. W. 1076.
(26) Against Governor to compel him
to issue commission to officer entitled to
386

Title X ] [§ 474Writs of Mandamus and Prohibition .
it. Traynor v. Beckham , 116 Ky . 13, 25
R. 283 ,74 S. W. 1105 ; Cochran v . Beck-
ham , 28 R. 370, 89 S. W. 262 .
(27) To compel city treasurerto ac-
cept proper amount charged for license
and clerk to issue receipt therefor. Met .
Ins. Co. v. Davenkamp , 23 R. 2249, 66
S. W. 1125 .
(28) Against judge of circuitcourt to
requirehim to grant an appeal. Com .v.
Bavarian Brewing Co., 114 Ky . 877, 24
R. 1663,72 S. W. 13 ; and to vacate the
bench on trial of a case . Com . v . Can-
trill, 25 R. 20, 74 S. W. 691; and see
further, Con.,sec. 110,and notes thereto.
(29 ) To compel fiscalcourt to erect
bridge on county road. Leslie Co. v.
Wooten , 115 Ky .850,25 R. 217,75 S. W.
208 ; and allow claim. O'Connor v . Weis-
singer, 142 Ky .452, 134 S. W. 1127.
(29a) A citizentaxpayerof a cityofthe firstclasshas a rightby mandamus
to obtain an inspectionof records in*the engineer'soffice. Barrickman
Lyman , 154 Ky . 630, 157 S. W. 924.
v .
(29b ) Mandamus lies to compel the
performance of a merely ministerial
duty, which is a duty prescribedand
definedby law with such precautionand
certainty as to make it mandatory and
to leave nothing to the exercise of dis-
cretionor judgment. Houston v. Boltz,
169 Ky . 640, 185 S. W. 76.
(30 ) Mandamus - when not granted .
A person who is not eligibleto hold office
can not have a mandamus to compel the
county judge to permit him to qualify,
although he receiveda majority of the
votes cast at the election . Atchinson v .
Lucas, 83 Ky . 451, 7 R. 424; and see
notes 4-8 .
(31) Nor will it lie against officersof
a private corporation. Cook v . College, 9
Bush 541 ; nor to compel the officersof a
benefit society to restore an expelled
member . Schmidt v . Lodge , 84 Ky . 490 ,
S R. 655 , 2 S. W. 156 .
(32) Nor will mandamus lie where
party has any other adequate remedy ,
such as right of appeal. Shine v . Ky .
Central R. R., 85 Ky . 177, 8 R. 748, 3 S.
W. 18 .
tained. Garrard Co. v . McKee , 11 Bush
234 .
(35) Will not lie to compel , perform-
ance of act forbidden by or contrary to
law. Eidson v.Flounbacker,115Ky .534,
24 R. 2441,74 S. W. 198.
(36) Motion for. Failure to make the
motion for a mandamus on the day speci-
fied is a waiver of the right to enter or
make it under that notice . 14 Bush 289 .
(37) Parties to action. Mandamus
against county court to compel it to levy
a tax must be against persons composing
the court. Montgomery Co. v. Menefee
Co., 93 Ky . 33, 13 R. 891, 18 S. W. 1021.
(38) Power of Court of Appeals to
issue writ against inferior courts. See
Con .,sec.110,and Kelly v.Tony ,95 Ky .
338, 15 R. 718, 25 S. W. 264 ; Shoemaker
v . Hodge , 111 Ky . 436, 23 R. 736. 63 S.
W. 979; Terryv. Baker,23 R. 2406, 67
S. W. 259 ; Com . v. Cantrell, 25 R. 20 ,
74 S. W. 691 ; Com . v . Bavarian Brewing
Co., 114 Ky . 877, 24 R. 1663, 72 S. W.
13; Alexander v. Moss , 28 R. 171, 89 S.
W. 118; Montgomery v.Viers,114 S. W.
251, 130 Ky . 694.
(39 ) Successor of officer. A change in
the membership of the board of council-
men does not abate the proceedings.
Maddox v. Graham , 2 Met . 56; City v.
Kean ,18 B. M. 9. As to Auditor, see 3
Bush 231 ; as to board of examiners to
compare poll-books,see casesin note 9.(40 ) Supersedeas . The execution of a
writ of mandamus may be superseded.
83 Ky .451,7 R. 424; Wyatt v.Ryan ,23
R. 1457 , 65 S. W. 129 .
(41) Petition summons . Petition need
not expresslyask for issualof writ,nor
is summons requiredas notice of mo-
tion takes place of summons . Gay v.
Haggard , 118 S. W. 299,133Ky .425.
(42 ) Vacation . Writ should not ex-
cept in rare cases be issued in vacation
on the unsupported averments of the
petition. Hager v . New South Brewing
Co.,90 S. W. 608,28 R. 895.
(43 ) Petition need not be filedten days
before term . Motion may be made ten
days afterpetitionisfiledalthoughitbe
filedduring term. McChesney v.Hager ,
104 S. W. 714, 31 R. 1039.
(44 ) Judge of inferior court against.
Applicationfor writ shouldbe made to
circuitcourt. Montgomery v.Viers, 114
S. W. 251, 130 Ky . 694; Com . v. Peters,
124 S. W. 896,136 Ky . 689.
(33 ) An ex parte allowance made by
the circuit court to guards summoned
by its order to guard the jailis only
prima facie evidence of its correctness,
and, when controvertedby the county
court, mandamus will not lie to compel
the county court to levy a tax to pay
the allowance . Justice's v. Moore , 2
Bush 108 .
(34) Nor is it the proper remedy when
the amount of the demand is not ascer- 689 .
(45) Inventory of estate. County
judge may be compelledby mandamus
to require personal representative to file.
Com . v. Peters, 124 S. W. 896, 136 Ky .

§ 475-477] [Title XWrits of Mandamus and Prohibition .
§ 475 [524] [Courttohearand decide- finalorder.] The courtshall
hearand decideallquestionsof law or factarisingon themotion,and the
grantingor refusingof thewritshallbe thefinalorderon themotion.*
Cross reference: See Miller'sKy .Appellate Practice and Forms , sec. 32.
*By an Act of May 15, 1886, с.1138, p. 111, itwas enacted, "That section
475 of the CivilCode of Practicebe,and itishereby,amended so as to read
as follows, to- wit: The court shallhear and decideallquestionsof law or
factarisingon the motion,and the grantingor refusingof the writ shallbe
the finalorder on the motion. The courtmay ,aftergrantingsuch writ,upon
applicationof the plaintiff, besidesor insteadof proceedingagainstthe de-
fendantby attachment, directthatthe actrequiredto be done may be done by
some otherpersonappointedby the courtat the expenseof the defendant."
By anAct of January13,1888,c.8,p.3,theforegoingactwas "repealed."
See Section464 of the Kentucky Statutes,which isthe same as section22,
of Chapter21,of theGeneral Statutes,thenin effect.----Ed.
§ 476 [525] [Temporary preventiveordersmay be made .] During
thependencyof the motion, the courtor judge,in vacation,may make
temporaryordersforpreventingdamage or injuryto theapplicantuntil
the motion isdecided .
§ 477 [526] [Mandamus defined.] The writ of mandamus ,as treated
of inthischapter,isan orderof a courtof competentand originaljuris-
diction, commanding an executiveor ministerialofficerto perform an act,
or omit to do an act,the performance or omissionof which isenjoinedby
law ; and isgranted on the motion of the party aggrieved, or of the Com-
monwealth when the publicinterestisaffected.
(46 ) Franchise. Private citizen may
have writ to compel publicauthoritiesto
offer for sale a franchise . Lou . Home
Tel. Co. v. City of Louisville, 113 S. W.
855,130 Ky . 611.
(47 ) Contract rights can not be en-
forcedby mandamus . Montenegro -Riehm
Co. v.Board of Ed ., 147 Ky .720, 145 S.
W. 740 .
(48 ) Defense by State should be al-
lowed to be made although not tendered

§ 489. [Sale of realestateof infantsand persons of unsound mind .]
A vestedestateof an infantor of a person of unsound mind ,in realprop-
erty,may be soldby order of a courtof equity--
§ 489. (1) Depositionsmust be taken
on interrogatoriesif all partiesagainst
whom they are to be read are infants
or non -residents. Womble v. Trice, 112
Ky . 533, 23 R. 1939, 66 S. W. 370 ; 67
S. W. 9 .
(2) Foreign guardian can not prose-
cuteactionfor infantsunlessauthorized
in the manner providedby sec.2041,Ky .
Stat. Williams v . Duncan , 92 Ky . 125,

son, 93 Ky . 495,14 R. 463, 20 S. W. 505 ;
Shelby v.Harrison,84 Ky . 144,8 R. 83;
Woolridge v. Woolridge,26 R. 97, 80 S.
W. 775 .
(3 ) Grounds of sale . Under this sec-
tionthe realestateof an infantcan only
be sold for his debts or a liabilitywith
which he is chargeable, or under a decree
in an actionbrought by his statutory
guardian to obtain means for his sup-
port or education or a reinvestment of
the proceeds. Tyler v.Jewell,10 R. 887,
11 S. W. 25. In Lyn v . Lyn , 17 R. 1364,
34 S. W. 1065; the court under the facts
of the case refusedto sellinfants' land
at the instanceof theirguardian for
their maintenance and education .
(5 ) Improvements . Sale or mortgage
of portion of infant's land for the pur-
pose of improvingremainder isvoid,be-
cause not authorized by statute. Hays
v.Bradley ,15 R. 387,23 S. W. 372 ; Posey
v.Dugan , 22 R. 1104, 59 S. W. 862.
(6 ) Purchaser under void sale of in-
fant'srealestate,who has in good faith
erected improvements, is entitled, asagainst the infants, to recover the
amount that improvements have en-
hanced the value of the land . Wornack
v . Loar , 11 R. 6, 11.S. W. 438 ; Hays v .
Bradley, 15 R. 387,23 S. W. 372.
(7 ) Lunatic's land can not be sold
under an executionissuedon a judgment
renderedbeforehe was adjudgedto be of
unsound mind. Buckler v. Reese, 100
Ky . 336,18 R. 959, 38 S. W. 492.
(8) Mortgage of infant's land is not
allowable even under decree of court,
except as provided in sec. 498. Posey v .
Dugan ,22 R. 1104,59 S.W. 862.
(4 ) Guardian ad litem- serviceof proc-
ess- as to guardian ad litem, see secs.
36-38; serviceof process, secs.52-53.
(9 ) Powers of courts of equity to sell
and reinvest infant'sreal estate, or that
of persons under disability, are statu-
tory, and not inherent. Walker v . Smy-
ser, 80 Ky . 620, 4 R. 662; Henning v .
Harrison , 13 Bush 723 ; Liter v. Fish-
back , 25 R. 260, 75 S. W. 232 ; Elliott v .
Fowler , 112 Ky .376,23 R. 1676, 65 S. W.
394

ant refusingto have thesalemade may have partitionaccordingtohis

uary 25, 1876, с.28 , p .3.)

§ 492 ] [Title XSales of Real Property ,Etc.
"ARTICLE V.
" Miscellaneous Provisions .
"§ 2. The court ordering a sale of an infant, idiot, lunatic, or infant mar-
riedwoman's realestate,may causethe proceedsof the same tobe reinvested
in lands in or out of this State, or in United States bonds or bonds of this
Commonwealth , or in the stock of a bank of the latter, subjectto the same
uses, limitations, and trusts as the estate sold was subject to ; if not so rein-
vested, the proceedsof the salesof an infant'srealestateshallbe paid to his
guardian; and thatof an infantmarried woman to thepetitioner,who has exe-
cutedbond as requiredherein; and that of an idiotor lunaticshallbe paid
to his committee, and kept at interest, under the controlof the court; and
shall,with the interest,be paid to thepersonentitledthereto,when orderedby
the court, or at the death of the idiotor lunatic. But the court may order
such part of the proceedsof salesto be laidout for the maintenanceor edu-
cation of an infant, as is authorized by the chapter on Guardian and Ward ;
and such partof the proceedsof the salesof an idiot'sor lunatic'srealestate
to be laidout for his maintenance, as isauthorizedby the chapteron Idiots
and Lunatics .
"§ 6. If landsbe heldby jointtenants,tenantsin common ,or coparceners,

( le) The execution of the bond re-
quired by this sectionin a suitby a com-

it is not essentialto the validity of the
bond that it be signed by the principal.
It is the sufficiency of the sureties to
make the bond and not the solvencyof
the principalthat is looked to. The
principalisalreadybound as fullyas he
couldbe by joiningin the executionof
this bond by the former bond that he
executed when he qualifiedas commit-
tee . Nor did the failure of the commit-
tee to sign the bond deprive it of its
character as a statutory bond or convert
it into a common law bond . It complied
with all the substantial requirements of
this section, and the sureties were as
fullybound as if the principalhad signed
it. The form of the bond given in this
section contemplates that it shall be
signed by the principal, but this form is
not of the substance of the bond . It is
the signatures and sufficiencyof the sure-
tiesthat make the bond valid, and when
they have signedittheirliabilityisfixed
by the terms of the obligation. Allen v .
Jenkins .157 Ky . 406, 163 S. W. 234.

R. 850, 59 S. W. 11.
(3a ) In an action under Civil Code , sec.
490 , subsec. 2 , it is not necessary that
the bond of sec . 493 be executed before
the sale is ordered. Hatterick v . Bruce ,
151 Ky . 12, 151 S. W. 31.
(3b) A guardian in an action under
Civil Code , sec. 490 , executed the bond
required by sec. 493, Civil Code , before
the order of sale ; the share of the infant
did not remain a lien on the land . Scott
v .Graves , 153 Ky . 221, 154 S. W. 1084.
(4 ) Interest on fund to which infants
are entitledmay be paid to their guard-
ian without execution of bond . Louis-
villeTrust Co. v. Kidd , 93 S. W. 38, 29
R. 382.
(5 ) Settlement of estate. Where land
is sold to settle an estate the surplus
due infants remains a lien on land until
bond is executed. Oldham v. McElroy,
121 S. W. 414, 134 Ky . 454. See further
Catlin v . United States F. & G. Co., 125
S. W. 297, 137 Ky .208; Com .v. Catlin,
112 S. W. 665, 129 Ky . 493; Foley v.
Graham , 110 S. W. 838.
(6 ) If by reason of indivisibilityland
is sold to pay the debts of an infant or
his ancestor or devisor, which brings
more than enough to pay the debts and
costs, the provisions of Code , secs. 493
and 497,as to bond and as to lienapply

unlessthe bond of sec.493 isgivenbefore

by a bond given after the sale; nor can it

bility; neitherthe pleadingsnor the evi-
dence showed facts within CivilCode , sec.
490. Melcher v. Yager's Gdn ., 159 Ky .

§ 498. (1) Construction of Act of
1882. Craig v.Wilcox,94 Ky .484, 14 R.
908, 22 S. W. 76 ; Burge v . F. T. & S. V.
Co., 112 Ky . 683, 23 R. 1925, 66 S. W.
763; Bullockv. Gudgell,25 R. 1413,77 S.
W. 1126; Goff v.Renick , 156 Ку .588, 161

(2) This statute does not apply to
land that is not held in trust by one per-
son for the life of another . Walton v .
Cleneay ,152 Ky .274,153 S. W. 423.
(3) Where a father owned a lifeestate
in land and his infant child was the re-
mainderman ,and the lifeestate was sold
for debt and the property is subjectto
liens, a judicialsale of the land at the
suit of the guardian of the infant re-
mainderman , under this section, for half
its value, will not be confirmed. Buck-
ner's Trustee v. Buckner , 168 Ky . 302,
181 S. W. 1107 .
(4) Under this statute, an .action for
the sale of real estate held in trust for
reinvestment should be brought by the
trusteeagainst the party'sremainder-
men either for judicial sale or for ap-
proval of a private sale provisionally
made . Noel v. Harper, 170 Ky .657, 186
S. W. 503 .
(5) Under this statute the court, on
applicationof a trustee under a will, has
the power to direct the sale of parcels
of business property for reinvestment
of the proceeds. Gillespiev. Winston's
Trustee , 170 Ky . 667, 186 S. W. 517.
(6) Land held by a woman for life
with remainder to her son if living at
her death or remarriage, otherwisere-
verting to grantor is within the Act of
April24,1882,partofthissection.Lowry
v. Berry, 178 Ky . 656, 199 S. W. 809.
(7 ) A lifetenant borrowed money with
which to make repairs and improve-
ments on land in which his children
owned the remainder in fee. An action
was broughtby the lenderof the money
to subject the land. The court had no
jurisdictionto sellthe remainder inter-
est of the infants . In this case the life
tenant and the trustee appointed by the
will under which title was obtained to
take charge of the life tenant and the
remaindermen,had executeda mortgage
to the creditor. Buckner v. Kelly, 151
Ky . 818, 152 S. W. 963.
(8) Validityof theamendment can not
now be questioned. Marshall v. Ander-
son's Trustee ,157 Ky .117, 162 S. W. 559.
(9 ) "That when lands are held in trust
by one person for the lifeof another ,"
includesproperty held in trust jointly
for two or more persons. Marshall v.
Anderson'sTrustee, 157 Ky . 117, 162 S.
W. 559 .
(10) A provision in a will empowering
the trustee named therein to sell and
convey the real estate in his discretion,
but withholdingsuch power from any
subsequent trustee will not be construed
as forbiddinga sale by the chancellor
for reinvestment under Civil Code , Car-
roll's, sec. 498 (Seymour's , sec. 491),
after another trustee has qualified. Mar-
shall v. Anderson's Trustee , 157 Ky . 117,
162 S. W. 559 .
(11) Where a testamentary trustee
holds lands in trust under the terms of a
will for the lives of two beneficiaries,
with remainder over to a class of per-
sons who can not be ascertained until
the death of the survivor of the two life
tenants, it is within the power of a chan-
cellorunder the provisions of the amend-
ment of 1882 , Civil Code , Carroll's, sec.
498 (Seymour's ,sec.491 ), to approve the
saleby the trusteeand reinvestthe pro-
ceeds. Marshall v . Anderson's Trustee ,.
157 Ky . 117, 162 S. W. 559.

§ 500. (1) Death of some of partieswill
not prevent prosecution of action against

Russell,24 R. 704, 69 S. W. 965.

(2) Where one member of a firm died
no revivor was necessary as the action
could be prosecuted in the name of the
surviving partners after a statement on
the record of the death of the deceased
member . Robinson v . Bank , 108 Ky .389,
22 R. 42 , 56 S. W. 660 .
(2a ) Death of personal representative.
An administratrix brought suit on notes
held by the decedent; upon her death
revivor ought not to have been made in
the name of her personal representative,
but in the name of her successor . Robert-
son v. Robertson's Admr ., 174 Ky . 836 ,
192 S. W. 878 .
(2b ) Deed - commissioners may be made
without revivor. It is not necessary to
revive an action for the purpose of mak-
ing a deed on account of the death of a
party after the sale has been confirmed.
Likens v. Pate, 160 Ky . 319, 169 S. W.
734 .
(3) Meaning of survives. It was held
under the Code of 1854 that the word
" survives," used in sec. 557 (sec. 500 of
this Code )," is used in its technical sense ,
and only appliesto cases in which the
right survives by operation of law , or
operation of the will of contract under
which the parties claim or are charged."
Smith v. Ferguson , 3 Met .424 .

§ 515. Void judgment - motion must
be made to set aside before appeal lies,
sec. 763 , and judgment to sell infant's
land without bond is void. Baldridge v.
Baldridge , 117 S. W. 253.
§ 516. Misprision not ground of ap-
peal until acted on in lower court. Mor-
rison v.Beckham ,96 Ky .72,16 R. 294, 27
S. W. 868 ; Armendt v . Perkins , 17 R.
1327, 32 S. W. 270 ; Baldridge v. Bald-
ridge, 117 S. W. 253 ; Jones v . Hazard
Dean Coal Co., 169 Ky . 588, 184 S. W.
1131 .
§ 517. (1) Clericalmisprision defined.
" The rule seems to be that whenever the
error complained of isascertained to con-
sist in the mistake of the clerk and not
in the judgment of the court,and there
exists anything in the record by which
it can be amended , the applicationshould
be made to amend in the court below ,
and , until thus made and refused, such
an error is not available for reversal in
this court." Dodds v . Combs , 3 Met . 29.
(2) "A clericalmisprision can only be
shown by the record. This is proved by
the familiarrule that a misprisioncan
only be corrected by the record." Ben-
nett v. Tiernay, 78 Ky . 580, 1 R. 312;
Boyd Co. v . Ross , 95 Ky . 167, 15 R. 520,
25 S. W. 8 .
(3) Correction of. Clericalmisprision
can not be corrected after the expiration
of the term at which the judgment was
rendered, except after notice to advrse
party, and where court attempts to do
so withoutnoticethe correctedjudgment
is void, and no appeal liesfrom it until
motion is made to set it aside. Seiler v .
Northern Bank , 86 Ky . 128, 9 R. 497, 5
S. W. 536 ; and see Treasy v . Moore , 18
R. 421 , 36 S. W. 1132, and notes to sec.
519.
(4 ) When the record of the trial court
is amended to correct a clerical mis-
prision, the effectis to make the amend-
ed recordthe only recordon the appeal.
Chambers v. Swango , 22 R. 923, 59 S.
W. 20.
(5) Instances of misprision. An error
in the judgment as to the date from
which interestis to be computed . Clark
v. Finnell, 16 В. М. 329 ; Johnson v .
Bank , 2 Duv . 521 ; Wilson v . Barnes , 13
B. M. 330 ; Wren v . Cooksey , 145 S. W.
1116, 147 Ky . 825 ; or to render judgment
for protest fees not claimed in the peti-
tion, 16 B. M. 329, is a misprision, and

so isa judgment forinterestinconsistent
with the prayer of the petition, Arınendt

note in a judgment, if improper, was a
clericalmisprision. Brady v. Equitable
Trust Co.,178 Ky .693 ,199 S. W. 1082.
(5b ) In a certain case the adjudging
to plaintiffa sum exceedingthe amount

242 .
(6b ) The rendition of judgment on a
note for more than is claimed in the

prision. Bratcher v.Ohio Co. Bank's As-

(6d) A demurrer to an answer was
overruled; afterward an order was en-
tered submitting the same demurrer;
afterward the action was submitted and
the courtdismissedthe petition. With-
out a motion in the lower court to vacate
the judgment , the Court of Appeals can
not review this action . Com . v . Pruden-

921 .
(7 ) In an action upon two notes fail-
ure to embrace one of them in a judg-
inent by default, Smith v. Mullins , 3
Met . 182 ; or to give judgment in favor
of A and B on a note alleged to have
been sold by A to B , and asking judg-
ment in B's favor alone,Oldham v .Bran-
non , 2 Met . 302 ; or where , pending an
ahction on a note in favor of A , an
amended petitionisfiledsettingup that
the note has been assigned to B , and
asking judgment in his favor alone, giv-
ing judgment in favor of A isa clerical
misprision. Cooper v. Poston , 1 Duv . 92.
(8) It isa misprisionto renderjudg-
ment before action stood for trial. Web-
ber v. Webber , 1 Met . 18; or to give
judgment upon an award not delivered
to the parties ten days before the com-
mencement of the term at which judg-

Met . 434 ; or upon constructive service
prematurely. Buckner v. Bush , 1 Duv .
394; or to give judgment againstsome
of the defendants before all the neces-

Breeding v. Stamper, 18 B. M. 175; or
to renderjudgment by defaultagainsta
defendantwho has fileda demurrer that
remains undisposed of. Warford v.Tem-
ple,24 R. 2268 ,73 S. W. 1023.
(9) It is misprisionto render judg-
ment against a defendant not summoned
in the county where action is brought ,
before judgment is or can be rendered
against a defendant summoned in the
county. Duckworthv.Lee,10 Bush 54;
Pottingerv. Mayfield, 14 B. M. 647; or
to render judgment by default where
summons is executed in the county in
which suit is brought not in time , and
out of that county in time , on the serv-
ice in the lattercounty. Raymon v.
Reed , 16 B. M. 345 ; see Ruby v. Grace , 2
Duv .540; or in an actionagainstseveral,
one of whom is a nominal and unneces-
saryparty, torenderjudgmentagainstthe " defendants " without discrimination .
C., H. & D. R. R. Co. v. Spratt, 2 Duv .
4; or to renderjudgment againstinfants
before defense filed. Morrison v . Beck-
ham ,96 Kv . 72,76 R. 294,27 S. W. 868.
(10) Mistake of clerk in entering
amount ofjudgment may be correctedby
motion,Vissman v.Bryant,14 R. 874,21
S. W. 759; as may an orderenteredby
mistake of clerk. Boro v . Holtzhauer ,
23 R. 2317 ,67 S. W. 30.
(11) Limitation. There is no limita-
tionexceptsuchas pertainsto thebring-
ing of actions to a motion to correct a
clericalmisprision. Lawless v. Sevier, 5
R. 239; 3 Met . 182; Com . v. Caudill, 28
R. 520, 89 S. W. 535.
(12) Not misprision. Failure of the
court to renderjudgment in conformity
with the law; Rogers v.Bradford,8 Bush
163; or to givejudgment on an invalid
bailbond, Pauer v. Simon , 6 Bush 514;
or to renderpersonaljudgment against
a defendant constructivelysummoned ,
Payne v.Witherspoon, 14 B. M. 270; is
a judicialerror; and seeDeshong v.Cain,
1 Duv . 309 .
(13) To renderjudgment againsta de-
fendant who has not been summoned ,
Joyce v. O'Toole, 6 Bush 31; Long v.
Montgomery ,6 Bush 394 (the lattercase
overrulingRobinson v. Mobley, 1 Bush
196); or who has been summoned out
of the county in which the action is

formity to opinion of Court of Appeals

(5) Where a judgment is renderedby
default against a garnishee, he can not
have it vacated on the ground that the
note on which he owed the defendant
the money garnisheedhad been assigned
by the defendant to another person be-
fore the judgment was rendered, and he
was unable , after diligent inquiry, to
learnthatfactuntilafterthejudgment
had been rendered . Coburn v . Currens ,
1 Bush 242 .
(6) Where his principalcounselwas
prevented from attendingthe court by
sickness, the plaintiffwas not entitledto
have the judgment againsthim vacated
on that ground,when he did not attend
the trialor make an effortto provide
other counsel. Landrum v . Farmer , 7
Bush , 46. See Yowell v. Gaines , 2 Bush
211 , and Triplett v. Scott, 5 Bush 81 ;
and notes 1-10, sec. 340 .
(7 ) A new trialwill not be granted on
account of neglect of parties or their
attorneys, but where the illnessof coun-
sel prevents his attention to case and
there is no neglect on the part of the
client a new trial should be granted .
Bone v. Blankenbaker , 24 R. 1438, 71 S.
W. 638 .
(8 ) A new trial should be granted
where the casualty or misfortune pre-
venting a party or his attorney from
appearing is such as ordinary care could
not guard against. Vittelow v . Ames ,
21 R. 225, 51 S. W. 1 ; Snelling v. Lewis ,
25 R. 1856, 78 S. W. 1124 ; French v .
Eversole , 17 R. 617, 32 S. W. 211 ; Cooley
v . Barbourville Land Co., 19 R. 1454, 43
S. W. 464 .
(9 ) A non -resident temporarily in this
State was sued by several parties, and
destroyedthe copiesof the summonsservedon him . Afterward he employed
counsel and went to the clerk's office to
look at the suits,but the clerk could not
find one of them , and judgment in that
case went against him by default. The
defendant under the facts in the case
was entitled to a new trial. McCall v .
Hitchcock , 9 Bush 66.
(10 ) That the defendant was of un-
sound mind (although not judicially
found to be ), and in consequence thereof
incompetent to make an intelligentde-
fense to the action, is such a misfortune
within the meaning of sec.579-7.Code of
1854 (same as sec. 518-7 of this Code ), as
will authorize the court to vacate or
modify a judgment against him . Bean
v . Hoffendorfer, 84 Ky . 685, 8 R. 739 , 2
S. W. 556 ; 3 S. W. 138 .
(10a) In an actionby a foreigncorpo-
ration to vacate a judgment against it
and obtain a new trial on the ground
that its agent on whom the summons

tained where the petition shows that

made no effortto defend. Noe v . Davis ,

set aside at the next term . Com . v .
Hurst , 149 Ky .435 , 149 S. W. 866.
(10d) Time until a certain rule day

setting up his claim. Before that rule
day he lodgedhis answer with the clerk
of the court to be filedon that rule day ,

notice thereof . The clerk failed to file
the answer . A judgment barring the de-
fendant's claim was affirmed . Kohlman
v .Moore ,175 Ky .710, 194 S. W. 933.
(10e) A new action. An action to set
asidea judgment and for new trialfor
unavoidable casualty or misfortune is
a new and independentaction,and brings
nothing to the attention of the court ex-
ceptwhat ispleaded. Noe v. Davis, 171
