(11) Clericalmisprision- see sec. 517
and notes thereto,
(12) Common law judgment - correct-
tionof in equity. See sec. 17 and notes
thereto .
(13) Death of party before appeal. A
judgment of the Court of Appeals re-
versing a judgment can not be disre-
garded because of the death of the ap-
pellantbefore the appeal is taken. His
death afterthe judgment in the circuit
courtand before appeal was prosecuted
did not take from the Court of Appeals
the right to entertain the appeal. His
death , if known , could have been taken
advantage of as providedin thissection
and sec.520. Spaldingv.Wathen ,7 Bush
659 .
(14 ) Erroneous judgment - court has
no power to correct it after term - rem-
edy isby appealunlessitcan be vacated
for some of the reasons mentioned in this
or sec.340. Lovelacev. Lovell, 107 Ky .
676, 21 R. 1433, 55 S. W. 549; Algee v .
Algee ,168 Ky , 362,182 S. W. 197; Noe v .
Davis ,171 Ky .482,188 S. W. 457; Naylor
v .Brown , 160 Ky . 617, 169 S. W. 983.
( 14a ) Courts of continuous session . A
circuit court of continuous session can
not set aside a judgment rendered by it
after sixty days from the date of rendi-
tion. Tritschv. City of Cov ., 161 Ку .
171, 170 S. W. 518.
(14b) Fiscal court orders. An order of
the fiscalcourt may be attacked under
this section. Kenton Co. v .Jameson , 150
Ky .440, 150 S. W. 528.
(15) Fraud. Money collectedunder a
judgment at law fraudulentlyobtained
may be recovered back in equity without
awarding a new trialof the ordinaryaс-
tionor settingasidethe judgment there-
in. Ellis v. Kelly , 8 Bush 621 .
(16) A cause once properly adjudged
should never be reopened at the instance
of one defendant on the ground of im-
puted omissions of his attorney and
fraud of his co-defendant . Dillingham v .
Mudd , 1 Bush 102; and see Baker v .
Grundy . 1 Duv .281.
(17) As to setting aside judgment ren-
dered by consent of an attorney who had
no authority to consent, see Smith v .
Dixon , 3 Met . 438 .
(18) The fact that there is neitheralle-
gation,exhibitsnor proof to sustainthe
judgment does not authorize itsvacation
by the circuitcourt. Anderson v . Ander-
son , 18 B. М. 95 .
(19 ) Infants . An infant defendant is
entitled to a new trial under this section
when the condition of the infant does
not appear in the record, nor the error
in the proceedings. Jamison v . Petit, 6
Bush 669 ; Bunnell v . Bunnell , 23 R. 800,
64 S. W. 420 ; Cox v . Story , 80 Ky . 64 ,
3 R. 551 ; Berryhill v. Holland , 99 S. W.
902, 30 R. 832, 124 Ky . 615; Spradlin
v . Stanley , 30 R. 928, 99 S. W. 965, 124
Ky . 701 ; but if error appears in the rec-
ord the remedy is by appeal. Ogden v.
Stevens , 98 Ky .564, 17 R. 1115. 33 S. W..
932; Oliverv.Park,101 Ky .1,19 R. 179,
39 S. W. 423 .
(19a) The failureof a petition for the
sale of a ward's real estate for his edu-
cation to state that the sale was neces--
sary for the maintenance and education
of the ward , and the failureof the judg-
ment order of sale to so state , renders
the judgment viodable only ,and is bind-
ing until remedied by appeal by the ward
within one year under this section after
removal of disability, or by proceeding
under secs. 391. 518 , within such time to
vacate or modify the judgment . Harris
v .Hopkins , 166 Ky . 147, 179 S. W. 14.
(19b ) If an infant under fourteen
years of age be served by delivery to her
of the summons and not as provided in
436

Title XII ] [§ 518ProceedingstoReverse and Vacate Judgments .
this section, and if the record of the
actionshows untruly that she was then
over fourteen years of age, the judg-
ment can not be collaterallyattacked.
Reliefmay be had under Code, sec. 518.
Fraize v.Walls, 180 Ky . 168, 202 S. W.
310 .
(20 ) See further, notes to sec. 391;
and Richards v.Richards , 10 Bush 617.
(21) Judgment can not be attackedor
vacated in a collateralproceedingor ex-
cept in the manner pointed out in the
Code , nor will partiesbe permitted to
defeata judgment reliedon as a counter-claim by a collateralattack on it.
Couchman v . Bush , 26 R. 1277, 83 S. W.
1039 .
(22 ) Judicialsales. The power of the
court to vacate an order confirming a
judicialsale,afterthe expirationirationof the
term,isnot dependentupon the existence
of a valid defense to the cause of action
or claim sued on . The question as to
the validityof the saleis distinctfrom
that in regard to the judgment under
which it is made , and there may exist
grounds forsettingasidea salewhich do
not affectthe judgment. Bean v. Hof-
fendorfer, 84 Ky . 685, 8 R. 739, 2 S. W.
556 ; 3 S. W. 138; Kincaid v . Tutt , 88
Ky . 392 , 10 R. 1006, 11 S. W. 297; Costi-
gan v. Truesdale,26 R. 971,83 S.W. 98.
(22a ) Under this section, where the
answer of a doweress in an action to
partition land to which her interestat-
taches, which is tendered at the next
term of court after the sale and confirma-
tion, and which does not constitute a
proper applicationfor new trialunder
this section,should be rejected. Vander-
pool'sHeirs v. Vanderpool'sHeirs and
Creditors, 171 Ky . 381, 188 S. W. 461.
(22b ) The fact that land is directed in
the judgment to be sold as a whole is not
conclusive in the circuit court as to the
fact that it is indivisible. On exceptions
to the report of sale the question may
be brought up . Should the sale be set
aside for thiscause the judgment may be
so modified as to direct on the resale a
sale in parcels. Guest v.Joster,159 Ky .
1 , 166 S. W. 620 .
(23) Limitation. There is no limita-
tion exceptsuch as appliesto the bring-
ing of actions to a proceeding to vacate
a judgmentforfraud. Lawlessv.Sevier,5 R. 239 ; L. & N. R. Co. v . Paynter , 101
S. W. 935 ,31 R. 163 ,125 Ky . 520.
(24) As a judgment againsta married
woman may be valid, in a proceeding to
enforce such a judgment it should not be
held conclusivelyto be void; but the de-
fendant may show that it is void. Par-
sons v. Spencer, 83 Ky . 305, 7 R. 329;
and see Spencer v . Parsons , 89 Ky . 577,
11 R. 769, 13 S. W. 72; Bagly v.Champ ,
83 Ky . 13, 6 R. 250. Personaljudgment
can be rendered against married woman ,
Ky . Stat ., sec. 2128.
(25 ) Motion for new trialisnot neces-
sary by the party whose judgment has
been set aside in an action for a new
trialbefore he can appeal. Reinicke v .
Morse , 10 R. 767, 10 S. W. 468.
(26 ) New trial. See secs. 340-344 and
notes thereto for causes authorizing
grantingof new trial,and rulesof prac-tivetiverelatrelatingthereto.
(27) Non -residents- vacationof judg-
ments by; seesec.414 and notesthereto.
(27a ) Partition. This section applies
to judgments of partitionrenderedby a
county court. Howard v.Deems ,178 Ky .
760, 199 S. W. 1079.
(27b ) Pleading. An allegation that
the petitionerdid not discoverthat the
judgment had been rendereduntilafter
the term at which it was entered and
could not sooner have discovered it by
reasonable diligenceis a mere conclusion
of law . A certainpetitionwas held to be
bad on demurrer. Beasly v. Furr, 154
Ky .286,157 S.W. 10.
(27c) Certain pleadingsin a case in
which a judgment was attacked under
subsecs. 5 and 7 were sufficient; a new
and independent action was not neces-
sary, althoughthat would be the better
practice. Sou .Nat .Life Ins.Co. v.Ford's
Admr .,151 Ky .476,152 S.W. 243.
(28) Power of court over its judg-
ments . The court has no power to cor-
rect its judgment after the term , except
for a clericalmisprision. Clark v. An-
derson , 13 Bush 111 ; but it may and
should disregard its own order, if it is
void. Jones v.Com ., 2 Duv . 81.
(29 ) The fact that there is no allega-
tion in the petition, and no proof to up-
hold the judgment, is not one of the
grounds forwhich a judginentcan be va-
cated. The power of the court to vacate
itsjudgments and award new trialsafter
the term is confined strictlyto the causes
mentioned in this section . Anderson v ..
Anderson ,18 B. M. 95 ; Hocker v .Gentry ,
3 Met . 463 ; Scott v . Scott , 9 Bush 174 .
(30) Right to new trial. After ex-
pirationof term, defendantswho are
summoned and failed to answer are not
entitled to a new trial merely because
of errors in judgment that would author-
ize its reversal. Coffey v. Proctor, 14
R. 415. 20 S. W. 286 .
(30a) In a suit seeking to vacate a
judgment and obtain a new trial, the
437

§ 518 ] [Title XIIProceedings to Reverse and Vacate Judgments .
courtmay set asidethe judgment in the
originalaction,and alsodeterminefinally
the rights of the parties, or the court
may set aside the judgment and leave
the rights of the parties to be finallyde-
termined on the hearing of the original
action. Gaar , Scott & Co. v . VanHook ,
162 Ky . 332, 172 S. W. 680.
(30b) A defendantin a judgment who
sues for a new trial under this section
must show that he has a valid defense to
the actionas wellas that he has ground
to vacatethe judgment. Burks v.Doug
lass, 156 Ky . 462, 161 S. W. 225 .
(31 ) The "successful party " does not
necessarilymean the one in whose favor
the judgment is rendered or who is en-
titledto enforceit, but appliesas well
to the defendantin the judgment. Law-
less v . Sevier, 5 R. 239 .
(32) Unsigned orders- minutes or or-
ders entered on last day of term may be
correctedor disapprovedby the courtat
next term before the order book con-
taining them is signed. Fristoe v.Gillen,
26 R. 149 , 80 S. W. 823 .
(33) Vacation after appeal. A judg-
ment may be vacated for any of the
causes mentioned in this section, even
afteran affirmanceby the Court of Ap-
peals,providedthe errorcouldnot have
been noticedby the Court of Appeals.
In such cases the petition should not be
fileduntil the mandate of the Appellate
Court has been carried into effect. Mc-
Lean v . Nixon , 18 B. M. 768 ; Scott v .
Scott, 9 Bush 174 ; Speak v . Mattingly ,
4 Bush 310 ; Fugate v .Gill, 99 S. W. 602,
30 R. 731 .
(34) Judgment that has been affirmed
by Court of Appeals, or enteredin pur-
suance to itsmandate , may be vacated
in lower court, but not for errors that
couldhave been correctedon the appeal.
Maddox v. Williams, 87 Ky . 147. 9 R.
975 , 7 S. W. 907 .
(34a ) If a circuitcourt misunderstands
the mandate of the Court of Appeals ,and
in consequence of that misunderstanding
enters an erroneous judgment the remedy
forthe errorisby appeal. JellicoHard-
ware Co. v . Pine Mountain R. Co., 180
Ky . 118,201 S. W. 450.
(35) Void judgment may be resistedin
any court,and not merely incourtwhich
renderedit. The provisionsauthorizing
the modificationand vacationof judg-
ments apply where a judgment is erro-neous ,but need not be resorted to when
judgment is void. Stevens v. Deering,
10 R. 393, 9 S. W. 292 ; Spencer v . Par-
sons, 89 Ky . 577, 11 R. 769, 13 S. W.
72; Francisv.Lilly,98 S. W. 996,30 R.
891, 124 Ky . 230; and se L. Iron Co. v .
S. Iron & SteelWorks ,91 Ky . 191,12 R.
848 , 15 S. W. 183 .
(35a) A judgment of a court of equity
approvingan exchangeof landsmade by
the guardian of inants is void, and not
simplyerroneous. Fordv.May ,157Ky .830, 164 S. W. 67.
(36) Sickness that prevents party
from making defense isground for grant-
ing new trial. Baker v.Owensboro Sav-
ingsBank , 140 Ky . 121, 130 S. W. 969;
Ray v. Arnett, 106 S. W. 828,32 R. 562.
(37) Scope of section. In every action
or proceedingby whatever name itmay
be calleda party who by frard,casualty
or misfortune, ispreventedfrom having
a hearing may obtain reliefunder this
section. Com . v. Weissinger, 143 Ky .
368 , 136 S. W. 875 .
(38) Failure to advertise land sold at
judicial sale does not render judgment
void . it is only erroneous . Bank v . Gard-
ner, 121 S. W. 608, 134 Ky . 632 .
(39) Fraud of successful party that
resultsin obtainingjudgment isground
fornew trial. May v. Vaughn ,91 S. W.
273, 28 R. 1088 ; Hall v . Wright , 127 S.
W. 516 , 137 Ky . 39 ; 138 Ky . 71 ; Estep
v.Estep,99 S.W. 280,30 R. 578,138 Ky .
71 .
(39a) Where a defendantisprevented
from making defense to an action by the
promise of the attorneyfor the plaintiff
to advise him should an offer of com-
promise be rejected, and that pending
said offer, defendant need make no de-
fense to the action; and the attorney for
plaintiff, without notice to defendant ,
takes a defaultjudgment, a new trial
will be granted. Kingsley v. Daniels,
157 Ky . 194, 162 S. W. 813.
(39b)A consent or agreed judgment
procured by frand may be vacated under
this section. Com . v . Helm , 163 Ky . 69 ,
173 S. W. 389 .
(39c) Though a judgment contained a
recital which is false and obtained by
fraud, the judgment will not be vacated
therefor if it would have been valid with-
out the recital. Anderson v . City Nat .
Bank of Cairo, 153 Ky . 268 , 155 S. W.
385 .
(39d ) A defendant who seeks to set
aside a judgment on the ground of fraud ,
must plead and prove sufficientgrounds
to authorize the vacation and the de-
fense which he would have interposed
except for the fraud. Gaar -Scott & Co.
v . VanHook , 162 Ky . 332, 172 S. W. 680 .
(39e) The purchaser of land at a de

cretalsalebecomes a party to the pro-
ceedingsfrom the time the reportof sale
ismade , and reliefmay be had against

more,171 Ky .575, 188 S.W. 666.
(40 ) Mandate of Court of Appeals di-
rectingjudgmentcan notbe modifiedby
amended pleadings, the only remedy is
by applicationfora new trialunder this
section. Western Bank v. Coldeway , 94
S. W. 1, 29 R. 651; Fugate v. Gill, 99

(42 ) Relief against erroneous judg-
ment may be obtainedby appealor under

W. 984,30 R. 375.
(43 ) Non -resident who does not pro-
ceed under sec.414 or thissectionto have
judgment set aside is without remedy
unlessjudgment isvoid. Highland L. &
B. Co. v . Andas , 110 S. W. 325.
(44) Infants- when judgmentordering

Clifton v . Pferman , 110 S. W. 406 .
(45) Accident that prevents party
from attending court is ground for new
trial. Hargis v.Begley, 129 Ky .477, 112
S. W. 602 .
(46) Married women are excepted
from secs.391 and 517 and occupy same

First Nat . Bank , 136 Ky . 362, 124 S. W.
360 .
(47) Final judgment can only be set
aside after term under this section . Wil-
liamson v. American Reserve Bond Co.,
140 Ky . 215 , 130 S. W. 1072.
(47a ) A commissioner's report in an
action to settle a decedent's estate dis-
allowed a claim. An order confirming
this report was a finaljudgment against
the claim . The circuit court after the
term had no power to vacate or modify
same in the absence of some of the

(48 ) Land erroneously included in and
sold under judgment may be recovered
under this section. Winkler v . Peters,
142 Ky . 83 ,133 S. W. 1144.
when party defendant has no noticeof
suitthat goes to judgment on a process

accept service and who fails to notify
defendant . Chicago Life Ins. Co. v . Rob-
ertson, 147 Ky . 61, 143 S. W. 740; see
also American Patriots v . Kinkead , 144
Ky .662,139S.W. 834.
(51 ) New evidence to impeach witness
-discovery of not ground for new trial
unless the discovered evidence is con-
vincingand would change resultof trial.
L. & N. R. Co. v. Ueltschi, 126 Ky . 556,
104 S. W. 320 .
(49 ) Relief under this section can not
be had when the party could have ob-
tained a new trial under sec. 340 , or an
appeal had he been diligent. Com . v,
Weissinger , 143 Ky .368, 136 S. W. 875.
(50) Casualty or misfortune exists
(52 ) An action was brought to vacate
a judgment and securea new trialin a
case as to the ownership of land. Upon
the evidence it was held that a charge
of fraud was not proven and the newly
discovered evidence was not such as
would warrant the setting aside of the
judgment. Murphy v.Lowe ,161Ky .243,
170 S. W. 613 .
(53) Criminal cases- section does not
apply . This section has no reference to
convictions of crime . The rules as to ob-
tainingnew trialsin criminalcasesareembraced in Criminal Code , secs. 271 to
274. Wellington v . Com ., 159 Ky . 462,
167 S. W. 427 .
(54) Wills- section does not apply .
This section has no application to judg-
ments of the county court probating or
rejectinga will. Patton v . Salle; Jett v.
Jett, 159 Ky . 285, 166 S. W. 1004.
(55) Appeal and action for new trial.
A party who has been defeated in an ac-
tion may prosecutean appeal and an
action for a new trialat the same time ,
when the ground upon which he seeks
a new trial is one of which he could not
avail himself on the appeal. Rice v.
Blair, 161 Ky . 280, 170 S. W. 657.
(56 ) Effect of other remedies . An ac-
tion to vacate a judgment can not be
maintained if it appears that the party
seeking to vacate the judgment has other
adequate remedies. Staton v . Staton ,
164 Ky . 688, 176 S. W. 21.
(57 ) Stranger to action- motion by .
A motion by one not a party to the ac-

swer , can not be treated as an applica-
tionforthe vacationof a judgment afterthe term . Houston v . Com ., 169 Ky .
445 , 184 S. W. 388 .
(58 ) Judgment after continuance. The
rendition of a judgment at a term after
an order had been entered for a continu-
ance of the case to another term is not
ground for vacating the judgment . Stat-
on v . Staton , 164 Ky . 698, 176 S. W. 21.

§ 519. (1) Misprision- how corrected.
A misprisioncan not be correctedby an
action; the proper mode is by motion in
the court renderingthe judgment (Mc-
Cown v. Macklin , 7 Bush 308 ); and no-
ticeshould be given to all partiesaf-
fected by the correction. Oldham v .
Brannon , 2 Met . 302 ; Seiler v . Northern
Bank , 86 Ky . 128, 9 R. 497, 5 S. W. 536 ;
Tracy v.Moore ,18 R. 421,36 S.W. 1132;
and the application should be made in
thecourtrenderingrenderingthejudgment,andif
it refusesto make correctionan appeal
may be taken. Dodds v. Combs ,3 Met .
29; Com . v.Caudill, 121 Ky . 537, 28 R.
520 , 89 S. W. 535 .
(2) There is no restrictionor limita-
tion as to the time in which a clerical
misprision,excepta premature judgment,
shall be corrected. A misprision made
in 1856 was corrected in 1859. Smith v .
Mullins ,3 Met . 182; Lawless v. Seiver, 5
R. 239 ; but a misprision occurring in a
judgment in another State, upon which
suit is brought in this State can not be
correctedhereas a misprision. Reynolds
v . Powers , 96 Ky . 481, 17 R. 1059, 29 S.
W. 299 .
(3) Premature judgment. Motion to
correct must be made within the first
three days of the succeeding term .Wing-
fieldv . Cotton , 9 R. 275 , 56 S. W. 813 ;
Delker v . Evans , 23 R. 2451 , 67 S. W.
837 ; Potter v . Potter, 101 S. W. 905, 31
R. 137 .
(4 ) Limitation. Time in which pro-
ceedingsmay be institutedto vacatejudgment. Com .v.Caudill,121 Ky .537,
89 S. W. 535 , 28 R. 520 .
§ 520 (1) Judgment - vacation of-
appeal. A judgment vacatinga former

from the originaljudgment againsthim.

O. & K. R. R., 1 R. 399. A judgment of
the Court of Appeals can not be vacated
by a billfiledin that court. Beazley v .
Marshon , 6 Bush 424 .

§ 527. Writings - when read as genuine .
§ 525 [586] [Party holdingaffirmativemust prove it.] The party
holding the affirmativeof an issuemust produce the evidenceto prove it.
(And introduceevidencefirst,sec.317.)
§ 526 [587] [Burthen of proof- who has.] The burthenof proof in
the whole actionlieson the party who would be defeated ifno evidence
were given on eitherside.
§ 525. (1) Affirmative of issue. In an
action on a written contract, if the de-
fendant denies the execution and deliv-
ery of the contract the burden of proof
is on the plaintiff. Thompson v. Ever-
sole,162 Ky . 836, 173 S. W. 165.
(2) In an action by the health officer
against the county to recover for serv-
ices rendered in an epidemic, on the
issue as to whether certain persons were
financiallyable to pay the bills, the
burden of proof was on the county .Hick-
man Co. v. Scarborough, 150 Ky . 1, 149
S. W. 1116 .
(3 ) In a ertain action for trespass to
land the burden of proof was on the
plaintiffto show that he was rightfully
in possession of the premises entered
upon . C. & O. Ry . Co. v.Collins, 158 Ky .
462, 165 S. W. 680.
(4 ) In an action for the sale of a tract
of land containing 159 acres, objection
was made by the defendant . The bur-
den of proof was upon the plaintiffto
show that the land was divisiblewith-
out materially impairing its value.
Cherry v. Cherry , 162 Ky . 245, 172 S. W.
505 .
See notes to sec . 526 .
§ 526. (1) Argument - introduction
of evidence. Where the court required a
party to introduce his evidence firstby
443

§ 526 ] [Title XIIIEvidence - By Whom to Be Produced .
ruling that he had burden of proof, and
after evidence was all in reversed its
rulingand gave adverseparty conclusion
of argument, the errorwas prejudicial.
Party who has burden must introduce
his evidence first,and is entitledto con-
cludeargument. O'Connor v.Henderson
Bridge Co., 95 Ky . 633, 16 R. 244,27 S.
W. 251, 983; Tarvin v. Timberlake, 18
R. 807, 38 S. W. 491.
(la) An erroneous ruling of the cir-
cuitcourtawarding the burden of proof
to the wrong party was not in thiscase
a prejudicialerror. Home Ins.Co. of N.
Y. v. Crowder, 164Ky . 792, 176 S. W.
344 .
(1b) The circuitcourterroneouslyheld
that the burden of proof in a certain
casewas on the plaintiff. This upon the
factsof thiscase was not a prejudicial
error and was not grounds for reversal.
Ditto v.Hopkins,164 Ky .412,175 S. W.
658 .
(2) Alterationof note. The burden of
explaining all alterations, erasures or
mutilations of a paper upon which a lia-
bilityissought to be establishedisupon
the holder. Elbertv.McClelland,8 Bush
577; 13 Bush 397.
(3) Assault and battery - plea of son
assaultde mesne or justification, places
burden on defendant . Johnson v .Strong ,
22 R. 577. 58 S. W. 430 ; Ashland R. W.
Co. v. Hoffman , 26 R. 778 ; but different
rule obtainsas to officers, see Connelly
v.Am . Bonding Co., 113 Ky .903, 24 R.
714 , 69 S. W. 959 .
(4 ),Burden of proof is on party who
would be defeated if no evidence was
offeredon either side. Royal Ins. Co. v .
Schwing, 87 Ky .410, 10 R. 380; 9 S. W.
242 .
(5) Contributorynegligence. Contrib-
utory negligence is purely a matter of
defense ,and as to it the burden is on the
defendant. Bogenschutz v. Smith, 84
Ky . 330, 8 R. 376, 1 S. W. 578 ; P. & M.
R. R. Co. v. Hoehl , 12 Bush 41.
(6) Claimant's bond - in action on
court must determinefrom pleadingsor
from statement of case if no pleadings
are filed who has burden . Borches v .
Bellis, 110 Ky . 620, 23 R. 37, 62 S. W.
486 .
(7) Client sued attorney who collected
money and retained it for fee- question
at issuebeing how much fee attorney
was entitled to burden was on client.
Tarvin v. Timberlake, 18 R. 807, 38 S.
W. 491 .
(8) Counter -claim - where defendant
admits plaintiff'sclaim and sets up a
counter -claim, burden is on defendant .
Asher v. Tomlinson , 22 R. 1494,60 S. W.
714; Am . Bridge Co. v. Glenmore Dis.
Co.,107 S.W. 279,32 R. 873.
(9 ) Damages - where the defendant in
an actionfordamages admits thatplain-
tiff is entitled to actual damages and
statesthe amount ,the burden is on de-
fendant. LL. & N. R. R. v.Champion , 24
R. 87, 68 S. W. 143.
(9a) Deliveryof deed. A deed whichhad been signed and acknowledged was
found at the time of the death of the
grantor in his possession, lockedin his
desk with his otherpapers. The burden
of proofwas upon the granteeto show
delivery. Dunbar v. Meadows , 165 Ky .
275, 176 S. W. 1167.
(10) Denial of ownership of note sued
on places burden of proving averment
on defendant . Vanbuskirk v. Levy , 3
Met . 134 .
(11) Denial of concludingargument to
party who has burden of proof is a re-
versibleerror. Royal Ins.Co. v.Schwing,
87 Ky .410,10 R. 380,9 S.W. 242; Fire-
man's Ins. Co. v . Schwing , 10 R. 883, 11
S. W. 14; Crabtreev. Atchinson,93 Ky .
338, 14 R. 313, 20 S. W. 260 ; Lucas v .

clude argument. Duffy v. Casey, 3 R.

insurancecompany ,givingitthe rightto
open and close. Home Ins.Co. of N. Y.

ulent, badges of fraud are shown , the

1383 , 43 S. W. 441 .

ceptor of billhad funds of drawer in his

216 ; that party accepts deed which it is

form their duties in the manner required

show, 12 Bush 592, and see Ky . Stat.,

has been absent and unheard of for seven

185; that person owning property in
this State who has not been heard of
for seven years and whose heirs are un-

mitted and claimedto be privileged,bur-

Where a passengeron a trainis injured

dent and consequent injury were caused

on party seeking to recover damages for

ing on it as a defense. 12 Bush 41 ; L.

eration . Plea of no consideration to

ant . Andrews v. Hayden ,88 Ky .455, 10

ment , the burden is on the defendant .

(27 ) Possession of note . The mere
possessionof a promissorynote not as-
signed is not prima facie evidence of

Gano v. McCarthy ,79 Ky .409,3 R. 32 .
(28 ) Possession - ownership . Where

was the owner of a slave, and that she
had been unlawfully taken from his pos-
session by defendant , and the answer

ship and alleged that defendant was the

fendant . Vance v . Vance , 2 Met . 581 .
(29 ) Practice- waiver - where a party
who has the burden permits the other
party without objectionto introducehis
evidence first he will not be allowed to
conclude the argument . Frey v. Mathias ,

on . When a payment indorsed on a note

den of proving it is on the holder of the

22 S. W. 323 .

rect. Ky . Stat ., sec. 1067.
(34 ) Several issues. On the trialof a
case presentingseveralissues, that if
tried singly would shift the burden , the

ingorderofproof,andasa generalrulethe plaintiffwill be required to conclude
his entire case on all issues; but as to
any issue upon which burden would be
on defendantifitwas the only one pre-
sented, the plaintiffshould be allowed to
introducetestimonyby way of rebuttal,
although in chief. Andrews v. Hayden ,

sumed to be of sound mind . Hawkins

party who has burden on the whole case

den ,88 Ky . 455,10 R. 1049, 11 S. W. 428.

fendant. Mattingly v. Shortwell, 120

den is on party who excepts to commis-
sioner'sreport. Chicago R. Co. v . Liebel,

§ 527. (1) Writings filedas evidence.
A party is not confinedin his evidence
to such writings as he has relied on in
his pleadings. The only benefitwhich a
party derivesfrom filingwith hisplead-
ing writings reliedon as evidence is
that, unlessdenied as providedin this
section, they may be read as genuine .
Haneylaneyv.Tempest,3 Met.96;Barrettv.
Coburn , 3 Met . 511 ; Wells v. Lewis , 4
Met . 269 .
(2) A paper referredto in a pleading
and not with it, or otherwise filed so as
to allow the adverse party an opportu-
nity to inspectit beforetrial, can not
be read without proof of its execution,
Beeler's Extrx . v. Cumb . Tel. & Tel. Co.,
150 Ky .257, 150 S. W. 335.
(2a) A paper purporting to be a re-
ceiptgiven by a decedentwas filedas
part of defendant'sanswer in an action
brought by the personalrepresentative
of decedent . It was error to admit evi-
dence controverting its genuineness , as
no affidavithad been fileddenying the
genuineness. Robertson v. Robertson's
Admr .,174 Ky .836,192 S. W. 878.
(3) The provisions of this section ap-
ply to defendants constructively sum-
moned , Buckner v. Bush, 1 Duv . 394;
and see note 1 to sec. 409 .
(4) This section applies only to writ-
ings that have been executed by the
party against whom it is proposed to
read them . Long v.Kerrigan, 13 R. 433,
16 S. W. 708, 17 S. W. 441.
(5 ) Denial may be made by verified
pleading as well as by affidavit. Har-
rison v . Rees , 19 R. 658, 41 S. W. 431 ;
Spencer v. Society of Shakers ,23 R. 854,
64 S. W. 468 .

not attend both is unreasonable . Waters

to take at same time that adverse party

§ 566. (1) Notice to take on a certain
day ,and ifnot then taken, to be taken
on a subsequentday , isgood. Moore v.

(2) Notice was given in Lexington,

§ 567. (1) Notice - what is reason-
able. Notice to take depositions at the
same time that the adverse party is
taking depositions in the same case is
not sufficient, nor does it remove the
objection that the party might have ob-
tained leave to cross-examine , and thus
have completed the depositions before
the case was submitted . Collins v . Rich-
art, 14 Bush 621.
(2 ) Party should be allowed a reason-
able time to notify or findhis attorney.
Greer v. Ludlow , 7 R. 290 ; and see notes
to sec . 565 .
(3) The word "day " in this section
does not mean a working day , nor the
time from sunrise to sunset . It means
from midnight to midnight . Fireman's
Ins. Co. v . McGill , 164 Ky . 621, 176 S.
W. 27 .

§ 585 [649] [Deposition must be filedbefore trial.] No deposition
shall be read on a trial, unless, before the commencement thereof, it be
filedwith the papers of the case.
§ 583. (1) Delivery to clerk. A depo-
sitiontaken for a party and by him de-
livered to the clerk, although sealed inan envelope. was properly rejected.
Breeding v. Stamper ,18 B. M. 175, and
so ifit is sent to commissioner. Sealy
v.Williston, 117 S. W. 959.
(2) Depositions may be sent by ex-
press. Standard Oil Co. v.Doyle,26 R.
544 , 82 S. W. 271 .
(3) Deposition should not be rejected
because by oversight of clerk it was not
marked filed. Burns v. Ingersoll, 6 R.
737 .
(4) Deposition must be filed before
trialor it can not be read. 140 Ky . 457 ,
131 S. W. 192 .
§ 585. (1) Depositions- against whom
read. Depositions can not be read
againsta party who was made defend-
ant by an amended petitionfiledafter
the depositions were taken . Depositions
taken in one suit may be read in another
when it appears that they were taken
between the same parties, or persons
claiming in privity with them . Kerr v .
Gibson , 8 Bush 129 .
(2 ) When a party takes a deposition
466

Title XIII ] [§ 586-587Evidence - Mode of Taking Testimony .
§ 586 [650] [Exceptions to- how taken. Exceptions to depositions
shallbe in writing, specifyingthe grounds of objection,filedwith the pa-
pers of thecase,and noted on the record.
Cross reference: See Miller'sKy . AppellatePracticeand Forms , sec.49.
§ 587 [651] [Exception- when to be made.] 1. No exception,
otherthan tothe competencyof thewitness,or to therelevancyor com-
petencyof the testimony, shallbe regarded,unlessitbe filedand noted
and filesit, and declines to read it, the
adverseparty has the rightto read the
deposition. Musick v. Ray , 3 Met . 427;
Weil v. Silverstone, 6 Bush 698 ; Sulli-
van v .Norris, 8 Bush 519 ; see 76 S. W.
167 .
(3) Practice- filing. Party taking
deposition can not make it a part of
record unless he filesit or lodges it with
clerk for filingbefore case is submitted .
Long v. Kerrigan, 13 R. 433, 16 S. W.
708; 17 S. W. 441; but where depositions
have been lodged in the case and are
consideredby the chancellorthey will
be considered on appeal and should be
made part of record, althoughclerkhas
failed to note or indorse them as filed.
Long v. Kerrigan , 15 R. 65, 21 S. W. 99.
(4) The fact that depositionsare en-
dorsed by the clerk as "received" from
the examiner does not constitute a filing
as required by this section. White v .
Moyers ,17 R. 402,31 S. W. 280.
(5) Depositions filedafter jury is
sworn should not be read over objection
of adverse party . Ky . Union Co. v .
Lovely , 110 Ky . 295,22 R. 1742, 61 S. W.
272 .
(5a ) This section prohibits the read-
ing of a depositionwhich has not been
filedbefore commencement of the trial,
but does not preventa party from prov-
ing any fact in the deposition not filed
in time , by other legal method . Ohio
Valley Millsv.Lou.Ry .Co.,168Ky .758,
182 S. W. 955 .
(5b ) If an oral hearing be had of an
equitable action in which action it has
been agreed that affidavitsmay be read
as depositions , an affidavit not filed be-
fore the beginning of the hearing can
not be read. Rallihan v . Motschmann ,
179 Ky . 180, 200 S. W. 358.
(6) Record . As to how depositions
made part of record on appeal, see notes
6-9 to sec . 335 .
(7) Depositions taken in another case.
When two actions growing out of same
transaction were brought against same
person depositions taken in one case
might be read in other. Andricus v.
PinevilleCoal Co., 121 Ky . 724, 90 S. W.
233 , 28 R. 704 .
(8) A deposition taken in a former
suit can not be read upon the trialunless
filedin the papers of the latter suit be-
fore the commencement of the trial.
Stevenson v . Ill.Cent . R. R. Co., 157 Ky .
561 , 163 S. W. 747 .
(9 ) In a suit by A against B , C , and
D ,a deposition was taken and the action
dismissed without prejudice. The right
of A to read such deposition in a later
suit against B and E over the same sub-
ject-matter is doubtful. Stevenson v .
Ill. Cent . R. R. Co., 157 Ky . 561, 163 S.
W. 747.
§ 586. (1) Exceptions to depositions
are deemed waived when the record does
not show they were acted on in lower
court. Patterson v . Hansel , 4 Bush 654 ;
Lewis v. Wright , 3 Bush 311 ; Hon v .
Harned , 18 R. 864, 38 S. W. 688.
(2 ) Although a party may not object
to the reading of a deposition on the first
trial,he does not thereby waive his right
to object at the next trial. Chapeze v .
Bane , 1 Bibb 612 ; and see Kincaid v .
Kincaid , 1 J. J. M. 100.
(3) Exception to whole of deposition
when part of it is competent should be
overruled. Bronston v. Bronston , 133 S.
W. 584, 141 Ky . 639.
(4) An exception to a depositionwhich
failed to specify the ground of objection
was properly overruled. Harrison
Ford , 158 Ky . 467, 165 S. W. 663.
V.
(5) The parties stipulated that a cer-
tain person , if his deposition had been
taken , would have made certain state-
ments ; an oral exception to the com-
petency of the statement was rightly
sustained . New Bell Jellico Co. v . Braz-
nell'sAdmr ., 149 Ky . 418, 149 S. W. 888.
(6 ) An exception of a purchaser at a
judicial sale that the depositions on
which the judgment for sale was ren-
dered were taken without notice was
rightfully overruled. Rothchild v. Wal-
lace . 155 Ky . 676 , 160 S. W. 170 .
§ 587. (1) Exceptions. An exception
that does not go to the, competency
467

§ 588 ] [Title XIIIEvidence - Mode of Taking Testimony .
on the recordbefore the commencement of the trialand before or during
the firstterm of the courtafterthe filingof the deposition.
2. [Exceptions as to competency and relevancy.] Exceptions to the
competency of the witness,or to the competency or relevancyof the testi-
mony ,may be made before or during the trial.
Cross reference: See Miller'sKy . AppellatePracticeand Forms ,sec.49.
§ 588 [652] [Courtto passon beforetrial- correctionof certificate.]
The courton the motion of eitherparty,shalldecideupon exceptions,other
of the witness, or to the competency or
relevancyof his testimony, can not be
considered unless filed and noted on the
record before the commencement of the
trial. Estham v. Curd , 15 B. M. 102 ;
Moore v . Smith , 88 Ky . 151, 10 R. 729,
10 S. W. 380 ; Western Union Co. v .
Corso, 89 S. W. 212, 28 R. 290, 121 Ky .
322; Andricus v. PinevilleCoal Co., 90
S. W. 233, 28 R. 704, 121 Ky . 724 ; Hall
v. Wilson , 116 S. W. 245 ; Sealy v . Wil-
liston, 117 S. W. 959 ; and before or dur-
ing the firstterm of the court after the
depositions are filed. L. & N. R. R. v .
Shaw , 21 R. 1041 ; 53 S. W. 1048. See
sec. 590, as to commencement of trial.
(la) Exceptions to a deposition be-
cause the witness resided in the county ,
if filedafter the jury was sworn , came
too late. Burchett v .Biggs , 9 Ky .Opin-
ions 22 .
(lb) An objection was made during
the trial to the reading of a deposition
of a witness who was then present in
court. The objection should have been
sustained. The opinion seems to involve
the doctrine that an objection to a depo-
sition because the witness does not fall
within any of the classes named in sec.
554 of the Code is not too late if made
duringthe progressof thetrial; but the
decision does not go so far as that.
Dailey v . Lexington & Eastern Ry . Co.,
180 Ky . 668, 203 S. W. 569.
(2) When an exception to a deposition
is general and goes to the whole deposi-
tion, if any part of it is competent the
exception should be overruled. L. & N.
R. R. Co. v . Graves , 78 Ky . 74 ; nor can
an objection to one question be raised by
an exception or objection to the whole
deposition. L. & N. R. R. v . Montgom-
ery, 17 R. 807, 32 S.W. 738. And when
exceptions have been overruled it is not
proper to present the same exceptions
again. Lewis v.Williams,4 Bush 678.
(3) It is not ncessary for a party to
file, before the commencement of the
trial, exceptions to depositions taken be-
fore he was made a party to the suit.
He can object to and save an exception

§ 589. (1) Exceptions- waiver of.
Exceptions can not be taken in the Court
of Appeals for the firsttime . All ob-
jectionsare waived which are not made

157 Ky . 756 , 164 S. W. 98.

ent testimony. Either or both of these

§ 597 [661] [Witnessmay be impeachedby adverseparty- how im-
peached.] A witnessmay be impeachedby thepartyagainstwhom he
isproduced,by contradictoryevidence,by showingthathe hasmade state
evidence preserved in a bill of exceр-
tions. Boner v.Com .,19 R. 409,40 S. W.
700 .
(2) A party may read a deposition
taken by the adverse party , and which
he declined to read,but when he does he
makes the witness his own ,and can not
impeach him . Musick v . Ray , 3 Met .
428; as to contradictionby proving
statements in a former deposition, see
Helm v . Hanly , 1 Litt. 220.
(3) When a party introduces a witness
to prove certain facts, and the witness
states that they did not transpire, he
can not then introduce other witnesses to
prove that the witness had said to them
that the facts he had inquired about did
transpire. The rule is that where a wit-
ness states a fact prejudicialto the party
callinghim, the lattermay be allowed
to show that the factdoes not existby
proving that the witness had made state-
ments to others inconsistent with his
presen testimony. Champ v. Com ., 2
Met . 17 ; Loving v . Com ., 80 Ky . 507, 4
R. 457; Blackburn v.Com .,12 Bush 181;
Nicholson v. Rust , 21 R. 645 , 52 S. W.
933; Left Fork Coal Co.v.Owen's Admx .,
155 Ky . 212, 159 S. W. 703 ; Liverpool &
L. & G. Ins.Co. v.Wright & Allen; Old
Colony Ins. Co. v. Same ; Cit. F. Ins.
Co. v . Same ; People's N. F. Ins. Co. v.
Same . 158 Ky .290,164 S. W. 952 ; Berg-
man v . Solomon , 143 Ky . 581, 136 S. W.
1010; Duke v. Davis, 101 S. W. 390, 30
R. 1348,125 Ky . 313; and see for excep-
tion to rule. Garrisonv. Com .,93 S. W.
594. 29 R. 411, 122 Ky . 882.
(4 ) A witnessmay be contradictedby
proving that he has at anothertime and
place made a different statement from
that containedin his presenttestimony
but when evidenceof such contradictory
statements is admitted the court should
instructthe jury that it is only admis-
siblefor the purpose of impeaching the
credibilityof the witness if it does so
impeach him and for no other purpose.
Ashcraft v . Com ..24 R. 488. 68 S. W.
847; White v .Com ., 84 S. W. 340. 27 R.
50; Tapscott v. Com ., 140 Ky . 573, 131
S. W. 487; Higgins v. Com ., 134 S. W.
1135, 142 Ку . 647.(4a ) On a trial of an indictment for
conspiracyit was proper to allow theCommonwealth to contradict its own
witness, by showing that he had made
statements contrary to his testimony
given in court. Rutland v. Com ., 160
Ky .77, 169 S. W. 584.
(5) A witnessmay be contradictedby
thestenographicreportofhistestimonyon a former trialand itisproperto in-
troduce as evidence the specificquestions
and answers to which his attention has
been called, the other party may then
have his entire evidence read if desired .
O'Donnell v. Lou . ElectricCo.,21 R. 1362,
55 S. W. 202 .
(6) See further, notes to sec. 221,
Criminal Code .
(7) A witness can not be cross-
examined as to any fact which is col-
lateraland irrelevantto the issue merely
for the purpose of contradictinghim by
other evidence if he should deny it,
therebyto discredithis testimony. If
a witness is cross -examined without ob-
jectionas to collateraland irrelevant
matters he can not be contradicted con-
cerning such matters . Kennedy v . Com .,
14 Bush 340 ; Cornelius v . Com . ,15 B. М.
539; Crittendenv. Com ., 82 Ky . 164.6

to be recalled and examined with a view
of laying a foundation for contradicting
bis testimony in chiefby showing that
he has made statements different there-
from. Scottv.Com .,4 Met .227.
(9 ) See further, sec. 598 and notes
thereto .
(10) Party may contradict his witness
on matter brought out on the cross-
examination . Southern R. Co. v . God-
dard , 89 S. W. 675, 28 R. 523, 121 Ky .
567 .
(11) Party introducing witness is
bound by his statements unless he can
contradict or impeach his evidence as
provided in this section. Herancourt v.
Taylor , 97 S. W. 359, 29 R. 1299.
(12) Deposition. Party who reads
deposition may contradict evidence
therein contained. Chesapeake Stone Co.
v . Fossett, 100 S. W. 825, 30 R. 1299.
(13) Impeached witness should be
given an opportunityto explaincontra-
dictory evidence. Southern R. Co. v.
Stewart , 141 Ky . 270, 132 S. W. 435.

dence to show good charactetr. Shields
v. Conway , 117 S. W. 340, 133 Ky . 35.
(10a ) In an action for the recovery of
damages forpersonalinjuries,the driver
of an ice cream wagon testifiedthat he
saw the accident which occasioned the
injury. It was not competent to im-
peach the witness by showing that the
books of the ice cream company showed
no orders for the delivery of ice cream
by the witness on that day at or west
of the place of the accident. Lou . Ry .
Co. v. Kritzky, 162 Ky . 652, 172 S. W.
1051 .
(11) Section applies to criminal cases.
Farmer v .Com .,91 S. W. 682, 28 R. 1168.
(12) Court may compel witness to an-
swer that he has been convicted of a
felony. Hendersonv. Com ., 91 S. W.1141, 28 R. 1212, 122 Ky . 296 ; Ochsner
v . Com ., 109 S. W. 326, 128 Ky . 761.
(12a) In a prosecution for felony, the
defendant testifiedin his own behalf; it
was proper to compel him to answer the
question whether he had been convicted
of a felony. It was the duty of the cir-
cuit judge to admonish the jury that this
was only competent for the purpose of
affecting his credibility as a witness .
GeBurk v . Com ., 153 Ky . 264, 155 S. W.
381 .
(13) Impeaching witness may be im-
peached. Holtheidev.Smith'sGdn.,84
S. W. 321 , 27 R. 60 .
(14) Contradiction by deposition. A
witness may be contradicted by material
statements in a deposition, contrary to
what he testifies at the trial . Border-
land Coal Co. v. Charles' Admr . (Small ),
160 Ky .738 , 170 S. W. 8.
(15) A witness may be impeached ,
where the proper foundation has been
laid,by proof of contrary statements , by
the testimony of the stenographer who
took his deposition, although the deposi-
tion has not been filed in the case . Ohio
ValleyMillsv. Lou. Ry . Co., 168 Ky .
758 , 182 S. W. 955 .

§ 599. (1) Good character- evidence
of. Evidence of the good character of a
witness is not admissible where he is
only impeached by proof of his having
made a different statement on another
occasion,or by proofof factswhich.con-

1094 .

CriminalCode.
(4 ) When it is shown that witness
has been convicted of a felony he may
introduce evidence to show his good
character. Shieldsv.Conway , 117 S. W.
341, 133 Ky . 35.

court-room . The question of excluding

him to remain . Johnson v. Clem , 82 Ky .

§ 606-6. (1) Books, production of.-
The court may compel by rule the ad-
verse party to produce in court any

mon with others . Marion Nat . Bank v .

ments testifies as to their contents he

time. Marion Nat .Bk . v. Abell, 88 Ky .

died or absconded , and then the entries
themselves must be proved when the
books can not be produced in the court,
and it is not sufficientto prove , as was
done in this case, the mere conclusions
of the witnesses to their substance and
effect. But the entry itself, or an exact
copy thereof, must be presentedto the
court, and, with the exceptionsjust

§ 1624. Judicialnoticeof legislativeacts and resolutions. The several
courtsof thisCommonwealth shalltakejudicialnoticeof allactsand resolu-
tionsof theGeneral Assembly. (Ky .Stat.,sec. 1624.)
§ 1625. Judicialnoticeof officialsignatures. All courts, tribunalsand
officersshalltake noticeof the officialsignatureof any officerof thisState,
of the United States,or of any Stateor Territoryin the United States. (Id.,
sec . 1625. )
§ 1626. Copiesof journalsof General Assembly- evidence. Copiesof the
journalof eitherHouse of the General Assembly, printedby the State, shall
be receivedas evidencefor any purpose for which the originaljournalcould
be received. (Id.,sec.1626.)
§ 1627. Publicrecords- copiesof evidence. A copy of any recordor paper,
properlyfiledor lodged in the clerk'sofficeof any court, or of the Secretary
of State, Treasurer, Registeror Auditor, of the surveyorof the county, or
assessor'sbooks attestedby the personhaving the legalcustodythereof,shall,
upon proof of the execution of the original, be admitted as evidence in lieu
thereof. (Id .,sec. 1627.)
§ 1628. Publicrecord- action on copy- answer of defendant. A copy of
any such writingas isembraced in the precedingsectionmay be filedin a suit
thereon, and the defendant shallplead theretoas if the originalwere filed.
(Id.,sec.1628.)
§ 1629. Records of towns or religioussocieties- copies. A copy from the
mayor's officeof any city,or from the officialbooks of any town or religious
society, or an ordinanceor by-law for the ruleof such city, town or society,
attestedby the keeper thereof, shallbe evidencefor any purpose for which
the originalcouldbe received. (Id., sec. 1629.)
§ 1630.Execution entriesof clerkconcerning. A copy from an entryin
a clerk'sor justice'sexecution book of the date of an execution that issued
from such office, and to whom deliveredand when returnable, shallbe evi-
dence in any proceedingagainstthe officerand his sureties,to whom itshall
be enteredas delivered. (Id.,sec.1630.)

ury upon the warrant of the Auditor to be issued when the order of the court

§ 1634. Records transcribedentitledto full credit. Any book so tran-
scribed,examined by the judge of the court, and an order made on the records
of the court that the same is correct, shallhave the same faith and creditthat
the book or record transcribed was entitledto. (Id ..sec. 1634.)
§ 1635. Records of courts of other States, and United States. The records
and judicialproceedingsof any court of any State, attestedby the clerk
thereof,with the sealof the courtannexed, iftherebe a seal,and certifiedby
the judge,chiefjusticeor presidingmagistrateof thecourt,to be attestedin

$ 1636. Publicrecordsof otherStatesor United States. All recordsand
exemplificationsof officebooks kept in any publicofficeof the United States,
or of a sisterState,not appertainingto a court,shallbe evidencein thisState
ifattestedby the keeper of such recordor books, and the sealof his office,
if there be a seal. ( Id ., sec.1636.)
§ 1637. Records of executive department of other State or United States.
Exemplificationsfrom the books of the executivedepartment of the United
States,or any papersfiledtherein,shallbe evidencewhen attestedby the Presi-
dent or the chiefof eitherof the departments; or from any Stateor Terri-
tory, of likebooks or paperswhen attestedby theGovernor or the Secretary
of Statethereof,under hisofficialseal. (Id., sec. 1637.)
§ 1638. Records from foreigncountries. A copy from the record-books of
any court,or of any registerof birthsand marriages,or other instrumentsof
writing,or a copy of any law or ordinanceof any State,nation,province,col-
ony, cityor town out of the United States,ifthe same shallhave been regis-
teredindue form,accordingto the laws of such sovereignty,certifiedand at-
testedby the keeper of such record or register, and the attestation, certified
under his officialseal, by any counsel, charge d'affairesor ministerof the
United States resident within the sovereignty where the record is kept , shall
be evidenceinthisState. (Id.,sec.1638.)
§ 1639. Death presumed afterabsenceof sevenyears. If any person,who
shall have resided in this State, go from and do not return to this State for
seven successiveyears,he shallbe presumed to be dead, in any case wherein
hisdeath shallcome inquestion,unlessproof be made thathe was alivewithin
that time . (Id., sec. 1639.)
§ 1640. Common law of otherStates- how proven. The unwrittenor com-
mon law of any other of the United Statesmay be proved as factsby the
parol evidenceof persons learnedin that law. The printedbooks of cases
adjudged in the courtsof a sisterStatemay alsobe admittedas evidenceof
such law . (Id .,sec. 1640.)
§ 1641. Foreign laws- how proven . The existence and the tenor or effect
of allforeignlaws beyond the limitsof theUnited Statesmay be proved by
the parolevidenceof personslearnedin thoselaws. But if itappear thatthe
law inquestioniscontainedinwrittenstatute,thecourtmay rejectsuch parol
evidenceunlessitbe accompaniedby a copy of the statute. (Id.,sec.1641.)
§ 1642. Printedlaws of United Statesor otherState,or copy. The printed
laws of the United States, or of any State or Territorythereof, which has
been or shall be received in the Secretary's officeof this State, and which
shallhave been printedunder the authorityof theUnited States,or such State
or Territory, or a copy thereof,when duly certifiedby the Secretaryof State
for thisCommonwealth , shallbe admitted and receivedas evidenceof such
laws. (Id., sec.1642.)
§ 1643. Lost papersin suitmay be suppliedby attestedcopies. When any
papers of any suitpending or decidedin any courthave been lost, destroyed,
defaced, or obliterated, and thereis in existencean attestedcopy thereof,
such copy may be filedin the officewhere such papersbelongedin lieuof the
originals,and shallbe prima facieevidenceof the contentsof such originals.

whose rightsor interestsare affectedby the filingof such copies,ifa resident

§ 616. (1) Costs. A person may , by

§ 884. Poor person allowed to sue without paying. A poor person resid-
ing in thisState may be allowed by a court to prosecuteor defend action
thereinwithout paying costs, whereupon he shallhave any counselthat the
courtmay assignhim and from allofficersallneedful servicesand process
without any fees,exceptsuch as may be includedinthe costsrecoveredfrom
the oppositeparty. (Ky .Stat.,sec.884.)
§ 885. Commonwealth not liablefor costs. If the Commonwealth shall be
unsuccessfulin any case prosecutedin her own right, no judgment for costs
shallbe renderedagainsther. (Id., sec.885.)
§ 886. Criminal prosecutions- defendant when liablefor costs. The de-
fendant in a criminalprosecutionshallbe adjudged to pay the costsof all
continuancesin his behalf, for which executionor otherprocessmay issue,
as in civilcases,and,ifconvictedof a misdemeanor, allcostsunpaid may be
collectedin the same manner as the fine; ifconvictedof a felony,by execu-
tion,or otherprocess,as in civilcases. (Id.,sec.886.)
§ 887. Continuances- amendments - dismissal. If a party to an action
obtainsa continuance, or filesan amendment that causes a continuance, he
shallpay the costsaccruingat thatterm; and the party in error,ifan action
be dismissedfor want of jurisdiction, shallpay the costs; and ifa party shall
obtainfurthertime to plead or to amend hispleadings, he shallpay the costs
occasionedthereby. (Id.,sec.887.)
§ 888. Witness- non-appearanceand attachment of. When a witness is
attachedhe shallpay the costsof the attachment, unlessitappears from evi-
dence he was not in fault; and when a witness,who has been duly summoned ,
shallfail, withoutgood excuse therefor, to appear on the trial, he shallpay
allcostsresultingfrom his failure. (Id.,sec.888.)
§ 889. Ordinary and equityaction- nominal parties- partners, jointten-
ants- settlements. The partysucceedingin any ordinaryaction, on the merits
or otherwise,shallrecoverhis costs, unlessdifferentlyprovidedin thischap-

§ 890. Appeal- tender- amount of recovery. If a party,before an appeal
istaken,tendersto the adversepartythe amount he iswillingto pay,and the

exceed the amount tendered, the appellantshallnot recoverhis costs, but

appealisfor more than the amount tenderedhe shallrecoverhiscosts. (Id.,
sec . 890. )
§ 891. Appeal to courtof appeals- discretionon reversal. On a reversal
of a judgment in the court of appeals, the appellant shall recover of the ap-
pelleesuch costsas the judges, in theirdiscretion, shallaward. (Id., sec.
891. )
§ 892. Personalrepresentativeand otherfiduciaries--liabilityof. A per-
sonalrepresentative, guardian, assignee, curator, committee, or trusteein an
actionshall,ifunsuccessful,be adjudged to pay costsas otherlitigants. The
judgment for costsin such case shallonly be againstthe assetswhich have,
or may ,come to hishands. (Id. sec.892.)
§ 893. Next friend or relator on a bond . A next friend or relator on a
bond shall, upon failurein the action,be adjudged to pay the defendant his
costs. If the plaintiffsucceed, the next friendor relatorshallbe adjudged
his costsagainstthe defendant. (Id.,sec.893.)
§ 894. Injunction- dissolutionof. When an injuntionshallbe dissolved
the plaintiffshallpay the costsoccasionedby the same , unlessotherwisead-
judged by thecourt. (Id.,sec.894.)
§ 895. Passway or gate--applicationconcerning. Any person applyingfor
a passway or to erecta gate, or to discontinuea passway or remove a gate,
whether successfulor not,shallpay the costsof the procedurein the inferior
court. (Id ., sec.895.)
§ 896. Proceeding to remove officer. Any person who shalltake stepsin
any courtto remove any officerfrom office, and failin so doing, shalipay
such officerhis costsexpended in the defense of such procedure. If the
officershallbe removed by such procedure, then the person institutingand
carrying on the same shallbe adjudged his costs against such officer. (Id .,
sec . 896.)
§ 897. Removal of cause- court givingfinaljudgment. When , by any
procedure, a cause shallbe taken from one tribunalto another,and the latter
shallgive finaljudgment, and proceed to executethe same without remand-
ing the cause to the inferiortribunal, the court renderingsuch finaldecision

§ 898. Motion or rule considered an action . A motion or rule of court
shallbe consideredan action; but when made in a pending suitor actionan
attorney'sfee shallnot be recoveredas a part of the costsof the motion or
rule. (Id.,sec.898.)
§ 899. New trialon payment of costs. The partyto whom a new trialis
granted, upon the payment of costs, shall, within fortydays thereafter, pay
the costsof the former trial. If he failsto pay the same withinsaidtime the
courtmay , upon motion of the oppositeparty made withinthreedays after
the commencement of the next regularterm, setasidethe order grantinga
new trialand enterjudgment on the verdictrenderedin the case. (Id., sec.
899. )
§ 900. Divorce and alimony. In actionsfor alimony and divorce,the hus-
band shallpay the costs of each party, unless it shallbe made to appear in the
actionthe wife is in faultand has ample estateto pay the same. (Id., sec.
900. )
§ 901. Insolvent party- rights of officersand witnesses. Whenever either
party to an actionis insolventor in doubtfulcircumstancesand such party
shallrecover a judgment for the costs of the action, or for a continuance, the
court, on motion of any person interested, shallindorsethe costsso recov-
ered, or so much thereofas may be necessary, for the benefitof the officers
and witnesseswho shallhave rendered servicesin the action, so far as the
same are embraced in the judgment then rendered, and they shallbe entitled
to so much of the costs as will satisfytheir fees. The indorsement shall se-
cure the officersand witnessesa preferenceto alltransfers, set-off, or at-
torney'slien. The indorsement shallbe copied on the executionthat issues
for the costs,and shallshow what parteach officerand witnessisentitledto,
and authorizeeach to receivethe amount so indorsed. (Id.,sec.901.)
§902 . Habeas corpus- transporting prisoner. In the proceedings by
habeas corpus,thejudge or court,beforewhom the caseshallbe returnedmay
award coststo be paid, includingpay for transportingthe prisoner, as shall
seem right. (Id .,sec. 902.)
$ 903. Settlementof insolventestates- party presentingclaims. In the
settlementof insolventestates, a partywho shallpresenta claim againstthe
estatewhich is not allowed shallpay the costoccasionedthereby, except an
attorney'sfee. No attorney'sfee shallbe allowed any claimantin any case
againstan insolventestate. (Id. sec.903.)
§ 904. Attorney's fee- taxation of costs- copies of pleadings- witnesses.
Clerks shalltax one attorney'sfee only in the billof costsof the successful

§ 1754. Illegalcharge renders whole billvoid. Every fee-billcontaining
one illegalcharge, or which, in any respect, is not accordingto what is re-
quiredby law, shallbe void for the whole amount thereof. (Ky . Stat., sec.
1754.)
§ 1755. Illegalor double charge- penaltyfor. Any officerwho shallsplit
up and divide his servicesso as to make two charges, when the law intended
but one charge or fee for the whole service,or who shallknowingly make an
illegalcharge,or issuean illegalfee-bill, or collect, or attemptto collect, his
fees twice for the same services, or shall, by any indirection, collect, or at-
tempt to collect, more for his servicesthan is allowed by law , shallbe guilty
of a misdemeanor , and fined not less than fiftynor more than two hundred
dollars. (Id ., sec. 1755.)
§ 1756. Illegalor double charge- high misdemeanor. Any officerwho
shallbe guiltyof any of the offensesspecifiedin the lastprecedingsection
shallbe consideredguiltyof a high misdemeanor,and his convictionthereof
shallbe prima facieevidenceof his guiltin any proceedingto remove him
from office. (Id.,sec. 1756.)

§ 641. (1) Action on bond . An execu-
tion defendant may maintain an action
on the bond to recover the value of prop-
erty sold, and which was exempt from
seizure and sale under the execution .
Dixon v . Bacon , 3 Bush 534 , and see
Winstead v. Hicks, 135 Ky . 154, 121 S.
W. 1018 .
(2 ) Action against purchaser. The ex-
ecution of a bond does not deprive a
claimant of the property seized of his
right to sue the purchaser at the execu-
tion sale for recovery of the property of
its value. Bethel v . Vanmeter ,9 R. 331 ;
and seeWinstead v.Hicks, 135 Ky . 154,
121 S. W. 1018 .
(3) Defective bond - action against of.
ficer. A bond which contains no cove-
nant to pay to the claimantof any prop-
erty seizedor sold the damages sustained
by such claimant , presents no bar to an
action against the officerby such claim-
ant. Jewellv. Mills, 3 Bush 62; Curry
v . Ball, 22 R. 592, 58 S. W. 534 .
(4 ) Equitable claimant- action by on
bond . A person who has a lien on the
propertysoldmay maintainan actionon
the bond . It is intended to protect all
legal as well as equitable claimants.
Watts v . Cook , 2 Bush 141.
(5) Liability of sheriff generally.
When the sheriffacts in good faith,hav-
ing no sufficientreason to doubt the title,
he is not responsible to the purchaser.
But when informed as to the existence of
an adverseclaim, or being in possession
of facts which should place one of ordi-
nary prudence on inquiryin regard to
the title,it is his duty to take a bond of
indemnity to protectthe purchaser, as
well as the claimant , or he should notify
the purchaser of the defect in the title;
and a failure to do either will render
him liablein such case to a suit by the
purchaser. Harrisonv.Shanks, 13 Bush
620; Anderson v . West , 80 Ky . 171, 3 R.
670 .

§ 2180. Applicationfor discharge- notice- duty of jailer- scheduleand
oath- papers preserved. Any person taken or charged in executionin any

cealed any part of my effectswhereby to secure the same ,to receive or expect
any profitor advantage from the same,to defraud any creditorto whom I am

§ 388 [418] Specificpersonalproperty- form of judgment for. In an ac-

§ 1665. Execution for thing recoveredor itsvalue--discretionof court.
When a judgment shallbe recoveredfor a specificthing,theplaintiffmay have

such property. (Id., sec. 1665.)

§ 1675. Surplus proceeds- dispositionof. When the propertysold on a

Bonds Having the Effectof Judgments.
§ 1676. Signingand attesting- return- executionon- indorsement. Every
bond taken on the saleof propertyunder an order of judgment in chancery,
or on thesaleof propertyunder execution,and everyreplevinand forthcoming
bond, shallbe signedby the principaland sureties,and attestedby the person
takingthesame ,or by some one inhispresence.
1. A bond so taken shallbe returnedto the properoffice,with a reportof
the actsof the person takingit; and if taken under an execution, the latter
must be returned with the bond .
2. All such bonds shallhave the forceand effectof a judgment, and on
which, ifnot paid at maturity, an executionmay issue, and shallbe indorsed
thatno suretyof any kind isto be taken. (Id.,sec. 1676.)
§ 1677. Officerliableif bond insufficient. The officertakingany of the
bonds aforesaid,and hissureties, or theirrepresentatives,shallbe jointlyand
severallyliableto thepersoninjuredfor any damage he may sustainby taking
suretythereonwho isnot solventwhen received. (Id.,sec.1677.)
§ 1678. Death of one of several obligees or obligors. If one of several
obligors, or obligees, in any bond having the force and effectof a judgment,
shalldie before the same is satisfied, execution may issue on such bond in the
name of the surviving obligee or obligees, against the obligor or the survivor.
(Id.,sec.1678.)
§ 1679. Death of allobligees. When allthe obligeesin such bond as is
specifiedin the precedingsectionshalldie,theirpersonalrepresentativesmay ,
if such bond is not satisfied, sue out executionthereon, afteritsmaturity,
againstthe obligorsor theirpersonalrepresentatives,ifthey are alldead; or,
ifonly partbe dead, againstthe survivorand the personalrepresentativesof
the deceased. (Id., sec.1679.)
§ 1680. Quashal of bond-effect. When a bond havingtheforceof a judg

ment is quashed , a new execution may issue on the originaljudgment at the
instance of the plaintiff, in the same manner as if such bond had never been
given. ( Id.,sec.1680.)

§ 1681. Land to which defendant has legal title. Land to which the de-
fendanthas a legaltitlein fee for life,or for a term,whether in possession,
reversionor remainder, may be taken and sold under execution. (Id., sec.
1681.)
§ 1682. Sale of land- placeof- advertisement--appraisement- officer'sre-
turn. Lands must be sold under execution to the highest bidder at the court-
house door of the county in which itlies,or iftherebe ne court-house,then
on the streetor publicsquare, in frontof the sitewhere the court-house has
been or isto be erected,on the firstday of a county or circuitcourtheld for
the county .
1. Only so much landcan be soldas willsatisfytheexecutionunder which
the sale is made .
2. The officermaking the salemust firstadvertisethe time and placeof
making thesame by writtennoticesetup at thecourt-housedoor and three
other publicplacesin the vicinityof the land for fifteendays next preceding
the sale,thereindescribingthe land to be sold.
3. Before a saleof land,the officershallcause itto be valued,under oath,

§ 2299. Distress- attachmentor action- interest. Rent may be recovered
by distress, attachmentor action, and shallbear six per centinterestper an-
num from the time itisdue. (Ky . Stat.,sec.2299.)
§ 2300. Compensation for use and occupation--verbal contract. Unless
the contract be in writing, a landlord may , by action, recover reasonable sat-
isfactionfor the use and occupationof his land. If, on the trial, a verbal
contractbe proven,whereby a rentcertainin amount was reserved, theplain-
tiffmay ,nevertheless, recover,the verbalcontractbeing regardedas evidence
of the amount recoverable. (Id.,sec.2300.)
§ 2301. Distresswarrant- by whom issuedand executed. When rent is
reservedin money a landlordmay ,beforea justiceof the peace,policejudge
or a judge of the quarterlycourt, in the county where the land lies, by him-

arrear, and thereuponsuch officershallissuea distresswarrant, directedto
the sheriff,marshal of the town,or a constableof thecounty,authorizingsuch
officertodistrainfor the amount due,with interestand costs. (Id.,sec.2301.
See , further, Civil Code , secs.641-658.)
§ 2302. Attachment for rent- affidavitand bond. When any person, who
shallbe liableto pay rent, whether the same be due or not, and whether the
same be payablein money or otherthing, ifthe rentbe due withinone year
thereafter, the person to whom the rent isowing, or his agent or attorney,
may filean affidavitbefore a justiceof the peace, policejudge, or a judge
of the quarterlycourt of the county in which the tenement lies, statingthat
there are reasonablegrounds for belief, and that he believes, unlessan at-
tachment be issued,he willlosehis rent; whereupon, such officershallissue
an attachmentfor the rent againstthe personalpropertyof the person liable
for the same to any county the person suing out the same may desire. But
no such attachmentshallissueuntilthe plaintiffhas given bond, with good
surety, to indemnify the defendant, if it appear that the attachment has
been wrongfullyobtained. (Id.,sec.2302. Sce,further, CivilCode,sec. 195.)
§ 2303. Attachment- beforewhat courtreturnable- defenses. All attach-
ments issued under this chapter, for a sum of fiftydollars or less, exclusive
of interestand costs, shall be returned before the officerissuing the attach-
ment ; and allthose for a sum exceedingfiftydollarsand not exceedingtwo
hundred shall, at the optionof the party attaching, be returnedbefore either
the quarterlyor circuitcourt of the county whence it issued'; if over two
hundred dollars,to thecircuitcourt;and in eithercasetheproceedingsthereon
shallbe the same as on other attachments according to the Civil Code ; and
the defendant shallalso have the privilegeof denying the tenancy, or his
liabilityto pay rent, as statedin the affidavit; and, moreover, may repossess
himself of the propertyby executingbond in a manner similarto that pre-
scribedin the CivilCode of Practicesubjectto similarproceedingsif for-

§ 4575. Process- executionafterexpirationof term, or when sheriffdies
or vacatesofficerightsof sureties. When a sheriffshalldie or otherwise
vacatehis office, his deputiesshallcomplete the executionof any processin
his or theirhands; and when the term of a sheriffshallexpire, he shall, by
himselfor deputies, completethe executionof any processin hishands,and
his or theiractsand omissionsin thisparticularshallbe consideredas em-
braced in the sheriff's officialbond . When the sheriff shall die or otherwise

ceed to the execution thereof in the same manner that such sheriff or his

4575.)
§ 4576. Demand for money collectedby. A person entitledto money col-
lectedby a sheriffor his deputy may demand the same from the sheriff,by
himself, his attorney-at-law or any otherperson having his writtenauthority

§ 4578. Persons may be summoned to aid in executingduties. Any sheriff
or deputy sheriff, or other likeofficer, may command and take with him , if
need be, the power of the county , or a part thereof, to aid him in the execu-
tionof thedutiesof hisoffice,civilor criminal. (Id.,sec.4578.)
§ 4579. Force may be used in executingcertainwrits. A sheriffor other
officermay , in the execution of a writ of habere facias possessionem , or writ
of seizin,break open eitherthe outeror innerdoor of a dwellingor any other
house on the premises , during the daytime . (Id., sec.4579.)

house of the defendant in the execution,to enable him to seizethe defendant's
property during the daytime . (Id ., sec.4580.)
§ 4581. Door- when may be broken to levy on property. The sheriffor
otherofficermay break open the outeror any other door of the dwellingor
otherhouse of a thirdperson in which the propertyof the defendant in the
executionisfraudulentlyconcealedor kept. (Id.,sec.4581.)
§ 4582. Powers in executingwritsgenerally. The sheriffor other officer
shall have the same power to break and enter the dwelling and other houses

sheriffor other officermay break open the outer or any other door of the
dwellingsor otherhouse of the defendant, or of any other person, if itbe
necessaryto enablehim to make the arrest. (Id.,sec.4583.)
§ 4584. Process not requiringarrest- force thatmay be used. But ifthe
processdo not requirean arrest, then the officershallhave the same powers,

§ 4585. Payment on executionafterreturnday- effectof. Any payment

turnday thereof,and whilethe same remains in hishands,shall,to theextent

(13) Lien notes not alldue . In an aс-
tion to enforce a lien, if some of the
notes are due and it appears from the
petition that the property is not sus-
ceptible of advantageous division, no
part of it should be sold until all the
notesfalldue,althoughthey are allheld

be advantageouslydivided, itwas error
to direct that any part of it be sold to
pay any of the debts before all of the
lien debts became due . Gibbs v . Ballard
County Bank's Assignee, 158 Ky . 500,

of 1916 (Sess.Acts ,p . 656).- Ed.]

Acts , p . 656) took effectit is not error
to sellenough land to satisfya lienon
it before notes secured by a later lien
falldue . It seems that this rule applies
whether the notes were executed before
or after that act took effect. Durham v .
Elliott, 180 Ky .724,203 S. W. 539.
(13c) On an action to enforce a junior
mortgage , the holder of a senior mort-
gage is a necessary party . Sale may be
made subject to the lien of the senior
mortgage if the debt secured thereby is
not due . Fisher v. Evans , 175 Ky . 300,
194 S. W. 367 .
(14) Allegation that a sale at once of
the entire tract was necessary, to pre-
vent loss to the creditor,did not author-
ize a sale to pay notes not due . Burton
v .McKinney , 6 Bush 428.
(15) Where the plaintiffheld two
notes , one of which was not due , it was
erroneous to order a sale of the land to
pay the note due, subjectto a lienfor
the note not due. Eminson v.Risque, 9
Bush 24.
(16) Where several lien notes are held
by same party,saleof enoughlandto
pay those due may be orderedunlessit
appears land is not susceptibleof advan-
tageous division, or for other reasons a
sale would cause a sacrifice. Ward v .
Coffey, 11 R. 339. 12 S. W. 145; Wheeler
v . Bleakley , 18 R. 254, 35 S. W. 1031 ;
Rowlett v . Harris , 28 R. 780, 90 S. W.
562 .
(17) When all the notes are executed
to the plaintiff, the presumption is that
he is the owner of the notes not due ,and
it is not necessary to allege that he has
not parted with them . Ward v. Coffey,
11 R. 339, 12 S. W. 145.
(18) Personal judgment . Where the
vendor seeks both a personaljudgment
and an enforcement of his lien, he is en-
titledto a personaljudginentifallega-
tionsof petitionauthorizeit, although

ment of his lien. Bullock v . Graham ,

a personal judgment may be rendered,
although defendant is not summoned in
county where action is pending . Collins
v . Park , 93 Ky . 6, 13 R. 905, 18 S. W.
1013 .
(19) Pleading . Allegation that lien
was reserved on land to secure payment
of note averred to have been executed
forpurchase-money isunnecessary.Cock-
rillv .Mize , 11 R. 637, 12 S. W. 1040 .

contract, and , if he has not already con-
veyed , must state the character of title
that he agreed to make , and that he is
ready and willingto make it; ifhe has
conveyed the land he should state the
fact. Bullock v. Graham , 87 Ky . 120,9

17 R. 1250, 29 S. W. 309 ; 34 S. W. 226 .
(10 ) Purchaser who applies for relief
before confirmation is entitled to an
abatement of the purchase -price to the
extent of the value of land that he
bought but did not get. Akin v. Under-
wood , 11 R. 757, 12 S. W. 1061 ; Trigg v .
Jones , 102 Ky . 44, 19 R. 1009, 42 S. W.
848; and may before confirmation have
the rights of all persons who are assert-
ing titleto the land determined . Bird v .
Smith , 101 Ky . 205, 19 R. 377, 40 S. W.
571; Wise v. Wolfe , 27 R. 610, 85 S. W.
1191 .
(11) The general rule does not apply
to a purchaser who is induced to make
purchase by the misrepresentationsof
the creditoror personmaking saleas to
the conditionof the title,when he could
not discover condition of title until after
confirmation. Williams v . Glenn ,87 Ky .
87 ,9 R. 941,7 S. W. 610 ; and see Wright
Underwood ,9 R. 712,6 S. W. 437.
(12) Where the purchaser acquires no
titlehe may recover of the debtor what
he has paid for the land . Williams v .
Glenn ,87 Ky .87 ,9 R. 941, 7 S. W. 610.
(13) Purchaser who has been evicted
may recover on the warranty of titlein
deed to person whose land he bought at
the sale. Thomas v. Bland, 91 Ky . 1,
12 R. 640, 14 S. W. 955.
(14) The purchaser is liable for taxes
assessed after the sale and before the
confirmation. Bond v. Brand, 115 Ky .
632. 25 R. 26 , 74 S. W. 673 ; and can not
unlesshe appliesbeforesaleisconfirmed
get an abatement for taxesdue on prop-
erty at time of purchase . Farmers Bank
v. Peters, 13 Bush 591; Trigg v. Jones,
102 Ky .44, 19 R. 1009,42 S. W. 848.
(15) The purchaserafterconfirmation
of sale will not be heard to complain
that there was a lienon the property and
that the lienholderwas not a party to
the action. Thompson v . Brownlee , 25
R. 622, 76 S. W. 172.
(16) Duty of court. The court should
ascertain that the right of redemption
does not exist before ordering a deed or
writ of possession. Rose v. Taylor, 8
R. 192 .
(17 ) Exceptions to report- confirma-
tion. Time report shall lie over for ex-
ceptionsis not prescribed; and in Rose
v. Taylor, 8 R. 182, the court held that
itwas not error to confirm on February
5th "a reportfiledFebruary 3d," there
being no exceptions, or it may be con-
firmed same day , and at special term .
Wise v. Wolfe , 27 R. 610, 85 S. W. 1191.
(18) Exceptions to a report admit
everythingnot excepted to. Taylor v.
Young , 2 Bush 428.
(19) Appeal liesto Court of Appeals
from order overruling or sustaining ex-
ceptions to report of sale. See notes,
sec . 734 .
(20) Fees of commissioner for collect-
ing and paying out money . Ky . Stat.,
sec. 1740; Wathen v. England , 102 Ky .
537, 19 R. 1601, 44 S. W. 92 ; Russell v .
Averitt, 19 R. 202, 39 S. W. 699; 41 S.
W. 769 .
(21) Power of commissioner after sale.
When the commissioner has sold the
property and accepted the bonds for the
purchase-price, his duties, so far as the
sale is concerned, are at an end , and
nothing remains for him to do but to re-
port the manner in which he has dis-
charged his duty to the court. He can
not afteracceptingthe purchaser'sbonds
demand additional security. If the bond
is insufficient, that fact should be
brought to the attention of the court,
and the court may require additional
securitybeforeconfirmingthe sale. Rea

§ 697] [TitleXVMiscellaneous Provisions .
inerv.Judah ,13 Bush 206 ; and see notes
17-25,sec. 696.
(22) Purchaser- rightsand liabilities.
A. purchaser at a sale made under a void
judgment does not acquireany lienon
the propertysoldfor the amount of the
debt paid by him when the sale is set
aside. Grigsby v.Barr, 14 Bush 330.
(23 ) Purchaser and sureties who fail
to pay bonds and surrenderpropertyfor
sale are liable for the difference between
price at firstand second sales. City of
Lou .v.Kaye ,10 R. 160,8 S. W. 869.
(24) When the purchaser at judicial
salefailsto comply with terms of sale
by executingbonds, the sale may be
reported to court and confirmed and the
purchaser proceeded against for con-
tempt or a resalemay be ordered and the
purchaser at firstsale will be liablefor
the differencebetween price at firstand
second sales, but if the firstsale is not
confirmed and a resale is ordered the
purchaser at firstsale will not be liable
for the difference. Cowper v . Weaver , 27
R. 48 ,84 S. W. 323 ; Makemson v .Braun ,
100 Ky . 88, 18 R. 584, 37 S. W. 495 ;
Tyler v. Guthrie , 17 R. 1193, 33 S. W.
934; Shirley v. Shewmaker , 23 R. 452, 63
S.W. 11; Napper v.Mutual LifeIns.Co.,
107 Ky . 134, 21 R. 791, 53 S. W. 28 ;
Blakely v. Hughes , 140 Ky . 174, 130 S.
W. 1067; Kelly v. Sale, 161 Ky . 148, 170
S. W. 513 .
(25) Purchaser is entitled to notice of
motion to quash sale bond because the
sureties are insufficient. Butts v . Alder-
son , 12 R. 443 , 14 S. W. 493 .
(26) Purchaser is entitled to a notice
of motion to quash sale. Butts v. Alder-
son, 12 R. 443, 14 S. W. 493 ; and is nec-
essary party to proceeding attacking
sale. Stone v. Myrtle , 148 Ky .57, 146 S.
W. 20 .
(27) A purchaser can not have a sale
set aside because the commissioner did
not advertise the property as required
by law . Watson v. Violett, 2 Duv . 332 ;
nor because the land is not sufficiently
describedin the judgment. Sullivanv.
Berry ,83 Ky . 198,7 R. 402 .
(28 ) A purchaser at commissioner's
sale excepted to the confirmation of the
report of sale on the ground that there
had been puffingand by-bidding at the
sale, but was held to have waived his
rightto objectby executingsalebonds
afterobtainingknowledgeof allthefacts connected with the sale. Robinson
v.Robinson , 11 Bush 174.
(29 ) A bidder will be required to take
the property, although the buildingon
ithas been destroyedby firebeforethe
saleisconfirmed,or he isplacedin pos-
session; and may be compelledby rule
to comply with the terms of his pur-
chase. Vance v. Foster, 9 Bush 389 .
(30) Purchaser at judicialsale does
not become investedwith the legaltitle
untildeed is made . Goodin v. Wilson,
114 Ky . 716, 24 R. 1521, 71 S. W. 866.
(31 ) Where there is a mistake as to
the quantity of land sold at a judicial
sale,the chancellorwill,in a subsequent
action, affordrelief, if the mistake be
such that reliefwould be granted if the
sale had been a private one . Miller v .
Craig,83 Ky .623,7 R. 712; seeTrigg v.
Jones, 102 Ky . 44, 19 R. 1009, 42 S. W.
848; Cooper v.Hargis,20 R. 41,45 S.W.
112 .
(32) Court may direct that a pur-
chaser who failed to execute bonds shall
not be allowed to bid at second sale .
Wigginton v.Nehan ,25 R. 617,76 S.W.
196 .
(33 ) The rule of caveat emptor applies
to purchasersat judicialsales. Bullock
v.Gudgell,25 R. 1413,77 S. W. 1126; 13
Bush 591 .
(34) Where the purchaser resistsan
effort to set aside a sale and is unsuc-
cessfulhe must pay the costs incurred
upon trialof exceptions, ifhe does not
resisthe should be allowed his ordinary
costs. Van Meter v.Van Meter, 88 Ky .
448 , 10 R. 906, 11 S. W. 80,289.
(35) A purchaserat a judicialsaleis
entitled to rent from the date of the
confirmation of the sale. Ball v . Bank ,
80 Ky . 501, 4 R. 400 ; Taliaferro v. Gay ,
78 Ky . 496; unlessthe court otherwise
directsor partiesbuy land under differ-ent arrangement.ement. Broadwillv. Sammon ,
24 R. 814.69 S.W. 1084; but not to rents
between date of sale and confirmation .
Brown v. Berkley, 3 R. 469; Elliottv.
Bush, 3 R. 466. See fullcase of Smith
v.Newman ,140Ky .80,130S.W.995.953
ent .
(36 ) If there are growing crops on
land when sale is confirmed and pur-
chaser can not be placed in possession
of whole of land, the owner of the land
in cultivationshould pay to the pur-
chaser a reasonable rent for such land
from date of confirmation.and purchaser
isentitledupon confirmationto the pos-
session of such lands as are not in cul-
tivation. Norris v . Williams , 139 Ky .
422, 23 R. 1497, 65 S. W. 439; Robb v.
Hanna , 12 R. 361, 14 S. W. 360.
(37) Reversal- effectof. The reversal
of a judgment under which a sale is
made does not affectthe titleof the pur-
chaserat the sale, althoughhe may be
the plaintiff. Yocum v. Foreman , 14

§ 715 [837]. Record with good cross-index kept. Each presidingjudge
and justiceof thepeace shall,in a book providedforthatpurpose,keep a full
and fair record of judicialproceedings in his court; and keep a good cross-

§ 1055. Record books of judge and court The judge of the quarterly
courtshallkeep a record of allhis officialactsout of term time, and shall
keep a docket,order book and executionbook, in which shallbe recorded, as
clerksare requiredto do, the proceedingsof the court, and shallfixa rule
day forthe returnof executionsand otherprocess. The recordbooks shallbe
paid forby the county. (Ky .Stat.,sec.1055.)
§ 1060. Record of ordersand judgments- when specialjudge may sign.
The judge shallcause allorders and judgments of the county court to be
spreadby the county clerkupon the recordbook to be kept for thatpurpose.
