§ 726. (1) Practice on appeal. The
only power the circuitcourt has on ap-
peal is to render judgment for what-
ever may be found due on the trial; or,
if nothing is found due , to dismiss the
action; or, ifthe appealisnot regularly
prosecuted , or the court has no jurisdic-
tion, to dismiss the appeal, and until
some of these things are done the case
remains in court . The court has no
power to reversethe judgment of the
justice. Bennett v. Thompson , 10 Bush
365 ; and see 150 Ky . 609 .
(2) When an appeal is taken to the
circuitcourt from the judgment of a
justice'scourt that had no jurisdiction
of the subject-matter , no recovery can
be had in the circuitcourt against the
defendant, and in such case the circuit
court should make such orders as will
render the proceedingsin the justice's
courtinoperative. Wigginton v.Moss ,2
Met . 38 .
(3) If, on an appeal from the judg-
ment of a justice, it appears that the
justice had no jurisdictionof the cause,
the court should dismiss the action .
Fleming v . Limebaugh , 2 Met . 265 .
(4 ) If the case appealed is an equity
action it should on appeal be transferred
to the equity docket , and so tried, and
depositions taken for trial in lower
courtmay be read on the appeal. Moc-
quot v. Meadows , 97 Ky . 543, 17 R. 371,
31 S. W. 129 .
(4a ) Appeal - when pending . An ap-
peal to a circuitcourt is pending from
the time the record is filed. It may be
heard at a special term called to begin
before the regular term to which the
summons on the appeal is returnable.
Young v . Trimble ; Hiter v . Waddy ;
Brannon v . Myall ; Lancaster v. Thacker ,
164 Ky . 177, 175 S. W. 366.
(4b) Appeal - dismissal of action. A
circuitor quarterlycourt in which an
appeal is pending can not dismiss the
action without the consent of the appel-
lee. An order dismissing the action was
held to have no effecton the appeal. In
such a case mandamus will not go
against the justice of the peace to issue
an executionon the judgment appealed
from. Calhoune v. Kidd, 150 Ky . 609,
150 S. W. 816 .
(4c) Appeal from judgment on award .
An appeal was taken to the circuitcourt
from a judgment of a county court in a
condemnation proceeding in which it had
been agreed of record that the award of
certain arbitrators should be treated as
the verdict of a jury ; there were no ex-
ceptions to the award . The appeal was
properly dismissed. Hensley v. The
Wasiota Black Mt. R. Co., 153 Ky . 17,
154 S. W. 385 .

judgment rendered againsthim ,itshallbe at the discretionof the court to
allowhim costsor not. (Effectof tenderbeforeappeal,seeKy .Stat.,sec.
890. )

§ 729 [852] [Time inwhich appealmust be taken.] No appealshall
Upon the trialof an appeal the cause
of action must be the same as that ex-
pressed in the warrant, and itmust be
a cause of action over which the justice
had jurisdiction. It must be the same
identical case which was tried by the
justice, and not a new case. Upon thetrialof the appealno new causeof actioncan be reliedupon . The same action
may be triedanew ,but no new actionis
to be tried. Burbage v . Squires, 3 Met .
78; and see Puff v.Hutcher,78 Ky . 146.
(6) "A plaintiffcan not,upon an ap-
peal, amend his pleadings so as to set up
a new and independent cause of action.
The case, i. e., the cause of action, to be
tried on the appeal must be the same
that was tried in the lower court; but
to that casethe defendanthas the right
to make all the defenses he has . If he
failsto set up any defense, and allows
judgment to go by default, it it within
the discretion of the court in which the
appealispending for a trialde novo to
allow an answer to be filed. So , too,
that courtmay in the same way allow
the defense to be changed, or new de-
fenses to be made , as if the case had
been originallybrought in that court."
Willis v. McNeal , 8 R. 411 ; and see
Southern Lumber Co. v. Wiseman , 19 R.
585, 41 S. W. 297 ; Roberts v . Abner , 19
R. 887, 42 S. W. 337, holding that the
plaintiffwillnot on appealbe allowedto
filean amendment changingthe cause of
action .
(6a ) New defenses. On an appeal by
the executrix and devisees of a decedent
to the circuitcourtfrom a judgment of
the county court fixingthe amount of
inheritance taxes,it was proper to allow
appellants to set up defenses not set
up in the county court. Sevier'sExtx .
v. Com ., 181 Ky . 49, 203 S. W. 1070.
(6b) It is within the discretionof the
courtin which the appealis pending for
a new trial de novo to allow the defense
changed or a new defense to be made as
if the case had been originallybroughtin that court. Sevier'sExtx , etetal., v .
Com .,181 Ky . 54. See also Willis v. Mc-
Nealm , 8 R. 411; Southern Lumber Co.
v . Wiseman , 19 R. 585 , 41 S. W. 297 ;
Robertsv.Abner,19 R. 887,42 S.W. 337.
§ 727. (1) Costs - how adjudged on
appeal . When the appellant succeeds in
reducing the amount of the judgment
against him , the court may adjudge to
or withhold from him his own costs,but
can not give judgment againsthim for
the costs of the appellee. Gentry v.
Doolin , 1 Bush 1; and see L., St. L. & T.
R. R. v. Barrett, 91 Ky . 487, 13.R. 57,
16 S. W. 278 ; Boggs v . Turner , 145 Ky .
833 , 141 S. W. 420 .
(2) Judgment against appellant for
costs on dismissal of his appeal for want
of jurisdictionis erroneous. Williams v .
Wilson , 5 Dana 596 .
(3) On appeal to the circuitcourt the
plaintiffhad no cause of action and was
properly adjudged to pay the costs in
both courts. Willis v . McNeal , 8 L. R.
411 .
(4 ) This section has no application to
bastardy proceedings. Wiggins v. Com .,
23 R. 475 , 63 S. W. 31 .
(5 ) In pass -way cases the costs on the
appeal are to be adjudged as in other
cases. Vice v. Eden , 113 Ky . 255, 24 R.
132, 68 S. W. 125; Barrall v . Quick , 24
R. 2393, 74 S. W. 214; Broadway Coal
Co.v.Smith,136Ky.745,125S.W. 157.

§ 730. (1) Construction of section.
This section was intended to prevent an
appellant from dismissing his appeal or
failingto prosecuteit to the prejudice
of the appellee. Wiggins v. Com ., 102
Ky . 40 , 19 R. 1017, 42 S. W. 1106. See
further, Com . v. Alexander , 99 S. W. 670,
30 R. 775 ; West v . Shepherd , 105 S. W.
419 , 32 R. 79 , 127 Ky . 323.
(2 ) A circuit or quarterly court may
dismiss an appeal if it be not taken in
the manner provided by law or if the
courthas no jurisdictionof the appeal;
itmay also dismiss the appeal upon mo- '
tion of the appellantif the appelleeis
not before the court; ifhe be before the
court,he has the optionprovidedby this
section. Calhoune v. Kidd , 150 Ky . 600,
150 S. W. 816 .

party in the lower court, and would be
a necessaryparty to any furtherpro-
ceedingsafterreversal, must be a party
to the appeal, and if not made a party
the appeal will be dismissed. Murphy
v. O'Reilly,78 Ky . 263; by priviesare
meant heirs, executors and ter-tenants.

waiver of the right to prosecute an ap-
peal foritsreversal. Kellarv.Williams,

Appeals to issue writ of is suggested ,
but not decided. in Preston v. Fidelity

W. 318 ; but has been exercised in other
cases , see notes to sec. 479 and Consti-
tution , sec. 110 .

ment . The court will presume , unless
the contrary appears , that the lower
court committed no error. Licking Roll-
ingMill v.Fischer,88 Ky . 176,10 R. 763,

versible error. Massie v. Com ., 93 Ky .

(63) Res judicata. Every question
presentedupon an appealmust be taken
to have been disposed of by the decision
upon the appeal, unlessit be expressly
left open for further litigation. Smith
v . Brannin , 79 Ky . 114 , 1 R. 414 ; Davis
v . McCorkle . 14 Bush 746 .
(64 ) "The plea of res judicata applies,
except in specialcases,not only to points
upon which the courtwas actuallyre-
quired by the parties to form an opinion
and pronounce judgment, but to every
point which properly belonged to the
subject of litigationand which the
partiesexercisingreasonablediligence
might have brought forward." Francis
v ..Wood , 81 Ky . 16, 4 R. 616 ; Hard-
wicke v . Young , 110 Ky . 504, 22 R. 1906,
62 S. W. 10 ; Elswick v.Matney ,132 Ky .
294 , 116 S. W. 718 ; and see further
notes to sec. 368, and Bitzer v . O'Bryan ,
107 Ky . 590, 21 R. 1307, 54 S. W. 951 ;
Bridges v. McAlister , 106 Ky . 791, 21 R.
428 , 51 S. W. 603 ; Thompson v . Lou .
Banking Co.,21 R. 1611, 55 S. W. 1080.
(65) Party who is in doubt as to
proper remedy , and prosecutes two ap-
peals at same time , will not , after the
merits of the litigationhave been de-
termined in one of them by the Court of
Appeals ,be heard to complain of judg-
ment on his other appeal. Masonic Tem-
ple v.Com ., 11 R. 383,12 S.W. 143.

rect must be made in lower court be-
fore appeal will lie. Sec. 763 and notes.

is only availableagainst parties and
privies. County of Hendersonv. Hen-
derson Bridge Co., 116 Ky . 164, 25 R.
421 , 75 S. W. 239, and see note 58.

which a demurrer was filednot having
been passed on in the firstappeal will
not be considered on second appeal. L.

S. W. 620 ; and the same rule applies to

Gibbons , 24 R. 1670, 72 S. W. 12.

§ 747. (1) Cross-appeal- supersedeas.
Appellee who prosecutes a cross-appealmay supersede the judgment on his
cross-appeal, but the bond must be ap-
proved by the court and not the clerk.
Cov . Transfer Co. v . Piel, 9 R. 665. 6 S.
W. 122; and where appellee supersedes
judgment and prevents appellantfrom
enjoying benefitof his judgment he is
not entitledto the ten per cent damages
ordinarily allowed. Cov . Transfer Co. v .
Piel, 87 Ky .267, 10 R. 146, 8 S. W. 449;
when judgment is affirmedon original
and reversed on cross-appeal, appellee is
not entitled to damages . Wade v . First
Nat .Bank , 11 Bush 697,but appellantis
entitled to damages if judgment on
cross-appeal is affirmed. O'Connor v .
Henderson Bridge Co., 95 Ky . 633, 16 R.
244, 27 S. W. 251, 983.
(2) Discharge of supersedeas. Where
an appealisprayedin thelowercourt,and a supersedeas bond executed in that
court and an order of supersedeas issued,
if that appeal is abandoned and an ap-
peal granted by the clerk of the Court
of Appeals,the appelleeisentitled,upon
motion made in the Court of Appeals ,
to have the supersedeas discharged with
damages . City v. Elrod, 14 Bush 216 ,
and so if appeal prayed in lower court
is not prosecuted in time . Fuson v.
Lambdin , 23 R. 840, 64 S. W. 448 ; and
see L. & N. R. R. v. Lucas , 27 R. 769,
86 S. W. 682 .
(3) Effect of supersedeas- a super-
sedeas is not retroactive; it only pre-
serves the condition of affairs as they
existed at the time it was issued .
Thompson v . Thompson , 24 R. 645, 69 S.
W. 714 ; and after a judgment has been
superseded no further steps can be taken
under it. Webber v . Tanner , 23 R. 1107,
64 S. W. 741; Hey v. Harding, 25 R.
1454 , 78 S. W. 136 ; Townsend v . Gorin ,
144 Ky . 671, 139 S. W. 865; Gardner v.

judgment,iftheappealbe grantedby thatcourt. In othercases,itmust

clerk'sofficeof the Court of Appeals pursuant to section738. In other
cases,itmust be issuedby theclerkof theCourt ofAppeals.

§ 755. (1) Cross-appeal. If a cross-peal .
appeal is desiredthe appelleemay file
his schedule in the court below , either
before or after the schedule is filedby
the appellant. No schedule is required
when the entire record is copied. L. &
N. R. R. Co. v . Brice, 83 Ky . 210, 7 R.
180; but party taking cross-appeal has
right to filescheduleand cause entire
record copied, and if he fail to do this
it will be presumed that omitted record
will sustain judgment against him .
Adams v.Bement ,96 Ky .334, 16 R. 676,
29 S. W. 22 .
(2) A cross-appeal can not be consid-
ered when the parties to be affected by
it are not before the court. Daviess Co.
v.Howard , 13 Bush 101, and see note 11.
(3 ) In an ordinary action if the ap-
pellee desires to avail himself on cross-
appeal of errorscommitted by trialcourt
he must filemotion and grounds for a
new trial in lower court . L. & N. R. R.
v. Whitehead ,24 R. 2314 , 73 S. W. 1128.
(4 ) "The legal effectof the affirmance
on the original appeal, there being a re-
versal of the judgment on the cross-
appeal , was not to leave the judgment
unreversed , but to decide that there was
no errorto the prejudiceof the appel-
lant, and that he was not entitled to
a reversal." In such case the appellee
is not entitled to damages on the bond
executed by the appellant. Wade v .
First National Bank , 11 Bush 697 .
(5 ) Cross-appeal is only allowable in
behalf of the appellee against the ap-
pellant and not against a co-appellee.
Smith v . Bank , 1 Met . 575 ; Gaar v .
Banking Co., 11 Bush 190; Home Ins.
Co. v .Gaddis ,3 R. 159 ; McKay v. Mayes ,
17 R. 827, 32 S. W. 606 ; Marion Nat .
Bank v. Phillips, 18 R. 159, 35 S. W. 910 ;
Asher v. Helton , 31 R. 9, 101 S. W. 350 ;
Matney v. Edmonds , 179 Ку . 243, 200
S. W. 365 .
(6) When eitherparty appealsfrom a
finaljudgment his adversary may have
a cross-appeal for the purpose of cor-
recting any error to his prejudice or any
interlocutory order that controlled or
influencedthe judgment,but he can not
have a cross-appeal from a judgment
wholly distinctfrom the judgment ap-
pealed from . Brown v .Vancleave ,86 Ky .
381,9 R. 593,6 S. W. 25 ; see also note 12 .
(7 ) Cross-appeal can not be granted by
circuit court. Murphy v . Blandford , 11
R. 125, 11 S. W. 715 ; it must be granted
by Court of Appeals. Mud v. Mullican,
11 R. 417 , 12 S. W. 263 , 385 ; Hancock v .
Hancock , 24 R. 664 , 69 S. W. 757 ; Hilton
v . Com ., 127 Ку . 486, 105 S. W. 956, 32
R. 377; and may be prosecuted within
two years from renditionof judgment .
Second Nat . Bk . v. Fitzpatrick, 111 Ky .
228, 23 R. 610,63 S. W. 459.

may supersedejudgment on his cross-

a judgment is renderedin favor of one

versed at the instance of the defendant

fore the court in any way , and a judg-

ants, the one who is before the court is

tion hand . Upon the entire record, how-
ever, this error was not prejudicialnor
was there a fatal variance between the
pleading and the proof. C. & O. Ry . Co.

wife, and alsoagainsther latehusband,

161 .
(7 ) Although a deposition was errone-
ously rejected, the court will consider
the effectof it, and if,when admitted , it

rath v . Viley, 1 Bush 266 ; Millett v .
Parker,2 Met . 608; Tudor v. Tudor, 17

Smith v . Bank , 1 Met . 575 .
(8 ) The refusal of the court to allow
a party to testify will not authorize a
reversalwhen it does not appear that he
would have stated any fact beneficialto
himself . Francis v . Com ., 3 Bush 4 .
(9 ) Refusal of court to pass upon ex-
ceptions to depositions on the ground
that they are incompetent and irrelevant
will not warrant a reversal, when the
depositionsare competent and relevant.
Root v . Merriwether , 8 Bush 397, Nor
is an error in overruling a demurrer to
a pleading prejudicialwhen no instruc-
tion based on the pleading is given.
Beaven v. Phillips, 83 Ky . 88, 7 R. 9.

irrelevantmatter in a pleading will not
warrant a reversal unless it appear that

Buckles v . Lambert , 4 Met . 330.

fense after the case has been called for

joinderafterevidencewas heard was not

It is error to inflict a fine for the vio-
lation of a void ordinance . United Fuel
& Gas Co. v.Com .,159 Ky . 34, 166 S. W.
783 .
(12) Incompetent evidence . In an
equity case the Court of Appeals will
consideronly the competent evidence-
even if incompetent is admitted by the
chancellor and from that determine
whether the judgment is erroneous or
not . Wicks v. Dean , 103 Ky . 69, 19 R.
1708 , 44 S. W. 397 .
(12a) Expert testimony . The intro-
duction of expert testimony where the
same was incompetent is not ground for
reversal, if such testimony was intro-
duced solelyto refute and contradict in-
competent and expert testimony intro-
duced by appellant. Interstate Coal Co.
v. Shelton , 160 Ky . 40, 169 S. W. 546.
(12b ) Injunctions. The court on an
appeal followed the rulings of an appel-
late judge on a motion to reinstate a
temporary injunctionwhich had been is-
sued by a circuitclerk; in considering
the motion six members of the court had
concurred. Walker v. Goode, 153 Ky .
795, 156 S. W. 893; Myrick v. Morgan ,
153 Ky . 795 , 156 S. W. 893 ; Myrick v .
Hogue , 153 Ky .795, 156 S. W. 893.
(13) Joint and several judgment . A
judgment againstpersons jointlyliable
isan entirety, and if void as to one is
void as to all; but where it is several
as to the parties it may be reversed as
to one and affirmed as to another . Joyes
v . Hamilton , 10 Bush 544.
(14) Judgment correct , but reason
wrong . A correctjudgment must be af-
firmed, although it may have been erro-
neously made to rest on a dierent ground
by the lower court. Poor v . Robinson ,
13 Bush 200 ; or given for a wrong or in-
sufficientreason. Ireland v .Berryman , 3
Bush 356; American Nail Co. v.Bayless,
91 Ky . 94, 12 R. 694, 15 S. W. 10; Com .
v . McCauley's Exr ., 166 Ky . 450, 179 S.
W. 411; Ballv.Wasioto,& c.,Ry .Co.,157
Ky . 166, 162 S. W. 818.
(15) Remitter of judgment will in
some cases prevent a reversal for ex-
cessive damages . City v . Gardner , 19 R.
302, 40 S. W. 779, and notes to sec. 340 .
(16) Three verdicts. Where there
have been three verdicts for the same
party the court will not disturb the last
one, although the weight of evidenceis
againstit. L. & N. R. R. Co. v. Graves,
78Ky .74;Sanfordv.Smith,5 Bush471.
See sec. 341. Wall v . Dimmitt , 141 Ky .
715, 133 S. W. 768.
(16a) On the third trial of a case a
verdict was rendered for defendant , and
plaintiffwas refused a new trial. Plain-
tiff'smotion to substitute the judgment
entered on the verdict , on the first and
second trials, was overruled. The Court
of Appeals reviewed the judgment and
rulings of the circuitcourt on the first
and second as well as the third trial . It
appeared that the granting of each of the
new trials was authorized because of
errors committed by the circuitcourt in
instructingthe jury and that the pro-
ceedings on the third trialwere free from
error. The judgment on the last trial
was affirmed. Keiner v.Collins, 161Ky .
696 , 171 S. W. 399 .
(17) Verdict against evidence. The
court will not reverse on the ground that
the verdict is not sustained by the evi-
dence, unlessit is clearlyand palpably
againstthe weight of evidence. Sanford
v . Smith , 5 Bush 471 ; Becker v . Crow ,
7 Bush 198 ; Varble v . Bigley, 14 Bush
698 ; Com . v . Williams , 14 Bush 297 ; L.
& N. R. Co. v . Parks ' Admr ., 154 Ку .
269 , 157 S. W. 27 ; Adams Ex .Co. v .Nat .
Bk . of Middlesboro , 162 Ky . 400, 172 S.
W. 660 ; L. & N. R. R. Co. v .Nethery ,160
Ky . 369, 169 S. W. 883; Williamson v.
Bently , 158 Ky . 346. 164 S. W. 951 ;
White Sewing Mach .Co. v.Mahoney , 156
Ky . 805, 162 S. W. 80 ; Music v. B. S. &
625

§ 756] [Title XVIII .Trial and Decision .
Ky . R. Ry . Co., 163 Ky . 628, 174 S. W.
44; Middleton v . Potts , 163 Ky . 550, 174
S. W. 9; Denker Trans . Co. v . Pugh , 162
Ky . 818. 173 S. W. 139. Stafford v . B. S.
& Ky .R. Ry . Co.,164 Ky . 582, 176 S. W.
14; SadievilleMillingCo. v. C., N. O. &
T. P. Ry .Co., 153 Ky .55,154 S.W. 396;
Chaney v. Com ., 149 Ky . 464, 149 S. W.
923 ; C., N. O. & T. P. Ry . Co. v . Bran-
denburg, 149 Ky . 582, 149 S. W. 988;
Sizemore v.Nance ,149 Ky . 819,149 S. W.
1126; Anderson Co. Bd . of Control v .
Sou. Ry . in Ky ., 152 Ky . 278, 153 S. W.
421 ; Sou .Ry . Co. v. Alford, 150 Ky .808,
150 S. W. 985 ; Aetna L , Ins . Co. v . Rus-
tin, 151 Ky . 103, 151 S. W. 366 ; Cas . Co.
of Am . v. Rustin , 151 Ky . 103, 151 S. W.
366; Fid . & Cas . Co. of N. Y. v . Rustin ,
151 Ky . 103, 151 S. W. 366 ; Travelers'
Ins.Co. of Hartford ,Conn .,v .Rustin , 151
Ky . 103, 151 S. W. 366.
(17a) Upon an appeal it was held that
a certain verdict for a plaintiffwas fla-
grantly against the evidence. On the
next trialthe evidence was not improved .
It was proper for the circuitcourt to let
the case go to the jury and afterward to
set aside a verdict for the plaintiffas
fiagrantlyagainstthe evidence. C. & O.
Ry . Co. v. Johnson, 151 Ky . 809, 152 S.
W. 962 .
(17b) In an actionagainsta railroad
company for damages resulting from a
fireallegedto have been caused by the
emission of sparks and cinders from a
locomotiveby reasonof a failuretopro-
vide a proper spark arrester, a verdict
based wholly upon circumstantialevi-
dence in favor of the plaintiff, may be
sustained. C. & O. Ry . Co. v .Snyder ,164
Ky . 432 , 175 S. W. 640.
(18) The judgment of the court, when
the law and facts are submitted to the
court, will not be disturbed unless fla-
grantly against the evidence. Helm v .
Coffey, 80 Ky . 176, 3 R. 677; Russellv.
M. & C. T. Co., 13 Bush 307; Coleman v .
Meade , 13 Bush 358 , 12 Bush 451 ; 12
Bush 469 .
(19 ) Waiver of error . No error can be
taken advantage of on the appeal unless
it was reliedupon in the grounds for a
new trial. Com . v . Williams , 14 Bush
297; McLain v . Dibble, 13 Bush 297;
Alexander v . Humber , 86 Kv . 565 , 9 R.
734, 6 S. W. 453; Acme Mills v. Rives ,
141 Ky .783. 133 S. W. 786. See further,
notes to secs. 333 , 335 , 340 .
(20 ) Unless there is a motion and
grounds for a new trial, there is nothing
before the court except the sufficiencyof
the pleadings. Helm v . Coffey, 80 Ky .
176, 3 R. 677; Henderson v . Dupree , $2
Ky . 678, 6 R. 702 ; Harper v . Harper , 10
Bush 447; see further, notes to sec. 340.
(20a ) Erroneous instructions not com-
plainedof in the grounds fora new trial
areno groundsforreversal. Mann Bros.
v . City of Henderson , 154 Ky . 154, 156
S. W. 1063 .
(20b ) No error committed during a
trialis available on appeal unless it has
been relied on as a ground for a new
trial. L. & N. R. Co. v . Com ., 154 Ky .
293 , 157 S. W. 369 .
(20c ) Errors in rulings of the circuit
court were not reviewed on appeal be-
cause no objection was made nor excep
tion taken in the circuit court . L. & N.
R. R. Co. v . Scalf, 155 Ky . 273, 159 S. W.
804 .
(20d ) The action of the circuitcourt
in giving a peremptory instruction will
not be reviewed by the Court of Appeals
in the absence of a motion for a new
trial. Witt v. L. & E. Ry .Co., 158 Ky .
401, 165 S. W. 399.
(20e ) An error in admitting evidence
was no ground for a reversal , because not
made a ground for a new trial. L. & I.
Ry . Co. v. Roemmele ,157 Ky .84, 162 S.
W. 547 .
(20f ) In a settlement suit the Court of
Appeals declined to consider certain ob-
jections to an order not presented in the
circuitcourt. Harding v. Harding, 151
Ky . 398,152 S.W. 259.
(20g ) If parol proof of title be re-
ceived without objectionon a trialin the
circuitcourt,thequestioquestionoftheincom-
petency of such testimony can not be
raisedforthe firsttime on appeal. Oney
v . Lovely , 151 Ky . 651, 152 S. W. 785.
(21 ) in an action attacking a convey-
ance as fraudulent , it is too late to ob-
ject for the firsttime in Court of Ap-
peals that plaintiffdid not have a "те-
turn of no property ." Behan v. Wartield,
90 Ky . 151 , 11 R. 960 , 13 S. W. 439 ; and
see further as to waiver , Hare v . Ander-
son , 12 R. 303 , 14 S. W. 216 ; Brown v .
Vancleave , 14 R. 821 , 21 S. W. 756 ; and
see further as to waiver by failure to
take proper exception, notes to sers. 333,
325 .
(21a) An objection that a city ordi-
nance was not properlyadopted can not
be made for the first time in the Court
of Appeals , Creekmore v . Cent . Const .
Co 157 Kv . 336 , 163 S. W 194 .
(21b ) The Court of Appeals will not
consider a federal question which was
suggestedforthe firsttime in the argu-
ment of the motion for a new trial in a
circuit court . L. & N. R. Co. v . Wood-
ford , 152 Ky . 398 , 153 S. W. 722 .
626

Title XVIII ] [§ 756Trial and Decision .
(21c) An allegationof negligencein
general terms will not authorize a re-
covery by a servant for failure of the
master to use careto furnisha safeplace
to work or safe appliances. The failure
to objectto evidenceas to unsafe ap-
plianceswas not a waiver of thisobjec-
tion,where the evidence so admitted was
competent on an issuein the case. Ohio
ValleyC. & M. Co. v.Heine, 159 Ky .586,
167 S. W. 873 .
(21d)Errornotpointedoutinappel-lant'sbrief. The briefforappellantsdidnotpointoutany error. The judg-
ment was affirmed. Brown v . Daniels,
154 Ky . 267, 157 S. W. 3.
(22) Second appeal. Where facts on
second appeal are same as on firstand
firstjudgment was reversed because con-
trary to evidence, the second will be re-
versed for same reason . I. C. R. Co. v .
Long , 146 Ky . 170, 142 S. W. 212.
(22a ) The opinion of the Court of Ap-
peals on a former appeal was held to be
the law of the case on a second trial.
Doherty v.FirstNat . Bank ,161 Ky .202,
170 S. W. 615 ; Nat . Cash Register Co.
v .Williams , 161 Ky . 550 , 171 S. W. 162;
Mut . Ben . L. Ins. Co. v . O'Brien, 149
Ky .514, 149 S. W. 870 ; Harcourt v.Red-
mon , 149 Kv . 612. 149 S. W. 938 ; Bauer
Cooperage Co. v.Ewell & Smith,149 Ky .
838, 149 S. W. 1137 ; Brooks v . City of
Maysville 151 Κv . 707, 152 S. W. 788.
Gossett v.Ky .Mfg .Co.,153 Ky . 101, 154
S. W. 897 ; Mahoney v Mentz , 153 Ky .
484 , 155 S. W. 1137 ; Bates v . Northern
C. & C. Co., 162 Ky . 459 , 172 S. W. 918 ;
Helm v C. N. O. & T. P. Ry . Co. , 156
Ky .240 , 160 S. W. 945 ; C., N. O. & Τ . Ρ .
Ry . Co. v. Padgett , 163 Ky . 284, 173 S.
W. 780 .
(22b) An opinion of the Court of Ap-
peals is the law of that case not only as
to matters stated therein , but as to
every matter brought before the court
on the appealand properlypresentedfor
adjudication . New Bell Jellico Coal Co.
v . Sowders , 162 Ky . 443 , 172 S. W. 914.
(22c) The mere circumstance that an
instruction, although a proper one, was
not given on the firsttrial, and therefore
not consideredand approved on appeal
does not of itselfprecludethe givingof
it on a second trial. Andonique v. Car-
men , 162 Ky .154,172 S. W. 112.
(22d ) The expression of an opinion on
the port of the Court of Appeals upon an
appeal as to the existenceof a certain
state of facts is not binding on a new
trial of the case if another state of facts
be shown by evidence. L. & N. R. R. Co.
v. Stewart'sAdmx ., 163 Ky . 823, 174 S.
W. 744 .
(22e) On a former appeal the Court of
Appeals ruledthat a peremptoryinstruc-
tionshouldhave been given; on the sec-
ond trialthe evidence differedmaterially
from the evidence on the first trial. It
was proper to submit the case to the
jury. L. & N. R. Co. v. Com ., 150 Ky .
176, 150 S. W. 79 .
(23 ) Instructions. When the court on
appealdirectsthe instructionsto begivenandtheevidenceon secondtrialissubstantiallysame as on first,theappel-lant can not complain of instructionson
second appeal if court followed direc-
tions. C. & O. R. Co. v. Long , 141 Ky .
592 , 133 S. W. 570 ; Goff v . Lowe , 141
Ky . 799, 133 S. W. 995.
(24 ) Unless an instruction is disap-
proved on appeal it must be treated as
correct on subsequent trial. Wall v .
Dimmitt , 141 Ky .715,133 S. W. 768.
(25) Errors passed on or ignored on
firstappeal can not be raised on second
appeal. The opinion on firstappeal is
law of case. Wall v . Dimmitt , 141 Kу .
715, 133 S. W. 768 ; Goff v. Lowe , 141
Ky . 799, 133 S. W. 995 : Samuels v . Gil-
more . 142 Ky . 166,134 S. W. 169.
(250 ) If on an appeal to the Court of
Appeals in the opinion certain instruc-
tions given below are not criticized, they
are thereby tacitlyapproved , and become
the law of the case; but the court is not
precluded from giving on a return of the
case a proper instructionon another sub-
ject which was not covered by an instruc-
tion given on the other trial. Standard
Oil Co. v. Marlow , 162 Ky . 1, 171 S. W.
436 .
(26 ) Waiver of error. Party who does
not appeal or prosecute a cross-appeal
can not complain of errors. Vanhoose
v . Wheeler . 141 Ky . 746. 133 S. W. 779 .
(27) Purchaser at judicialsale can not
appeal from order confirming sale after
he has accepted deed and been put in
possession. Haggin v . Montague , 101 S.
W. 893 , 125 Ky . 507, 31 R. 123.
(28) Divorce- death of husband appel-
lee. Ordinarily, the death of an appellee
after submission of the appeal is not
material to the decision. Pending an ap-
peal by a wife from a judgment refusing
her divorce or alimony , the husband died
after submission and before decision . The
court on motion dismissed the appeal at
the husband's costs. Barger v . Barger ,
151 Ky . 234, 151 S. W. 406.
(29 ) Consent judgment . No appeal lies
from a consent judgment . Com .v.Helm ,
163 Ky . 69, 173 S. W. 389.
627

§ 756 ] [Title XVIIITrial and Decision .
(30) Peremptory instructions. If in a
common law action there is any evi-
dence to support thee plaintiff'sclaim,
the issue should be submitted to the jury .
Royal Neighbors of A. v . Laufman , 158
Ky . 358, 164 S. W. 966.
(31) It was not error to refuse to give
a peremptory instructionin a case where
there was some evidence authorizing the
submission of the case to the jury . L. &
L Ry . Co. v. Roemmele , 157 Ky . 84, 162
S. W. 547 .
(32) Supplied papers. Papers supplied
in the circuitcourt in place of lost pa-
pers may take the place of such papers
for the purposes of appeal ; objection to
the correctness of the supplied record
must be made by appeal from an order
of the circuitcourt supplying the record.
Such objectionscan not be considered on
the appeal taken on the merits of the
case. Turner v. Hamlin , 152 Ky . 469,
153 S. W. 778 .
(33 ) Absence of bill of exceptions. In
the absence of a bill of exceptions the
Court of Appeals will not review an al-
leged errorof the court in suspending
the trialof a case and using some of the
jurors sworn in that case to try othercases. Sams v. Gray , 156 Ky . 615, 161
S. W. 553 .
(34 ) In the absence of a bill of excep-
tions the Court of Appeals will consider
only the sufficiencyof the pleadings.
'Tyler v. Woerner , 158 Ky . 110, 166 S. W.
178 ; Bobbitt v. Blakemore , 153 Ky . 170,
154 S. W. 941 .
(35 ) Absence of transcript of evi-
dence . Where there is no transcript of
the evidence in the record filed in the
Court of Appeals ,the only question to be
considered is whether the pleadings sup-
port the judgment. Myers v.Saltry,163
Ку . 481, 173 S. W. 1138.
(36 ) On an appeal from a judgment
affirming the removal of a guardian the
transcript failed to contain the exhibits
or evidence upon which the circuitcourt
acted; the judgment was affirmed. Ex
Parte Spurling'sGdn., 165 Ky . 349, 176
S. W. 1139 .
(37) Absence of some of thepleadings.
In the absence from the record of cer-
tain pleadings and evidence it was pre-
sumed that the omitted portions of the
record would support the judgment .
Smith v . Berry , 164 Ky . 621, 176 S ,
W. 17 .
(38 ) Instructions not identified. In-
structions which were not identifiedby
a billof exceptions were disregarded by
the Court of Appeals . Gardner v. Alex-
ander , 159 Ky .713, 169 S. W. 466 ; Gooch
v.Collins,156 Ky .282,160 S. W. 1038.
(39) Reversal of judgment on verdict
with instructionsto enter judgment for
part of the finding. The jury made sep-
arate findings on two different items .
The Court of Appeals reversedthe judg-
ment as to one item and affirmed as to
the other. Swann -Day Lumb . Co. v. Cor-
nett, 161 Ky . 98, 170 S. W. 516.
(40) A judgment renderedupon a ver-
dict may be reversedwith instructions
to exclude certain items included in the
verdict . A new trial need not be awarded .
West v.King , 165 Ky . 561, 174 S. W. 11.
(41) Where in an action on contract
part of the amount awarded is due to
an erroneousinstruction, the Court of
Appeals may set aside so much as is
assessed under the erroneous instruc-
tion and sustain the amount properly
awarded . Sturgeon's Admr . v . McCorkle ,
163 Ky . 8, 173 S. W. 149.
(42 ) Error to prejudice of appellee
where no cross-appeal. Where there was
no cross-appeal the court refused to con-
sider errors committed to the prejudice
of the appellee. Stark v. Scott, 158 Ky .
596, 165 S. W. 976.
(43 ) When judgment for costs on ap-
peal a clericalmisprision. On an ap-
peal on which costs ought not to be ad-
judged , the opinion did not directa judg-
ment for costs. A judgment for costs
was a clericalmisprision and was set
aside after the term . C. & O. Ry .Co. v .
Harmon , 159 Ky . 59, 166 S. W. 786.
(44 ) Right to prosecute appeal and
also an action for new trial. A party
who has been defeated in an action may
prosecute an appeal and an action for a
new trial at the same time when the
ground upon which he seeks a new trial
is one of which he could not avail him-
selfon the appeal. Rice v.Blair,161Ky .
280 , 170 S. W. 657 .
(45 ) Correction of record by consent
after reversal. After a judgment of re-
versal it was agreed by counsel that
there was a mistake in the record . This
matter was corrected; a rehearing was
granted, and the judgment of the lower
court was affirmed . Com .v .Gold & Stock
Tel . Co. , 164 Ky . 570 , 175 S. W. 997 .
(46 ) Grant of a new trialby circuit
court pending an appeal . While an ap-
peal was pending in the Court of Ap-
peals the circuit court granted a new
trial on petition. The pending appeal
was thereupon dismissed by the court
without a consideration of its merits .
Anshutz v. Lou .Ry .Co.,152 Ky .741, 154
628

Title XVIII ] [§ 757Trial and Decision .
§ 757 [897] [Dismissalof appeal- enforcementof judgment forpart
ofdemand ,effecton appeal.) Ifitappearfrom therecordthatan appeal
was improperlygranted,or thattheappellant'srightstoprosecuteitfur-
ther has ceased, the appelleemay , upon statingthe grounds in writing,
move thecourttodismisstheappeal. The motionshallnotbe heard,with-
outtheappellant'sconsent,beforetheday on whichthecaseissetfortrial
on thedocket[exceptupon fivedays'noticeservedupon theappellantor
upon hisattorneyof record]. [But when a partyrecoversjudgment for
only part of the demand or propertyhe sues for,the enforcement of such
judgment shallnotpreventhim from prosecutingan appealtherefromas

Cross reference: See Miller'sKy . Appellate Practiceand Forms ,secs.79,85,111, 112.
S. W. 13 ; Lou . Ry . Co. v . Anshutz , 152
Ky . 741, 154 S. W. 13.
(47 ) Appeal from allowance of attor-
ney's fees- attorneysnecessaryparties.
Complaint was made that certain attor-
neys' fees were improperly allowed in an
action, but the attorneys were not made
partiesto the appeal. The courtrefused
to review the action. Angel v. Wood ,
153 Ky . 195, 154 S. W. 1103.
(48) Repleader awarded . Where the
record is so imperfect that the court can
not determine with fairness to the liti-
gants what judgment shouldbe entered,
the case will be rewarded to reform the
pleadings and take such evidence as may
be necessary to present clearlythe issues
between the parties, so that justicemay
be done . Nickels v. Collins, 158 Ky . 117,
164 S. W. 984 .
(49 ) Dismissal by Court of Appeals ,
where no real issue between parties.
Where it appeared on a trialin the cir-
cuit court upon a writ of forcible de-
tainer that the defendant had already
been dispossessed,and that the plaintiff
or his vendee was then in actual posses-
sion of the property, there was no real
issue between the parties. Upon appeal
to the Court of Appeals , the court upon
its own motion dismissed the appeal.
Pittinger v . Gratz , 157 Ky . 401, 163 S.
W. 203 .
§ 757. (1) Dismissal of appeal. When
an appeal is sought to be dismissed on
the ground that it is barred by limita-
tion, a writtenplea must be filed. The
objection can not be made by motion .
Riley v. Reed , 13 Bush 411 ; Herman v.
Martin , 107 Ky . 642, 21 R. 1396 , 55 S.
W. 429 ; see sec. 758.
(2) A motion to dismissan appeal on
the ground that appellant had compro-
mised with appelleewill not be enter-
tained unless the plea is presented by a
verified answer , as required in sc. 758 .
Empire Co. v. McIntosh , 82 Ky .554, 6 R.
631; and see Spaldingv.Wathen ,7 Bush
659 , 6 R. 631 .
(2a) Where pending the prosecution of
an appeal the defendant effected a set-
tlement with plaintiff, the appeal must
be dismissed. Ohio River Contract Co. v .
Pennybacher , 168 Ky . 78 , 181 S. W. 946.
(3) The fact that appellant had agreed
to pay, and appelleeto receive, a less
sum than the judgment as a compromise
in fullsatisfactionof the judgment ap-
pealed from can not be pleaded in the
Court of Appeals to show that appellant
had no right to prosecute the appeal.
Parks v . Doty , 13 Bush 727 .
(4) Accepting satisfactionof judg-
ments bars right of appeal. See note 1,
sec. 734 ; but where party only recovers
judgment for part of debt that he sues
for he may enforce collection of that
part and appeal as to part he failed to
recover. O'Connor v. Henderson Bridge
Co.,95 Ky .633,16 R. 244, 27 S. W. 251.
(5 ) Whenever any person occupying a
fiducialrelation resigns or is removed
from officehis right to prosecute an ap-
peal terminates, and this fact may be
brought to the notice of the court by an
answer ; so, when the right of a bank-
rupt passes to his assignee. Parks v.
Doty , 13 Bush 727 .
(6 ) When appellant dies after judg-
ment and before appeal, or pending the
appeal, his death should be taken ad-
vantage of in the Court of Appeals by
a plea under the provisions of sec. 758.
When an appeal is prosecuted in the
name of a dead person the judgment re-
versing the case is not void. Spalding
v . Wathen , 7 Bush 659 ; and see further
cases cited in note to sec. 767 .

judicialnoticeof the law of a case as

(8) Notice of motion to dismiss ap-
pealisnecessaryifmotion ismade with-
out consent and before day case is set
for hearing. Empire Coal Co. v . MeIn-
tosh , 5 R. 599 .
(9) Ifan appealisirregularlygranted
the proper remedy is by a motion to
dismiss the appeal or dischargethe su-
persedeas. Schmidt v . Mitchell, 95 Ky .

(10) Motion to dismiss appeal should
be made when case is submitted or it
willbe waived. Baileyv. L & N. R. R.,
19 R. 1617,44 S. W. 105 ; and see Welch
v. Nat . Cash RegisterCo., 103 Ky . 192,
19 R. 1857,44 S. W. 124; Dearing v .Wil-

Com ..26 R. 142 , 80 S. W. 1150 .
(11) Where a party failsto recover
amount he sues for he may enforce
judgment he does obtain, and appeal
from judgment against him as to bal-
ance of his claim, and if the appellee
supersedes the collectionof judgment ob-

§ 762. (1) Enforcement of mandates .
The Court of Appeals can not undertake
to dictate or control the manner in
which the judgment of the lower court,
when affirmed by it, is to be enforced,
unless the court should absolutely refuse
to enforce it; in such case mandamus
might be issued by Court of Appeals ,
but where the lower court attempts to
enforcethe judgment renderedon appeal
by a judgment,the remedy isby appeal.
Rohmeiser v.Bannon , 15 R. 114,26 S. W.
721 ; see further , notes 6-10 , sec. 761 .
(2 ) Mandate must be considered with
referenceto the opinion. Watson v.
Avery , 3 Bush 635 : but this rule is not
applicableto a response to a petition for
a rehearing. Denny v . Wickliffe, 1 Met .
216 , and see sec. 761 and notes .
(3) Powers of court. "This court ha's
no power to determine the question of
the liabilityof a sherifffor failing to

obedience to its process. Marchand v .
Russell, 78 Ky . 517, 1 R. 126; and see
Shackelford v . Patterson , 110 Ky . 863,
23 R. 316 , 62 S. W. 1040 .
(4 ) As to itspower to punish for con-
tempt, see In re Woolley, 11 Bush 95;
and as to power to enforce itsmandates ,
see Watson v . Avery , 3 Bush 635 ; Smith
v . Cochran , 7 Bush 548 ; as to power to
change itsdecision of a former term , see
Beazley v . Mershon , 6 Bush 424 ; Finnell
v . Jones , 7 Bush 359; 11 Bush 697.
(5) See further, Constitution, sec. 110,
and cases cited in note thereto .

334 ; the court held that a sheriff had a

of the common law and it will be pre-

§ 39. (1) Manner of arrest. An ar
rest made in substantial compliance with
the terms of this sectionis a legalarrest,
but to make , or attempt to make , an ar-
rest in disregard or violationof this sec-
tion is an illegalact which the person
about to be arrested is not required to
submit to. Wright v . Com ., 85 Ky . 123,
8 R. 718, 2 S. W. 904 ; Hamlin v. Com .,
11 R. 348 , 12 S. W. 146 .
(2 ) If there be reasonable opportunity,
the person attempting to make the ar-
rest must inform the party about to be
arrested of his intention , of the offense
charged , and , if acting under a warrant ,
must say so, and , if demanded , show it.
Bates v . Com ., 13 R. 132, 16 S. W. 528 ;
Com . v.West , 113 S. W. 76.
(3) When a person knows that he is
about to be arrested and the offense
charged against him , it is not necessary
that the person making the arrest should
give him the information required by
this section. Bowling v . Com ., 7 R. 821.
(4 ) If the defendant knows the officer
who is attempting to arrest him and
knows for what he is arrested and makes
an immediate attack upon the officerhe
can not complain that the warrant was
not read to him . Tuck v . Belliles, 153
Ky . 848, 156 S. W. 883.
§ 40. Breaking door to arrest. The
sheriff, in order to make an arrest, has
the right to break the outer or inner
doors,but he can not break the house of
a third person to arrest a criminal who
does not dwell in that house unless the
person to be arrested be actually in the
house at the time of the entrance . Haw-
kins v . Com . , 14 B. M. 395 ; Ky . Stat .,
sec .4583 .
§41 . Persons not summoned have no
right to aid an officerin making an ar-
rest. Hamlin v .Com ., 11 R. 348, 12 S. W.
146; as to right of person to resistarrest

§ 43. (1) Force officermay use.Where
an officeris attempting to make an ar-
restfor felonyhe may use such forceas
is necessary to capture the felon,even to
killinghim when in flight; but where it
is a misdemeanor the officer can not kill
the offenderunless he is resistingto such
an extent as to place the officerin dan-
ger of loss of lifeor great bodily harm .
Dilger v. Com ., 88 Ky . 550, 11 R. 67, 11
S. W. 651 ; and an officerwithout a war-
rant is not authorized to kill a person
fleeing from him , although he may be-
lieve he has committed a felony. Petrie
v. Cartwright, 114 Ky . 103, 24 R. 903,
70 S. W. 297 .
(2 ) The above rule applies when an
officeris attempting to arrest a felon
who has escaped . but in misdemeanor
cases when the offenderis not resisting,
but fleeing, the officerhas no right to kill
or shoot and wound . Head v . Martin , 85
Ky . 480 , 9 R. 45 , 3 S. W. 622 ; Doolin v .
Com . , 15 R. 408 , 23 S. W. 663 ; Bowman
v .Com .,96 Ky .8, 16 R. 186,27 S. W. 870.
(3) The provision that " no unneces-
sary force or violence shall be used"
pecessarily mean that no more force
shallbe used than "reasonablyappears"
to the person making the arrest to be
necessary. Bowling v . Com ., 7 R. 821 ;
Hamlin v . Com ., 11 R. 348 , 12 S. W. 146 .
(4 ) An officerin making an arrest for
a misdemeanor if resisted may use such
force as is necessary and if he be placed
in peril he may use force for self-protec-
tion. Tuck v. Belliles, 153 Ky . 848, 156
S. W. Y. 883 .

§ 49 [46] [Magistratetoexaminecharge- judgment.) A magistrate
of thecountyinwhich a publicoffensehas been committedisauthorized
to examine the charge, and commit to jailor hold to bail the person
chargedwithitscommission,exceptas providedinsection71. (See sec-
tion71a. Bailmay be sworn; qualificationsof,secs.76,77.)

§ 49. (1) Magistrates - power of.
Judges of county or policeor city courts,
and justicesof the peace are magistrates
(sec.26 ),and any one of them may com-
mit or hold to bail except as provided
in sec. 71 ; see amendment to that sec-
tion. Com . v. Cummings , 18 B. M. 26 ;

(2) There must be a personin custody

trate jurisdictionto hold an examining
court. Com . v. Thompson , 98 Ky . 593, 17
R. 1132, 33 S. W. 1103.
(3) Recognizance . Bail may be taken
by recognizancenceenteredinto before ex-
amining court. Roberts v . Com ., 7 Bush
430 ; see form of recognizance, Form 228.
(4 ) Sunday - bail. A bail bond or rec-
ognizance executed on Sunday before an
officer authorized to take the same is
legal and binding on the defendant and
his sureties. Rice v . Com ., 3 Bush 14 ;
Watts v . Com ., 5 Bush 309.

§ 68. (1) Bail after commitment .
After the commencement of the term of
the circuitcourt, the committing magis-
trate or county judge has no authority to
admit the defendant to bail; if the bail
is fixed the clerk of the court may take
it. A bail bond executed before an officer
not authorized by law to take it is not
obligatory on the bail. Branham v.Com .,
2 Bush 3 .
(2) The clerk has no authority to take
bail when the amount has not been fixed
by the court. Wallenweber v. Com ., 3
Bush 68; but when amount of bail has
been fixedthe clerk may at any time
after the expiration of the firstterm
take bail,even during a subsequent term
of the court. Wilson v . Com ., 99 Ky .
167 , 18 R. 51 , 35 S. W. 274 .
(3) The defendant , having been in-
dicted for murder and confinedin jail
without any orderas to bailbeingmade ,
sued out a writ of habeas corpus, and
was admitted to bail by the two justices
who heard the application(sec. 423);
failingto appear, the suretiesin the
bond were held liable. Creekmore v .
Com .. 5 Bush 312 ; and see Bethuram v .
Black , 11 Bush 628 .
(4 ) A jailerhas no authority to take
bail and a bail bond taken by him is not
binding on the suretieseither as a statu-
torv or common law bond . Com . v .Rob-
erts, 1 Duv . 199.
§ 70. Failure to comply with many of
the provisions of this section are mere
irregularitiesbut itisindispensablethat
the decisionof the courtrequiringparty
to give bail shallbe filedin circuitcourt
before there is a forfeiture. Morgan v.
Com ., 12 Bush 84.

victionthe defendant may be admitted to bail-
1. For his appearance before a magistratefor an examination of the
charge,ifthe offensecharged be a misdemeanor ;or,
2. For hisappearance in the courtto which he issentfortrial;or,
3. For his appearance to answer an indictmentwhich has been found
againsthim ; or,
4. For hisappearancein a penalaction. (Defendantmay remain on
bailduring trial,secs.183, 184 ; bailin policeor justices' courts,secs.309 ,
324 ; bailto answer indictment- who may take,secs. 141, 143, 144 ; or for
misdemeanor ,secs.28,46 ;peace officerswho may takebail,sec.84.)
Cross reference: See Gregory'sKy .Crim .Law ,sec.933.
§ 73. (1) Bond of defendant . A bail
bond or recognizanceby a defendant in
an indictment is unauthorized by law ,
and is not obligatory on him either as a
statutory or common law bond , but is
binding on his surety. Com .v.Radford,
2 Duv . 9 .
(2) Invalid bail bonds . Bond taken by
county judge after commencement of
term of court to which defendant was
heldtoappear,sec.68;Branham v.Com .,
2 Bush 3 ; Creekmore v. Com ., 5 Bush
312; bond taken by jailer,Com . v.Rob-
erts, 1 Duv . 199 ; bond taken by sheriff
from person whom he had arrested by
authority of his bail, Schneider v. Com .,
3 Met. 410. In each of the foregoing
cases it isheld that a bond executed be-
fore an officernot authorized by law to
take bailis not obligatoryon the bail;
and see further notes to section 85 , and
as to exoneration of bail, see notes to
sec . 93 .
(3 ) Person not to be accepted as bail.
See sec. 80 ; but if such person become
bailthey are liable. 11 Bush 617.
§ 74. (1) Bond taken by examining
court is invalid unless it appears from
the record that an examining trial was
held and the accused admitted to bail.
Morgan v. Com ., 12 Bush 84.
(2) Duty of bail. It isthe legalduty
of the bail to act as the friendly cus-
todianofhisprincipal,and to seethathe
appears to answer the charge against
him . Ratcliffv . Smith , 13 Bush 172.
(3) See further, secs. 46-55 as to bail
by examining court; and sec. 68 as to
bail after commitment .
(4 ) Sunday - bail. A bail bond or
recognizance executed on Sunday before
an officerauthorized to take bail is bind-
ing. Ricev.Com.,3 Bush 14; Watts v.Com ., 5 Bush 309 .
(5 ) Surety - authority to fillbond .
Where a surety authorizes an officerto
fillup a bailbond that he has signed,
the officerhas no power to delegate the
authorityto fillup thebond to another
person. Com . v. Ball, 6 Bush 291 ; nor is
surety who authorizes another verbally
to sign his name bound , although the
person so authorized signed name in
presenceof surety. Billingtonv. Com .,
79 Ky .400.3 R. 19; and ifone suretyis
releasedbecause his name was signed
without authoritythe other suretywho
reliedon hisname as protectionwillalso
be discharged. Com . v. Belt, 21 R. 339,
51 S. W. 431.
(6) Warrant for felony - magistrate
can not endorse thereon amount of bail
- this is only allowable in misdemeanors .
Sec. 28; Com . v. Phillips, 116 Ky . 329,
25 R. 544,76 S.W. 118.

§ 85. (1) Jointrecognizance. The de-
fendant being indicted for burglary and
larceny in separate indictments, but for
the same offense, he and his surety en-
tered into a joint recognizancefor his
appearance to answer both indictments.
The bond was valid, as there could be
but one convictionand one punishment,
the two indictments being in effectno
more than two counts in the same in-
dictment . Baird v . Com ..2 Duv . 78 .
(2) Requisites of bond . It is essential

fense, Com .v. Thompson ,98 Ky .593, 17
R. 1132, 33 S. W. 1103; and was dis-
charged therefrom by reason of the giv-
ing of the bond or recognizance, and that
it can be ascertained from the bond or
recognizance that the bail undertook
that the defendant should appear before
a magistrate for an examination of the
charge or before a court for the trial
thereof. Henry v. Com ., 4 Bush 427 ;
Com .v . Cummings ,18 B. M. 26 ; 12 Bush
84 ; Com . v . Thompson , 98 Ky . 593, 17 R.
1132 , 33 S. W. 1103 .

fense 'with which the accused was
charged , but recitedthat he was in cus-
tody charged with a felon. It did not
appear that any order or memorandum
was made before the execution of the
bond, fixingthe bailor admittingto bail.
The bond was valid. Com . v.Rasner , 175
Ky . 133,193 S. W. 1011.
(3) The omission of the concluding
undertaking , that if the defendant failed
to perform either of the conditions of
the bond the suretieswould pay to the
Commonwealth the amount of the bail,
did not render the bond invalid . Com .
v . O'Daniel, 9 Bush 551 ; and see Miller

(4) It is indispensableto the validity
of the bond that some judgment should

ing, signedby the magistrate, showing

(6) Void bond . A surety whose name
is signed to a bond by another person
(althoughin his presence) without writ-
ten authority so to do is not bound . Bill-
ington v. Com .,79 Ky . 400, 3 R. 19; nor
is a co-surety who signed the bond when
the other surety is released. Com . v .

(7) The defendant being required to
give bond in the penalty of two hundred
and fiftydollarsin each of four separate
and distinctprosecutions, executed a
bond with surety for one thousand dol-
lars, conditioned in the usual form . The
sureties were held not bound , as the
bond was for too great a penalty, they
being liableunder it for one thousand
dollars in each case. Cooper v . Com ., 13
Bush 654 .
(8 ) Unless the bond is taken by an
officerauthorizedby law to acceptbail,
the bond will be void. Covington v. Com .,
3 Bush 478. Where a surety signed a
blank bond with authority to the magis-
trate to fillit up , the magistrate had no

(9) Where the minutes of the examin-
ing court recited only "that the defend-
ant and A and B , his sureties, were duly

the sureties were not bound ; the condi-

amining court. Roberts v . Com ., 7 Bush
430 .

mitted in another county is invalid.

(11) Sureties are estopped to deny re-
citals in bond to the effect that the ac-
cused was in custody , although in fact

essentialto validityof bond. Husbands

purposeof surrenderingthedefendantthebail,atany timebeforejudg-
§ 86. (1) Surrender of defendant .
Before the forfeiture of their bond the
bail have a right to surrender the de-
fendant to the jailer,whose duty it will
be to detain him in custody " as upon a
commitment ," he may then be admitted
to bail in the mode prescribed by secs.
68 and 81. Schneider v. Com ., 3 Met .

onlyby puttingthe accusedinthe apart-
ment of the jail where prisonersare

corpus the surety will be exonerated .
Smith v. Com ., 91 Ky . 588, 13 R. 186, 16
S. W. 532 .

§ 93. Forfeiture of bail if defendant failsto appear .

§ 93 [88] [Forfeitureofbailifdefendantfailtoappear.) Ifthede-
fendantfailtoappearfortrialorjudgment,or atany othertimewhen his
presencein courtmay be lawfullyrequired,or to surrenderhimselfin
executionof thejudgment,thecourtmust directthefacttobe enteredon
the record,and thereupon the bailbond ,or the money depositedin lieuof
bail,isforfeited. (Forfeitureof bailin examining court,sec.58.)
Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky .Crim . Law , sec.938.
§ 93. (1) Exoneration of bail. The
surety in the bail bond will be released,
if, after the execution of the bond , the
defendant is arrested by the United
States authoritiesand thereby prevented
from appearing . Com . v . Overby , 80 Ky .
208, 3 R. 704 ; overruling Com . v .House ,
13 Bush 680 ; and see Com . v . Webster ,
1 Bush 616 .
(2) That the defendant was absent in
the Federal army at the time he was
bound by a recognizanceto appear in
court to answer an indictment and was
unable to procure a furlough to attend
the court is a valid defense against the
recognizance. Com . v. Terry , 2 Duv . 383.
(3) "If the Commonwealth by her own
act prevented an appearance in discharge
of the recognizance she certainly should
not enforce the penalty for non -appear-
ance ." as where the defendant is arrested
in another county and thereby prevented
from appearing. Kirby v. Com., 1 Bush
113 ; or is a lunatic and confined in an
asylum . Wood v . Com ., 17 R. 1076, 33
S. W. 729 .
(3a) The fact that one who is on bail
becomes insane and wanders away and
so failsto appear when required by his
bond does not release the surety. Com .
v .Allen,157 Ky . 6, 162 S. W. 116.
(4) Sureties in a bail bond are dis-
charged by the rearrest of the accused
and his commitment to jail, from which
he made his escape during the pendency
of a second examining trialfor the same
offense. Medlin v . Com ., 11 Bush 605 .
(5 ) It is indispensablethat the deci-
sion of the examining court requiring the
defendant to give bail should be filedin
the circuit court before there is a for-
feitureof the bond . Morgan v . Com ., 12
Bush 84 .
(6 ) See further, as to exoneration of
bail by surrender of accused, sec. 86 and
notes; and by factthat bond is invalid,
notes to secs.73, 82, 85; and by failure
to indict, secs. 115, 117.
(7 ) The surrender of the defendant
in executionof the judgment rendered
against him releasesthe surety, although
he may fail to pay the fine imposed ex-
cept by laying it out in jail. Mitchell
v . Com ., 12 Bush 247.
(8) Order of court abating an indict-
ment terminated the prosecutionunder
that indictment ; and the indictment
having been reinstated on the docket and
the defendant arrested , the sureties on
the bond for his appearance were not
bound . Henry v . Com ., 4 Bush 427 ; and
filingaway an indictment releases the
bail. Hall v . Com ., 17 R. 231, 30 S. W.
877; and see Com . v . Bottoms , 105 Ky .
222. 20 R. 1159,48 S. W. 974 ; as to effect
of filingindictment away . See further
notes to sec . 85 .
(9) Where a defendantwho had given
682

Title V ] [§ 93Forfeiture of Bail .
bailfor hisappearanceto answer an in-
dictment for felony appeared in dis-
charge of the recognizance and was put
upon trial,during the progressof which
he escaped, his bailwas not liable. A
differentrule would apply in misde-meanors . Askins v. Com ., 1 Duv . 275 ;
compare with Com . v. Coleman, 2 Met .
382 .
(10) Remissionof bailby court- or by
governor . See sec. 98 and notes thereto.
(10a ) Where accused under bond for
appearance fails to appear when the
cause comes on for trial, it is the duty
of the court on motion of the Common-
wealth's attorney to have entered of rec-
ord the fact of the failureto appear , and
to enter an order forfeitingthe bail
bond ,and thereupon the summons should
issueagainst the bail. as provided by
sec.94. Com . v. Delk, 171 Ky . 263, 188
S. W. 376 .
(11) Liability of bail. The defendant
was held by an examining court to an-
swer at the ensuing October term of the
circuitcourt; no indictment was found
against him at that term ; at the next
April term an indictment was found ,
and.failingto appear,the bond was for-
feited and the sureties held liable. It is
not necessary that there should be a
trial and conviction in a misdemeanor
case before the bond is forfeited . Wal-
ker v.Com .,79 Ky .292,2 R. 197.
(12) Bail during trialof felony, see
sec 183 and notes .
( 13 ) The continuance of the case at
the instance of the defendant , and the
permission given him to stand upon his
bond without the consent of his sureties ,
did not release them . The controlling
principleis that untilthe defendant is
surrendered, or his appearance entered
in discharge of his bond , the sureties
have the control of him and the court
has not . Miller v.Com ., 1 Duv . 14; Com .
v .Coleman ,2 Met .382.
(14) Where by consent an order was
entered granting a change of venue , and
afterward at same term the order was
set aside, the sureties were held liable
for the failureof the accused to appear
when calledfor trial. Gray v . Com ., 100
Ky . 645, 18 R. 1093, 38 S. W. 1092.
(15) The accused being under bail, ap-
peared in court, pleaded not guilty, but
before the jury was impaneled , on his
motion , the indictment was set aside.
The court ordered the case submitted to
another grand jury and that the defend-
ant be permitted to stand upon his bond .
Another indictment being found , the de-
fendant failed to appear . The sureties
were held liable. Brewer v. Com ., 3
Bush 550 ; sec. 159 .
(16) The failureto forfeitthe bond at
the appearanceterm operatedto extend
the time during which the bail might
surrenderthe defendant, as providedin
sec. 86. Com . v . Branch , 1 Bush 59 .
(17) The defendant was held in the
sum of five hundred dollars to answer in
the circuit court ; an indictment was
found against him , and , failing to ap-
pear, a bench warrant fixingthe bail at
two hundred and fiftydollarswas issued.
Under this warrant the defendant was
arrested and made his escape from the
officer; failing to appear at the next
term , the bond was forfeited and the
suretiesheld bound for the payment of
five hundred dollars . Com . v . Branch , 1
Bush 59 .
(18) Several bonds . Where there are
several indictments and the same sure-
ties are on the bonds , in each case the
trialand acquittalunder one indictment ,
and continuance of other cases does not
exonerate the sureties. Combs v . Com .,
103 Ку . 385,20 R. 129,45 S. W. 359.
(19 ) Where a defendant charged with
felonywas admitted to bailby the ex-
amining court, to appear before the next
May term of the circuit court, and no
court was held at that term , the suretics
were held bound for his appearance at
the next ensuing term . Com . v. Branch ,
1 Bush 59 .
(20 ) Bail are not discharged until they
have placed the defendant in the custody
of the court , or have surrendered him
to the jailer. A continuance from one
term to another does not release the bail .
Where a new court is created and all
cases pending in the circuit court are
transferred to it, the bail are bound for
the defendant's appearance in the new
court. Ramey v . Com ., 83 Ky . 534, 7 R.
704 .
(21) A defendant charged with bas-
tardy who had given bail for his ap .
pearance, appeared and pleaded not
guilty,but was not surrenderedor taken
into custody, and, having escaped, his
bail was not released . Wintersoll V.
Com ., 1 Duv . 177 ; and bail in a misde-
meanor case is liable if accused fails to
surrender himself in satisfaction of the
judgment,althoughhe ispresentat trial.
Com v. Turpin, 98 Ky .9, 17 R. 546, 32
S. W. 133 .
(22 ) The arrest and imprisonment in
another State of one who is charged with
crime in this State, and out on bail, is
not a good defense by the surety to pro-
ceedingson the forfeitedrecognizance.

§ 94. (1) Forfeiture- practice- plead-
ing. When a defendant in a criminal
case has appeared in dischargeof each

of the defendant "to appear in dis-

(3) A summons which recitesthe date

trialand judgment upon the merits, is
a waiver ofpreviousirregularities. Com .
v.Marks ,2 Duv . 387.

bond the defendant must make a writ-
ten statement of the facts constituting
his defense. Brown v . Com ., 4 Met . 222 ;
Bonner v .Com .,27 R. 647,85 S. W. 1185 ;
and if a demurrer is sustained to his re-
sponsemay amend . Wilson v. Com ., 99
Ky . 167, 18 R. 51, 35 S. W. 274.
(6 ) The omission, in order of for-
feiture, to name the sureties or to give
the date of the recognizance is not a
fatal defect when the recognizance is
otherwisesufficientlyidentified. Miller
v . Com ., 1 Duv . 14 .

cognizance, the court, on motion of the
surety, quashed the indictment, and ren-

§ 118 [117] [Indictmentdefined.) An indictmentisan accusationin
writing, found and presentedby a grand jury to the court in which they
areempaneled,charginga personwiththecommissionof a publicoffense.
( Lost or stolenindictment,seeKy .Stat., sec.1140.)

Cross reference: See Gregory'sKy .Crim.Law ,sec.948.
[Nine jurorsto concur- foreman to indorse.] The con
is exonerated, although no order is en-
tered discharging the bond . Bryant v .

12 ; Com . v. Roberts , 4 Met . 219. Sec.
116, Code of 1854, providedthat thede-

ing reasons for dismissal. Ky . Stat .,

§ 118. (1) Court no power to change
charge in an indictment, even with con-
sent of accused,although the facts stated
in indictment constitute a different of-
fensefrom thatcharged. Com .v.Adams ,
92 Ky . 134, 13 R. 440, 17 S. W. 276.
(2) Dismissal of prosecution by ex-
amining court is not a bar to an indict-
ment . Com . v. Weber , 17 R. 1131, 33 S.
W. 821 .
(3) Failure to dispose of an indict-
ment . Where a defendant was indicted
for murder , and after the expiration of
several years another indictment was
found againsthim for same offense, un-
der which he was tried without any
order having been made disposing of for-
mer indictment, the court held that the
substantial rights of the accused were
not prejudiced. Blyew v. Com ., 91 Ky .
200, 12 R. 742, 15 S. W. 356.
(4 ) Filing indictment away is not a
discontinuance of the prosecution, and
the indictment may be redocketed. Com .
v . Bottoms , 105 Ky . 222, 20 R. 1159, 48
S. W. 974 ; Gross v . Com ., 26 R. 870 , 82
S. W. 618 ; but it is not proper to file
an indictment away when the defend-
ant is in court demanding a trial. Jones
v. Com., 114 Ky . 599,24 R. 1434, 71 S.
W. 643.
(5 ) Indictment lost or stolen- limita-
tion. See Ky . Stat., sec. 1140.
(6 ) New indictment may be found in
county to which a change of venue is
taken . Ky . Stat., sec. 1177, and defend-
ant can not object to it because found
in that county. Parker v.Com ., 12 Bush
191; Jenning v . Com ., 13 R. 79, 16 S. W.
348 .
(7 ) See further , sec. 158 and notes .
§ 119. (1) Construction of section.
This section is mandatory , and the in-
dictment must be indorsed a " true bill "
and signed by the foreman . Oliver v.

§ 120. (1) Names of witnesses ex-
amined should be written on indictment ,
and a failureto do so isground forset-
ting it aside if motion is made in proper
time as provided in sec. 157. Sutton v .
Com ., 97 Ky . 308, 17 R. 184, 30 S. W.
661; but this rule will not be applied
when it appears name is omitted byoversightor witness is not material.
Com . v. Glass, 107 Ky . 160, 21 R. 819,
53 S. W. 18; and witnesses whose names
are not on indictinentor in subpoena for
Commonwealth may be calledon to tes-
tify. Underwood v . Com ., 27 R. 8 , 84
S. W. 310 ; Hendrickson v . Com ., 146 Kу .
742, 143 S. W. 433 ; Young v . Com ., 141
Ky . 708, 133 S. W. 791 ; Dowell v . Com .,
28 R. 642, 108 S. W. 847.
(2 ) Practice- Objection to indictment
because names of witnesses do not ap-
pear on it should be made by motion to
quash. Com .v. Brewer,113 Ky . 217, 24
R. 72 , 67 S. W. 994. Schroufe v. Com .,
141 Ky . 554, 133 S. W. 205 .
(3) Witnesses that were not examined
by grand jury may be added to names
on indictment before it is returned . Mc-
Intire v.Com ., 26 R. 469,4 S. W. 1.
(4 ) The failureof the circuitcourt to
set aside an indictment on the ground
that it did not contain the names of the
witnesses who testifiedbefore the grand
jury can not be reviewed on appeal.
Slaughterv. Com ., 152 Ку . 128, 153 S.
W. 46.
§ 121. (1) Presenting indictment.When
the record shows that an indictment is
returned into court as required by Code
and ordered to be filed, the failure of
the clerk to sign the indorsement on
back of indictment to the effect that it
was filedin open court will not author-
ize its dismissal . Patterson v . Com .. 86
Ky . 313, 9 R. 481, 5 S. W. 387, 765 ;
Pearce v . Com ., 10 R. 178, 8 S. W. 893.
(2 ) Clerk's indorsement of filingof in-
dictment is not essential, and when
omitted may be supplied. Pence v . Com .,
95 Ky . 618, 16 R. 148, 26 S. W. 810.
(3) An indictment for grand larceny
for stealing sheep was indorsed on its
back and described in the order filingit
as an indictment for "sheep stealing."
Held sufficient identification of the in-
dictment. Com . v. English, 6 Bush 431.
(4 ) It is not necessary to the validity
of an indictment that it should show
upon its face that it had been presented
on oath by the grand jury . Jane v.Com .,
3 Met . 18 .
(5) An indictment was presented and
filed; the record book did not contain a
copy of the indorsement of the clerk on
the indictment . The indictment was
not defective. Daniel v. Com ., 154 Ky .
601 , 157 S. W. 1127 .
(6) See further, sec. 158 and notes.

§ 122 [121,128,129] [Requisitesof an indictment.) The indictment
must contain-
1. [Titleof prosecution,name of courtand parties.] The titleof the
prosecution, specifyingthename of thecourtin which theindictmentis
presentedand the names of the parties.
2. [Statementof actsconstitutingoffense.] A statementof theacts
constitutingthe offense, in ordinary and conciselanguage, and in such a
manner as to enablea person of common understandingto know what is
intended;and withsuchdegreeof certaintyas to enablethecourttopro-
nouncejudgment,on conviction,accordingtotherightof thecase. (Facts
thatmust be statedwith certainty, sec.124.)
Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky . Crim .Law , secs.966,969, 982.
§ 122. (1) Abortion . It is necessary
that an indictment for abortion should
allege that the mother was quick with
child, and that the potionwas adminis-
tered with the intention to destroy the
lifeof the child, and that such was its
effect. Mitchell v . Com ., 78 Ky . 204 ;
but where the indictment is for the
death of the mother as the result of an
abortion, it is not necessary to allege
that deceased was "quick" with child.
Peoples,v. Com ., 87 Ky . 487, 10 R. 517,
9 S. W. 509, 810.
(2) Accessory . An accessory before
the fact is inlegalcontemplationguilty
of murder ifguilty at all; and an indict-
ment in which the offense charged is
murder is good , although the particular
circumstances of the offense as set out
in the indictmentconstitutethe person
an accessory before the fact and not a
principal. Stricklinv.Com ., 83 Ky . 566,
7 R. 627 ; and see further as to acces-
sories before and after fact, Tully v .
Com ., 11 Bush 154; Tully v. Com ., 13
Bush 142; Com . v. Hargis, 124 Ky . 356;
30 R. 510 , 99 S. W. 348 ; Tramwell v .
Com .,148 Ky .624, 147 S. W. 36 ; Reed v .
Com ., 138 Ky . 577.
(3 ) See further, as to accesory before

the fact, Ky . Stat., sec. 1128 ; after the
fact, Ky . Stat., sec. 1129.
(3a) A certain indictment for conspir-
acy sufficientlycharged all the defend-
ants with being principals. Daniel v.
Com .,154 Ky .601, 157 S. W. 1127.
(4 ) Aiders and abettors. When two
persons are jointlyindictedfor murder,
one as principaland the other as aider
and abettor,the one chargedas principal
may be found guiltyof aidingand abet-
ting, and the one chargedwith aiding
and abetting may be found guilty as
principal. Benge v. Com ., 92 Ky . 1, 13
R. 308, 17 S. W. 146; Gaskinsv. Com .,
97 Ky . 494, 17 R. 352 , 30 S. W. 1017 ;
Howard v.Com ., 110 Ky . 356,22 R. 1845,
61 S. W. 756 ; Taylor v . Com ., 90 S. W.
581,28 R. 819 ; Com . v . Hargis , 99 S. W.
348, 30 R. 510, 124 Ky . 356 ; Terhune v .
Com ., 144 Ky . 370, 138 S. W. 274 ; Reed
v. Com ., 125 Ky . 126, 100 S. W. 856, 30
R. 1212 .
(4a ) Where an indictment is found
againstone persononly as principalitis
error to convict him of the crime of aid-
ing and abetting. Hollin v. Com ., 158
Ky .427, 165 S. W. 407 .
(5) Aider and abettor in crime created
by statute punishable. Com . v. Carter,
94 Ky .527, 15 R. 253,23 S. W. 344 ; over-
rulingStamper v.Com .,7 Bush 612; and
see Frey v. Com ., 83 Ky . 191, 7 R. 174.
(6) An indictmentagainstA and B ,which in one count charges both of them
as principals, and in another count
charges A as principaland B as an aider
and abettor, and in the third count
charges Bas principal and A as aider
and abettor, is good , and one of them
may be found guiltyof a felonyand the
other of a misdemeanor . Cupp v. Com ..
87 Ky . 35, 9 R. 877, 7 S. W. 405 ; and
see Ky . Stat., secs. 1128, 1129 and cases
cited in note 4 .
(7) Aiders and abettors in crime of
housebreaking . Ward v . Com ., 14 Bush
233 ; and see Ky . Stat ..1162.
See
(8) Aiders and abettors in crime of
malicious shooting or wounding .
Ky . Stat.,sec. 1166, and Com . v. Patrick,
80 Ky . 605, 4 R. 660 ; where it is held
that under this statute the person who
committed the offense must be charged
with it, and then others may also be

but all of them can not be charged as

35,9 R. 877,7 S. W. 405.
(9 ) Aiders and abettors in crime of
rape. Kessler v. Com ., 12 Bush 18 ; Ky .
Stat., 1152; in crime of housebreaking,
Mullins v. Com ., 14 R. 569, 20 S. W.
1035 .
(10) "In order to convict one as aider
and abettor the indictment must include
the principalactor jointly, or disclose
who he is,togetherwith a descriptionof
his acts." Mulligan v . Com ., 84 Ky . 229 ,
8 R. 211, 1 S. W. 417 ; but see Benge v .
Com ., 92 Ky . 1, 13 R. 308. 17 S. W. 146 ;
Howard v.Com ., 110 Ky .356,22 R. 1845,
61 S. W. 756 ; Powers v . Com ., 110 Ky .
386,22 R. 1807, 61 S. W. 735; and seecases cited in note 4 .
(10a) Appropriation of property in
custodyof carrier. An indictmentforappropriatingpropertyin the custody of
a common carrier based on Ky . Stat .,
sec.1201b,was sufficient,althoughitdid
not state the name of the consignee or
owner of the property . Williams v. Com .,
152 Ky . 610, 153 S. W. 961.
(11) Arson. The ownership of the
propertywas sufficientlyallallegedin an
indictment that charged that the house
was occupiedby HattieTaylor as a resi-
dence . It isnot necessary that the name
of the owner in fee be given. Under an
indictment for arson the defendant may
be convicted of the crime of an attempt
to commit arson.Young v.Com ., 12 Bush
243 .
(12) An indictment charging that of-
fense was committed "feloniously and
wilfully," omitting the word "mali-
ciously." is sufficientfor the common
law offense. Aikman v . Com ., 13 R. 894 ,
18 S. W. 937 ; Young v . Com ., 12 Bush
243. The Ky . Stat ., sec. 1170, in defining
certain kinds of arson, uses the word
"maliciously," but in cases supra it is
held that the word "feloniously" em-
braces the word "maliciously."
(13) The failure of indictment to ex-
pressly aver that dwelling house was
occupied as a residence does not vitiate
it, if it is charged that the dwelling
house was occupied. Com . v . Elliston,
14 R. 216, 20 S. W. 214 ; Ky . Stat ., sec.
1170 .
(14) See further, as to indictment for
burning a barn or warehouse , Deshazer
v . Com ., 12 R. 453. 14 S. W. 542 ; Evans
v . Com ., 11 R. 573, 12 S. W. 768, 769 ;
Ky . Stat ., sec. 1169 ; or church edifice,
McDonald v . Com ., 86 Ky . 10, 9 R. 230, 4
S. W. 687 : or storehouse, Overstreet v .
Com ., 147 Ky . 471, 144 S. W. 751.
( 15) Assault and battery. An assault
and battery is punishable by either fine
or imprisonment or both fine and im-
prisonment,in the discretionof the jury.
Cornelison v. Com ., 84 Ky . 583, 2 S. W.
235,8 R. 793.

Title VI ] [§ 122Indictment - Form and Requisites.
(16) An indictment charging that the
defendant "did in sudden heat and pas-
sion, without previous malice and not in
self-defense, strike and wound G with
a pair of blacksmith tongs, which said
tongs was then and there a deadly
weapon " is good only as an indictment
for assault and battery . Com . v . Haw-
kins , 11 Bush 603 .
(17) Under an indictment for a simple
assault it is error to admit proof of a
battery ; every battery includes an as-
sault . but an assault does not include a
battery ; an assault and battery is an
offense of a higher grade than a simple
assault. Furnish v. Com ., 14 Bush 180 ;
see further,as to degrees of offensesecs.
262-264 , and notes thereto, and Com . v .
Heath , 99 Ky . 182, 18 R. 57, 35 S. W.
277 ; holding that an assault and battery
is not necessarily a lower degree of an
assault with intent to kill.
(18) Bigamy . An indictment for big-
amy chargingthat "ElizaDavis on the
day of May , 1877, in the county
and State aforesaid, and before the find-
ing of this indictment, having a husband
then living, unlawfully married John
Mackey ," is not sufficient; it should aver
the place of the firstmarriage and set
out the name of the first husband or
wife . Davis v . Com ., 13 Bush 318 , over-
ruling Com . v . Whaley , 6 Bush 266 ; and
see Ky . Stat., sec. 1216.
(19) "It is immaterial in an indict-
ment for bigamy at what particulardate
the firstmarriage occurred, if the ac-
cused had at time of second marriage a
living wife to whom he had been previ-
cusly married." Faustre v . Com .,92 Ky .
34, 13 R. 347, 17 S. W. 189 ; and see as
to immaterial variance in name of first
wife , note 3, sec. 124.
(20 ) The offenseof bigamy committed
by marrying in anotherState while the
former marriage is in force is not an
offense against the laws of this State.
Johnson v. Com .,86 Ky . 122,9 R. 419,5
S. W. 365 .
(21) Bribery. As to sufficiencyof in-
dictment for, see Ky . Stat., secs. 1586 ,
1587; and Johnson v . Com ., 90 Ky . 53,
12 R. 20, 13 S. W. 520 ; Cheek v . Com .,
87 Ky ..42, 9 R. 880, 7 S. W. 403 ; Com .
v. Selby , 87 Ky . 594. 10 R. 621, 9 S. W.
819 ; Com . v. Root , 16 R. 491, 29 S. W.
351 ; Com . v . Headly , 111 Ky . 815, 23 R.
1104 , 64 S. W. 744 .
(22 ) Burglary . An indictment for bur-
glary described the house "as the house
of A. Dominick " ; held sufficientdescrip-
tion . To make out the offense of bur-
glary it is not necessary to prove that
goods were actuallystolenand carried
away ; it is sufficientif the storehouse
was feloniouslybroken with the inten-
tion of so doing ; nor is it necessary to
charge that the house was "burglarious-
ly" broken and entered. Charles Olive
v . Com ., 5 Bush 376 ; see Com . v. Bruce ,
79 Ky . 560, 3 R. 366 ; and see Ky . Stat .,
sec. 1159. The indictment should charge
the specificfelony intended to be com-
mitted ; it is not sufficientto charge
"with intent to commit a felony." Kyle
v . Com ., 111 Ky . 404,23 R. 708, 63 S. W.
782; Radley v . Com ., 28 R. 477, 89 S. W.
519 ,121 Ky .506.
(23) Caption of indictment which
omits name of county in which it was
found is not defective when the body of
indictment states the county . Johnson
v.Com ., 12 R. 835, 15 S. W. 662.
(23a )Fraudulent use of checks- "cold
check law ." In an indictment under Ky .
Stat., sec. 1213a (the Cold Check law) ,
it is not necessary to allege that the
bank on which the check was drawn or
the bank to which it was delivered was
authorized by a law of the United States
or of any State of the United States or
any foreigngovernment. Siegelv.Com .,
177 Ky .232, 197 S. W. 809.
(23b ) See note 82a .
(24 ) Confederating and banding to-
gether to intimidate. Ky . Stat ., sec.
1223 ; Buchanan v . Com ., 95 Ky . 334, 15
R. 738,25 S. W. 265; Com .v.Hghtower ,
149 Ky .563, 149 S. W. 971.
(25 ) Conspiracy to commit crime .
Definition of and necessary allegations
in indictment for. Com . v . Ward , 92 Ky .
158, 13 R. 422, 17 S. W. 283.
(25a) One personmay be indictedfor
the offenseof conspiracy without joining
his fellow conspirator. Rutland v .Com .,
160 Ky .77, 169 S. W. 584.
(26 ) Corporation liable to indictment
for an offense which is either a mis-
feasance or a nonfeasance of duty to the
public, the punishment for which is a
fine, as for permittinggaming on prem-
ises under its control . Com . v . Pulaski
Co. A. & M. Association, 92 Ky . 197, 13
R. 468, 17 S. W. 442.
(27 ) Counterfeiting. In an indict-
ment for counterfeiting it is necessary
to allege that the bank on which the bill
purported to be was a bank incorporated
by law . Kennedy v . Com ., 2 Met . 36 ;
Clark v . Com ., 16 B. M. 206 ; and to
identify with reasonable certainty the
coin . Com . v . Fields , 5 R. 610 ; Waller
v . Com ., 97 Ky . 509, 17 R. 348, 30 S. W.
1023 ; and see Ky . Stat.,sec. 1181.
