§ 182. Plea of not guilty raises an
issue of fact and court must submit is-
sue to jury ifdemanded . Lucas v .Com .,
26 R. 740 , 82 S. W. 440 .
§ 183. (1) Accused to be present dur-
ing trialfor felony. "One charged with
the commission of a felony can not be
tried during his absence from the court-
room , and when any step is taken dur-
ing the trialin the absence of the pris-
oner the record must show affirmatively
that he could in nowise have been preju-
diced by it." Meece v.Com .,78 Ky . 586,
1 R. 337 ; Rutherford v. Com ., 78 Ky .
639 , 1 R. 410 ; Yarbrough v .Com .,89 Ky .
151,11 R. 351,12 S.W. 143; but thisrule
does not apply to motion for new trial,
Howard v.Com .,24 R. 612, 69 S. W. 721.
(2) If accused escapes from custody
aftertrialforfelonyis commenced it
may proceed in his absence . Collier v .
Com .,110 Ky .516,22 R. 1929, 62 S. W. 4 .
(2a) A judgment of conviction for
felony was not reversed because of the
absence of the defendant during the
progressof the trial. May v. Com ., 153
Ky . 141, 154 S. W. 1074.
(2b ) Two persons were indictedjointly
for murder . During a greater part of
the trial of one of them , the other was
absent from the courtroom . After the
verdicthad been returned, he requested
that the same jury be empaneled and
sworn to try him on the evidence it had
already heard on the trialof the other
defendant , which was done . The verdict
against him was sustained. Bonar v.
Com ., 180 Ky . 338, 202 S. W. 676.
(3) Bail during trial- accusedentitled
to. During the trialof a felonywhen
the bailissufficientand agreesto stand
on the bond , the accused is entitled to
remain on bail until case is finallysub-
mitted to thejury,and where itappears
that the personal liberty of the accused
is necessary to the preparationof the
case, the refusal to allow him to remain
on bail will be a reversible error . White
v .Com .,80 Ky .480,4 R. 373.
(4 ) Discharge of jury for failure to
agree, in absence of accused, does not
prejudicehis substantialrights. Yar-
brough v.Com .,89 Ky . 151,11 R. 351,12
S. W. 143 ; nor does failure to discharge
jury upon request of accused. McCler-
nand v.Com .,11 R. 301,12 S.W. 148.
(5 ) Jury instructingin absence of ac-
cused is error. Meece v. Com ., 78 Ky .
586, 1 R. 337; McClernand v . Com ., 11 R.
301, 12 S. W. 148 ; Bailey v . Com ., 24 R.
1419, 71 S. W. 632. See as to private
examination of juror by judge , Howard
v . Com .,25 R. 2213 , 80 S. W. 211.
(6) Occasional absence from courtroom
on account of temporary illnessfor a few
minutes at time , the trial continuing
in the absence,did not prejudice the sub-
stantial rights of the accused. Hite v.
Com .,14 R. 308,20 S. W. 217; and see,to
same effect, Doyle v. Com ., 18 R. 518, 37
S. W. 153.

§ 186-188] [Title VITrial- Postponement Of .
§ 186 [187] [Docketingprosecutions,settingfortrial.) All criminal
prosecutionsand penal actionsnot triedat any term of a court shallbe
docketedby the clerkfor the next term, beginningwith the firstday
thereof and settingthem for as many days as may be necessary, in the
same manner as ordinary civilactionsare docketed. But the court may
directthenumber of days forwhich such prosecutionsand actionsshall
be docketed;and may order any cause tobe setfor trialat a time specified
in the order .
Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky .Crim .Law , sec.1024 .
§ 187 [188] [Time prosecutionsstand for trial.] All prosecutions
shallstandfortrialon theday towhich theyaredocketed,ifthedefendant
be in custody, or on bail, or have been summoned or arrestedthreedays
before the day fixedfor the trial. (Sec .as amended by Act of March 22,
1910,с.56,p .187,sec.2.)
Cross reference: See Gregory'sKy .Crim.Law , sec.1024.

§ 188. Trialmay be postponed for cause.

§ 188 [189] [Trialmay be postponedfor cause.] When an indict-
ment iscalledfortrial,or at any timepreviousthereto,the court,upon
sufficientcauseshown by eitherparty,may directthetrialtobe postponed
to any time in the same term,or to another term.
Crossreference: See Gregory'sKy .Crim. Law ,sec.1020.
§ 886. Criminalprosecutions- defendant liablefor costs, when. The de-
fendantina criminalprosecutionshallbe adjudged to pay thecostsof allcon-
tinuancesin hisbehalf, for which executionor otherprocessmay issue,as in
civilcases, and, if convictedof a misdemeanor, allcostsunpaid may be col-
lectedinthesame manner as the fine; ifconvictedof a felony,by execution,or
otherprocess,as in civilcases. (Ky .Stat., sec.886.)
§ 188. (1) Continuances . A continu-
ance was asked on account of illness of
counsel and absence of witnesses; the
case was laid over from time to time
during the term and the attendance of
the counsel and the witnesses was ob-
tained. The refusal of the continuance
was not error. May v . Com ., 153 Ky .
141 , 154 S. W. 1074 .
(2 ) In these cases the absence of de-
fendant when a misdemeanor case was
calledfor trial,was not ground for con-
tinuance. Truitt v . Com ., 177 Ky . 397,
197 S. W. 797; Long v. Com ., 177 Ky .
391 , 197 S. W. 843 .
(3 ) It was error to deny a continu-
ance of the prosecution on the ground
that no written request had been filed
with the clerk of the court,directing him
to issue a subpoena for the absent wit-
ness. Com . v.Adkins, 171 Ky .299, 188
S. W. 401 .

ment of prosecutions,on applicationof thedefendant,exceptthat,when
theground of applicationfora continuanceistheabsenceof a material

§ 2264. Panel to be called- how deficiencysupplied. On the day the jury
shallbe summoned to attendcourt,the panelshallbe called,and the names of
such as attend,and are not excused by the court, shallbe enteredof record;
and any deficiencyin the panel shallbe suppliedas directedin thischapter.
(Ky .Stat., sec.2264.)
§ 2265. Names to be placedin box and jury drawn from . The clerkshall
writethe names of the jurorsenteredof recordon separateslipsof paper, as
near the same sizeand appearanceas may be,and when a jury iswanted for
the trialof a case the same shallbe drawn from a box afterthe papersshall
have been depositedthereinand wellmixed . The clerkshallprovideand keep
forthatpurpose a suitablebox with a slidinglid. (Id., sec.2265.)
§ 2266. Criminalcases- how jury selected. In criminalcases, where the
defendantisentitledto a peremptory challengeof more than four jurors, the
clerk, under the directionof the court, shalldraw from the box twelve names ,
who shallcompose the jury to try the cause, unlesssome one or allof them
shallbe challenged,in which case,the clerkshalldraw from the box as many
more as may be required, and as often as requireduntila jury be obtained
or the whole panelbe exhausted; then the courtshallorderthesherifftomake
up the deficiencyas directedin thischapter. (Id., sec.2266.)
§ 191 [192] [Clerkto draw jury as directedby statute.] When an
issueof factin a criminalprosecutionisabout to be tried,the clerkshall
draw, in the manner directedby the GeneralStatutes[now Kentucky
Statutes],thenames of twelvejurors,who ,ifnot challengedby thepar-
ties,norexcusedby thecourt,shallcomposethetrialjury. (See Ky .Stat.,
sec.2266.)

to fillthe paneluntilthelistof standingjurorsisexhausted, when the
court shallorder such a number of qualifiedjurorsas itshalldeem suffi-
cientto complete the jury to be summoned by the sheriff, and the panel
shallbe filledfrom timetotimefrom thejurorssosummoned ,and ifthey
be exhausted, similarordersmay be made forsummoning otherjurors,
when all the witnesses to the homicide
are present unless it is desired to show
other material facts. Thurman v. Com .,

Ky .620,185 S.W. 146.

Com ., 18 R. 367, 36 S. W. 550.

§ 215. (1) Challenge- practice. "Un-
der the Code , each party is entitled to
have a fullpanelof twelvejurors, found
upon examination qualified to try the
case, before being required to exercise
the right of challenge to the individual
juror; and whenever the number is les-
sened by challengeof eitherparty, the
panel must be filledbeforebeing passed
on ,and so on until the jury is completed .
When a fullpanel of qualifiedjurorsis
presented, the Commonwealth must then
either accept or challenge. Having ac-
cepted or declined to challenge, the chal-
lengesas to those then passed on are,
in the language of the Code, exhausted;
or, having challengedone or more, the
others must be considered as accepted.
The right of challenge is to be exer-
cisedby the Commonwealth and by the
defendant in the same manner and upon
the same conditions, the only difference
being as to the number of peremptory
challenges allowed to each . The defend-
ant , as well as the Commonwealth , is
therefore required to exhaust his chal-
lenges to each juror of a panel when
presented to be passed upon ." Munday
v . Com ., 81 Ky . 233 , 5 R. 67 ; Edrington
v.Com.,7 R.377; Wigginsv.Com.,104
Ky . 765,20 R. 908, 47 S. W. 1073 ; Colvin
v .Com ..22 R. 1407,60 S. W. 701.
(2) "A full panel of twelve jurors
found upon examinationqualifiedto try
the case should have been firstpresented
to the Commonwealth for its acceptance
or challenge, and all not then challenged
should have been regarded as accepted;
then the fullpanel should have been pre-
sented to the accused for his acceptance
or challenge, and all not then challenged
should have been regarded as accepted
by him and not subject to be again

amination and before they were sworn

nation of the individual juror as to his

(4 ) Where a portion of the regular

bystanders may be summoned to make

12 S. W. 148 ; Ky . Stat ., sec. 2266 .
(5) After a jury had been selected,but
before they were sworn, the Common-
wealth's attorney filedan affidavitshow-
ing that one of them had expressedan
opinion, thereupon the entirejury was

jurors, including the eleven who had been

jury , but the failure to do so was not

118 Ky . 1; Ellisv. Com ., 146 Ky . 715,

(6 ) After twelve jurors had qualified
but before the jury had been accepted
itwas not error to permit the Common-
wealth to excuse one of the jurors who

(7) See notes to secs. 198-210, 281.
(8) Defendant has rightto challenge
each individualjuror. Apkins v. Com .,
148 Ky . 662, 147 S. W. 376.

§ 218 [219] [Oath if no issue made .) If no issue be made by the
pleadings, the words "well and trulytry the issue, and" shallbe omitted
from the oath .
Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky .Crim . Law , sec. 1045.
§ 219 [220] [Indictmentto be read and pleastated.) The clerk, or
Commonwealth'sattorney,shallthenreadto thejurytheindictment,and
statethe defendant'splea. (What pleasmay be entered,sec.172. Ar-

§ 220. [StatementforCommonwealth .] The attorneyforthe Com-
monwealthmay thenstatetothejurythenatureof thechargeagainstthe

§ 219. (1) Reading of indictment-
plea. Where an indictment charges two
offenses and one of them is dismissed it
is not error for Commonwealth's attor-
ney to read entire indictment to jury
when he states offensebeing tried. Green-
wood v. Com ., 11 R. 220,11 S. W. 811.
(2) Requirement that indictment shall
be read and plea stated is mandatory ,
but fact that indictment is read by an
attorney employed to prosecute and not
until after evidence has been introduced
for Commonwealth is not error . Gallo-
way v. Com ., 5 R. 213 ; Farris v . Com .,
111 Ky . 236, 23 R. 580, 63 S. W. 615 ;
and it is not necessary to again state de-
fendant's plea after indictment has been
read when it has been theretofore en-
tered . Howard v . Com ., 24 R. 91 , 67 S.
W. 1003; and when record is silent it
willbe presumed issuewas made . Meece
v.Com .,78 Ky .586,1 R. 337; seeholding
contrary,Hendrickson v .Com .,23 R. 1191,
64 S. W. 954 ; error in this respect must
appear in motion for new trial. Ison v .
Com .,23 R. 1805, 66 S. W. 184; Griffinv .
Com .,23 R. 2148 ,66 S. W. 740 .
(3) See further, section 154 and notes
thereto .
(4 ) An order recitingthat " came par-
ties, and defendant having heretofore
pleaded not guilty of the offensecharged
in the indictmentfor his trialputs him-
self upon his country and the attorney
for the Commonwealth does likewise,"
although it does not state in terms that
indictmentwas read to jury or plea
stated, is sufficient. Patterson v . Com .,
86 Ky . 313, 9 R. 481, 5 S. W. 387, 765 ;
Pattersonv.Com .,99 Ky .610, 9 R. 481,
5 S. W. 387, 765 .
(4a ) Where defendant , after the read-
ing of the indictment and prior to the
statement by the prosecuting attorney ,
entered his plea of not guilty,he can not
complain that such plea was not stated
to the jury by the clerk. Donehy v .
Com ., 170 Ky . 474,186 S. W. 161.
(5) Failureto observerequirementsof
this section is not reversible error unless
excepted to and assigned as a ground
for a new trial. Bischoff v . Com ., 96 S.
W. 538. 29 R. 871, 123 Ky . 340 ; see fur-
ther, Combs v . Com ., 104 S. W. 270, 31
R. 822 .
§ 220. (1) Statement for Common-
wealth may be made by an attorney
other than attorney for Commonwealth .
Roberts v. Com ., 94 Ky . 499, 15 R. 341.
22 S. W. 845 .
(2 ) Attorney for Commonwealth
should not be permitted to read writ-
ings he intends to introduce as evidence,
but when the writings are introduced
the error will not be reversible if writ-
ings were competent . O'Brien v . Com .,
89 Ky . 354, 11 R. 534, 12 S. W. 471 ; nor
is it proper for attorney to state that
defendant has the right to testify. Strut-
ton v. Com ., 23 R. 307, 62 S. W. 875 ,

§ 221 [221] The[Introductionof evidencefor Commonwealth .]
counselfortheCommonwealth must thenoffertheevidenceinsupportof
theindictment. (Exclusionof young spectatorsfrom courtroom,seeKy .
Stat.,sec.979. Separationof witnesses,seeCivilCode,sec.601.)
(3) Commonwealth attorney is not
requiredto make a statement, but in-
dictment must be read and plea stated.
Hendrickson v . Com ., 23 R. 1191, 64 S.
W. 954; see note 5, sec. 219.
§ 221. (1) Accomplice . See section
241 and notes .
(2) Affidavitfor continuance admitted
to be read as true can not be contro-
verted. Pace v . Com ., 89 Ky . 204, 11 R.
407,12 S.W. 271; Young v.Com .,8 Bush
366; O'Brienv. Com ., 89 Ky . 354, 11 R.
534 , 12 S. W. 471; and see notes to sec-
tion 189 .
(3) Belief of accused that he was in
danger when he killed deceased may be
shown by his testimony . Williams v .
Com .,90 Ky .596,12 R. 525, 14 S. W. 595.
(4 ) Burden of proof on defendant . In
all statutory crimes it is competent for
the Legislature to say that certain acts
proven by the Commonwealth shallbe
sufficientto make out a presumptive case
against the accused and cast the burden
on him ; but the burden can not be cast
on defendant until the Commonwealth
has firstproven some material fact con-
ducing to his guilt. Com . v . Minor , 88
Ky . 422 , 10 R. 1008, 11 S. W. 472 ; as il-
lustrations of such laws , see Ky . Stat ..
secs. 1967, 2571 ; and Com . v . Standard
Oil Co., 112 S. W. 633, 129 Ky . 546.
(5 ) Character of deceased - evidence to
sustain can not be introduced until it
has been attacked . Parker v . Com ., 96
Ky . 212, 16 R. 449, 28 S. W. 500 ; Webb
v . Com ., 11 R. 642 , 12 S. W. 769 .
(6) Upon the trial of the accused for
murder it was held proper under the
facts of this case to permit him to prove
that the deceased was a man of violent ,
cruel and bloodthirsty disposition, and
in the habit of carrying concealed deadly
weapons . Payne v . Com ., 1 Met . 370 ;
Riley v.Com .,94 Ky .266, 15 R. 246,22 S.
W. 222 .
(7) Character of accused, if he testify,
may be put in issue as that of any other
witness , and the Commonwealth is not
confined to his character for truth , but
may assail his general moral character.
Lockard v. Com ..87 Ky . 201, 10 R. 102.
8 S. W. 266; Burdette v. Com ., 93 Ky .
76 , 13 R. 960 , 18 S. W. 1011 ; Pace v .
Com .,89 Ky .204,11 R. 407, 12 S. W. 271 ;
but the inquiryas to his characterfor
morality and truth must relate to the
time he testifies and not when he com-
mitted the crime. Com . v.Hourigan, 89
Ky . 305, 11 R. 509, 12 S. W. 550 ; 78 Ky .
219 ; McDonald v . Com ., 86 Ky . 10, 9 R.
230, 4 S. W. 687; and itisnot properto
inquireabout particularacts or crimes
making up character, whether good or
bad. Com . v. Welch, 111 Ky . 530, 23 R.
151, 63 S. W. 984; Smith v . Com ., 13 R.
31, 16 S. W. 137 : and see Leslie v. Com ..
19 R. 1201,42 S. W. 1095; Saylor v .Com ..
97 Ky . 184, 17 R. 100, 30 S. W. 390 ;
Baker v. Com .,20 R. 1778, 50 S. W. 54;
Parker v . Com ., 21 R. 406, 51 S. W. 573 ;
Pennington v. Com ., 21 R. 542, 51 S. W.
818; Britton v . Com ., 96 S. W. 556, 123
Ky . 411, 29 R. 860 ; Haydon v . Com ., 140
Ky . 634,131 S. W. 521.
(8) The Commonwealth is not allowed
to call witnesses to prove the bad char-
acter of the accused unless to rebut the
evidence of good character already in.
troduced by him , and evidence as to
character must be restricted to the trait
of characterwhich isin issue. Young v.
Com ., 6 Bush 312.
(9 ) Proof of character in aid of the
presumption of innocence is admissible.
6 Bush 312 ; but should be limited to the
time of the discovery of the commission
of the offense , and should not extend to
the date of the arrest, and is not al-
lowed to extend to particular acts or
conduct in specialcases. White v . Com .,
80 Ky . 480 , 4 R. 373 ; nor should proof
of the bad character of any of the family
of the accused be admitted . McClure v
Com ., 81 Ky . 448, 5 R. 468 .
(10) Character of witness. The Com-
monwealth can not introduce evidence
of particular facts for the purpose of
affectingthe credibilityof the witnesses
for the defendant , as that " one of them
has been prosecuted for false swearing ,
and that all of the witnesses introduced
by the defendant belonged to a clique
that were banded together to swear ne-
groes out of any offensecharged against
them ." Taylor v .Com .,3 Bush 508. Nor
is proof that the family or associates
of the witnesses are in bad repute com-
petent. Kean v. Com ., 10 Bush 190. In
impeaching the creditof a witness by
general evidence, the examination must
be confinedto hisgeneralreputation,and

§ 221 ] [Title VITrial- Conduct of the Jury .
isnot permittedas to particularfacts.
Thurman v.Virgin,18 B. M. 785; Young
v. Com ., 6 Bush 312 ; and see cases cited
in note 7 and notes to secs. 597-599, Civil
Code .
(11) "In order to impeach a witness
the evidence of bad character should be
directed to the time of the trial, and
to this end , under proper circumstances,
evidence of previous bad character is
competent; while the evidence of bad
characterat a time previousmay tend
to show the character of the witness
at the present, it should be admitted
with some caution." Mitchellv. Com .,
78 Ky . 219.
(12) "An impeaching witness should
be ableto say that he knows the general
estimation in which the witness sought
to be impeached is held among those
who know him ,or among whom he lives.
If he does, then he may say whether the
witnessisesteemedby hisneighborsand
thosewho know him as a personof good
moral character or not ." Furnish v .
Com .,14 Bush 180.
(13) Impeachment of character of wit-
ness,whose affidavitisread as a deposi-
tion, is allowable, and witness may be
impeached without regardto materiality
of his evidence. Davis v . Com ., 95 Ky .
19, 15 R. 396, 23 S. W. 585.
(14) Circumstantial evidence. Circum-
stantial evidence, "when of a satisfac-
tory character, sufficientto warrant a
conviction, should be left, like direct or
positive evidence, to be considered by the
jury, and to have such weight as they
deem it entitledto." Brady v. Com ., 11
Bush 282 .
(15) There is no law requiring the
court, at the instance of the accused, to
have dead bodiestaken up and examined
at the expenseof the Stateor county for
the purpose of furnishinghim with evi-
dence. Salisburyv. Com ., 79 Ky . 425,
3 R. 211 .
(16) Circumstantial evidence is com-
petent to establishthe fact that the per-
son charged to have been murdered is
dead,and itisforthe jury to determine
its conclusiveness. Johnson v . Com ., 81
Ky . 325, 5 R. 197 ; and see O'Brien v .
Com .,89 Ky .354,11 R. 534,12 S. W. 471 ;
holding that where circumstantial evi-
dence has to be reliedon , any fact which
isnecessarytointroduceorexplainan-other may be proved; and see further,
Riley v. Com ., 94 Ky . 266, 15 R. 46, 22
S.W. 222; Com .v.Hide,94 Ky .517,15 R.
264,23 S. W. 195.
(17) Civil Code . Provisions contained
in section 606 are not adopted as part
of the CriminalCode. Com .v.Minor,89
Ky . 555, 11 R. 775, 13 S. W. 5; Hilbert
v .Com .,21 R. 537, 51 S. W. 817 .
(18) Competency. The Court of Ap-
peals can not " speculate" on the effect
of incompetent evidence. It is enough
to know it was wrong , and may have
operatedto the prejudiceof the defend-
ant. Kennedy v. Com ., 14 Bush 340;
Coppage v.Com .,3 Bush 532.
(19) Any person can testifywithout
regard to his religiousbeliefor disbe-
lief, and it is not proper to make any
inquiryconcerningit. Bush v. Com ., 80
Ky.244,3 R.740.
(20) All personsare competent except
thoseexcludedby section1180,Ky .Stat.,
Com . v. McGuire, 84 Ky . 57, 7 R. 814;
Com .v.Minor, 89 Ky .555, 11 R. 775, 13
S. W. 5 ; Combs v .Com ., 15 R. 660, 25 S.
W. 590 ; but depositions can not be read
against the defendant . Kaelin v . Com .,
84 Ky .354,8 R. 293 , 1 S. W. 594.
(21) Confessions. See sec. 240 and
notes .
(22 ) Conspiracy . See notes to section
234 , and observe that that section has
been repealed. Now personsjointlyin-
dicted as conspiratorsmay testifyfor
each other. Kidwellv.Com .,97 Ky .538,
17 R. 79,31 S.W. 131; seefurther,as to
testimony of conspirators, Tywman v.
Com .,17 R. 1038, 33 S. W. 409; and full
cases in Powers v . Com ., 110 Ky . 386,22
R. 1807, 61 S. W. 735; Howard v. Com .,
110 Ky . 356, 22 R. 1845, 61 S. W. 756;
Com . v.Hargis,99 S. W. 348,30 R. 510,
124 Ky . 356 .(23 ) Contradiction of witness. Before
evidence can be introduced to contradict
a witnesshe must firstbe inquiredof as
to such statements with circumstances
of time, placeand persons present, and
this rule applies where the witness
sought to be impeached isdead,and evi-
dence is introduced as to his statements
in the former trial; and statements made
by the witness (who is dead , and whose
testimony is reproduced) subsequent to
the former trial, showing that the testi-
mony given by him was false,were not
competent. Craft v. Com ., 81 Ky . 250,
5 R. 53 .
(24 ) Where the evidence of an accom-
plice is corroborated, he occupies the
same position as any other witness so far
as the method of contradictingor im-
peaching him is concerned. Com . V.
Craft,81 Ky . 250, 5 R. 53 .
(25) "Where a witness states a fact
prejudicialto the party callinghim, the
lattermay be allowed to show that such
factdoes not existby proving that the
752

Title VI ] [§ 221Trial- Conduct of the Jury .
witness had made statements to others
inconsistentwith hispresenttestimony."
Blackburn v.Com .,12 Bush 181; Champ
v . Com ., 2 Met . 17 ; but the court should
say to the jury that the evidence is
only admissibleto affectcredibilityof
witness contradicted. Ashcraft v. Com .,
24 R. 488 ,68 S. W. 847.
(26 ) "A witness can not be cross-ex-
amined upon factscollateraland irrele-
vant to the issue for the purpose of con-
tradictinghim ,his answers to such facts
being conclusiveagainstthe party call-
ing for them ; nor can a witness who
fails to testify to substantive facts be
asked if he has not made statements to
others out of court that such facts exist ,
for the purpose of proving that he has
made such statements . Loving v . Com .,
80 Ky . 507, 4 R. 457 ; Kennedy v . Com .,
14 Bush 340; Crittendenv.Com .,82 Ky .
164,6 R.20;Champ v.Com.,2 Met.17;Cornelius v.Com .,15 B. Μ .539 .
(27 ) Where a witness on cross-exam-
ination stated that he had not said he
heard deceased make certain statements ,
it was error to allow proof that witness
had made such a statement . Com . v .
Hourigan, 89 Ky . 305, 11 R. 509, 12 S.
W. 550 .
(28 ) Declarations. Statements of the
accused explanatory of certain acts
which he did shortlybeforekillingwere
not competent. Oder v.Com ..80 Ky .32,
4 R. 18; Terrell v. Com ., 13 Bush 246 .
"Declarations of one conspirator are only
admissible against the others when made
before the object of the conspiracy is ac-
complished ." Miller v. Com ., 78 Ky . 15 ;
Thompson v.Com .,1 Met .13; Hudson v.
Com .,2 Duv . 531 ; Jones v . Com ., 2 Duv .
554 ; and cases cited in note 22 .
(29) A plea of guiltymade in an ex-
amining court may be proved by oral
testimony. Rector v. Com ., 80 Ky . 468,
4 R. 323 .
(30) Statements of persons who had
agreed to act in concert with the accused
in the event of a hostile meeting between
him and deceased are competent for the
Commonwalth ; and , when such state-
ments are asked for , the accused has the
right to have the whole of the conversa-
tion at that time . Cornelius v . Com ., 15
B. M. 539 ; and see Rapp v . Com ., 14 В.
M. 614 ; Merriweather v.Com ., 26 R. 793,
82 S. W. 592 .
(31) Defendant as a witness- may be
cross-examined as any other witness.
Welsh v . Com ., 111 Ky . 530, 23 R. 151,
60 S. W. 185, 948, 1118; 63 S. W. 984;
64 S. W. 262 ; Warren v. Com ., 99 Ky .
370 , 18 R. 141, 35 S. W. 1028 ; Burdette
v. Com ., 93 Ky . 76, 13 R. 960, 18 S. W.
1011; Lesliev.Com .,19 R. 1201,42 S.W.
1095; Saylor v. Com ., 97 Ky . 184, 17 R.
100, 30 S. W. 390 ; Baker v . Com ., 106
Ky . 212, 20 R. 1778, 50 S. W. 54 ; Parker
v . Com ., 21 R. 406, 51 S. W. 573 ; Pen-
ningtonv.Com .,21 R. 542,51 S.W. 818;
and may be recalledas any other witness,
Abbott v. Com ., 23 R. 226,62 S. W. 715 .
(3la ) In a prosecution for felony, the
defendant testifiedin his own behalf; it
was proper to compel him to answer the
question whether he had been convicted
of a felony. It was the duty of the Cir-
cuit Judge to admonish the jury that
this was only competent for the purpose
of affectinghis credibilityas a witness .
GeBurk v. Com ., 153 Ky .264, 155 S. W.
381 .
(32) It is error to allow comment by
Commonwealth'sattorneyon failureof
accused to testifyon his examining trial.
Parrott v. Com ., 20 R. 761,47 S. W. 452 ;
in Taylor v . Com ., 17 R. 1214, 34 S. W.
227 , the court held that such comment
was not error .
(33) Diagram of premises where kill-
ing occurred may be examined and re-
ferred to by witness who made it. Com .
v.Hourigan,89 Ky .305, 11 R. 509, 12 S.
W. 550.
(33a ) It is error to require the de-
fendant to stand up by the side of the
prosecutingwitnessduringthe argument
of the Commonwealth's attorney in or-
der that thejury may seethe sizeof the
two . Balee v.Com ., 153 Ky . 558, 156 S.
W. 147 .
(34) Dogs as witnesses. In some cases
the trialingof bloodhounds of good char-
acter and pedigree is admissible to locate
the accused and establish his connection
with the crime. Pedigo v.Com .,103 Ky .
41 , 19 R. 1723 , 44 S. W. 143; Denham v .
Com ., 27 R. 171, 84 S. W. 538, 119 Ky .
508 .
(35) Drunkenness is no excuse for
crime , but evidence of drunkenness is
competent to show want of malice.
Buckhanan v.Com .,86 Ky . 110,9 R. 411,
5 S. W. 358 ; Wilkerson v . Com . , 88 Ky .
20 , 10 R. 658, 9 S. W. 836 ; Shannahan
v .Com ., 8 Bush 463 ; Bishop v. Com ., 109
Ky . 558, 22 R. 1161, 60 S. W. 190 ; and
where a feloniousintentis necessaryto
constitute an offense- such as robbery-
the accused may show that he was too
drunk to have any intent when he com-
mitted the act. Keeton v. Com ., 92 Ky .
522 , 13 R. 748, 18 S. W. 359 ; and so in
prosecutionfor false swearing. Wil-
liams v . Com ., 113 Ky . 652, 24 R. 465, 68
S. W. 871.
753

§ 221 ] [Title VITrial- Conduct of the Jury .
(36) Dying declaration. The admis-
sion of proof of dying declarationsis
not an infringement of the constitutional
right of the accused to confront his wit-
nesses face to face; but ,to be admissible,
the declaration must be made in ex-
tremis , and under a solemn sense of im-
pending dissolution. Walston v . Com .,
16 Β . Μ . 15 ; and wife is competent to
prove declarationsof her husband ,Arnett
v.Com .,114 Ky .593,24 R. 1440, 71 S. W.
635; Hilbert v. Com ., 21 R. 537, 51 S. W.
817 .
(37) A statement by the deceased as
to how and by whom he was injured,
made about twenty minutes before he
became insensible , in which condition he
remained until he died, coupled with the
statement "that he was ruined and a
dead man , unless he got speedy relief,"
was held not to have been made under
a sense of impending dissolution. Ad-
well v . Com ., 17 B. M. 310.
(38 ) A declaration of a wounded per-
son, made and reduced to writing two
hours before his death ,when he believed
he would recover, if afterward referred
to by him shortly before his death , and
when he knew he must die ,and its truth
affirmed, is competent evidence. "Dying
declarations are not necessarily either
written or spoken . Any method of com-
munication between mind and mind may
be adopted that will develop the thought ,
as the pressure of the hand , a nod of the
head or a glance of the eve." Mockabee
v. Com ., 78 Ky . 380; and see Young v.
Com ., 6 Bush 312.
(39 ) Dying declarations should be re-
strictedto the act of killingand the cir-
cumstances immediately attending it and
forming a part of the res gestae. Leiber
v . Com ., 9 Bush 11; and see Collins v .
Com ., 12 Bush 271 ; Terrell v . Com ., 13
Bush 246 ; Luby v.Com ..12 Bush 1; Com .
v . Hargis , 30 R. 510, 99 S. W. 348, 124
Ky . 356 ; Kennedy v . Com ., 100 S. W.
242, 30 R. 1065; and may be impeached
by showing differentdeclarations or by
showing them to be untrue, Com . V.
Lawson , 25 R. 2187. 80 S. W. 206 .
(40 ) Dying declaration must relate to
the circumstances attending the crime ;
only so much of it is competent as de-
tails the manner of it. Statement that
"he was shot for nothing" was incom-
petent, but ,as this was proved by other
witnesses ,itsintroduction was not preju-
dicial . Pace v . Com ., 89 Kv . 204 , 11 R.
407 , 12 S. W. 271; and see Chittenden v .
Com ., 10 R. 330. 9 S. W. 386 ; Penning-
ton v .Com .,24 R. 321, 68 S. W. 451 ; and
the length of time between declarations
and death does not affect its compe-
tency, Burton v . Com ., 24 R. 1162 , 70 S.
W. 831 .
(41) To be admissible they must be
made when the party has given up all
hope of life; but whether thisbe the case
or not may be determined by the sur-
rounding circumstances and by the evi-
dent danger of the deceased,and he need
not in express words or in equivalent
language declare that he knows he is
about to die. Com . v . Matthews , 89 Ky .
287, 11 R. 505 , 12 S. W. 333 ; Peoples v .
Com .,87 Ky .487, 10 R. 517, 10 S. W. 642;
Cleveland v . Com ., 101 S. W. 931, 31 R.
116,Biggs v . Com ., 150 Ky . 675.
(42 ) A mere belief of the party that
he would die is not sufficient to warrant
the introduction of his statements as a
dying declaration. Vaughan v . Com ., 86
Ky .431, 9 R. 644,6 S. W. 153.
(43 ) If dying declaration has been re-
duced to writing, the writing should be
introduced as the best evidence, but if
writing is excluded by objection of ac-
cused then oral evidence is competent .
Statements made at differenttimes may
be proved ifotherwise competent . Hines
v . Com ., 90 Ky . 64. 11 R. 865, 13 S. W.
445 ; Blyew v . Com ., 91 Ky . 200, 12 R.
742, 15 S. W. 356 ; Saylor v .Com ., 97 Ку .
184 , 17 R. 100. 30 S. W. 390 .
(44 ) "To the general rule excluding
matters of opinion or beliefan exception
should be made , allowing the declarations
of the deceased in behalf of the accused
where they will explain the acts and con-
duct of deceased, or show his feelingsor
motives , intent or belief, when they are
essentialto qualify or aggravate his con-
duct ." Haney v . Com ., 5 R. 203 .
(45 ) Defendant may introduce dying
declaration in his behalf , and the state-
ment of deceased that he and accused
were playing and that the shooting was
an accident. Com . v . Matthews , 89 Ку .
287 , 11 R. 505 , 12 S. W. 333 ; or that he
"was wholly to blame for the difficulty
and brought on the trouble himself, and
did not want accused prosecuted," is
competent . Brock v. Com ., 92 Ky . 183 ,
13 R. 450 , 17 S. W. 337 ; Haney v . Com .,
5 R. 203 .
(46) Any fact which the deceased
could testifyto, if alive, is admissible as
his dying declaration. Brock v . Com .,
92 Ky . 183, 13 R. 450, 17 S. W. 337.
(47 ) Upon trialof one accomplice it is
not competent to prove a dying declara-
tion of the other exculpating the one on
trial. Mitchell v. Com ., 12 R. 458, 14 S.
W. 489 ; nor can defendant prove that
another person made a dying declaration
754

Title VI ] [§ 221Trial- Conduct of the Jury .
that he and not accused committed crime .
Davis v. Com ., 95 Ky . 19, 15 R. 396, 23
S. W. 585 .
(48) Nor is it any objectionthat de-
ceased was at the time under influence
of narcotics; such objection would go
to credibilityalone. Hays v . Com ., 12
R. 611, 14 S. W. 833.
(49 ) Error in admitting incompetent
evidence under an indictment for murder
prejudicial, although accused found
guiltyof manslaughter. Scott v. Com. ,
94 Ky .511, 15 R. 251,23 S. W. 219.
(50) False swearing . Evidence of one
witness not sufficient to convict - cor-
roborating circumstances- written evi-
dence . Com . v.Davis , 92 Ky . 460, 13 R.
676 , 18 S. W. 10.
(51) Flight- feigned insanity of ac-
cused competent against him . Basham
v. Com ., 87 Ky . 440, 10 R. 434, 9 S. W.
384 .
(52 ) Foreign records- proper authenti-
cation of. Faustre v. Com ., 92 Ky . 34,
13 R. 347, 17 S. W. 189; Mount v . Com .,
1 Duv . 90 .
(53 ) Husband and wife . Wife is a
competent witness against her husband
where he is charged with committing or
attemptingto commit a crimeagainsther. Com. v. Sapp , 90 Ky . 580, 12 R.
484, 14 S. W. 834; overrulingTurnbull
v . Com ., 79 Ky . 495, 3 R. 275 ; and is
competent to prove dying declarationsof
husband , Arnett v. Com ., 114 Ky . 593,
24 R. 1440, 71 S. W. 635.
(54 ) A divorced wife is a competent
witness in behalf of her husband , to
prove factswhich came to her knowl
edge while the marriage relationexisted
between them , but which did not come
to her confidentially, nor by means of
her situation as his wife . Elswick v .
Com .,13 Bush 155.
(55) The wife of one of three defend-
ants in an indictment was a competent
witnessagainstthe othertwo ,afterthe
indictment had been dismissed as to her
husband . Ray v. Com ., 12 Bush 397.
(56 ) Under a joint indictment against
severalfor murder, one of them being
awarded a separate trial, the wives of
the others were competent witnesses for
him . Thompson v. Com ., 1 Met . 13 ;
Cornelius v.Com .,3 Met .481 .
(57) " Communication between hus-
band and wife- meaning of. Com . v .
Sapp ,90 Ky .580. 12 R. 484, 14 S. W. 834 ;
and see as to competency of letterswrit-
ten by husband to wife , Scott v . Com .,
94 Ky . 511, 15 R. 251, 23 S. W. 219 ; see
further,notes to section606,CivilCode .
(58) Insanity. Opinions of non -pro-
fessional witnesses as to the sanity of
the defendant may be admitted as evi-
dence , but the court should be satisfied
beforeadmittingsuch evidencethat the
witness has had an opportunity , by as-
sociation and observation, to form an
opinion as to the sanity of the accused.
Brown v . Com ., 14 Bush 398 ; Cotrell v .
Com ., 13 R. 305, 17 S. W. 149; Massie v .
Com ., 15 R. 562, 24 S. W. 611 ; Phelps v .
Com ., 17 R. 706, 32 S. W. 470 ; Abbott v .
Com ., 107 Ky . 624, 21 R. 1372, 55 S. W.
196 ; Banks v. Com ., 141 S. W. 380, 145
Ky . 800.
(59 ) An expert should not be allowed
toexpresshisprofprofessionalopinionas to
the sanity of the accused unless there is
a hypothetical case or agreed facts sub-
mitted to him . 14 Bush 398 ; McCarthy
v. Com ., 14 R. 285, 20 S. W. 229 ; Davis
v . Com .,6 R. 658.
(60 ) Evidence that the accused has
been from infancy afflictedwith som-
nolentia or somnambulism is competent .
Fain v . Com ., 78 Ky . 183 .
(61) As to moral insanity, see Scott v .
Com ., 4 Met . 227 ; Graham v . Com ., 16
B. M. 587 ; Smith , v . Com ., 1 Duv . 224 .
Uncontrollableimpulse, see Abott v.
Com., 107Ky .624,21 R. 1372,55 S.W.
196 .
(62) Competency of physician who
does not claim to be an expert. Mont-
gomery v . Com ., 88 Ky . 509, 11 R. 40 ,
11 S. W. 475 ; Abbott v . Com ., 107 Ky .
624 , 21 R. 1372 , 55 S. W. 196 .
(63 ) Contradiction of physician by
showing former statements in conflict
with his testimony. Montgomery v.
Com ., 88 Ky . 509,11 R. 40, 11 S. W. 475.
(64) Evidence of insanity both before
and after commission of crime is com-
petent. Montgomery v . Com ., 88 Ky .
509 , 11 R. 40 , 11 S. W. 475 ; Moore v .
Com .,92 Ky .630,13 R. 738, 18 S. W. 833.
(65 ) To authorize admission of testi-
mony of insanity of parents or relatives
of accused it should firstappear by other
evidence that defendant was insane when
he committed act; but see this case for
exceptionto rule. Murphy v. Com ., 92
Ky . 485. 13 R. 695, 18 S. W. 163.
(66) Defense for crime - not sufficient
to show alone that accused was insane
when he committed crime ; it must ap-
pear that by reason of his insanityhe
did not know right from wrong , or did
not have sufficientwill power to control
his actions. Montgomery v . Com ., 88
Ky . 509, 11 R. 40, 11 S. W. 475 ; Abbott
v . Com ., 107 Ky . 624, 21 R. 1372, 55 S.
W. 196 .

(67) Incompetentevidence. Exception
-practice. See notes,sec.340.
(68) Limiting effectof testimony by
instructionis properwhere the evidence
isonly introducedfor or competent for
the purpose of contradicting a witness .
Fueston v. Com ., 91 Ky . 230, 12 R. 854,
15 S. W. 177; Collins v . Com ., 15 R. 691,
25 S. W. 743 .
(69) Liquor sellingillegally. Evidence
upon which convictionmay be had. Ky .
Stat ., sec. 2571. Smith v . Com ., 17 R.
416, 31 S. W. 471; Stamper v.Com .,102
Ky . 33, 19 R. 1014, 42 S. W. 915; and
Ky . Stat., secs.2557a,b .
(70) Malice- manslaughter. Incompe-
tenttoprovedeclarationsshowing malice
under indictment for manslaughter . Com .
v . Matthews , 89 Ky . 287 , 11 R. 505, 12
S. W. 333 .
(71) Motive for crime . Competent to
show . O'Brien v . Com ., 89 Ky . 354, 11
R. 334. 12 S. W. 471 ; Martin v . Com ., 93
Ky . 189, 14 R. 95, 19 S. W. 580 ; Carpen-
ter v . Com ., 92 Ky . 452 , 13 R. 658, 18 S.
W. 9 .
(72 ) Motive of accused---evidence in
his behalf. See Massie v . Com ., 15 R.
562, 24 S. W. 611 ; Riggs v. Com ., 103 Ky .
610, 20 R. 276 , 45 S. W. 866 ; Ellis v .
Com ., 146 Ky .715, 143 S. W. 425.
(73 ) Part of a statement being proved
by defendant,itisproperto allow Com-
monwealth to prove remainder . O'Brien
v . Com ., 89 Ky . 354, 11 R. 534, 12 S. W.
471 .
(74 ) Prisoner in penitentiary may tes-
tify in criminal case unless he is dis-
qualifiedunder sec.1180,Ky .Stat. Com .
v .Minor ,89 Ку .555, 11 R. 775, 13 S. W.
5; Combs v. Com ., 15 R. 660, 25 S. W.
590; Hancock v. Parker,100 Ky . 143, 18
R. 622 , 37 S. W. 594 .
(75) Provocation . Words tending to
excite passions- competent to reduce
crime to manslaughter . Massive v.Com .,
15 R. 562, 24 S. W. 611; Stott v . Com .,
17 R. 308 , 29 S. W. 141 .
(76 ) Questions that degrade or dis-
grace may be asked for the purpose of
impairing credibilityof witness , but he
can not be required to expose himself to
punishment or prosecutionfor crime.
Burdette v. Com ., 93 Ky . 76, 13 R. 960,
18 S. W. 1011; nor asked about particu-
laracts or crimes except that he has been
convicted of a felony, Civil Code , sec.
597 ; Howard v. Com ., 110 Ky . 356, 22 R.
1845 61 S. W. 756 ; Com . v . Welch , 111
Ky .530, 23 R. 151,63 S. W. 984; Leach
v .Com ..112 S. W. 595, 120 Ку . 497 .
(77) Rape . Evidence sufficientto con-
vict. Malone v. Com ., 91 Ky . 307, 12 R.
895,15 S.W. 856;and seeBrown v.Com .,
102 Ky . 227, 19 R. 1174,43 S. W. 214.
(78 ) Records . The record of a former
trialand convictionis incompetentevi-
dence against the accused on the second
trialfor the same offense. Tully v.Com .,
13 Bush 142. See Clark v . Com ., 14 Bush
166, and see Com . v. Foster, 3 Met . 1.
(79 ) The certificateof the Secretary
of State of Ohio was held admissibleto
prove, with other evidence, that a bank
in Ohio was legallyincorporated. Mount
v. Com ., 1 Duv . 90. But an incomplete
recordof divorceproceedingsis incom-
petent to prove that a party indictedfor
bigamy has been divorced. Davis V.
Com ., 13 Bush 318; and see Faustre v.
Com .,92 Ky .34,13 R. 347,17 S.W. 189.
(80) On the trialof a person indicted
for an illegalescape from the peniten-
tiary, a certifiedcopy of the judgment
sentencing him to serve in the peniten-
tiary was competent to prove that he
was legallyin custody. Hudgens v.
Com ., 2 Duv . 239 .
(81) Res gestae. "Contemporaneous
expressions or exclamations of the as-
sailant, or of coadjutors, or of the de-
ceased in case of homicide ,may be proved
for the purpose of illustratingthe char-
acter or quality of the act." Bradshaw
v . Com ., 10 Bush 576. See Sherley v .
Billings,8 Bush 147; Rutherford v .Com .,
13 Bush 608. As to declarations of ac-
cused in his favor, see Tipper v. Com ..
1 Met .6,and see Terrellv.Com ..13 Bush
246 ; Johnson v . Com ., 22 R. 1885, 61 S.
W. 1005 .
(82) Cries or exclamations of bystand-
ers in no way acting in concert with
either of the parties to the transaction
do not constitutea partof the resgestae,
and are not competent . Kaelin v . Com .,
84 Ky . 354,8 R. 293, 1 S. W. 594. 10 Bush
576 ; Stroud v . Com ., 14 R. 179, 19 S. W.
976 ; Omer v. Com ., 95 Ky .353 ,15 R. 694,
25 S. W. 594 ; nor are statements made
in presence of accused but not adopted
by him . Merriweather v. Com ., 26 R. 793,
82 S. W. 592. The exclamation of per-
sons as to who committed an offense are
competenton the trialof personschargedwith resistingan officer, who , when
wounded , was attempting to make an
arrest,and was aidedin ascertainingthe
offender by the declarations of persons
present. Werner v . Com ., 80 Ky . 387, 4
R. 203 .
(83) Separation of witnesses. Permit-
ting the Commonwealth's attorney to
talk with the witnesses after they were
sworn and separated did not prejudice
the accused, as the same permission was
756

Title VI ] [§ 221Trial- Conduct of the Jury .
granted to and exercisedby his counsel.
Farris v. Com ., 14 Bush 362.
(84) It iserrorto permit the prosecut-
ing witness to remain in the court-room
and testify,when the other witnesses are
excluded, on a trial for murder . Salis-
bury v.Com .,79 Ky .425,3 R. 211.
(85 ) It is proper to allow a witness
for prosecution to remain in court-room
after he has testified, if his presence
willaid the Commonwealth'sattorney.
Galloway v. Com ., 7 R. 165 ; Marcum v .
Com ., 8 R. 418, 1 S. W. 727; and where
a witness remains in the court -room con-
trary to instructions and hears witnesses
testify, court may rejector permit his
testimony ; and unless it appear that
court abused its discretion its action will
not be revised. Haskins v . Com ., 8 R.
419 , 1 S. W. 730 .
(86 ) Slanderous words . Proper to ad-
mit evidence of to reduce crime to man-
slaughter. Massie v. Com ., 15 R. 562,
24 S. W. 611; Riggs v.Com .,103 Ky .610,
20 R. 276, 45 S. W. 866; Stott v . Com . ,
17 R. 308 , 29 S. W. 141 .
(87 ) Testimony on former trial- re-
production of. When the witness has
died since the former trial of the case ,
and his testimony on the former trial
is reproduced,evidence that he had made
statements since the former trial that
his testimony then given was false is
not competent . The rule that a witness
can not be impeached until he has been
inquiredof concerningthe statementap-
plies. Craft v. Com ., 81 Ky . 250, 5 R.
53 ; as to evidence taken down by official
stenographer, see Ky . Stat., sec. 4643,
and Austin v. Com ., 98 S. W. 295, 30 R.
296, 124 Ky . 55 .
(88 ) " The law is that when the wit-
ness states that he remembers the sub-
stance of all the deceased witness testi-
fied to, both on the direct and cross-ex-
amination , he is a competent witness ;
and when the evidence is heard , if it does
not clearlyappear that the witnessdoes
not remember the substance of all that
the deceased person testifiedto, the evi-
dence should be permitted to go to the
jury; but if it be manifest to the court
that he does not so remember , the evi-
dence should be rejected. The testimony
of each witness must be tested by the
same rule,and ,if admissible, must stand
by itself." Bush v. Com ., 80 Ky . 244, 3
R. 740 .
(89 ) Where it is not shown that it is
impossible to secure the attendance on
the final trial of a witness who testified
at theexaminingtrial,itisnot properto
permit others to recitehis testimony on
examining trial. Dye v. Com .,3 Bush 3.
(90 ) The testimony of an absent wit-
ness on the former trial can not be
proved unless the witness is dead . Col-
lins v . Com ., 12 Bush 271; O'Brian v .
Com ., 6 Bush 563 .
(91) The written statement in the bill
of exceptionsof the testimony of a de-
ceased witness on a former trial is not
competent. Kean v.Com .,10 Bush 190;Boner v . Com ., 19 R. 409 , 40 S. W. 700 .
As to evidence taken down by official
stenographer, see Ky . Stat ., sec. 4643 ,
and Beavers v . Bowen , 26 R. 291, 80 S.
W. 1165 ; and cases cited in notes, sec.
605, Civil Code ; minutes of examining
trialnot competent , sec. 64 ; Beavers v .
Bowen , 26 R. 291, 80 S. W. 1165.
(92 ) Testimony out of time . Evidence
in chief after close of testimony may be
admitted for good cause, but in these
cases it was held error . Williams v .
Com ., 90 Ky . 596 , 12 R. 525, 14 S. W.
595 ; Abbott v . Com ., 23 R. 226, 62 S. W.
715 ; court has large discretion, and , un-
less manifestlyabused, its action will
not be reversed. Cargill v . Com ., 93 Ky .
578 , 14 R. 517, 20 S. W. 782 ; Collins v .
Com ., 15 R. 691, 25 S. W. 743 ; Williams
v . Com ., 90 Ky . 596, 12 R. 525, 14 S. W.
595 .
(93) Threats . It is competent for de-
fendant to prove a threat made by de-
ceased,although not in terms directed at
him if all the circumstances show that
it referred to him . Sparks v. Com ., 89
Ky . 644,7 R. 163; but opinion of witness
as to whom threats are directed at is
not competent. Johnson v.Com .,9 Bush
224 .
(94 ) Evidence of general threats, not
directedat any particularperson, was
held competent in Whittaker v.Com .,13
R. 504 ,17 S. W. 358 ; Brooks v .Com ., 100
Ky . 194, 18 R. 702, 37 S. W. 1043 ; and
see Madison v . Com ., 13 R. 313, 17 S. W.
164; Ellisv. Com ..146 Ky . 715, 143 S.
W. 425 .
(95 ) Threats by deceased to killmoth-
er of accused were held competent in
Rutherford v .Com ., 13 Bush 608 .
(96 ) Threats by accused are compe-
tent to show malice. Nichols v . Com .,
11 Bush 575 ; but not under a man-
slaughter indictment. Com .v .Matthews ,
89 Ky .287,11 R. 505,12 S.W. 333.(97 ) Uncommunicated threats are
competent where it is material to show
who began the difficulty. Miller v. Com .,
89 Ky .653, 10 R. 672, 10 S. W. 137; Hart
v .Com .,85 Ky .77, 8 R. 714,2 S. W. 673 ;
Wheeler v . Com ., 27 R. 1090, 87 S. W.
757

§ 222-223] [Title VITrial- Conduct of the Jury .
§ 222. [Statementfor defendant.] The defendantor his attorney
may thenstatethenatureof hisdefense,and thelaw and evidenceupon
which he reliesinsupportof it: Provided, He may ,at hisoption,make
the statementhereinreferredto immediatelybeforethe Commonwealth
introducesany of itstestimony,and aftertheattorneyfortheCommon-
wealth, if he has so desired, has made the statement for the Common-
wealth. (Sectionas amended by Act of March 24, 1902,c.89,p .210.)
Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky . Crim .Law , secs. 1046, 1050.
§ 223 [222] [Introductionof evidencefordefendant.] The defend-
ant,or hiscounsel,must then offerhisevidencein supportof hisdefense.
(Exclusion of young spectatorsfrom courtroom ,see Ky . Stat., sec.979 .
Separationof witnesses,see CivilCode ,sec.601.)
Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky .Crim , Law , secs.214, 990, 1050, 1091.
1106; and where there is evidence of
communicated threats it is competent to
prove uncommunicated threats.
nelius v . Com ..15 B. M. 539 .
Cor-
(98) Accused may prove that a person
then dead had told him a short time be-
fore homicide that deceased had threat-
ened to killhim . Carico v. Com ., 7 Bush
124 .
(99 ) Length of time threats are made
before offense does not affect their com-
petency. Abbott v. Com ., 24 R. 148, 68
S. W. 124 .
(100) Venue of offense. Verdict of con-
victionwill not be disturbed, although
there is no evidence showing county in
which offense was committed . Hays v .
Com ., 12 R. 611, 14 S. W. 833 ; Com . v .
Patterson , 10 R. 167, 8 S. W. 694 ; Pick-
erel v. Com ., 17 R. 120, 30 S. W. 617 ;
but see Wilkey v . Com ., 104 Ку . 325, 20
R. 578,47 S. W. 219 ; Bailey v. Com ., 24
R. 1419, 71 S. W. 632 ; and it is proper,
after Commonwealth has announced
through , to permit evidence of venue .
Com . v.Patterson ,10 R. 167, 8 S. W. 694.
(101) Order in which evidence shallbe
introduced islargely in discretionof trial
judge. Moore v.Com ., 143 Ку .405, 136
S. W. 608 .
(102) Corporation- existence of may
be shown by parol evidence. Standard
Oil Co. v . Com ., 29 R. 5 , 91 S. W. 1128,
122 Ky .440 ; Morse v.Com ., 129 Ky .294,
111 S. W. 714 .
(103) Sick witness- evidence of may
be taken at convenient place to court
house . Davis v. Com ., 121 S. W. 429.
(104 ) Other crimes evidence of when
admissible. Raymond v . Com ., 96 S. W.
515, 29 R. 785 , 123 Ky . 368 ; Morse v .
Com ., 111 S. W. 714, 129 Ky .294 .
§ 223. (1) Order of proof- court has
discretionin regulating. Moore v. Com .,
143 Ky .405 ,136 S. W. 608 .
( la) Impeaching dying declaration.
Where a dying declaration of a deceased
person was introduced on a trialfor mur-
der it was competent for the defense to
show his statements inconsistent there-
with . Tolliver v. Com ., 161 Ky . 81, 170
S. W. 515 .
(2) Defendant - failure to testify not
subject to comment . Begley v. Com .,
107 S. W. 243, 32 R. 890 ; Boggs v .Com .,
128 S. W. 73 ; Hinton v . Com ., 121 S. W.
434 , 134 Ky . 511 .
(2a ) Upon a trial of one accused of
felony if he failsto testifyand the at-
torney for the Commonwealth in the
course of the argument refersto the fact
that certain evidence adduced was not
contradicted this does not constitute
ground forreversal. Farleyv.Com .,165
Ky . 600, 177 S. W. 431.
(2b ) If accused does not testify as a
witness, evidenceattackinghis veracity
is inadmissible, and evidenceattacking
his moral character is inadmissible when
he has not testified, unless he has intro-
duced other witnesses as to his moral
character. Bennett v.Com ., 171 Ky .63,
186 S. W. 933 .
(2c) In a prosecution for felony, the
defendant testifiedin his own behalf; it
was proper to compel him to answer the
question whether he had been convicted
of a felony. It was theduty of theCircuitJudge to admonish the jury that
this was only competent for the purpose
of affectinghis credibilityas a witness.
Ge Burk v. Com ., 153 Ky . 264, 155 S.
W. 381.
(3) Disqualified defendant not com-
petent to testify. Hinton v . Com ., 121
S. W. 434, 134 Ky .511.

Title VI ] [ § 223Trial- Conduct of the Jury .
§ 1645. Defendant in criminalcase may testify- effectof failure. In all
criminaland penal prosecutionsnow pending or hereafterinstitutedin any
of the courtsof thisCommonwealth , the defendant on trial, on his own re-
quest, shallbe allowedto testifyin his own behalf, but his failureto do so
shallnot be commented upon,or be allowedto createany presumptionagainst
him. (Ky . Stat.,sec. 1645.)
§ 1646. Must testifybefore introducingother testimony. The defendant
requestingthathe be allowed to testify, shallnot be allowed to testifyin
chief,afterany other witnesshas testifiedfor the defense. (Id.,sec. 1646.)
§ 1647. Conspiratorsmay testifyunlessconspiracyproven. If two or more
persons are jointlyindictedthey may testifyfor each other, unlessa con-
spiracyis charged in the indictmentand proven to the satisfactionof the
court.* (Id.,sec.1647.)
§ 1648. Conspiratormay testifyfor himself. If a conspiracyis charged
in the indictment, and proven to the satisfactionof the court, then each de--
fendantnamed in the indictmentmay testifyon hisown behalf,as above pro-
vided.* (Id ., sec. 1648.)
*Sections1645-1648, inclusive, of the Kentucky Statutes, are respectively,
sections22,23,24 and 25 of an Act of June 17,1893,с.227,p.1163. They are
a verbatim copy of sections1-4, inclusive,of an Act of May 1,1886,с.898,p .83.
This Act of 1886was insertedby Judge Bullittfollowingand in the nature
of an amendment , or addition, to section 223 of his 1895 edition of the Code .
It was also insertedin Carroll's1888 editionof the Code following section223.
By an Act of February 21, 1890, с. 217, p. 20, this1888 editionof Carroll's
Codes of Practice, including" sections1 to 450 ,inclusive, of the Criminal Code ,
includingamendments to saidsections,or any of them ," were "adoptedas the
laws of the land ."
As the Act of 1886 was an " amendment to" section 223 of Carroll's 1888
editionof the CriminalCode itwas ,by theAct of 1890,ratifiedand confirmed
as such by the Legislature. The Act of 1893,beinganotheractupon the same
subject and a verbatim copy of the Act of 1886, impliedly repealed the Act
of 1886 and the amendment to section 223 of the Criminal Code .
By an Act of March 23, 1894,с.112,p.342,"subsections3 and 4 of section
223 of the CriminalCode of Practicebe,and theyarehereby,repealed." How-
ever, the titleto this Act of 1894 was , "An act to amend section 223 of the
Criminal Code of Practice; an act to repeal section234 and subsections 3 and
4 of section 223 of the Criminal Code of Practice ." It is submitted that sub-
sections1-4,inclusive, of section223,being the Act of 1886, were repealedby
the Act of 1893, compiled as sections1645-1648, inclusive, of the Kentucky
Statutes. It is also submitted that the said Act of 1894 can not be construed
as repealing sections24 and 25 of the Act of 1893 (identicalwith sections 4
and 5 of the Act of 1886 and , therefore, with subsections 4 and 5 of section
223 of Carroll's1888 edition), for the reasonthatthe intentionto amend the
Act of 1893 was not declared in the titleto the Act of 1894, as required by
section51 of the Constitutionas construedby the Court of Appeals. Further-
more , the expressionof an intentionto repealsection234, if, indeed, that
section also was not affectedby the Act of 1893, could not be said to inform
the readerof any intentionto repealany partof theAct of 1893,now theKen-
tucky Statutes. See Ogden v. Cronan, Sheriff, 171 Ky . 254, 186 S. W. 941;
Exall v.Holland, 166 Ky .315, 179 S. W. 241; South v.Fish,181 Ky .349, and
other cases cited in the latter case . Ed .

§ 224-225 ] [Title VITrial- Conduct of the Jury .
.§ 224 [223] [Rebuttingevidence.] The partiesmay thenrespectively
offerrebuttingevidenceonly,unlessthecourt,forgood reasoninfurther-
anceof justice,permitthem toofferevidenceupon theiroriginalcase.
Cross reference: See Gregory'sKy .Crim .Law ,sec.1051.
§ 225 [226] [Instructionsto be inwriting.) The courtshall,on the
motionof eitherpartyand beforeany argumentto thejury, instructthe
jury on the law applicableto the case, which shallalwaysbe given in
writing.
Cross reference: See Gregory'sKy .Crim.Law ,sec.1110.
§ 224. (1) Rebuttal evidence- rulings
of court in respect to will not be inter-
fered with unless prejudicial. Tetter-
ton v .Com .,89 S. W. 8,28 R. 146.
(2) The admission of testimony in
chiefgiven by rebuttalwitnesseswas
not an abuse of discretion in this case .
Traux v. Com ., 149 Ky . 699, 149 S. W.
1033 .
§ 225. (1) Abstract instructions. It
ought to appear, before an instruction
containingmerely an abstractproposi-
tion is made the sole ground of reversal,
that it is at least probable that the pris-
oner may have been prejudicedby it.
Nichols v. Com ., 11 Bush 575.
(2) The followinginstructioncontains
a mere abstractprinciple,and shouldnot
be given: "Where there is a conflictin
the testimony of witnesses, the one side
being of an affirmativeand the other of
a negative character, the affirmative
characterof the testimony is preferred,
and is entitledto the greater weight by
the jury in making up theirverdict."
Lou . Chemical Works v . Com ., 8 Bush
179 .
(3) Accomplice . The court, in in-
structingthe jury as to the weight that
should be given to the evidenceof an
accomplice, should follow substantially
section 241. Craft v . Com ., 80 Ky . 349,
4 R. 182 , and notes to sec . 241 .
(4 ) After argument begun . It is bad
practice,but not reversibleerror to giveadditional or explanatory instructions
afterthe argument has commenced . Mc-
Daniel v. Com ., 6 Bush 326 ; West v .
Com ., 14 R. 217, 20 S. W. 219 ; and see
Pearce v. Com ., 19 R. 782, 42 S. W. 107 ;
Wilhelm v .Com ..16 R. 428. 28 S. W. 783 ;
Powers v. Com ., 110 Ky . 386, 22 R. 1807,
61 S. W. 735 , 63 S. W. 976 ; Middleton v .
Com .,124 S. W. 355, 136 Ky . 354.
(5 ) Aiders and abettors . It is error to
say that defendant is guilty if he was
present and "approved of" or "consented
to" the killing, but it is proper to use
the words " encourage , aid or abet," or
the words "counsel, advise or assist."
True v . Com ., 90 Ky . 651, 12 R. 594, 14
S. W. 684 ; Omer v . Com ., 95 Ky . 353 ,
15 R. 694, 25 S. W. 594 ; and see Plum-
mer v . Com ., 1 Bush 76. Chittenden v.
Com ., 10 R. 330. 9 S. W. 386 ; Reed v .
Com ., 100 S. W. 84, 30 R. 1215.
(5a) An indictment against a single
defendant charged him only as a princi-
pal; an instructionauthorizinghis con-
viction as aider and abettor was error .
Hollin v. Com ., 158 Ky . 427, 165 S. W.
407 .
(6 ) Alibi. As to proper instructions,
see Young v . Com ., 8 Bush 366.
(7 ) Assault and battery- proper in-
structions. Cornelisonv. Com ., 84 Ky .
583. 8 R. 793. 2 S. W. 235 .
(8 ) Assuming facts. Instructions that
assume as a fact certain propositions
which are controverted, and that the
jury have a rightto determine,are erro-
neous . Leiber v . Com .,9 Bush 11 ; Berry
v . Com ., 10 Bush 15 ; Edgerton v. Com .,
7 Bush 142.
(9) Bigamy . For instructionsconcern-
ing, see Com . v . Jackson , 11 Bush 679 .
( 10) "Brought on difficulty"-error to
instruct jury that defendant can not
avail himself of right of self-defense if
he brought on difficulty. Allen v. Com .,
86 Ky . 643, 9 R. 784 , 6 S. W. 645, and
see note 49 .
(11) Where right of self-defense is at-
tempted to be denied because of the fault
of the accused the jury should be in-
structedthat they must believebeyond
a reasonable doubt accused was in fault .
Allen v . Com ., 86 Ky . 643 , 9 R. 784, 6 S.
W. 645 ; Riley v . Com ., 94 Ky . 266, 15
R. 46 , 22 S. W. 222 ; Cockrill v. Com ..95
Kv .22 , 15 R. 328, 23 S. W. 659.
(12) If one by his own wrongful act
makes the harm or danger to himself
necessary or excusable he can not rely on
plea of self-defense,but words spoken in
jestor act not intendedor calculatedto
760

Title VI ] [§ 225Trial- Conduct of the Jury .
provoke can not depriveaccusedof right
of self-defense,although they may have
caused difficulty. Allen v . Com ., 86 Ky .
643 , 9 R. 784, 6 S. W. 645; Wilcoxen v .
Com .,15 R. 261,23 S. W. 195 ; Combs v .
Com ., 15 R. 659, 25 S. W. 592.
(13) Characterof accused. It isproper
where character of accused has been im-
peached to say to jury that "evidenceof
his bad character is admitted only to
affecthis credibilityas a witness and for
no other purpose." Hasson v. Com ., 10
R. 1054,11 S. W. 286 ; and see note 41 .
(14) Circumstantial evidence "should
be left, likedirector positiveevidence
to be considered by the jury ,and to have
such weight as they deem it entitledto,
without caution or suggestion on the
part of the court as to its value or the
necessityto scrutinizeitclosely." Brady
v. Com ., 11 Bush 282.
(15) Confessions. It is error to in-
struct the jury, on the trial of one
charged with murder , that if they be-
lieve the accused confessed the killing,
they ought to findhim guilty. Butlerv.
Com ., 2 Duv . 435 ; or to assume in an
instruction that a confession has been
made . The jury should be left to deter-
mine from the evidence whether or not
any confession of guilt has been made .
Cunningham v. Com ., 9 Bush 149.
(16) An instructionin the language of
section240 isheldto be properinWig-gington v. Com ., 92 Ky . 282, 13 R. 641,
17 S. W. 634 ; and see notes to sec.240.
(17) Consequences of his own acts.
Instruction in murder case that a sane
man intends probable consequences of his
own acts deliberately done erroneous.
Rogers v.Com ., 16 R. 199, 27 S. W. 813.
(18) Correction of instruction, after
verdict has been announced , and while
jury is being polled, is error. Roberts v .
Com .,90 Ky .654,12 R. 681, 14 S. W. 832.
(19 ) Deadly weapon - instruction de-
fining. Evans v. Com ., 11 R. 551, 12 S.
W. 767; Cosby v. Com ., 115 Ky . 221, 24
R. 2051 , 72 S. W. 1098 .
(20 ) Deadly weapon - what is, under
an indictment for striking or injuring
with . is a question that should be left to
jury if there is room for doubt as to
whether it was or not a deadly weapon .
Com . v . Duncan . 91 Kv . 592 , 16 S. W.
530 , 13 R. 162 ; McWilliams v . Com ., 18
R. 92. 35 S. W. 538 .
(21) Defense of dwelling. Proper in-
structions. Wright v. Com ., 85 Ky . 123,
8 R. 718 , 2 S. W. 904 ; Sparks v . Com .,
89 Ky . 644 , 7 R. 163 ; Estep v . Com ., 86
Ky . 39 , 9 R. 278 , 4 S. W. 820 .
(22 ) Defense of others. Proper in-
structions in cases of killing in defense
of another. Stanleyv.Com .,86 Ky .440,
9 R. 655, 6 S. W. 155 : Chittenden v .
Com ., 10 R. 330, 9 S. W. 386 ; Gills v .
Com ., 18 R. 560, 37 S. W. 269.
(23) Drunkenness. The jury should
not be told that drunkenness mitigates
the offense. Shannahan v . Com ., 8 Bush
463 , expressly overruling Smith v. Com .,
1 Duv .224 ; Blimm v . Com ., 7 Bush 320 ;
and impliedly overruling Golliher
Com ., 2 Duv . 163 ; Curry v. Com ., 2 Bush
67,and see Marshall v .Com .,141 Ky .222,
132 S. W. 139 .
v .
(24) Not proper to give insanityin-structions where accused was merely
drunk when he committed crime . Wilk-
erson v . Com ., 88 Ky . 29, 10 R. 656, 9 S.
W. 836 ; Buchanan v . Com ., 86 Ky . 110, 9
R. 411, 5 S. W. 358 ; nor is it proper to
single out from the evidence the fact of
drunkenness and tellthe jury why they
are permitted to hear evidence bearing
upon it. Buchanan v. Com ., 86 Ky . 110,
9 R. 411,5 S. W. 358 ; Bishop v.Com ., 109
Ky . 558, 22 R. 1161,60 S. W. 190; as to
defense of drunkenness in crimes that a
felonious intention is an essentialingre-
dient of, as robbery , see Kecton v . Com .,
92 Ky . 522, 13 R. 748, 18 S. W. 359 ; Wil-
liams v. Com ., 113 Ky . 652, 24 R. 465,
68 S. W. 871 .
(25 ) Erroneous instruction- failure to
properlyinstruct. Althoughjuryfound
defendant guilty of manslaughter , when
under the instructionsthey should have
found him guilty of murder or acquit-
ted, did not cure the error in failing to
give manslaughter instructions. Camp-
bell v . Com ., 88 Ky . 402 , 10 R. 975, 11 S.
W. 290 .
(25a ) False swearing --instructions as
to. On the trial of an indictment for an
offense under Kentucky Statutes, 1174,
it is the duty of the circuitcourt to in-
struct the jury that before they can con-
vict, the guilt of the accused must be
established beyond a reasonable doubt
by the testimony of two witnesses, or
of one witness and strong corroborating
circumstances. Walker v . Com ., 162 Ky .
111 , 172 S. W. 109 .
(26 ) Former convictions- proper in-
structions where indictment charges
former convictions . Rector v . Com ., 80
Ky . 468 , 4 R. 323 ; and see Ky . Stat .,
sec. 1130 ; Brown v . Cont ., 100 Ky . 127,
18 R. 630 , 37 S. W. 496 ; Hall v . Com .,
106 Ky . 894, 21 R. 520, 51 S. W. 814 ;
Herndon v .Com ., 105 Ky . 197,20 R. 1114,
48 S. W. 989 .
(27) Grouping facts. Prominent facts
should not be grouped together in an
761

§ 225 ] [Title VITrial- Conduct of the Jury .
instruction. Williams v . Com ., 9 Bush
274 ; and see Elswick v. Com ., 13 Bush
155. Nor should an attempt be made to
enumerate the collateral facts which the
evidence tended to prove. "Instructions
ought ,as a general rule, to be based only
upon such facts as must be found by the
jury in order to establishguilt or to
make out a defense." Coffman v . Com .,
10 Bush 495 .
(28) Incompetent evidence cured by .
Evidence that the defendant had sold
liquoron a Sunday subsequent to the
dateof thewarrantwas incompetent;
but the error in admitting it was cured
by an instructionthat unless the jury
believed from the evidence that the de-
fendant sold liquoron Sunday within the
time covered by the warrant . they must
acquit him . Megowan v . Com ., 2 Met . 3.
Where incompetent testimony was ad-
mitted, an instructiontellingthe jury
to disregard such evidence " except so
far as it harmonized with facts after-
ward developed" did not cure the error.
Dye v. Com ., 3 Bush 3.
(29 ) Instruction to be based on evi-dence . When there is no testimony
showing that the homicide was com-
mitted in self-defense it is not necessary
togive any instructionon that question.
Fitzpatrick v.Com .,81 Ky . 357,5 R. 363 ;
Slagel v . Com ., 81 Ky . 485, 5 R. 545.
(29a ) An instruction should not be
given upon a theory of the case wholly
unsupportedby evidence.Barker v.Com .,
159 Ky . 304 , 166 S. W. 981 ; Gamble v .
Com .,151 Ky . 372, 151 S. W. 924 ; McCoy
v . Com ., 149 Kv . 447. 149 S. W. 903 .
(30 ) "Impending danger ." If accused,
when killing occurs, believes and has
reasonable grounds to believe that dan-
ger to him is impending and that there
is no other apparent means of escape,
then he may strike in self-defense. Coff-
man v . Com ., 10 Bush 495 ; Radford v .
Com ., 9 R. 378, 5 S. W. 343 ; and see
Kennedy v. Com ., 14 Bush 340 .
(31 ) Insanity. Defense of need only
be sustained by a preponderance of the
evidence. Moore v. Com .. 92 Ky . 630,
13 R. 738 , 18 S. W. 833 ; Ball v . Com .,
81 Ky . 662, 5 R. 787 ; Phelps v . Com .,
17 R. 706, 32 S. W. 470; and see Gra-
ham v . Com ., 16 B. M. 587 ; Scott v .
Com ., 4 Met . 227 ; Portwood v . Com .,
104 Ky . 496, 20 R. 680 , 47 S. W. 339 ;
Abbott v . Com ., 107 Ky . 624, 21 R. 1372 ,
55 S. W. 196; Jolly v . Com ., 110 Ky .
190. 22 R. 1622, 61 S. W. 49 ; McCarty
v. Com ., 114 Ky . 620, 24 R. 1427, 71 S.
W. 656; Wilcoxin v. Com ..138 Ky . 846,
129 S. W. 310 ; Banks v . Com . , 141 S.
W. 381, 145 Ky . 800 ; in last three cases
instructionsare copiedin opinions.
(32 ) Where the evidence only showed
that the defendant was illiterate, igno-
rant and passionate, the court properly
refused to instruct the jury on the ques-
tion of insanity. Fitzpatrick v. Com ., 81
Ky . 357,5 R. 363.
(33 ) Instructions that where accused
was insane before committing crime the
law presumes him insane when he com-
mits itisnot proper even where the prior
insanity has been established by an in-
quisition. Montgomeryv.Com.,88 Ky .
509, 11 R. 40, 11 S. W. 475.
(34) See statements of facts authoriz-
ing insanity instructionwhere person ac-
cused was an infant . McClure v . Com ..
81 Ky . 448, 5 R. 468.
(35 ) Involuntary manslaughter - in-
struction as to should be given when no
witness saw homicide committed or the
parties on occasion when killing ос-
curred. Rutherford v . Com ., 13 Bush
608 .
(36) See , for facts authorizing such
an instruction, Smith v . Com ., 93 Ку .
318,14 R. 260,20 S. W. 229 ; and Bush v .
Com ., 78 Ky 268 ; Com . v . Matthews , 89
Ky .287, 11 R. 505, 12 S. W. 333; Mont-
gomery v.Com .,26 R. 356,81 S. W. 264.
(37) Involuntarymanslaughter- defi-
nition of. Conner v . Com ., 13 Bush 714 ;
Trimble v. Com ., 78 Ky . 176; Montgom-
ery v . Com ., 26 R. 356, 81 S. W. 264.
(38) Jury should accept instructions
as law of the case, and , although they
have the power , they have not the right
to disregard the law as expounded to
them by the court. Com . v . Van Tuvl ,
1 Met . 1 .
(39) Law of whole case. Duty of court
to give, even in the absence of a request.
Cook v . Com ., 10 R. 222 , 8 S. W. 872 ;
Trimble v . Com ., 78 Ky . 176 ; Heilman v .
Com . , 84 Ky . 457, 8 R. 451. 1 S. W. 731 ;
Buckles v. Com ., 113 Ky . 795, 24 R. 571,
68 S. W. 1084 ; Lewis v. Com ., 156 Ку .
336, 160 S. W. 1061.
(39a ) A person indictedfor murder by
shooting testifiedthat he did not shoot
deceased; it was error for the court not
to give an instructionupon self-defense.
Elliottv .Com .,152 Ky .791, 154 S. W. 25 .
(396) On an indictment for stealing a
mule there was evidence tending to show
that the accused had authority from one
claiming to be the owner of the mule
to take the mule away and sell it; an
instructionshould have been given cover-
ing thisphase of the case. Hobbs v.
Corn ., 156 Ky . 847. 162 S. W. 104.
(39c) In a trialfor embezzlement there
762

Title VI ] [§ 225Trial- Conduct of the Jury .
was evidence tending to show that the
money had been lost and not embezzled .
It was error to failto instruct the jury
as to this defense. Cline v . Com ., 161
Ky . 678. 171 S. W. 412 .
man-
(39d ) In the trial of an indictment for
murder it is not necessary to give aninstruction as to involuntary
slaughterif it appears upon the whole
evidence that the killingcould not have
been involuntary manslaughter . Cox v.
Com ., 158 Ky . 435, 165 S. W. 411.
(39e) In a prosecutionfor homicide
the evidence was held sufficientto justify
an instruction on the reckless handling
of a pistol; but in this case failure to
give an instructionon involuntary man-
slaughter was not a prejudicial error.
Begley v.Com .,154 Ky .30, 156 S. W. 886.
(39f) On the trialof an indictment for
murder there was no evidence tending
to or consistent with the theory of vol-
untary manslaughter . It was not error
for the Circuit Court to fail to instruct
on voluntary manslaughter . McElwain
v . Com ., 154 Ky . 242, 157 S. W. 6.
(39g) On the trial of an indictment
for larceny where the undisputed evi-
dence showed the value of the stolen
property to be in excess of twenty dol-
lars, it was not necessary for the court
to instruct on petty larceny. Klette v .
Com ., 165 Ky . 430, 177 S. W. 258 .
(40 ) Less punishment than statute
fixed- instruction prescribing, not pre-
judicial. Logsden v. Com ., 9 R. 431, 5 S.
W. 393 .
(41) Limiting effect of testimony .
Where testimonyis only competent for
the purpose of contradicting the evi-
dence of other witnesses the jury should
be so instructed . Fueston v . Com .. 91
Ky . 230 , 12 R. 854, 15 S. W. 177 ; Collins
v. Com ., 15 R. 691, 25 S. W. 743 ; Ash-
craft v. Com .,24 R. 488 , 68 S. W. 847.
(42 ) Malice . It is error to instruct
the jury that "maliceis impliedby the
law from any crueland unnecessaryact
done by one person to another , and from
the deliberate and unnecessary use of
a deadly weapon ." The jury may infer
malice from the circumstances of the
killing, but an instruction tellingthem
that they may infer malice from certain
acts is erroneous. Farris v . Com . , 14
Bush 362 ; and see Buckner v. Com ., 14
Bush 601 ; Trimble v. Com ., 78 Ky . 176 ;
Bush v. Com ., 78 Ky . 268; Salisburyv.
Com .,79 Ky .425 ,3 R. 211.
(43 ) Instruction defining malice as a
" predetermination to do the act without
lawful excuse, it being immaterial how
suddenly or recentlythispredetermina-
tion is formed ," is proper. Armstrong v.
Com ., 15 R. 344 ; 22 S. W. 750; 23 S.
W. 654 .
(43a ) Malicious cutting. On a trial
under an indictment for malicious cut-
ting based on Kentucky Statutes, 1166,
instruction should be given under that
section and under Kentucky Statutes,
1242. Lewis v.Com ., 156 Ky .336, 160 S.
W. 1061 .
(44) Manslaughter . "It is not the
province of the lower court to weigh
evidence for the purpose of determining
whether a person on trialfor his life is
entitled to an instruction as to man-
slaughter. If there is any evidence tend-
ing to show the homicide is of the de-
gree of manslaughter the accused is en-
titledto an instruction upon that hypo-
thesis." Bowlin v . Com ., 94 Ky . 391, 15
R. 149, 22 S. W. 543 .
(45 ) Means of escape. The question
is not whether the jury believe that
defendant had no safe means of escape;
they should be instructed that if defend-
ant believed that he had no safe means
of escape. Oakley v. Com ., 10 R. 885,
11 S. W. 72 .
(45a ) Murder . An instructionauthor-
izing the jury to find the defendant
guilty of murder, if they believedbe-
yond reasonable doubt that he was pres-
ent, aiding or assisting another person
to kill the deceased, was not prejudicial
error, inasmuch as there was no evi-
dence that any one other than the ac-
cused killedthe deceased.Caudill v.Com .,
155 Ky . 578, 159 S. W. 1149.
(45b ) Certain instructionsgiven on a
trial for murder were approved by the
Court of Appeals . Graham v . Com ., 164
Ky . 317, 175 S. W. 981.
(46 ) Murder - manslaughter - involun-
tary manslaughter . Where the indict-
ment is for murder the accused may be
convicted of any degree of homicide as
fixed by the common law , viz., Murder ,
voluntary manslaughter or involuntary
manslaughter , and the court should, un-
der an indictment for murder or volun-
tary manslaughter , when the facts jus-
tifyit, instructthe jury as to involun-
tary manslaughter . Buckner v . Com .,
14 Bush 601 ; Bush v . Com ., 78 Ky . 268 .
But when there is no evidence showing
the existence of facts constituting in-
voluntary manslaughter an instruction
as to it should not be given. York v .
Com ., 82 Ky . 360, 6 R. 334 ; Mitchell v .
Com ., 78 Ky . 219 .
(47 ) When no witness saw the homi-
cide committed , or the parties on the
occasion when the killing occurred, the
763

§ 225 ] [Title VITrial- Conduct of the Jury .
law applicableto murder, manslaughter
and involuntarymanslaughtershouldbe
given inorderto meet any stateof facts
the jury may find from the circum-
stances in evidence to have existed .
Rutherford v. Com ., 13 Bush 608 ; and
see cases cited in note 36 .
(48 ) Under an indictment for murder
or manslaughter, an instructionas to
the statutory offense defined in sec. 2 ,
art. 4, chap . 29 , Gen. Stat . [now Ky .
Stat., sec. 1151], isnot proper. Trimble
v. Com ., 78 Ky . 176 ; Buckner v . Com .,
14 Bush 601 ; Conner v . Com ., 13 Bush
714 .
(49 ) Murder to manslaughter - provo-
cation that will reduce " must be such
as was ordinarilycalculatedto excite
the passions beyond control," and it is
proper to give an instructionin these
terms . Lewis v. Com ., 93 Ky .238, 14 R.
212 , 19 S. W. 664 ; Crockett v . Com ., 100
Ky . 382, 18 R. 835, 38 S. W. 674 ; Camp-
bell v. Com .,88 Ky . 402, 10 R. 975, 11 S.
W. 290 ; Cottrell v . Com ., 13 R. 305, 17
S. W. 149 .
(50) It is improper to say that the
provocation that will reduce murder to
manslaughter must be "considerable
provocation." Lewis v. Com ., 93 Ky .
238 , 14 R. 212, 19 S. W. 664 ; Slaughter
v . Com ., 15 R. 230. 22 S. W. 645 ; or
"legalprovocation." Payne v. Com ., 1
Met . 370 ; Donnellan v . Com ., 7 Bush
676; or to say that "mere words, how-
ever opprobrious or insulting, are not
sufficientprovocationto reducea killing
from murder to manslaughter ." Com . v .
Hourigan , 89 Ky . 305, 11 R. 509 , 12 S.
W. 550 .
(51 ) "Mere words or gestures, though
they may excite passion, do not consti-
tute such provocationas will of itself
extenuate a homicide , committed with a
deadly weapon , and make itmanslaugh-
ter." Rapp v. Com ., 14 B. M. 614; and
see Donnellan v.Com .,7 Bush 676 ; Payne
v . Com .. 1 Met . 370 ; Williams v . Com .,
80 Ky . 313, 4 R. 3 ; Oder v . Com .,80 Ky .
32. 4 R. 18; Coffman v . Com ., 10 Bush
495 .
(52) Mutual rencontre. As to instruc-
tions in cases of mutual recontre , both
parties being in fault at the commence-
ment of the difficulty, or where it is un-
certain which commenced it, see Smaltz
v . Com ., 3 Bush 32 ; Berry v. Com ., 10
Bush 15; Luby v. Com ., 12 Bush 1 ; Во-
hannon v . Com ., 8 Bush 481 ; Minton v .
Com ., 79 Ky . 461, 3 R. 321 ; Terrell v .
Com .. 13 Bush 246 .
(53) Negligence, resulting in injury ordeath , proper instructions. Com . V.
Matthews , 89 Ky . 287, 11 R. 505, 12 S.
W. 333 .
(54 ) Officer- killingof. As to instruc-
tions,where the accusedischargedwith
killing an officer, see Mockabee v. Com .,
78 Ky . 380 ; Fleetwood v . Com ., 80 Ky . 1,
3 R. 497. The instructionsgiven in these
casesare setout in fullin the opinions.
(55 ) Where an officer, in attempting
an arrest of one who knows him to be
an officer, iskilled, it is murder and the
jury need not be told that killingmust
have been malicious. Dilger v . Com ., 88
Ky . 550, 11 R. 67 , 11 S. W. 651 ; and see
notes to sec . 36 .
(56) Oral instructions. The Code re-
quires that all instructionsshallbe writ-
ten, and no departure from this rule can
be tolerated; verbal instructionsare not
cured by subsequentlyreducingthem to
writing and handing writing to jury.
Payne v. Com ., 1 Met . 370; nor should
the court orally state to the jury the
effectof testimony. Lubyv . Com ., 12
Bush 1 ; or make any statement in pres-
ence of jury of the reasons for decisions
upon questionsthat might prejudicethe
rightsof the accused. Coppage v.Com.,
3 Bush 532 ; Kennedy v . Com .. 14 Bush
340. But defendantmay consentto oral
instructions. Allen v .Com ..148 Ку . 327,
146 S. W. 762 ; Ferguson v .Com .,141 Ky .
457, 132 S. W. 1030 ; Harris v . Com ., 141
Ky . 70 , 132 S. W. 148.
(56a ) In a misdemeanor case the re-
quirement that instructions are to be
given in writingmay be waived; unless
there has been such a waiver , the giv-
ing of unwritten instructions is ground
for reversal . Adams Ex . Co. v . Com .,
163 Ky . 275, 173 S. W. 764.
(56b ) On a trial for felony, the in-
structionsby consentwere given orally,
and were taken down by a stenographer,
and were reduced to writing, and were
in writingdeliveredto the jury before
a verdict was returned . In this there
was no error. Spence v . Com ., 181 Ky .
206,204 S. W. 80.
(56c) The trialcourt should in every
felony case submit its instructions to
the jury in writing; yet defendantmay ,
by agreement , waive the Code provision
by consenting that instructions be oral,
and such an agreement will be binding
on him . Spence v. Com ., 181 Ку . 206.
(57) Presumption that instruction
prejudicial. "The correct rule, we think ,
is that every improper instructionshould
be taken as prima facieprejudicialto
the accused , subject of course to be
shown otherwise by the evidence in the
case." Barnett v. Com ., 84 Ky . 449, 8 R.
764

cation to reduce crime to manslaughter .

§ 227. (1) Argument . As to the power
of the trialcourt to limit the argument ,
the court says : "The argument of a case
may be reasonably limited.but where the
libertyor lifeof the citizenis involved
it is a power to be carefullyexercised.
It would ,perhaps ,be best in capitalcases
that this power should never be exer-
cised, or, if so, only under peculiarand
extraordinary circumstances." Williams
v . Com ., 82 Ky . 640, 6 R. 764 ; Sewell v.
Com .,3 R. 86 ; but court will not reverse

149 S. W. 861 .

tainingthe law; but if they disregard

accused, the judge can not forthat rea-
